topics.title: Origin of Petroleum, Oil and Gas Traps, Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Crude Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Origin of Petroleum, Oil and Gas Traps, Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Crude Oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Origin of Petroleum — The Foundational Concept

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
**Petroleum** (from Latin *petra* = rock + *oleum* = oil) is a naturally occurring, flammable, complex mixture of hydrocarbons—primarily alkanes (paraffins), cycloalkanes (naphthenes), and aromatics—along with minor amounts of sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen compounds, and trace metals. It exists in liquid (crude oil), gaseous (natural gas), and solid (bitumen/asphalt) phases within porous subsurface geological formations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Petroleum is a fossil fuel of biogenic origin, formed over millions of years through the anaerobic decomposition of buried organic matter (predominantly marine plankton, algae, and terrestrial plant debris) under controlled thermal maturation regimes within sedimentary basins."*

### The Two Competing Origin Theories

#### A) Organic Theory (Universally Accepted Today — 99% Geologists)
The hydrocarbons were formed from **once-living organisms** buried under sediments millions of years ago.

#### B) Inorganic Theory (Abiogenic — Historically Proposed by Mendeleev & Berthelot)
Hydrocarbons formed from deep-Earth carbides reacting with water at extreme temperatures. *Rejected for surface deposits but partially explains deep mantle methane.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Organic Theory Wins in Kerala Board Examinations:**
> - Explains the presence of **optical activity** (chirality) in crude oil—only biological molecules exhibit this.
> - Explains the **biomarker molecules** (e.g., porphyrins derived from chlorophyll) found in crude.
> - Correlates geographically with sedimentary basins (Kerala's coastal sedimentary belt, Bombay High offshore basin).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Compost Bin" Model
Imagine you bury vegetable scraps (organic matter) deep in a sealed earthen pot (sediment), under heavy stones (pressure), and leave the pot on a slow-heated stove (geothermal gradient ≈ **30 °C/km**) for **10–300 million years**. What comes out is a thick, dark, energy-rich liquid—essentially "fossilized sunlight." That is petroleum.

The four required ingredients in the "recipe":
1. **Source material** (organic-rich sediment — kerogen precursor)
2. **Heat** (geothermal gradient — ~60–150 °C optimal window)
3. **Pressure** (lithostatic + tectonic stress)
4. **Time** (geological time scale ≥ 1 million years)

### The Three Stages of Petroleum Formation (The Kerogen → Oil/Gas Pathway)

| Stage | Process | Temperature | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diagenesis** | Microbial/bacterial decay of organic matter in shallow sediment | 0–60 °C | **Kerogen** (insoluble organic solid) |
| **Catagenesis** | Thermal cracking of kerogen by burial heat | 60–150 °C | **Crude Oil + Wet Gas** |
| **Metagenesis** | Further cracking at greater depths | >150 °C | **Dry Methane Gas only** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Oil Window"** is the temperature range where maximum liquid hydrocarbon generation occurs: **60 °C – 150 °C** (typically at burial depths of **1–4 km**). Beyond this, only dry gas remains. This is a frequent KTU 2-mark and 7-mark question.

### Kerogen Types — The Four Organic Families

| Kerogen Type | Source Material | Hydrogen/Carbon Ratio | Produces |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** (Algal) | Algae, lacustrine deposits | High (H/C > 1.5) | Excellent oil |
| **Type II** (Marine) | Marine plankton, spores | High (H/C ≈ 1.2–1.5) | Good oil + gas |
| **Type III** (Humic) | Terrestrial plants, coal | Lower (H/C < 1.0) | Mainly gas |
| **Type IV** (Inert) | Oxidized/recycled carbon | Very low | None (dead carbon) |

### Migration: From Source Rock to Reservoir

- **Primary Migration (Expulsion):** Oil/gas squeezes out of the fine-grained **source rock** (e.g., shale) due to compaction.
- **Secondary Migration (Buoyancy-Driven):** Hydrocarbons rise through permeable carrier beds (e.g., sandstone) due to **buoyancy** (oil/gas are lighter than formation water).
- **Accumulation:** Migration halts when hydrocarbons encounter an impermeable **cap rock** (e.g., shale, salt) above a porous **reservoir rock** (e.g., sandstone, limestone) → forms an **oil/gas trap**.

### The Petroleum System — Essential Elements
For a working petroleum reservoir to exist, **FIVE** elements and **TWO** processes must align:

**Elements:** Source Rock, Reservoir Rock, Cap Rock, Trap, Hydrocarbon Charge
**Processes:** Generation, Migration

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Context:** The Kerala-Konkan offshore basin and the onshore sedimentary tracts of the state are areas of petroleum geological interest studied at the KTU level. Bombay High (Mumbai High) is the closest major Indian analogue referenced in KTU question papers.

---

## 1.2 Oil and Gas Traps — Where Hydrocarbons Accumulate

### Formal Definition
A **hydrocarbon trap** is a geometric configuration of subsurface rock layers (reservoir + cap) that prevents further migration of buoyant hydrocarbons, allowing them to accumulate in economically recoverable volumes.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Inverted Cup Underwater" Model
Imagine pouring oil into a swimming pool: oil (lighter than water) floats up but gets trapped if you hold an inverted cup over it. The cup = **cap rock**, the air inside the cup = **trap**, the oil pooled at the cup's roof = **hydrocarbon reservoir**. The deeper reservoir rock (porous sandstone) acts like a sponge holding the oil.

### The Two Master Categories of Traps

#### 1. Structural Traps (formed by rock deformation)
#### 2. Stratigraphic Traps (formed by sedimentation/depositional changes)

---

## 1.3 Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Crude Oil

### Why These Properties Matter in Refinery Instrumentation
A refinery's **entire instrument architecture** (distillation column temperature profile, desalter chemistry, reformer catalyst selection, viscosity controllers) is dictated by the **physical and chemical fingerprint** of the incoming crude. Hence this topic is the foundation for PEICT631.

### Key Physical Properties (Each paired with the instrument that measures it)

| Property | Definition | Typical Range | Measuring Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|
| **API Gravity** | Inverse density scale, °API = (141.5/SG) − 131.5 | 10° (heavy) to 45° (light) | Hydrometer / Densitometer |
| **Specific Gravity (SG)** | Ratio of oil density to water density at 15.6 °C | 0.7 – 1.0 | Pycnometer, Oscillating U-tube |
| **Pour Point** | Lowest temperature at which oil flows | −36 °C to +32 °C | ASTM D97 Pour Point Apparatus |
| **Flash Point** | Temp at which vapors ignite momentarily | < 0 °C (gasoline) to > 200 °C (residual) | Pensky-Martens Closed Cup |
| **Fire Point** | Temp at which vapors sustain combustion | Flash + 5–30 °C | Open Cup Tester |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | Resistance to flow at given T | 0.5 – 10,000 cSt | Capillary viscometer / Saybolt |
| **Sulfur Content** | Mass % of sulfur compounds | 0.1 % (sweet) to 5 % (sour) | XRF Analyzer, LECO Sulfur Analyzer |
| **Water & Sediment** | % of BS&W (Bottom Sediment & Water) | < 1 % pipeline spec | Centrifuge (ASTM D1796) |
| **Salt Content** | mg/L of chlorides | < 50 mg/L for refinery | Conductometric / Titration |
| **Carbon Residue** | Coke-forming tendency (Conradson/Ramsbottom) | 1 – 20 wt % | Conradson Apparatus |
| **Wax Content** | % of long-chain paraffins crystallizing on cooling | 0 – 30 % | UOP Method 46 |
| **Asphaltene Content** | n-heptane insoluble fraction | 0 – 20 % | IP 143 |

### Chemical Composition (Elemental & Molecular)

**Elemental Composition (typical):**
- Carbon (C): **83 – 87 wt%**
- Hydrogen (H): **11 – 14 wt%**
- Sulfur (S): **0.1 – 5 wt%**
- Nitrogen (N): **0.1 – 1.5 wt%**
- Oxygen (O): **0.1 – 2 wt%**
- Metals (Ni, V, Fe): **< 1000 ppm**

**Molecular (Hydrocarbon Family) Composition:**
1. **Paraffins (Alkanes)** — straight or branched saturated chains (C₁ to C₆₀⁺). n-Paraffins give good cetane number; iso-paraffins give better octane.
2. **Naphthenes (Cycloalkanes)** — saturated rings (cyclopentane, cyclohexane). Improve diesel quality.
3. **Aromatics** — benzene, toluene, xylenes (BTX) and polycyclic aromatics. High octane but poor smoke point in kerosene.
4. **Olefins (Alkenes)** — unsaturated; *almost absent in crude* (form during catalytic cracking).
5. **Resins & Asphaltenes** — high-MW polar aromatics containing N, S, O; cause fouling and catalyst poisoning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SARA Analysis** (Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, Asphaltenes) is the standard 4-fraction chemical characterization that KTU frequently tests as a 7-mark analytical question.

---

## 1.4 The "Refinery Angle" — Why Module 1 Starts Here

The course PEICT631 is *Refinary Instrumentation*. The reason Module 1 begins with crude oil fundamentals is the **input-output dependency**:
- **Input properties** (API, S%, salt%, pour point) → **dictate** the entire refinery configuration → **dictate** the instrumentation suite (level, flow, temperature, analytical sensors) required to safely and economically process it.

Thus, every instrument you will study later in this course (e.g., a sulfur analyzer in Module 3, a distillation column DPC in Module 4) is selected based on the physical/chemical property it monitors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geological Time-Chronology of Petroleum

Petroleum formation is not an instantaneous process—it is a **three-stage thermal maturation pathway** that requires millions of years. Understanding the depth-temperature-time relationship is crucial because KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is oil found at depths of 1–4 km but not deeper?"*

### Step-by-Step Thermal Maturation Logic

**Step 1 — Deposition of Organic Matter (Biogenic Phase)**
- Dead plankton, algae, spores, and terrestrial plant debris accumulate on the floors of anoxic (oxygen-poor) aquatic basins.
- Anoxic conditions prevent complete oxidation to CO₂.
- Mixed with fine clays and silts, the organic ooze begins compacting into **sapropel**.

**Step 2 — Diagenesis (0–60 °C, 0–1 km depth)**
- Bacterial action continues. Complex biopolymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) break down into **humic substances** and ultimately into **kerogen**.
- Kerogen is a **geopolymer**—insoluble in organic solvents, dark brown to black, with H/C and O/C ratios defining its type (I–IV).

**Step 3 — Catagenesis (60–150 °C, 1–4 km depth) — THE OIL WINDOW**
- Thermal cracking (C–C bond rupture) begins via free-radical mechanisms.
- Kerogen → bitumen → crude oil + wet gas (C₁–C₅⁺).
- Maximum oil generation typically peaks around **100–120 °C**.

**Step 4 — Metagenesis (>150 °C, >4 km depth)**
- Remaining kerogen and oil crack further into **dry gas** (mostly methane).
- Below ~200 °C, only graphite and methane survive.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Logical Hook:** The "Oil Window" concept is the answer to the question *"Why deep wells (e.g., Bhubaneswar's KG Basin at 3 km) find oil but ultra-deep wells (Koyna at 5+ km) often find only gas or nothing?"*

---

## 2.2 Trap Classification — Engineering-Grade Analysis

### A) Structural Traps (formed by tectonic deformation of strata)

| Trap Subtype | Geometry | Real-World Example | Formation Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Anticlinal Trap** | Upward arch of strata | Ghawar Field (Saudi Arabia), Assam | Compressional folding |
| **Fault Trap** | Strata displaced along a fracture plane | Los Angeles Basin fields | Normal/reverse fault seals migration |
| **Salt Dome Trap** | Piercement of diapiric salt | Gulf of Mexico, Texas | Buoyant salt pierces overburden |
| **Fracture Trap** | Hydrocarbons held in fracture porosity | Spraberry Trend, Texas | Brittle rock fracturing |

### B) Stratigraphic Traps (formed by depositional or erosional variability)

| Trap Subtype | Geometry | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Lens (Sandstone) Trap** | Porous lens pinches out in shale | Many channel sandstones |
| **Unconformity Trap** | Erosion surface truncates permeable bed | East Texas Field |
| **Reef Trap** | Porous carbonate reef in impermeable shale | Alberta (Canada), Leduc |
| **Tar Mat Trap** | Heavy asphalt seals lighter oil below | Some California fields |

### C) Combination Traps
Real reservoirs often combine structural + stratigraphic elements (e.g., anticlinal closure modified by facies change). These are the most common in mature basins.

---

## 2.3 Physical Property Theory & Equations

### A) API Gravity — The Industry Standard Density Scale
Developed by the American Petroleum Institute, it is **inversely** related to specific gravity. Lighter crudes (higher API) are more valuable because they yield more gasoline and diesel.

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60°F}} - 131.5$$

where $SG_{60/60°F}$ is the specific gravity at 60 °F (15.6 °C) referenced to water at the same temperature.

**Classification by API Gravity:**
- Light Crude: **> 31.1 °API** (e.g., Brent at 38°)
- Medium Crude: **22.3 – 31.1 °API**
- Heavy Crude: **10 – 22.3 °API** (e.g., Mexican Maya at 22°)
- Extra Heavy: **< 10 °API** (e.g., Venezuelan Orinoco Bitumen)

### B) Density from API
$$SG = \frac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5}$$

### C) Specific Gravity to Density (kg/m³)
$$\rho_{\text{oil}} = SG \times \rho_{\text{water at }T} = SG \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \text{ (at 4 °C)}$$

### D) Pour Point Theory
Pour point = (Wax Appearance Temperature) − typically **2–5 °C**.
Pour point is governed by the **n-paraffin content** and their carbon chain length. Long-chain n-paraffins (C₂₀⁺) crystallize first, immobilizing the oil.

### E) Flash Point & Fire Point (Fire Safety Theory)
- **Flash Point** = lowest T where vapor + air mixture above oil surface ignites momentarily when exposed to a flame.
- **Fire Point** = lowest T where vapor continues to burn for ≥ 5 seconds.

Difference: **Fire Point − Flash Point = 5–30 °C** (varies with crude).

### F) Watson Characterization Factor (K_W or UOP K)
Aromaticity index. Used to predict yield structure of crude.

$$K_W = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG}$$

where $T_B$ is the **mean average boiling point** in **°R (Rankine)**.

| K_W Value | Crude Type | Yield Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **10 – 10.5** | Paraffinic (light) | High gasoline |
| **11 – 11.5** | Paraffinic-naphthenic | Balanced |
| **12+** | Highly aromatic/naphthenic | Lube oil, asphalt |

### G) Viscosity Conversion (Saybolt Universal Seconds → Kinematic)
$$\nu \text{ (cSt)} = 0.226 \times t_S - \frac{195}{t_S}$$

where $t_S$ is Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS) measured at the same temperature. Valid for $t_S$ between 32 and 100 seconds.

### H) Sulfur Classification
- **Sweet Crude:** S content < **0.5 wt%** (preferred by refineries)
- **Sour Crude:** S content > **0.5 wt%** (requires hydrodesulfurization HDS unit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 0.5 wt% sulfur threshold is a hard KTU fact.** Memorize it.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute-value notation to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $^\circ\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | API gravity from specific gravity | 10° to 45° |
| 2 | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5}$ | Specific gravity from °API | 0.7 to 1.0 |
| 3 | $\rho_{\text{oil}} = SG \cdot \rho_w$ | Density of oil | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $K_W = \dfrac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG}$ | Watson Characterization Factor | 10 – 13 (dimensionless) |
| 5 | $\nu \text{ (cSt)} = 0.226 t_S - \dfrac{195}{t_S}$ | Kinematic viscosity from SUS | cSt |
| 6 | $T_{\text{optimum oil gen}} = 100 - 120\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Oil window peak | °C |
| 7 | Geothermal gradient $= 30\ ^\circ\text{C/km}$ | Average crustal gradient | °C/km |
| 8 | $T_{pour} = T_{WAT} - \Delta T$ | Pour point relation | °C ($\Delta T = 2$ to $5\ ^\circ\text{C}$) |
| 9 | Sulfur Sweet Limit $= 0.5\ \text{wt\%}$ | Sweet vs Sour crude threshold | wt% |
| 10 | C + H $\approx$ 97 – 99 wt% | Sum of C and H in crude | wt% |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of these concepts |
|---|---|
| **Refinery Process Selection** | Light sweet crude → simple atmospheric distillation. Heavy sour → requires coker, hydrocracker, HDS. |
| **Pipeline Design** | API determines pumping cost; pour point determines need for heating/insulation. |
| **Storage Tank Selection** | API ≤ 22 → fixed cone roof; high vapor pressure → floating roof. |
| **Catalyst Selection** | High metals (Ni+V) → catalyst poisoning; need guard beds. |
| **Refinery Instrumentation** | Each property above maps to a specific online analyzer (densitometer, viscometer, NIR analyzer, sulfur analyzer). |
| **Blending Operations** | Refinery blend optimization uses API, sulfur, and octane as primary constraints. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity ↔ Specific Gravity Equivalence

**Starting Point:** API defined by the American Petroleum Institute as a hyperbolic function of specific gravity, designed so that:
- Water has °API = **10**
- Lighter liquids give *higher* °API
- The scale resembles the **Baumé scale** but is reversed in sense

**Given Definition:**

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$

**Step 1:** Add 131.5 to both sides:

$$^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5 = \frac{141.5}{SG}$$

**Step 2:** Invert both sides:

$$\frac{SG}{1} = \frac{1}{\left(^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5\right) / 141.5} = \frac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;SG = \frac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5}\;}$$

**Verification:**
- For water: $SG = 1.0$, so °API = 141.5/1 − 131.5 = 141.5 − 131.5 = **10** ✓
- For oil with SG = 0.85: °API = 141.5/0.85 − 131.5 = 166.47 − 131.5 = **34.97 ≈ 35 °API** ✓ (Light crude)

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — API Gravity Conversion

**Problem:** A crude oil sample has a measured density of **875 kg/m³** at 15.6 °C. Calculate its API gravity and classify it.

**Given:** $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 875\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{\text{water at }15.6°C} = 999\ \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1:** Specific Gravity:

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{875}{999}$$

$$SG = 0.8759 \approx 0.876$$

**Step 2:** API Gravity:

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 = \frac{141.5}{0.876} - 131.5$$

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = 161.53 - 131.5 = 30.03\ ^\circ\text{API}$$

**Step 3:** Classification:

Since **22.3 < 30.03 < 31.1**, this is a **Medium Crude**, marginally close to light crude threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tags API calculations with `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`-style headers. Practice rounding SG to 3 decimals before final answer.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Watson Characterization Factor

**Problem:** A crude oil has a specific gravity of **0.892** and a mean average boiling point of **620 °F**. Calculate the Watson K-factor and identify the crude type.

**Given:** $SG = 0.892$, $T_B = 620\ ^\circ\text{F}$

**Step 1:** Convert boiling point to Rankine:

$$T_B\ (^\circ R) = T_B\ (^\circ F) + 460 = 620 + 460 = 1080\ ^\circ R$$

**Step 2:** Compute cube root:

$$\sqrt[3]{T_B} = \sqrt[3]{1080} = (1080)^{1/3}$$

Using logarithms: $\log(1080) = 3.0334$; $(1/3)\cdot 3.0334 = 1.0111$; $10^{1.0111} = 10.26$

$$\sqrt[3]{1080} \approx 10.26$$

**Step 3:** Watson K:

$$K_W = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG} = \frac{10.26}{0.892}$$

$$\boxed{\;K_W \approx 11.50\;}$$

**Step 4:** Crude Type:
Since $K_W = 11.50$ falls in the range **11–11.5**, the crude is **Paraffinic–Naphthenic** with balanced yield structure.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Saybolt-to-Kinematic Viscosity Conversion

**Problem:** At 100 °F, a lube-oil stock has Saybolt Universal Viscosity of **210 seconds**. Compute the kinematic viscosity in centistokes.

**Given:** $t_S = 210\ \text{SUS}$

**Step 1:** Apply conversion formula:

$$\nu = 0.226 \cdot t_S - \frac{195}{t_S}$$

$$\nu = 0.226 \times 210 - \frac{195}{210}$$

$$\nu = 47.46 - 0.9286$$

$$\nu = 46.53\ \text{cSt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For $t_S > 100$ SUS, a different kinematic form is used: $\nu = 0.226 t_S - 0.60$. KTU boards typically only test the $32 \le t_S \le 100$ range, but for completeness we used the standard form above for $t_S = 210$ which actually exceeds the primary formula range; the alternate formula gives: $\nu = 0.226(210) - 0.60 = 47.46 - 0.60 = 46.86\ \text{cSt}$. Both values are within typical experimental scatter.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Oil Window Depth Calculation

**Problem:** Using an average geothermal gradient of **30 °C/km** and a surface mean annual temperature of **25 °C**, determine the depth range corresponding to the **Oil Window (60–150 °C)**.

**Given:** $G = 30\ ^\circ\text{C/km}$, $T_0 = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$

**Step 1:** Depth-temperature relationship:

$$T(z) = T_0 + G \cdot z$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $z$ at $T = 60\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (start of oil window):

$$60 = 25 + 30 \cdot z$$

$$z = \frac{60 - 25}{30} = \frac{35}{30} = 1.167\ \text{km} \approx 1167\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $z$ at $T = 150\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (end of oil window):

$$150 = 25 + 30 \cdot z$$

$$z = \frac{150 - 25}{30} = \frac{125}{30} = 4.167\ \text{km} \approx 4167\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4:** Depth Window:

$$\boxed{\;z_{\text{oil window}} = 1.17\ \text{km} \text{ to } 4.17\ \text{km}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU answer must clearly state: *At depths shallower than 1.17 km, kerogen is immature (diagenesis). Between 1.17–4.17 km, oil generation peaks. Deeper than 4.17 km, only dry methane gas (metagenesis) survives.*

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Sulfur Content Mass Balance (Sour vs Sweet)

**Problem:** A refinery blends **5000 m³/day** of sweet crude (S = 0.20 wt%) with **3000 m³/day** of sour crude (S = 1.80 wt%). The crude densities are 0.85 and 0.92 g/cm³ respectively. Determine the sulfur content of the blend (wt%).

**Given:**
- Stream A: $Q_A = 5000\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $S_A = 0.20\%$, $\rho_A = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Stream B: $Q_B = 3000\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $S_B = 1.80\%$, $\rho_B = 920\ \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1:** Mass flow of each stream:

$$\dot{m}_A = 5000 \times 850 = 4.25 \times 10^6\ \text{kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_B = 3000 \times 920 = 2.76 \times 10^6\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 2:** Sulfur mass in each:

$$\dot{m}_{S,A} = 4.25 \times 10^6 \times 0.0020 = 8500\ \text{kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{S,B} = 2.76 \times 10^6 \times 0.0180 = 49{,}680\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Total sulfur and total mass:

$$\dot{m}_{S,\text{total}} = 8500 + 49{,}680 = 58{,}180\ \text{kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{total}} = 4.25 \times 10^6 + 2.76 \times 10^6 = 7.01 \times 10^6\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4:** Blend sulfur content:

$$S_{\text{blend}} = \frac{58{,}180}{7.01 \times 10^6} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\;S_{\text{blend}} = 0.830\ \text{wt\%}\;}$$

**Step 5:** Classification:
Since $S > 0.5\%$, the blend is classified as **Sour Crude** — HDS unit mandatory.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Problem Map

| Problem Type | Given | Required Formula | KTU Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| °API ↔ SG conversion | Either value | $^\circ\text{API} = 141.5/SG - 131.5$ | 3 marks |
| K_W calculation | T_B, SG | $K_W = \sqrt[3]{T_B}/SG$ | 3 marks |
| Oil window depth | Geothermal gradient | $z = (T - T_0)/G$ | 7 marks |
| Sulfur classification | S wt% | Compare with 0.5% | 3 marks |
| Viscosity conversion | t_S (SUS) | $\nu = 0.226 t_S - 195/t_S$ | 3 marks |
| Trap identification | Stratigraphic column | Match geometry to category | 7 marks |
| Pour point relation | T_WAT | $T_{pour} = T_{WAT} - \Delta T$ | 3 marks |
| Blend property | Two streams | Mass-weighted average | 7 marks |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Petroleum Formation & Migration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    orgM[Organic Matter Dead Plankton and Algae] --> depo[Deposition in Anoxic Basin]
    depo --> sed[Sediment Burial Compaction]
    sed --> diag[Diagenesis 0 to 60 C Kerogen Formation]
    diag --> cat[Catagenesis 60 to 150 C Oil Window Thermal Cracking]
    cat --> meta[Metagenesis above 150 C Dry Gas Only]
    cat --> priMig[Primary Migration Expulsion from Source Rock]
    priMig --> secMig[Secondary Migration Buoyancy Rise through Carrier Bed]
    secMig --> trap[Hydrocarbon Trap Accumulation under Cap Rock]
    trap --> res[Reservoir Rock Sandstone or Limestone]
    meta --> gasOnly[Deep Dry Methane Accumulation]
    trap --> well[Production Well Drilling]
    well --> ref[Refinery Crude Oil Input]
    ref --> prod[Refined Products Gasoline Diesel Jet Fuel]
    style orgM fill:#cce5ff
    style cat fill:#ffe680
    style meta fill:#ffcccc
    style trap fill:#b3ffb3
    style ref fill:#ffcc99
    style prod fill:#d9b3ff
```

## 4.2 Trap Architecture — Structural vs Stratigraphic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Structural["STRUCTURAL TRAPS"]
        A1[Anticlinal Trap Upward Arch] --> A2[Reservoir Rock Sandstone]
        A2 --> A3[Cap Rock Shale Seal]
        A3 --> A4[Hydrocarbon Accumulation]
    end
    subgraph Fault["FAULT TRAP"]
        F1[Normal Fault Plane] --> F2[Displaced Permeable Strata]
        F2 --> F3[Impermeable Block Across Fault]
        F3 --> F4[Hydrocarbon Pool Against Fault]
    end
    subgraph Salt["SALT DOME TRAP"]
        S1[Buoyant Salt Diapir Pierces Overburden] --> S2[Reservoir Rock Draped Over Salt]
        S2 --> S3[Cap Rock Shale above Reservoir]
        S3 --> S4[Hydrocarbon Accumulation Flanks of Salt]
    end
    subgraph Strati["STRATIGRAPHIC TRAPS"]
        T1[Sandstone Lens Pinching out in Shale] --> T2[Hydrocarbon Trapped in Lens]
        U1[Unconformity Erosion Surface] --> U2[Truncated Permeable Bed]
        U2 --> U3[Impermeable Layer above Unconformity]
        U3 --> U4[Hydrocarbon Pool below Unconformity]
        R1[Reef Buildup Carbonate] --> R2[Porous Reef Encased in Shale]
        R2 --> R3[Hydrocarbon Pool in Reef]
    end
    style Structural fill:#e6f3ff
    style Fault fill:#e6f3ff
    style Salt fill:#e6f3ff
    style Strati fill:#fff0e6
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define API gravity. A crude oil has specific gravity 0.886. Calculate its API gravity and classify it as light, medium, or heavy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** API (American Petroleum Institute) gravity is an inverse measure of crude oil density relative to water, expressed in degrees. The higher the °API, the lighter the crude.

**Formula:**

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$

**Calculation:**

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.886} - 131.5 = 159.71 - 131.5 = 28.21\ ^\circ\text{API}$$

**Classification:**
- Light Crude: °API > 31.1
- Medium Crude: 22.3 ≤ °API ≤ 31.1
- Heavy Crude: 10 ≤ °API ≤ 22.3

Since 22.3 < 28.21 < 31.1, the crude is classified as **Medium Crude**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between sweet and sour crude. State the sulfur threshold used in the industry.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Sweet Crude | Sour Crude |
|---|---|---|
| Sulfur Content (wt%) | Less than 0.5 % | Greater than or equal to 0.5 % |
| Refining Cost | Lower | Higher (needs HDS unit) |
| Hydrogen Consumption | Lower | Higher (for desulfurization) |
| Catalyst Life | Longer | Shorter (sulfur poisoning) |
| Market Price | Premium | Discounted |
| Corrosion in Pipelines | Low | High (forms H₂S) |

**Threshold:** **0.5 wt%** is the internationally accepted industry threshold (KTU 2024 syllabus-mandated value).

Sweet crudes are preferred because they require simpler, cheaper processing. Sour crudes are increasingly processed due to depleting sweet reserves, but require HDS (Hydrodesulfurization) units.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **organic theory of petroleum formation**. Describe the three stages of thermal maturation with temperature ranges and end products. What is the significance of the "Oil Window"?

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat sketch, explain the different **types of structural and stratigraphic hydrocarbon traps** giving one example of each.

---

#### Part (a) — Detailed Model Solution

**1. Introduction to Organic Theory [1 mark]**
The organic theory (proposed by Engler in 1911, later refined by Treibs via porphyrin biomarker discovery) states that petroleum originates from **once-living organic matter**—primarily marine plankton, algae, and terrestrial plant debris—buried in anoxic sedimentary basins and transformed by heat and pressure over geological timescales.

**2. Source Material [1 mark]**
The principal precursors are:
- **Marine plankton & algae** (Type I and II kerogen) — the dominant source.
- **Terrestrial plant debris** (Type III kerogen) — secondary, gas-prone.
- **Bacterial biomass** (Type II kerogen) — minor contributor.

These materials accumulate on the floors of shallow seas, deep ocean basins, or lakes where oxygen is depleted (anoxic conditions prevent complete oxidation to CO₂).

**3. Three Stages of Thermal Maturation [4 marks]**

| Stage | Depth Range | Temperature Range | Mechanism | Products |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Diagenesis | 0 – 1 km | 0 – 60 °C | Bacterial decay, microbial action | Kerogen (insoluble organic solid) |
| Catagenesis (Oil Window) | 1 – 4 km | 60 – 150 °C | Thermal cracking via free radicals | Crude oil + Wet gas |
| Metagenesis | > 4 km | > 150 °C | High-temperature cracking | Dry methane gas + graphite |

**4. Significance of the Oil Window [1 mark]**
The Oil Window (60–150 °C) defines the temperature-depth regime where **maximum liquid hydrocarbon generation** occurs. Geologists use this window to identify prospective exploration zones. Drilling outside the oil window (too shallow = immature kerogen, too deep = only dry gas) is economically wasteful.

---

#### Part (b) — Detailed Model Solution

**Hydrocarbon Trap Classification:**

**A. Structural Traps (formed by deformation of strata):**
1. **Anticlinal Trap:** Upward fold of strata; hydrocarbons migrate upward and accumulate at the crest of the arch. Example: **Ghawar Field (Saudi Arabia)**, the world's largest onshore oil field.
2. **Fault Trap:** Impermeable fault block seals the migration path of hydrocarbons rising through permeable strata. Example: Many fields in the **Los Angeles Basin**.
3. **Salt Dome Trap:** A buoyant salt diapir rises and pierces overlying strata, deforming them into traps on the salt's flanks. Example: **Gulf of Mexico (USA)**.

**B. Stratigraphic Traps (formed by depositional variation):**
1. **Sandstone Lens Trap:** A porous sandstone body pinches out laterally into impermeable shale, sealing hydrocarbons inside. Example: Channel sandstones in **Alberta, Canada**.
2. **Unconformity Trap:** A buried erosional surface truncates a permeable layer, with impermeable strata sealing it above. Example: **East Texas Field**.
3. **Reef Trap:** A porous carbonate reef buildup is encased in impermeable shale. Example: **Leduc Field, Alberta**.

*(Sketch to be drawn on answer script: simple cross-section showing permeable reservoir layer sealed by cap rock in each trap type.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **physical properties of crude oil** that are most important in refinery design and operation. Explain the API gravity, pour point, and flash point in detail with their measurement methods.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** What is **SARA analysis**? Explain the four fractions obtained and their industrial significance in refinery operations.

---

#### Part (a) — Detailed Model Solution

**Introduction [1 mark]**
Crude oil's physical properties dictate every aspect of refinery design—from storage tank selection to distillation column temperature profile. Three of the most critical properties are API gravity, pour point, and flash point.

**1. API Gravity [2 marks]**
- **Definition:** Inverse density scale (°API) measuring how heavy or light a petroleum liquid is compared to water.
- **Formula:**

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60°F}} - 131.5$$

- **Significance:** Lighter crudes (higher °API) yield more gasoline/diesel; heavier crudes (lower °API) yield more residual fuel oil and asphalt.
- **Classification:** Light (> 31.1°), Medium (22.3 – 31.1°), Heavy (< 22.3°), Extra Heavy (< 10°).
- **Measurement:** Hydrometer (manual) or oscillating U-tube digital densitometer (online).

**2. Pour Point [2 marks]**
- **Definition:** The lowest temperature at which the oil will flow under standard test conditions.
- **Mechanism:** Governed by **n-paraffin (wax) crystallization**. Long-chain n-paraffins (C₂₀⁺) freeze first and immobilize the oil.
- **Significance:** Critical for pipeline transport in cold climates (e.g., North Sea, Alaska). Determines need for heated pipelines or pour-point depressant additives.
- **Measurement:** ASTM D97 standard method; oil is cooled in steps and tilted every 3 °C until it does not flow.
- **Typical Range:** −36 °C to +32 °C.

**3. Flash Point [2 marks]**
- **Definition:** The lowest temperature at which vapors above the oil momentarily ignite (flash) when exposed to an ignition source.
- **Significance:** Governs **fire safety classification** of crude storage and transport (PESO regulations, IMDG code). Light crudes have low flash points (fire hazard); heavy crudes/residuals have high flash points.
- **Measurement:** **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)** apparatus per ASTM D93. The closed cup prevents vapor loss, giving a more realistic (lower) flash point than open cup.
- **Typical Values:** Gasoline: < 0 °C; Diesel: 52–96 °C; Residual fuel oil: > 100 °C.

---

#### Part (b) — Detailed Model Solution

**SARA Analysis [7 Marks]**

**Definition [1 mark]:**
SARA (Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, Asphaltenes) is a **four-fraction chemical separation** of crude oil based on polarity and solubility. It is the standard compositional fingerprint used in refinery process selection and crude oil characterization.

**Procedure Overview [1 mark]:**
Crude is first deasphaltened using **n-heptane** (insoluble = asphaltene). The remaining maltenes are separated by **column chromatography** (silica gel + alumina) using solvents of increasing polarity: n-heptane → toluene → dichloromethane/methanol mixture.

**The Four Fractions [4 marks]:**

| Fraction | Polarity | Composition | % in Crude | Industrial Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Saturates** | Non-polar | n-Paraffins, iso-paraffins, naphthenes | 60 – 90 % | Highest quality feedstock for diesel, jet fuel, lube base oils |
| **Aromatics** | Weakly polar | BTEX, naphthalenes, polycyclic aromatics | 10 – 30 % | Octane boosters in gasoline; BTX extraction unit feedstock |
| **Resins** | Polar | Polar aromatics with N, S, O heteroatoms | 1 – 10 % | Stabilize emulsions (problem in desalter); contribute to gum formation |
| **Asphaltenes** | Highly polar, high MW | Polycyclic aromatics, N, S, O, metals (Ni, V) | 1 – 20 % | Coke precursor in thermal processes (coker, visbreaker); catalyst poison; cause fouling |

**Refinery Significance [1 mark]:**
- High saturates + low asphaltenes → premium light crude, easy processing.
- High asphaltenes + high metals → requires severe hydrotreating or coking.
- SARA composition determines **crude compatibility** for blending—blending incompatible crudes can precipitate asphaltenes, causing fouling and shutdown.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Mark-Loss Triggers — Read Carefully!**
>
> **1. Missing the 0.5 wt% sulfur threshold** — Many students write "sour crude contains more sulfur" without stating the exact 0.5 % numerical cutoff. **Lose 1 mark** for not quoting the industry threshold.
>
> **2. Forgetting the °API = 10 for water sanity check** — If your formula manipulation yields °API = 0 for water, you have inverted the equation. Examiners look for a "verification step" that plugs back SG = 1 → °API = 10. Always include this.
>
> **3. Confusing Flash Point with Fire Point** — Flash = momentary ignition; Fire = sustained combustion (≥ 5 s). **Lose 2 marks** if these are interchanged.
>
> **4. Trap classification error** — Many students list "anticline" and "fault" but forget to mention stratigraphic traps entirely. KTU expects at least **two structural AND two stratigraphic** examples for full marks.
>
> **5. Missing the "Oil Window" terminology** — Never just write "petroleum forms at high temperature." Always specify the **60–150 °C range, 1–4 km depth, with the catagenesis stage name**. Examiners reward specific technical vocabulary.
>
> **6. Wrong units in viscosity conversion** — SUS ≠ cSt. The formula $\nu = 0.226 t_S - 195/t_S$ requires $t_S$ in **seconds** and yields $\nu$ in **centistokes**. Wrong units = full marks lost.
>
> **7. Sketches without labels** — In trap questions, a poorly labeled diagram loses 2–3 marks. Always label: **Reservoir Rock, Cap Rock, Source Rock, Hydrocarbon, Migration Direction, Water Leg**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

**Origin of Petroleum:**
- ✅ Organic theory is universally accepted (porphyrin biomarker proof, optical activity)
- ✅ Three stages: Diagenesis (0–60 °C) → Catagenesis (60–150 °C, **Oil Window**) → Metagenesis (>150 °C, dry gas only)
- ✅ Four kerogen types: I (algal, best oil), II (marine, good oil), III (humic, gas), IV (inert, none)
- ✅ Geothermal gradient ≈ **30 °C/km**; surface T ≈ 25 °C → oil window depth = **1.17–4.17 km**
- ✅ Petroleum system = 5 elements (source, reservoir, cap, trap, charge) + 2 processes (generation, migration)

**Oil & Gas Traps:**
- ✅ Structural traps: **Anticline, Fault, Salt Dome, Fracture**
- ✅ Stratigraphic traps: **Lens, Unconformity, Reef, Tar Mat**
- ✅ Combination traps are most common in mature basins
- ✅ Buoyancy-driven migration + impermeable seal = trap formation

**Physical Properties of Crude:**
- ✅ °API formula: $141.5/SG - 131.5$; water = 10 °API; light crude > 31.1 °API
- ✅ Pour point: governed by n-paraffin wax crystallization; ASTM D97
- ✅ Flash point: Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC), ASTM D93
- ✅ Fire point: Flash + 5–30 °C (sustained burning)
- ✅ Viscosity conversion (SUS → cSt): $\nu = 0.226 t_S - 195/t_S$
- ✅ Watson K factor: $K_W = \sqrt[3]{T_B(°R)} / SG$; K > 12 indicates aromatic

**Chemical Properties of Crude:**
- ✅ Sulfur threshold for sweet/sour: **0.5 wt%**
- ✅ Elemental: C (83–87%), H (11–14%), S (0.1–5%), N (0.1–1.5%), O (0.1–2%), metals < 1000 ppm
- ✅ SARA: Saturates (60–90%), Aromatics (10–30%), Resins (1–10%), Asphaltenes (1–20%)
- ✅ Asphaltenes are n-heptane insoluble; resin-asphaltene ratio determines stability
- ✅ Trace metals Ni and V are catalyst poisons; measured by ICP-OES

**Refinery Instrumentation Hooks:**
- ✅ Each property has a corresponding online analyzer in modern DCS-controlled refineries
- ✅ Properties are continuously monitored at the refinery gate before crude is allocated to processing units
- ✅ API gravity → determines whether crude goes to simple CDU or complex refining scheme with coker/HDS

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize **five numerical values** cold: (1) °API = 10 for water; (2) Sulfur threshold = 0.5 wt%; (3) Geothermal gradient = 30 °C/km; (4) Oil Window = 60–150 °C; (5) Watson K for paraffinic ≈ 12, aromatic ≈ 10. These five facts alone can rescue 5–7 marks across any combination of questions on this topic.

topics.title: Source Rock and Maturation, Migration of Oil Mechanism, Pattern and Barriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Source Rock and Maturation, Migration of Oil Mechanism, Pattern and Barriers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Source Rock — The Geological Cradle of Hydrocarbons

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
A **Source Rock** is a fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically shale, mudstone, marl, or carbonate) that contains sufficient quantities of **organic matter (kerogen)** capable of generating and expelling hydrocarbons upon thermal maturation. The source rock is the *first and most essential element* of a functioning petroleum system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Lock**
> For KTU valuation purposes, a rock qualifies as a source rock **ONLY** if it satisfies all three conditions simultaneously:
> 1. **Quantity** → Adequate Total Organic Carbon (TOC > 0.5 wt%)
> 2. **Quality** → Kerogen of the right type (I, II, or IIS)
> 3. **Maturity** → Subjected to sufficient thermal stress (Vitrinite Reflectance $R_o \geq 0.6\%$)

### Intuitive Analogy
> Think of a source rock as a **slow-cooking pressure cooker buried in the earth**. The organic matter (kerogen) is the "food" inside. Over millions of years, the Earth's internal heat acts as the burner, gradually breaking down the kerogen into crude oil and natural gas — just as a pressure cooker slowly transforms raw ingredients into a finished dish.

### Key Source Rock Parameters (Board-Important)
| Parameter | Symbol | Threshold Value | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Total Organic Carbon | $TOC$ | $\geq 0.5$ (poor), $\geq 2.0$ (good) | wt% |
| Hydrogen Index | $HI$ | $200 - 600$ | mg HC/g TOC |
| Vitrinite Reflectance | $R_o$ | $0.6 - 1.35$ (oil window) | % |
| $T_{max}$ (Rock-Eval Pyrolysis) | $T_{max}$ | $435 - 470$ | °C |
| Kerogen Yield | $S_2$ | $>5$ | mg HC/g rock |

---

## 1.2 Maturation — The Thermal Transformation Engine

### Formal Definition
**Maturation** is the irreversible, thermally-driven process by which kerogen within a source rock is progressively converted into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons through the breaking of carbon-carbon bonds (cracking) as burial depth and temperature increase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerogen** is the insoluble, high-molecular-weight organic matter dispersed in sedimentary rocks. It is the *parent material* of all petroleum; without kerogen, there is no oil or gas.

### The Three Thermal Windows of Maturation
| Stage | Temperature Range | $R_o$ Range | Hydrocarbon Product |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Diagenesis** | $<60°C$ | $< 0.5\%$ | Biogenic methane only |
| **Catagenesis** | $60°C - 175°C$ | $0.5\% - 2.0\%$ | Crude oil → Wet gas → Condensate |
| **Metagenesis** | $>175°C$ | $> 2.0\%$ | Dry methane, graphite residue |

### Intuitive Analogy
> Maturation is analogous to **roasting coffee beans** — a green, useless bean is gradually transformed by heat into a valuable aromatic product. The longer and hotter the "roast" (i.e., the deeper the burial), the darker and more "cooked" the hydrocarbon product becomes, evolving from light crude → heavy crude → wet gas → dry gas → nothing but carbon.

---

## 1.3 Migration of Oil — The Journey to the Reservoir

### Formal Definition
**Migration of Oil** refers to the bulk movement of hydrocarbons from the source rock to a reservoir rock, encompassing three sequential stages: **Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Migration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why migration is necessary:** Oil and gas are generated at depths and pressures where the source rock's pore network is too tight to hold commercial volumes. Expulsion is therefore a thermodynamic and mechanical necessity.

### Three Stages of Migration
1. **Primary Migration (Expulsion):** Hydrocarbons squeezed out of the source rock into adjacent permeable beds. Driven by compaction, pressure, and kerogen network expansion.
2. **Secondary Migration:** Upward/lateral movement of hydrocarbons through **carrier beds** (e.g., porous sandstones) towards a **trap**.
3. **Tertiary Migration:** Re-migration of already-accumulated hydrocarbons due to seal failure, faulting, or biodegradation.

### Intuitive Analogy
> Imagine **honey escaping from a compressed sponge (source rock)**. The honey first oozes out of the sponge (primary migration), then trickles through cracks in a brick wall (carrier beds — secondary migration), and finally pools in a small cavity at the top of the wall (the reservoir trap). Any leak from the cavity represents tertiary migration.

---

## 1.4 Migration Patterns & Barriers

### Patterns
- **Vertical Migration:** Dominated by buoyancy; hydrocarbons move upward against gravity.
- **Lateral Migration:** Along inclined permeable carrier beds from basin depocenters to basin flanks.
- **Fault-Controlled Migration:** Along zones of structural weakness (faults and fractures).
- **Carrier-Bed Controlled:** Migration pathway dictated by geometry of high-permeability strata.

### Barriers (Seal Rocks)
A **Seal Rock** (or *cap rock*) is an impermeable rock that prevents the further upward migration of hydrocarbons, causing them to accumulate in the underlying reservoir.

| Seal Type | Lithology | Sealing Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lithological Seal** | Shale, Mudstone, Salt | Low permeability, high capillary entry pressure |
| **Fault Seal** | Smear of shale along fault plane | Juxtaposition of impermeable against permeable layer |
| **Diagenetic Seal** | Cemented sandstone, dolomitized zone | Pore-blocking cementation |
| **Hydraulic Seal** | Overpressured shale | Pressure differential exceeding hydrocarbon buoyancy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** Migration patterns are governed by the **Force Balance Equation** — hydrocarbons migrate whenever the *driving forces* (buoyancy, pressure gradient) exceed the *resisting forces* (capillary pressure, adsorption, viscosity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Kerogen — The Source of All Petroleum

### Classification of Kerogen (Van Krevelen Diagram Logic)
Kerogen is classified into **four major types** based on atomic H/C and O/C ratios, derived from elemental analysis.

| Type | Origin | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Hydrocarbon Potential |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Type I** | Algal (lacustrine/marine) | $>1.5$ | $<0.1$ | Excellent — oil-prone |
| **Type II** | Marine planktonic | $\approx 1.2$ | $\approx 0.15$ | Very good — oil + gas |
| **Type IIS** | Sulfur-rich marine | $\approx 1.3$ | $\approx 0.15$ | Excellent at low maturity |
| **Type III** | Terrestrial (humic, coaly) | $<1.0$ | $>0.2$ | Gas-prone |
| **Type IV** | Oxidized/recycled | $<0.5$ | Variable | Inert — no HC potential |

---

## 2.2 Mechanisms of Primary Migration (Expulsion)

The four accepted mechanisms for expulsion of hydrocarbons from source rocks are:

1. **Solution/Diffusion Mechanism** — Hydrocarbons dissolve in kerogen-bitumen network and diffuse out.
2. **Molecular Diffusion** — Continuous phase migration through interconnected pores.
3. **Pressure-Driven Expulsion** — Compaction overpressure forces hydrocarbons out of the source.
4. **Network Expansion (Molecular Sieve Effect)** — As kerogen cracks, internal pore network expands, expelling generated hydrocarbons.

---

## 2.3 Mechanisms of Secondary Migration

Secondary migration is fundamentally a **buoyancy-driven, capillary-resistance-controlled** process.

### Driving Forces (Promote Migration)
- **Buoyancy Force:** $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V$
- **Hydrodynamic Force:** Pressure gradient from compaction
- **Expulsion Pressure:** From kerogen cracking

### Resisting Forces (Oppose Migration)
- **Capillary Entry Pressure:** $P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$
- **Adsorption onto mineral surfaces**
- **Frictional/viscous drag**

> Migration occurs when: $$\sum F_{driving} > \sum F_{resisting}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Total Organic Carbon** | $TOC = \dfrac{(C_{total} - C_{inorganic})}{M_{sample}} \times 100$ | Source rock richness |
| 2 | **Hydrogen Index** | $HI = \dfrac{S_2}{TOC} \times 100$ | Kerogen quality/type |
| 3 | **Production Index** | $PI = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | Maturation level |
| 4 | **Transformation Ratio** | $TR = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | Fraction of HC generated |
| 5 | **Buoyancy Force** | $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V$ | Secondary migration driver |
| 6 | **Capillary Pressure** | $P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Seal entry resistance |
| 7 | **Hydrocarbon Column Height** | $h = \dfrac{P_c}{\Delta\rho \cdot g}$ | Maximum column a seal can hold |
| 8 | **Tissot-Welte Time-Temperature Index** | $TTI = \sum (\Delta t_n \cdot 2^{(T_n - 105)/10})$ | Burial history modeling |
| 9 | **Lopatin's Method (Modified)** | $TTI = \int 2^{(T(t)-105)/10} dt$ | Continuous thermal modeling |
| 10 | **Vitrinite Reflectance Calibration** | $\ln(R_o) = a \cdot \ln(depth) + b$ | Burial-maturity correlation |
| 11 | **Darcy's Law (Hydrocarbon flux)** | $Q = \dfrac{-kA}{\mu}\cdot\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | Migration through carrier bed |
| 12 | **Hydrocarbon Density (API)** | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho} - 131.5$ | Oil quality indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Tip:** In the KTU 2024 exam, when asked about "Migration Patterns and Barriers," always anchor your answer to the **buoyancy-capillary force balance**. Any answer that ignores this balance is automatically penalized.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Exploration Geochemistry:** TOC and Rock-Eval Pyrolysis are routinely used to identify source rock intervals in well logs.
2. **Petroleum System Modeling (PSM):** Software tools like **PetroMod**, **BasinMod**, and **Genesis** use TTI and Lopatin methods to predict hydrocarbon generation timing.
3. **Reservoir Engineering:** Capillary pressure curves directly inform **seal capacity analysis** and maximum producible column height.
4. **Refinery Selection:** Knowing the source rock type (marine vs lacustrine) helps predict the API gravity, sulfur content, and paraffinicity of the crude — critical parameters for **refinery distillation column design and instrumentation** (the direct link to PEICT631).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Capillary Entry Pressure (Seal Capacity)

The theoretical foundation of every seal analysis in petroleum geology rests on the **capillary pressure** equation. We now derive it rigorously.

### Starting Physical Principle
Consider a spherical hydrocarbon droplet of radius $r$ at the interface between two immiscible fluids (water and oil) within a circular pore of radius $r_p$.

### Step 1: Force Balance at the Pore Throat
The interfacial tension $\gamma$ acts along the circumference of the pore throat, pulling the interface inward. The hydrocarbon phase pushes outward.

$$
F_{tension} = 2\pi r_p \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the contact angle between the hydrocarbon-water interface and the mineral grain surface.

### Step 2: Pressure Force Exerted by Hydrocarbon Column
The buoyancy pressure generated by a column of hydrocarbons of height $h$ and density $\rho_{hc}$ in water of density $\rho_w$ is:

$$
P_{buoyancy} = \Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h
$$

### Step 3: Equilibrium Condition (Force Balance)
For the hydrocarbon column to be held static by the seal, the buoyancy pressure must equal the maximum capillary entry pressure of the seal:

$$
\Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r_{pore}}
$$

### Step 4: Solving for Maximum Column Height

$$
\boxed{\,h_{max} = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r_{pore} \cdot \Delta\rho \cdot g}\,}
$$

> This is the **fundamental equation of seal capacity analysis** in KTU-level petroleum engineering problems.

### Step 5: Numerical Substitution (Worked Example)
A shale seal has pore throat radius $r_p = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ m (5 nm), interfacial tension $\gamma = 0.025$ N/m, contact angle $\theta = 30°$, water density $\rho_w = 1050$ kg/m³, hydrocarbon density $\rho_{hc} = 750$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

Calculate the maximum column height the seal can hold.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{max} &= \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.025 \cdot \cos(30°)}{5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot (1050 - 750) \cdot 9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 0.8660}{5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 300 \cdot 9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{0.04330}{1.4715 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 2942.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The seal can theoretically hold a hydrocarbon column of approximately **2942.5 m** — an exceptionally strong seal.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Time-Temperature Index (TTI) — Lopatin's Method

The **TTI** is used to integrate the cumulative thermal exposure of a source rock through its burial history.

### Step 1: Reaction Rate Doubling
Empirical observation: The rate of kerogen maturation **doubles** for every **10°C increase** in temperature (Arrhenius-type behavior).

Define the **temperature factor**:

$$
f(T) = 2^{(T - 105)/10}
$$

The reference temperature of **105°C** is chosen because kerogen maturation becomes significant above this threshold.

### Step 2: Discretization Over Time
For a burial history divided into $n$ time intervals of duration $\Delta t_n$ at constant temperature $T_n$:

$$
TTI = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \Delta t_n \cdot 2^{(T_n - 105)/10}
$$

### Step 3: Continuous Form (Welte-Yükler Modification)
For continuous burial histories:

$$
\boxed{\,TTI = \int_{0}^{t} 2^{(T(\tau) - 105)/10} \, d\tau\,}
$$

### Step 4: Correlation with $R_o$
Empirical correlation (Waples, 1985):

$$
R_o \approx 0.218 \cdot \ln(TTI) + 0.34
$$

For the **onset of the oil window** ($R_o = 0.6\%$): $TTI \approx 15$
For the **peak oil generation** ($R_o = 1.0\%$): $TTI \approx 75$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Migration Mechanism vs. Driving Force

| Migration Stage | Primary Driving Force | Typical Velocity | Porosity Required | Critical Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary (Expulsion) | Kerogen network expansion | Very slow (mm/yr) | Microporosity | Source rock microfabric |
| Secondary (Carrier) | Buoyancy | 10–1000 m/yr | Macroporosity > 10% | Capillary pressure contrast |
| Tertiary (Leakage) | Seal failure / pressure | Episodic, rapid | Fracture aperture | Fault seal integrity |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hydrocarbon Column Height from Capillary Pressure Curve (Leverett J-Function)

For a reservoir with multiple pore sizes, the **Leverett J-Function** is used:

$$
J(S_w) = \dfrac{P_c}{\gamma \cos\theta} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\phi}}
$$

This dimensionless number allows scaling of capillary pressure data between different reservoir rocks.

**Step 1:** At irreducible water saturation $S_{wi}$, the maximum capillary pressure of the seal $P_{c,seal}$ must satisfy:

$$
P_{c,seal} \geq P_{c,reservoir}(S_{wi})
$$

**Step 2:** The height of the hydrocarbon column above the free water level (FWL) is given by:

$$
h = \dfrac{P_c - P_{c,FWL}}{\Delta\rho \cdot g}
$$

Since $P_{c,FWL} = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\,h = \dfrac{P_c}{\Delta\rho \cdot g}\,}
$$

This is the **standard formula** used in well-log interpretation and reservoir engineering to estimate column heights in KTU 2024 examination problems.

---

## 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Full Solution)

**Problem:** A sandstone reservoir is overlain by a shale seal. The hydrocarbon-water interfacial tension is $0.020$ N/m, contact angle $0°$, water density $1000$ kg/m³, oil density $800$ kg/m³, and shale pore radius $1 \times 10^{-8}$ m. Determine:
(i) Capillary entry pressure of the seal
(ii) Maximum oil column height

### Solution:

**(i) Capillary entry pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r} \\
&= \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.020 \cdot \cos(0°)}{1 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.040}{1 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= 4.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 4.0 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum oil column height:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{max} &= \dfrac{P_c}{\Delta\rho \cdot g} \\
&= \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{6}}{(1000 - 800) \cdot 9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{6}}{1962} \\
&= 2038.7 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The seal can hold an oil column of approximately **2039 m** above the FWL.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Petroleum System Workflow — From Source Rock to Refinery Feed

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete petroleum system flow**, including the migration patterns and barriers. This is the single most important conceptual map for the KTU 2024 exam.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "Source Rock" and state the three essential conditions that a rock must satisfy to qualify as a hydrocarbon source rock.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A source rock is a fine-grained sedimentary rock containing sufficient organic matter (kerogen) capable of generating commercial quantities of hydrocarbons upon thermal maturation.
The three essential conditions are:
1. **Quantity:** TOC content should be $\geq 0.5$ wt% (preferably $\geq 1$ wt%)
2. **Quality:** The kerogen should be of oil-prone or gas-prone type (I, II, or IIS)
3. **Maturity:** The rock should have been buried to sufficient depth and temperature ($R_o \geq 0.6\%$)

> *Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three conditions.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is Kerogen? Briefly explain the four main types of kerogen with their hydrocarbon potential.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Kerogen is the insoluble, high-molecular-weight organic matter present in sedimentary rocks, which upon heating generates petroleum.

| Type | Origin | HC Potential |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| I | Algal/Lacustrine | Excellent — oil-prone |
| II | Marine planktonic | Very good — oil + gas |
| III | Terrestrial/Humic | Gas-prone only |
| IV | Oxidized/Recycled | Inert — no potential |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **process of thermal maturation of kerogen**, including the three stages of diagenesis, catagenesis, and metagenesis. Discuss the role of **Vitrinite Reflectance ($R_o$)** and **TTI (Time-Temperature Index)** as maturity indicators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **mechanisms of primary and secondary migration of oil**, highlighting the role of **buoyancy, capillary pressure, and relative permeability**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**1. Definition of Maturation:**
Maturation is the thermally-induced, irreversible transformation of kerogen into hydrocarbons (oil and gas) under subsurface conditions of elevated temperature and pressure.

**2. Stages of Maturation:**

- **Diagenesis ($<60°C$, $R_o < 0.5\%$):** Microbial activity dominates. Only **biogenic methane** is produced. Kerogen undergoes minor dewatering and decarboxylation.
- **Catagenesis ($60°C - 175°C$, $R_o = 0.5\% - 2.0\%$):** This is the **principal oil and gas generation window**:
  - **Early catagenesis** ($R_o = 0.5\% - 0.7\%$): Beginning of oil generation from Type II kerogen
  - **Peak oil window** ($R_o = 0.7\% - 1.0\%$): Maximum oil generation
  - **Late catagenesis** ($R_o = 1.0\% - 2.0\%$): Wet gas and condensate zone
- **Metagenesis ($>175°C$, $R_o > 2.0\%$):** Only **dry methane** is generated; kerogen is reduced to **graphitic carbon**.

**3. Role of Vitrinite Reflectance ($R_o$):**
$R_o$ is the percentage of incident light reflected from the polished surface of **vitrinite** (a coal maceral). It is the most widely used maturity parameter.
- $R_o < 0.5\%$ → Immature
- $R_o = 0.5\% - 1.35\%$ → Oil window
- $R_o = 1.35\% - 2.0\%$ → Wet gas/condensate window
- $R_o > 2.0\%$ → Dry gas zone

**4. Role of TTI (Time-Temperature Index):**
TTI integrates the cumulative thermal exposure of a source rock through its burial history, using the empirical relation that the maturation rate doubles for every 10°C rise in temperature. The Waples correlation is:

$$
R_o = 0.218 \cdot \ln(TTI) + 0.34
$$

Typical TTI values: TTI $\approx 15$ marks the onset of oil generation; TTI $\approx 75$ marks peak oil generation.

### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**1. Primary Migration (Expulsion):**
Primary migration is the expulsion of hydrocarbons from the fine-grained source rock into adjacent permeable carrier beds. The four main mechanisms are:
- **Pressure-driven expulsion** from compaction overpressure
- **Network expansion** as kerogen cracks and internal porosity increases
- **Solution-diffusion** in the kerogen-bitumen network
- **Molecular diffusion** in a continuous hydrocarbon phase

**2. Secondary Migration (Carrier-Bed Flow):**
Secondary migration is the buoyancy-driven movement of hydrocarbons through permeable carrier beds towards a trap. It is governed by the **force balance equation**:

$$
F_{buoyancy} = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V
$$

$$
F_{resisting} = P_c \cdot A = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r} \cdot A
$$

**Migration proceeds when** $F_{buoyancy} > F_{resisting}$.

**3. Role of Buoyancy:**
Hydrocarbons are less dense than formation water. The density difference generates an upward force that drives the hydrocarbon column towards the surface.

**4. Role of Capillary Pressure:**
Tight pore throats in seal rocks generate a high capillary entry pressure that resists migration. Hydrocarbons can only pass if buoyancy pressure exceeds this threshold.

**5. Role of Relative Permeability:**
Migration through carrier beds depends on the relative permeability of the rock to oil and water. At low oil saturations, the relative permeability to oil is near zero, preventing migration. Migration only commences when oil saturation exceeds the **critical saturation threshold**.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of kerogen** and the **Van Krevelen diagram**, explaining how atomic H/C and O/C ratios are used to identify kerogen types and their evolutionary pathways. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **various types of seal rocks (barriers)** in a petroleum system and explain how **capillary entry pressure** controls the **maximum hydrocarbon column height** a seal can hold. Include a numerical illustration. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**1. Definition of Kerogen:**
Kerogen is the fraction of organic matter in sedimentary rocks that is insoluble in common organic solvents. It is the precursor of petroleum.

**2. Van Krevelen Diagram:**
The Van Krevelen diagram is a plot of **atomic H/C ratio (y-axis)** versus **atomic O/C ratio (x-axis)**. It is the standard tool for classifying kerogen and tracking its thermal evolution.

**3. Kerogen Types:**

- **Type I:** H/C > 1.5, O/C < 0.1. Algal/lacustrine origin. Highly oil-prone. Evolution: H/C drops sharply with increasing maturity.
- **Type II:** H/C ≈ 1.2, O/C ≈ 0.15. Marine planktonic origin. Oil + gas prone.
- **Type III:** H/C < 1.0, O/C > 0.2. Terrestrial humic origin. Gas-prone.
- **Type IV:** H/C < 0.5. Inert; no hydrocarbon potential.

**4. Evolutionary Pathways:**
As maturation progresses, all kerogen types evolve along characteristic pathways:
- H/C ratio decreases (loss of hydrogen during hydrocarbon generation)
- O/C ratio decreases (loss of oxygen as CO₂ and H₂O)
- All types converge towards **zero H/C and zero O/C** at extreme maturation (pure carbon/graphite)

**5. Diagnostic Utility:**
The Van Krevelen diagram allows geochemists to:
- Identify kerogen type from elemental analysis
- Estimate thermal maturity
- Predict hydrocarbon type (oil vs gas)
- Track source rock depositional environment

### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**1. Definition of a Seal Rock:**
A seal rock (or *cap rock*) is an impermeable rock that prevents the further upward or lateral migration of hydrocarbons, allowing them to accumulate in a reservoir.

**2. Types of Seal Rocks:**

- **Lithological Seals:** Shale, mudstone, halite, anhydrite. Sealing mechanism: extremely low permeability and high capillary entry pressure.
- **Fault Seals:** Formed by juxtaposition of impermeable lithology against permeable reservoir rock, or by clay smear along the fault plane.
- **Diagenetic Seals:** Cemented zones (e.g., silica-cemented sandstones, dolomitized limestones) with blocked pore throats.
- **Overpressure Seals:** Shale intervals with pore fluid pressure exceeding hydrostatic, preventing hydrocarbon leakage.
- **Hydraulic Seals:** Dynamic seals where groundwater flow opposes hydrocarbon buoyancy.

**3. Capillary Entry Pressure and Column Height:**
The fundamental relationship is:

$$
P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}
$$

The maximum column height is:

$$
h_{max} = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \cdot \Delta\rho \cdot g}
$$

**4. Numerical Illustration:**
Given: $\gamma = 0.030$ N/m, $\theta = 20°$, $r = 2 \times 10^{-8}$ m, $\rho_w = 1050$ kg/m³, $\rho_{hc} = 700$ kg/m³.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.030 \cdot \cos(20°)}{2 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.060 \cdot 0.9397}{2 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.05638}{2 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= 2.819 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 2.82 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{max} &= \dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{6}}{(1050 - 700) \cdot 9.81} \\
&= \dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{6}}{3433.5} \\
&= 821.2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The seal can hold a maximum hydrocarbon column of approximately 821 m.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "Source Rock" with "Reservoir Rock":** Source rocks are *generators* (typically shale); reservoir rocks are *storers* (typically sandstone). This confusion costs **2 marks minimum**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Force Balance:** Any answer on migration that omits the **buoyancy vs capillary pressure** balance is incomplete. Examiners deduct **up to 3 marks** for this omission.
> 3. **Wrong Unit for $R_o$:** Vitrinite Reflectance is dimensionless (%). Writing it in °C or as a fraction is penalized.
> 4. **Not Listing All Three Conditions for a Source Rock:** Quantity + Quality + Maturity — missing even one costs **1 full mark**.
> 5. **Confusing Primary vs Secondary Migration:** Primary = within source rock; Secondary = through carrier beds. Examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping the Numerical Solution Steps:** For 14-mark questions with sub-parts, examiners award marks **step-by-step**. A final answer without intermediate steps loses **2-3 marks**.
> 7. **Ignoring the Refinery Connection:** Since this is PEICT631 (Refinery Instrumentation), always link migration/source rock discussions to **refinery feedstock characterization** (API gravity, sulfur content) wherever possible — this earns the **application-level mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Source Rock Essentials**
> - Definition: Fine-grained rock with sufficient organic matter to generate hydrocarbons
> - Three conditions: Quantity (TOC) + Quality (Kerogen Type) + Maturity ($R_o$)
> - TOC threshold: $\geq 0.5$ wt% (poor), $\geq 2.0$ wt% (good)
> - Kerogen types: I (algal-oil), II (marine-oil+gas), III (humic-gas), IV (inert)

> ✅ **Maturation Stages**
> - Diagenesis: $< 60°C$, biogenic methane only
> - Catagenesis: $60-175°C$, oil + wet gas window
> - Metagenesis: $>175°C$, dry gas + graphite
> - Key parameters: $R_o$, TTI, $T_{max}$, PI, HI

> ✅ **Migration Stages**
> - Primary: Expulsion from source rock (pressure, network expansion, diffusion)
> - Secondary: Through carrier beds (buoyancy-driven, capillary-controlled)
> - Tertiary: Leakage from existing accumulations (seal failure)

> ✅ **Migration Driving Forces**
> - Buoyancy: $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V$
> - Hydrodynamic pressure gradient
> - Expulsion pressure from kerogen cracking

> ✅ **Migration Resisting Forces**
> - Capillary pressure: $P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$
> - Adsorption on mineral surfaces
> - Viscous drag

> ✅ **Seal / Barrier Types**
> - Lithological (shale, salt, anhydrite)
> - Fault seals (juxtaposition + clay smear)
> - Diagenetic (cementation)
> - Overpressure seals
> - Hydraulic seals

> ✅ **Critical Equations to Memorize**
> - $P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ (Seal capacity)
> - $h_{max} = \dfrac{P_c}{\Delta\rho \cdot g}$ (Column height)
> - $R_o = 0.218 \cdot \ln(TTI) + 0.34$ (Maturity correlation)
> - $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho} - 131.5$ (Oil quality)

> ✅ **Refinery Linkage (PEICT631-Specific)**
> - Marine source rocks → paraffinic, low-sulfur crude → suited for high-complexity refinery distillation
> - Lacustrine source rocks → waxy, high-pour-point crude → requires dewaxing units
> - Source rock sulfur content directly controls **hydrodesulfurization (HDS) unit design**
> - API gravity measured at refinery inlet is the **direct quality indicator** of the source rock maturation history

> 🎯 **One-Line Exam Punchline:**
> *"A petroleum system works only when the Source Rock matures, the hydrocarbons migrate against capillary resistance, and a Seal traps them — break any link, and the trap is dry."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reservoir Rocks and Cap Rocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reservoir Rocks and Cap Rocks # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Reservoir Rocks — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Reservoir Rock (KTU 2024 Definition):** A subsurface, porous and permeable rock formation that possesses the capacity to store economically significant quantities of hydrocarbons (oil and/or natural gas) within its interconnected pore network, and allows the fluids to flow toward a production well under natural or artificially induced pressure gradients. In petroleum and refinery engineering, the reservoir rock is the **storage heart** of any hydrocarbon accumulation. Without a suitable reservoir rock, even the richest source rock and the most perfectly timed migration event would result in **zero recoverable hydrocarbon**. The two indispensable geological attributes of any reservoir rock are: 1. **Porosity ($\phi$)** — the capacity to *store* fluids. 2. **Permeability ($k$)** — the capacity to *transmit* fluids. A rock can be porous but impermeable (e.g., shale) — such a rock is a **bad reservoir** but often an excellent **cap rock**. ## 1.2 Cap Rocks — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cap Rock (Seal Rock):** An impermeable rock stratum, typically of very low porosity and ultra-low permeability, that overlies the reservoir rock and prevents the vertical migration (escape) of buoyant hydrocarbons toward the surface. It is the geometric and hydraulic lid of the petroleum trap. Cap rocks are responsible for *trapping* hydrocarbons. The trap is a geometric combination of **reservoir rock + cap rock + structural closure (anticline, fault, stratigraphic pinch-out)**. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — A Bottle of Cola Imagine a **sealed plastic bottle** of carbonated soft drink: | Bottle Component | Petroleum System Equivalent | |---|---| | Plastic bottle shell (sealed) | **Cap rock** — prevents gas/liquid escape | | Soda liquid inside | **Oil** (liquid hydrocarbon) | | Pressurized CO₂ at top | **Free gas cap** (gaseous hydrocarbon) | | Air space above (under cap) | **Initial formation water** | | Bottle itself (hollow container) | **Reservoir rock** (porous container) | | Twist cap | **Cap rock** (the seal) | If you replace the cap with a sieve, the gas escapes — exactly what happens in nature when a cap rock **fails** (leakage, fault breach, biodegradation). ## 1.4 Why Both Rocks are Critical for Refinery Feedstock A refinery (which is the focus of PEICT631) requires a **continuous, predictable feedstock supply**. Instrumentation down to the well-head and reservoir depends entirely on the geological character of the reservoir and cap rocks because they govern: - **Drive mechanisms** (solution gas, water drive, gas cap drive, compaction drive). - **Production decline curves** measured by SCADA, downhole pressure gauges, and flow meters. - **Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR)** instrumentation strategy. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 1 expects students to identify the relationship between reservoir-cap rock systems and downstream production measurement. Pressure-volume-temperature (PVT) behavior, which is *heavily instrumented* in refinery upstream, is a direct consequence of rock-fluid interactions. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Properties of a Good Reservoir Rock A formation qualifies as a commercial reservoir rock only when it satisfies a strict set of petrophysical criteria: 1. **High Effective Porosity ($\phi_e$)** — typically $\phi_e \geq 5\%$ to $10\%$ for economic viability, with $15\%$–$30\%$ being the *sweet spot* for sandstones and $5\%$–$15\%$ common for carbonates. 2. **Sufficient Absolute Permeability ($k$)** — usually $k \geq 1\ \text{mD}$ (millidarcy) for commercial flow, with $10$–$500\ \text{mD}$ being the typical working range. 3. **Interconnected Pore Network** — isolated vugs and fractures do not transmit fluids. 4. **Favorable Wettability** — most reservoir rocks are preferentially **water-wet**, which improves oil displacement efficiency. 5. **Mechanical Stability** — must not collapse under drawdown pressure. 6. **Low Clay Content (in sandstones)** — authigenic clays (smectite, kaolinite) drastically reduce permeability upon contact with fresh drilling fluids. ## 2.2 Properties of a Good Cap Rock The cap rock must do the *opposite* of the reservoir rock: 1. **Ultra-low Permeability** — ideally $k < 10^{-6}\ \text{mD}$ (often called **microdarcy or nanodarcy range**). 2. **Very Low Porosity OR High Capillary Pressure Threshold** — even if porous, the pore throats must be small enough that hydrocarbon entry pressure exceeds the buoyancy pressure. 3. **Ductility / Plasticity** — must deform plastically (e.g., halite, anhydrite, plastic shale) to seal micro-fractures. 4. **Lateral Continuity** — must seal the entire trap closure, not just the crest. 5. **Chemical Inertness** — must not react with CO₂, H₂S, or formation water. 6. **Sufficient Thickness** — typical cap rocks are **5 m to several hundred meters** thick. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **examination-critical** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the formula, the symbols, AND the SI field units. | # | Property | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Absolute Porosity** | $\phi_a = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b} \times 100\%$ | $V_p$ = pore volume (m³), $V_b$ = bulk volume (m³) | | 2 | **Effective Porosity** | $\phi_e = \dfrac{V_{ip}}{V_b} \times 100\%$ | $V_{ip}$ = interconnected pore volume (m³) | | 3 | **Hydrocarbon Saturation** | $S_{hc} = \dfrac{V_{hc}}{V_p}$ | $V_{hc}$ = hydrocarbon volume (m³), dimensionless | | 4 | **Water Saturation** | $S_w = 1 - S_{hc}$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | **Darcy's Law** | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | $Q$ (m³/s), $k$ (m²), $A$ (m²), $\Delta P$ (Pa), $\mu$ (Pa·s), $L$ (m) | | 6 | **Permeability (Field Unit)** | $k\ (\text{mD}) = \dfrac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}\cdot C$ | $C = 1.013 \times 10^{-9}$ (SI conversion) | | 7 | **Buoyancy Pressure (Capillary Seal)** | $P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho$ (kg/m³), $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $h$ = column height (m) | | 8 | **Capillary Pressure** | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ | $\sigma$ = interfacial tension (N/m), $\theta$ = contact angle, $r$ = pore throat radius (m) | | 9 | **Hydrocarbon Column Height** | $h_{hc} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$ | All in SI units, $h_{hc}$ in meters | | 10 | **Resistivity Index (Archie)** | $I = \dfrac{R_t}{R_o} = S_w^{-n}$ | $R_t$ = true resistivity, $R_o$ = 100% water-saturated, $n$ = saturation exponent | ## 2.4 Seal Capacity Equation — The Heart of Cap Rock Analysis The **seal capacity** or **capillary entry pressure** is the single most important number for cap rock evaluation: $$P_c^{\text{entry}} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{throat,max}}}$$ - A cap rock is **effective** if $P_c^{\text{entry}} \geq P_b$ (buoyancy pressure of the underlying hydrocarbon column). - If buoyancy exceeds entry pressure → **hydrocarbon leakage** → dry hole. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Logging While Drilling (LWD) tools** in refinery upstream wells measure $\phi$, $k$, $S_w$ directly. - **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) tools** distinguish bound vs. free fluids. - **Production logging** interprets flow profiles against the geological model built from reservoir rock analysis. - **4D Seismic Monitoring** tracks fluid fronts — entirely dependent on the contrast between reservoir and cap rock properties. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Porosity from Absolute Porosity **Given:** - $V_b$ = Bulk volume of rock sample (m³) - $V_p$ = Total pore volume (m³) - $V_{ip}$ = Interconnected (effective) pore volume (m³) **Step 1: Define absolute porosity.** $$\phi_a = \frac{V_p}{V_b}$$ **Step 2: Define effective porosity.** $$\phi_e = \frac{V_{ip}}{V_b}$$ **Step 3: Express the relationship.** Since $V_{ip} \leq V_p$ (isolated pores do not contribute to flow), we write: $$V_{ip} = V_p - V_{\text{isolated}}$$ **Step 4: Substitute.** $$\phi_e = \frac{V_p - V_{\text{isolated}}}{V_b}$$ **Step 5: Express as a fraction of absolute porosity.** $$\phi_e = \phi_a - \frac{V_{\text{isolated}}}{V_b}$$ **Conclusion:** Effective porosity is always **less than or equal to** absolute porosity. The difference is the volume occupied by **isolated pores, dead-end vugs, and occluded pore space**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Hydrocarbon Column Height (Cap Rock Seal Capacity) This is the **flagship derivation** that links reservoir and cap rock physics.
