It tells the engineer the maximum hydrocarbon column the cap rock can hold. **Starting principle:** Hydrocarbons leak when buoyancy pressure $>$ capillary entry pressure. $$P_b = P_c^{\text{entry}}$$ **Step 1: Expand the buoyancy pressure of a hydrocarbon column of height $h_{hc}$.** The hydrostatic pressure difference between formation water and hydrocarbon column is: $$P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h_{hc}$$ **Step 2: Expand the capillary entry pressure of the cap rock.** $$P_c^{\text{entry}} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{max}}}$$ **Step 3: Equate the two.** $$(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h_{hc} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{max}}}$$ **Step 4: Solve for $h_{hc}$.** $$h_{hc} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{max}} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$ **Step 5: Practical interpretation.** - **Smaller pore throats ($r_{\text{max}}$)** → *taller* hydrocarbon column can be trapped. - **Larger density contrast ($\rho_w - \rho_{hc}$)** → *taller* column (gas traps are typically taller than oil traps because $\rho_g \ll \rho_w$). - **Higher interfacial tension ($\sigma$)** → *taller* column. - **Higher contact angle ($\theta > 90°$)** → *shorter* column (worse seal). **Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):** > Given: $\sigma = 0.025\ \text{N/m}$, $\theta = 30°$, $r_{\text{max}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$, $\rho_w = 1050\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{hc} = 750\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. **Step 1: Compute numerator.** $$2 \sigma \cos\theta = 2 \cdot 0.025 \cdot \cos(30°) = 2 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 0.866 = 0.0433\ \text{N/m}$$ **Step 2: Compute denominator.** $$r_{\text{max}} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1050 - 750) \cdot 9.81$$ $$= (1 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 300 \cdot 9.81 = 2.943 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N/m}^2$$ **Step 3: Final answer.** $$h_{hc} = \frac{0.0433}{2.943 \times 10^{-3}} = 14.71\ \text{m}$$ **Interpretation:** This cap rock can trap a hydrocarbon column of approximately **14.7 m**. A larger column would breach the seal. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Darcy's Law Applied to Reservoir Flow For a refinery-instrumentation student, Darcy's Law is critical because it governs flow rate measurement at every flow meter and pressure transducer. **Step 1: Empirical observation** (Henry Darcy, 1856) — flow rate is proportional to cross-section and pressure gradient. $$Q \propto A \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$ **Step 2: Introduce fluid viscosity $\mu$** — viscous fluids flow slower. $$Q \propto \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$ **Step 3: Introduce the rock property $k$** (intrinsic permeability). $$Q = k \cdot \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$ **Step 4: Rearrange for permeability** (the form used in core analysis labs). $$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$ **Step 5: SI unit check.** $[k] = \dfrac{(\text{m}^3/\text{s}) \cdot (\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}) \cdot (\text{m})}{(\text{m}^2) \cdot (\text{Pa})} = \text{m}^2$ **Note:** $1\ \text{Darcy} = 9.869 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{m}^2$; $1\ \text{millidarcy (mD)} = 9.869 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{m}^2$. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Petroleum System Block Diagram (Reservoir + Cap Rock) ```mermaid flowchart TB SRC["Source Rock
Organic-rich Shale
Kerogen Maturation"] MIG["Migration Pathway
Carrier Bed"] RES["Reservoir Rock
Sandstone / Carbonate
Porous + Permeable"] CAP["Cap Rock Seal
Shale / Halite / Anhydrite
Impermeable"] TRAP["Structural Trap
Anticline / Fault / Salt Dome"] WELL["Production Well
Downhole Sensors"] REF["Refinery Feedstock
Pipeline"] SRC --> MIG MIG --> RES RES --> CAP CAP --> TRAP RES --> WELL WELL --> REF TRAP -.protects.-> RES ``` ## 4.2 Reservoir Rock Pore-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Matrix["Reservoir Rock Matrix"] GRAINS["Quartz / Calcite Grains
Solid Framework"] P1["Intergranular Pore A
Connected"] P2["Intergranular Pore B
Connected"] P3["Isolated Vug
Dead-end"] P4["Intercrystalline Pore
Connected"] FLUID1["Oil in Pore A"] FLUID2["Water in Pore B"] FLUID3["Gas in Pore D"] end GRAINS --> P1 GRAINS --> P2 GRAINS --> P3 GRAINS --> P4 P1 --> FLUID1 P2 --> FLUID2 P4 --> FLUID3 P1 -.flow path.-> P2 P2 -.flow path.-> P4 ``` ## 4.3 Cap Rock Sealing Mechanism Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB BUOY["Buoyancy Pressure
Pb = dH * g * h"] CAP_PR["Capillary Entry Pressure
Pc = 2 sigma cos theta over r"] LEV1["If Pb less than Pc
Seal Holds"] LEAK["If Pb greater than Pc
Leakage Occurs"] BUOY --> COMPARE{Comparison} CAP_PR --> COMPARE COMPARE -->|Seal Intact| LEV1 COMPARE -->|Seal Breached| LEAK ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Property Matrix — Reservoir vs Cap Rock | Property | Reservoir Rock | Cap Rock | |---|---|---| | Porosity | High (10–30%) | Very Low (<5%) or High but small throats | | Permeability | High (>1 mD) | Ultra-low (<10⁻⁶ mD) | | Mechanical Behaviour | Brittle / Friable | Ductile / Plastic | | Wettability | Water-wet preferred | Not critical | | Typical Lithology | Sandstone, Limestone, Dolomite | Shale, Halite, Anhydrite, Mudstone | | Geological Age | Variable | Variable but laterally extensive | | Capillary Pressure | Low | Very High | # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **Q: Define "effective porosity" of a reservoir rock. How does it differ from absolute porosity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) **Effective porosity** $\phi_e$ is the ratio of interconnected pore volume to bulk volume of the rock: $\phi_e = V_{ip}/V_b$. - (1 mark) **Absolute porosity** $\phi_a$ includes ALL pore space (interconnected + isolated): $\phi_a = V_p/V_b$. - (1 mark) Key difference: $\phi_e \leq \phi_a$. Isolated pores contribute to $\phi_a$ but not to $\phi_e$, hence $\phi_e$ governs fluid storage-transmission while $\phi_a$ is purely volumetric. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **Q: List any THREE essential properties of a good cap rock.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Ultra-low permeability** (typically $k < 10^{-6}\ \text{mD}$) to prevent fluid leakage. 2. **High capillary entry pressure** exceeding the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column. 3. **Lateral continuity** and sufficient **thickness** to seal the entire trap closure. 4. *(Optional 4th)* **Ductility** to self-seal microfractures. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 marks):** When using Darcy's Law, students often forget to convert between field units (mD, ft, psi) and SI units (m², m, Pa). Always state the system you are using explicitly. > 2. **Confusing porosity vs permeability (–2 marks):** Porosity is *storage*; permeability is *transmission*. Examiners deduct heavily for interchange. > 3. **Skipping the assumption of $\theta$ and $\sigma$ (–1 mark):** In capillary pressure problems, explicitly state assumed values: $\theta = 0°$ (perfectly water-wet) and $\sigma \approx 0.025\ \text{N/m}$ for oil-water unless given. > 4. **Forgetting 100% in porosity (–1 mark):** Porosity is a dimensionless ratio but typically expressed in percentage. Convert appropriately. > 5. **Drawing cap rock vs reservoir rock upside down in trap diagrams (–2 marks):** The cap rock ALWAYS overlies the reservoir rock. Drawing them in reverse position guarantees heavy deduction. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model] **Q (a): Define reservoir rock. Discuss in detail the petrophysical properties that make a formation a "good" reservoir rock. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** A reservoir rock is a subsurface porous and permeable lithology that stores hydrocarbons in its interconnected pore network and yields them economically to a production well. Examples: sandstone, limestone, dolomite, fractured shale (in unconventional plays). **Petrophysical Properties (5 marks):** 1. **Porosity** — must be $\geq 5\%$–$10\%$ for commercial storage; classified as primary (intergranular) or secondary (vuggy, fractured). 2. **Permeability** — must allow flow; classified as absolute, effective, and relative. Typical range: $1\ \text{mD}$ to $1\ \text{Darcy}$. 3. **Saturation** — fraction of pore volume occupied by each fluid (oil, gas, water). Determined via Archie equation from resistivity logs. 4. **Wettability** — preference of rock surface for water or oil; affects relative permeability and recovery factor. 5. **Capillary Pressure** — controls fluid distribution and initial water saturation in the transition zone. --- **Q (b): A reservoir rock sample has a bulk volume of $200\ \text{cm}^3$ and a grain volume of $140\ \text{cm}^3$. Calculate the absolute porosity. If $10\ \text{cm}^3$ of the pore space is isolated, find the effective porosity. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given (1 mark):** - $V_b = 200\ \text{cm}^3$ - $V_{\text{grain}} = 140\ \text{cm}^3$ - $V_{\text{isolated}} = 10\ \text{cm}^3$ **Step 1: Find pore volume (1 mark).** $$V_p = V_b - V_{\text{grain}} = 200 - 140 = 60\ \text{cm}^3$$ **Step 2: Absolute porosity (2 marks).** $$\phi_a = \frac{V_p}{V_b} = \frac{60}{200} = 0.30 = 30\%$$ **Step 3: Effective pore volume (1 mark).** $$V_{ip} = V_p - V_{\text{isolated}} = 60 - 10 = 50\ \text{cm}^3$$ **Step 4: Effective porosity (2 marks).** $$\phi_e = \frac{V_{ip}}{V_b} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25 = 25\%$$ **Final Answer:** $\phi_a = 30\%$, $\phi_e = 25\%$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **Q (a): Define cap rock. Explain the mechanism by which a cap rock seals hydrocarbons. Derive the equation for maximum hydrocarbon column height a cap rock can hold. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 mark):** A cap rock is an impermeable rock stratum overlying a reservoir that prevents the upward escape of hydrocarbons. **Sealing Mechanism (2 marks):** The cap rock seals by **capillary seal mechanism**: hydrocarbons must displace formation water from the smallest interconnected pore throats of the cap rock. The pressure required for this displacement is the **capillary entry pressure** $P_c$. If buoyancy pressure $P_b$ is less than $P_c$, the seal holds. **Derivation (4 marks):** Setting $P_b = P_c$ at the seal-failure limit: $$(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h_{hc} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{max}}}$$ Solving: $$h_{hc} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{max}} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$ Where $h_{hc}$ is the maximum hydrocarbon column height. --- **Q (b): A cap rock has a maximum interconnected pore throat radius of $2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$. The interfacial tension between oil and water is $0.030\ \text{N/m}$ and the contact angle is $20°$. The density of formation water is $1080\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and that of oil is $780\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate the maximum height of the oil column that this cap rock can hold. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given (1 mark):** - $r_{\text{max}} = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$ - $\sigma = 0.030\ \text{N/m}$ - $\theta = 20°$, so $\cos(20°) = 0.9397$ - $\rho_w = 1080\ \text{kg/m}^3$ - $\rho_{hc} = 780\ \text{kg/m}^3$ - $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ **Step 1: Apply the formula (1 mark).** $$h_{hc} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{max}} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$ **Step 2: Compute numerator (2 marks).** $$2 \sigma \cos\theta = 2 \cdot 0.030 \cdot 0.9397 = 0.0564\ \text{N/m}$$ **Step 3: Compute denominator (2 marks).** $$(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g = (1080 - 780) \cdot 9.81 = 300 \cdot 9.81 = 2943\ \text{N/m}^3$$ $$r_{\text{max}} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g = (2 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 2943 = 5.886 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{N/m}^2$$ **Step 4: Final answer (1 mark).** $$h_{hc} = \frac{0.0564}{5.886 \times 10^{-4}} = 95.82\ \text{m}$$ **Interpretation:** This cap rock can support an oil column of approximately **95.8 m** before leakage occurs. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes — KTU 2024 Module 1** - **Reservoir rock** must have **HIGH porosity AND permeability** — both are non-negotiable. - **Cap rock** must have **LOW permeability AND HIGH capillary entry pressure**. - The **porosity-permeability relationship** is non-linear; high porosity does not guarantee high permeability (e.g., shale). - **Common reservoir rocks:** Sandstone, Limestone, Dolomite, Fractured Shale. - **Common cap rocks:** Shale (most common), Halite (best seal — self-healing), Anhydrite, Tight Chalk. - **Hydrocarbon column height** $h_{hc} = \dfrac{2\sigma\cos\theta}{r_{\text{max}}(\rho_w - \rho_{hc})g}$ — memorize this equation verbatim. - **Gas columns** can be **taller** than oil columns for the same seal because $\rho_g \ll \rho_o$. - **Capillary entry pressure** $P_c$ is **inversely proportional** to pore throat radius — smaller throats → stronger seal. - **Darcy's Law:** $Q = \dfrac{kA\Delta P}{\mu L}$ — the foundation of all reservoir flow calculations. - **Wettability:** Most reservoir rocks are **water-wet** by default; surfactants in EOR can alter wettability. - **Trap types:** Anticline (most common), Fault trap, Stratigraphic pinch-out, Salt dome, Combination traps. - **Seal failure mechanisms:** Membrane seal failure, Hydraulic fracturing (overpressure), Fault reactivation, Cap rock biodegradation. - **Archie's Equation** connects resistivity logs to water saturation — critical for determining hydrocarbon saturation in instrumented well logging. - **Production instrumentation link:** Reservoir rock + cap rock control **bottom-hole pressure (BHP)**, **flow rate (Q)**, **GOR**, and **water cut** — all measured continuously by refinery upstream sensors. - **Unit conversions to remember:** - $1\ \text{Darcy} = 9.869 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{m}^2$ - $1\ \text{mD} = 9.869 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{m}^2$ - $1\ \text{psi} = 6894.76\ \text{Pa}$ - $1\ \text{ft} = 0.3048\ \text{m}$

topics.title: Entrapment of Oil-Types and Mechanism, Application of Remote Sensing in Petroleum Resource Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Entrapment of Oil-Types and Mechanism, Application of Remote Sensing in Petroleum Resource Development # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Entrapment of Oil — The Petroleum Trap Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > *Petroleum entrapment* refers to the geological configuration and physico-chemical process by which hydrocarbons (oil and natural gas), after migrating from their source rock, become concentrated and held within a permeable reservoir formation beneath an impermeable cap rock, forming an economically viable accumulation that can be tapped by drilling. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Underground Water Tank Imagine a multi-storey building's underground water harvesting system. Rainwater (the hydrocarbon precursor) falls on the roof, seeps through cracks (migration pathway), and collects in a hollow cavity (reservoir rock). Above the cavity is a concrete slab (cap rock) that prevents the water from escaping upwards. The shape and tilt of the cavity, combined with the slab, define the geometry of the "trap." If any one of these elements (cavity, slab, or geometry) is missing, the water escapes. Petroleum entrapment works on **exactly the same principle**, except the fluid is hydrocarbon migrating upward through millions of years of geologic time. ### The Four Pillars of a Petroleum Accumulation For oil to accumulate in any geological setting, **four essential elements** must coexist: 1. **Source Rock** — Fine-grained organic-rich sedimentary rock (typically shale or marl) where kerogen matures under heat and pressure to generate hydrocarbons. 2. **Migration Pathway** — Permeable carrier beds (sandstone, fractured limestone) allowing buoyant hydrocarbons to move upward. 3. **Reservoir Rock** — Porous and permeable rock capable of storing hydrocarbons (e.g., sandstone, porous limestone, dolomite). 4. **Cap Rock (Seal)** — Impermeable rock (shale, salt, anhydrite) preventing further vertical migration. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** > A commercially viable reservoir typically requires **porosity $\phi \geq 8\%$** and **permeability $k \geq 1$ millidarcy (mD)**. The industry benchmark for "good" reservoir quality is $\phi = 15-25\%$ and $k = 100-1000$ mD. --- ## 1.2 Types of Petroleum Traps — The Classification Framework Traps are classified by the **mechanism that creates the closure** for hydrocarbons. The two master categories are **Structural Traps** and **Stratigraphic Traps**, with combination types forming the third class. ### A. Structural Traps (created by tectonic deformation) | Trap Type | Geometry | Typical Setting | |-----------|----------|-----------------| | **Anticlinal Trap** | Upward arch of strata | Fold belts, foreland basins | | **Fault Trap** | Strata displaced along fracture | Extensional/compressional regimes | | **Salt Dome Trap** | Piercement by halite plugs | Passive margins (Gulf of Mexico) | | **Fracture Trap** | Hydrocarbons in joint/fault zones | Crystalline basement rocks | ### B. Stratigraphic Traps (created by depositional/erosional variations) | Trap Type | Mechanism | Classic Example | |-----------|-----------|-----------------| | **Lenticular (Pinch-out) Trap** | Sand body thins to zero | Channel sand in shale | | **Unconformity Trap** | Truncation beneath erosional surface | Pre-Cambrian basement | | **Reef Trap** | Carbonate build-up | Devonian reefs of Alberta | | **Tar Mat Trap** | Heavy asphalt seals reservoir | California fields | ### C. Combination Traps Hybrid traps where both structural and stratigraphic elements contribute to closure (e.g., a sand lens sealed against a fault plane). --- ## 1.3 Application of Remote Sensing in Petroleum Resource Development > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > *Remote sensing for petroleum exploration* is the acquisition and interpretation of data about the Earth's surface and subsurface from airborne or space-borne sensors, using electromagnetic radiation (visible, infrared, microwave) to detect indirect indicators of hydrocarbon presence and favorable structural settings. ### Intuitive Overview — The Aerial Geologist Think of a farmer scanning a vast wheat field from a small aircraft to detect patches of yellowed (diseased) crop amidst healthy green wheat. Remote sensing works the same way: a satellite "looks down" at hundreds of square kilometers of terrain and detects subtle anomalies — stressed vegetation, soil chemistry, structural lineaments — that correlate with **subsurface hydrocarbon microseepage** or favorable **trap geometry**. The petroleum geologist then narrows the search area from a country-scale (millions of km²) to a prospect-scale (tens of km²), saving enormous drilling costs. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard:** > Multi-spectral sensors such as **Landsat 8/9 (30 m resolution)**, **Sentinel-2 (10–20 m)**, and **hyperspectral sensors like AVIRIS or PRISMA** are routinely used. Radar (SAR) data from **Sentinel-1** penetrates cloud cover and is critical for tropical regions like Kerala and the Western Ghats. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of Oil Entrapment — Step-by-Step Logic The complete mechanism operates over **geologic timescales (10⁶–10⁸ years)** and follows a strict thermodynamic sequence: ### Step 1: Generation (Catagenesis) Organic matter buried in fine-grained sediments is subjected to temperatures of **60°C to 150°C** at depths of **1000 m to 4500 m**. Thermal cracking of kerogen yields liquid hydrocarbons. ### Step 2: Primary Migration (Expulsion) Hydrocarbons are expelled from the low-permeability source rock into adjacent permeable carrier beds. The driving force is **compaction-induced overpressure** combined with **buoyancy**. ### Step 3: Secondary Migration (Buoyancy-Driven Upward Movement) Hydrocarbons migrate upward because their density (oil: **800–950 kg/m³**; gas: **0.1–400 kg/m³**) is less than that of formation water (**1000–1200 kg/m³**). The buoyant force per unit volume is governed by: $$F_{buoy} = (\rho_{water} - \rho_{HC}) \cdot g$$ ### Step 4: Accumulation (Entrapment) Migration halts when hydrocarbons encounter a **trap** — a geometry where the impermeable seal prevents further upward escape. Hydrocarbons then **pool** at the highest point of the closure, displacing water downward under gravity. ### Step 5: Preservation The accumulation remains viable only if the seal integrity is maintained against subsequent tectonic, erosional, or biodegradation events. --- ## 2.2 Why Structural Traps Dominate World Reserves Anticlinal traps alone account for an estimated **~60–65%** of the world's discovered conventional oil reserves. This dominance arises because: - Folding creates large closed closures with predictable geometry. - Fracturing associated with folding enhances reservoir permeability. - Regional seals (shales) drape conformably over anticlines. --- ## 2.3 Remote Sensing Workflow for Petroleum Exploration The end-to-end remote sensing workflow for hydrocarbon prospect identification follows a standardized pipeline: 1. **Data Acquisition** — Multi-spectral, hyperspectral, thermal, and SAR imagery. 2. **Pre-processing** — Atmospheric correction, geometric rectification, noise reduction. 3. **Image Enhancement** — Band ratios, principal component analysis (PCA), texture analysis. 4. **Lineament Extraction** — Edge detection filters (Sobel, Canny) to map fractures. 5. **Spectral Anomaly Detection** — Identification of hydrocarbon-induced vegetation stress (e.g., red-edge shift in chlorophyll). 6. **Integration with Seismic/Gravity/Magnetic Data** — Calibration and ground-truthing. 7. **Prospect Ranking and Drilling Recommendation**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** > Hydrocarbon microseepage alters near-surface soil chemistry, producing **carbonate enrichment, magnetic mineral depletion (loss of iron oxides), and bleaching of red beds**. These phenomena are detectable via hyperspectral remote sensing and form the basis of the *Direct Detection Method*. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units | |---|------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $F_{buoy} = (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC}) \cdot g$ | Buoyant force on hydrocarbon column | N/m³ | | 2 | $\phi = \frac{V_{pores}}{V_{bulk}}$ | Porosity (fraction) | dimensionless | | 3 | $k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$ (Darcy's Law) | Permeability of reservoir rock | m² or Darcy | | 4 | $\text{API Gravity} = \frac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5$ | API gravity of crude oil | °API | | 5 | $G = \rho_{w} - \rho_{oil}$ | API correlation (specific gravity) | dimensionless | | 6 | $L = \frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda_{0}}$ (lineament density) | Lineament density per unit area | km/km² | | 7 | $\text{GRVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | Green-Red Vegetation Index | dimensionless | | 8 | $R = \frac{\lambda^{2} \sigma}{8 \pi^{3}} \cdot \sin\theta$ (Bragg's for SAR) | Bragg scattering from rough surfaces | m | | 9 | $S_{h} = \frac{h_{trap}}{h_{res}}$ | Hydrocarbon saturation height ratio | dimensionless | | 10 | $\text{TDT} = \text{True Depth} \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | True dip from apparent dip | m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why It Matters in Refinery Instrumentation:** > Although remote sensing is an *upstream* (exploration) tool, the integration of exploration data with downstream refinery feedstock planning requires shared physical-property databases (API gravity, sulfur content, viscosity). The instrumentation principles learned here directly support SCADA systems that track crude from well-head to refinery inlet. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Exploration Geoscientists** use these trap concepts to plan 2D/3D seismic surveys costing **USD 10,000–100,000 per km²**. - **Reservoir Engineers** use porosity, permeability, and saturation to compute *Original Oil In Place (OOIP)* and plan Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR). - **Pipeline & Refinery Planners** use API gravity forecasts to schedule crude processing units (light sweet vs. heavy sour). - **Environmental Engineers** use remote sensing to monitor methane plumes and oil spills at wellheads and along pipelines. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Buoyancy Force Driving Secondary Migration Starting from the fundamental principle that hydrocarbons migrate upward because of density contrast with formation water: ### Step 1 — Define the densities involved - $\rho_{w}$ = density of formation water (typically 1000–1200 kg/m³ in saline basins) - $\rho_{HC}$ = density of hydrocarbon (oil: 700–950 kg/m³; gas: 0.1–400 kg/m³) ### Step 2 — Apply Archimedes' principle A hydrocarbon element of volume $V$ immersed in formation water experiences: $$F_{buoy} = \rho_{w} \cdot V \cdot g \quad \text{(upward, supporting force)}$$ $$F_{grav} = \rho_{HC} \cdot V \cdot g \quad \text{(downward, gravitational force)}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the net upward force $$ \begin{aligned} F_{net} &= F_{buoy} - F_{grav} \\ F_{net} &= \rho_{w} \cdot V \cdot g - \rho_{HC} \cdot V \cdot g \\ F_{net} &= (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC}) \cdot V \cdot g \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Express per unit volume (force density) $$\frac{F_{net}}{V} = (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC}) \cdot g$$ This is the **buoyancy force density** driving secondary migration. Since $(\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC}) > 0$ always for oil and gas, the direction is unambiguously upward. ### Step 5 — Practical numerical example (KTU exam-style) > **Problem:** Formation water density is 1100 kg/m³ and crude oil density is 850 kg/m³. Calculate the buoyancy force density acting on the oil column. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². $$ \begin{aligned} F_{buoy}/V &= (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC}) \cdot g \\ &= (1100 - 850) \cdot 9.81 \\ &= 250 \cdot 9.81 \\ &= 2452.5 \text{ N/m}^{3} \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** A positive value of **2452.5 N/m³** confirms an upward-directed force. The oil will continue migrating upward until it meets an impermeable seal. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: API Gravity Conversion from Density API (American Petroleum Institute) gravity is the standard industry unit for crude oil density. ### Step 1 — Define specific gravity Specific gravity $G$ is the ratio of oil density to water density at 15.6°C: $$G = \frac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$$ With $\rho_{water}(15.6°C) = 999$ kg/m³ (approximately 1.000 g/cm³). ### Step 2 — Apply the API definition $$ \begin{aligned} \text{°API} &= \frac{141.5}{G} - 131.5 \\ &= \frac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}/\rho_{water}} - 131.5 \\ &= \frac{141.5 \cdot \rho_{water}}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Numerical example Given $\rho_{oil} = 850$ kg/m³ = 0.850 g/cm³, so $G = 0.850$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{°API} &= \frac{141.5}{0.850} - 131.5 \\ &= 166.47 - 131.5 \\ &= 34.97 \approx 35°\text{API} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** 35°API classifies this crude as **light crude** (commercial threshold > 31.1°API). Such crude flows easily through pipelines and yields high gasoline fraction in refining — directly relevant to refinery instrumentation for blending and fractionator control. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trap Selection for Remote Sensing The matrix below maps each trap type to its **best remote sensing indicator** and expected signal strength: | Trap Type | Surface Expression | Optimal Sensor | Detection Confidence | |-----------|-------------------|----------------|----------------------| | Anticline | Vegetation stress, lineament curvature | Hyperspectral + SAR | High (75–85%) | | Fault trap | Sharp tonal lineaments | Multispectral + DEM | High (70–80%) | | Salt dome | Circular dome, gravity low | Radar altimetry + Gravity | Very High (85–95%) | | Reef build-up | Karst topography, sinkholes | LiDAR + Hyperspectral | Moderate (60–70%) | | Unconformity | Erosional flat, soil anomaly | Thermal IR + Hyperspectral | Moderate (55–65%) | | Stratigraphic lens | No surface expression | Indirect (seismic needed) | Low (40–50%) | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Hydrocarbon Column Height from Buoyancy Equilibrium The maximum hydrocarbon column height $h_{max}$ that a trap can hold before leakage is governed by **capillary pressure balance** at the seal-reservoir interface: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{cap} &= P_{buoy} \\ \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r} &= (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC}) \cdot g \cdot h_{max} \end{aligned} $$ Solving for $h_{max}$: $$h_{max} = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC})}$$ Where: - $\gamma$ = interfacial tension (oil-water: ~25 mN/m; gas-water: ~50 mN/m) - $\theta$ = contact angle (typically 30°–60°) - $r$ = pore throat radius of seal rock (m) ### Numerical Example For a shale cap rock with $r = 10^{-7}$ m, $\gamma = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, $\cos\theta = 0.85$, $\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC} = 250$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{max} &= \frac{2 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.85}{10^{-7} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 250} \\ &= \frac{42.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4525 \times 10^{-4}} \\ &= 173.3 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** A trap with this shale seal can support an oil column up to **~173 m** before capillary leakage occurs — a critical parameter in volumetric reserve estimation. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Petroleum System Workflow (Entrapment → Detection → Production) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Rock
Organic-rich Shale] -->|Catagenesis
60-150 deg C| B[Hydrocarbon
Generation] B -->|Primary Migration
Expulsion| C[Carrier Bed
Sandstone / Limestone] C -->|Secondary Migration
Buoyancy| D{Trap Encounter} D -->|Seal Present| E[Petroleum
Accumulation] D -->|No Seal| F[Leakage to Surface
Microseepage] E -->|Surface Anomaly| G[Remote Sensing
Detection] F -->|Vegetation Stress| G G -->|Seismic Survey| H[Subsurface
Confirmation] H -->|Reserve Estimate| I[Drilling and
Production] I -->|Crude Transport| J[Refinery Feedstock
PEICT631 Domain] G -.->|Lineament Analysis| H ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Trap Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD T[Petroleum Traps] --> S[Structural Traps] T --> ST[Stratigraphic Traps] T --> C[Combination Traps] S --> A1[Anticlinal Trap] S --> A2[Fault Trap] S --> A3[Salt Dome Trap] S --> A4[Fracture Trap] ST --> B1[Lenticular Sand] ST --> B2[Unconformity] ST --> B3[Reef Buildup] ST --> B4[Tar Mat Seal] C --> CB1[Faulted Sand Lens] C --> CB2[Faulted Unconformity] A1 --> A1d[Detected via
Hyperspectral] A2 --> A2d[Detected via
SAR Lineaments] A3 --> A3d[Detected via
Gravity Survey] B1 --> B1d[Indirect Detection
Seismic Only] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Remote Sensing Detection Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Satellite
Acquisition] --> P2[Atmospheric
Correction] P2 --> P3[Band Ratios
and PCA] P3 --> P4[Anomaly
Detection] P4 --> P5{Anomaly
Confirmed?} P5 -->|Yes| P6[Ground
Geochemistry] P5 -->|No| P7[Discard] P6 --> P8[Seismic 2D or 3D] P8 --> P9[Drilling
Decision] P9 --> P10[Production
Telemetry] P10 --> P11[Refinery
Instrumentation] ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Refinery Instrumentation Linkage to Upstream Data ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Upstream U1[Reservoir
Characterization] U2[API Gravity
at Wellhead] U3[Sulfur Content
Online Analyzer] end subgraph Pipeline PL1[Multiphase
Flow Meter] PL2[Custody
Transfer Unit] end subgraph Refinery R1[Crude
Distillation Unit] R2[Hydrotreater
Reactor] R3[SCADA
Control System] end U1 --> PL1 U2 --> PL2 U3 --> PL1 PL1 --> R1 PL2 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 --> R1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define petroleum entrapment. List the four essential elements required for a commercial hydrocarbon accumulation. [3 Marks] > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]** **Model Answer:** *Petroleum entrapment is the geological process by which migrating hydrocarbons become concentrated beneath an impermeable seal within a porous reservoir formation, forming an economically viable accumulation.* The four essential elements are: 1. **Source Rock** — generates hydrocarbons from organic matter. 2. **Reservoir Rock** — porous and permeable rock that stores hydrocarbons. 3. **Cap Rock (Seal)** — impermeable rock preventing vertical escape. 4. **Migration Pathway** — permeable conduits connecting source to reservoir. *(Trap geometry is often added as the fifth element — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing elements.)* --- ### Question 2: What is the role of remote sensing in petroleum exploration? Mention any two types of sensors used. [3 Marks] > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** Remote sensing plays a critical role in petroleum exploration by providing **regional-scale mapping** of geological structures, lineaments, and surface anomalies that indicate subsurface hydrocarbon presence. It dramatically reduces exploration costs by narrowing target areas before expensive seismic surveys. Two types of sensors used: 1. **Multispectral sensors** (e.g., Landsat 8/9 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI) — capture spectral reflectance in multiple bands to detect vegetation stress and soil anomalies. 2. **Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)** (e.g., Sentinel-1) — uses microwave radiation to map surface roughness, lineaments, and structural features independent of cloud cover or daylight. *(1 mark for role, 1 mark per sensor type.)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE – Module 1 Comprehensive Question – Dec 2024 Scheme Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of structural traps** with neat sketches. Discuss why anticlinal traps host the largest share of world oil reserves. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Describe the **mechanism of oil entrapment** from generation to accumulation, highlighting the role of buoyancy, capillary pressure, and seal integrity. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Structural Traps — Types:** 1. **Anticlinal Trap:** An upward arch of stratified rock layers. Hydrocarbons migrate upward and accumulate at the crest of the arch beneath the cap rock. This is the **most productive trap type globally**, exemplified by the Ghawar Field (Saudi Arabia), the world's largest oil field. 2. **Fault Trap:** Formed when strata are displaced along a fault plane. If the up-thrown block juxtaposes permeable reservoir rock against impermeable shale, hydrocarbons are sealed laterally. Examples include many fields in the North Sea and the Gulf of Suez. 3. **Salt Dome Trap:** A piercement structure where a buoyant salt diapir intrudes upward through overlying sedimentary layers, creating a dome-shaped trap on its flanks. Reservoirs drape over the salt or are trapped against its vertical walls. Example: Gulf of Mexico offshore fields. 4. **Fracture Trap:** Hydrocarbons accumulate in fractured and jointed zones within otherwise impermeable crystalline basement rocks (e.g., granites, metamorphics). Example: Bach Ho field, Vietnam. **Why anticlines dominate world reserves:** - Folding produces **large closure areas** (often tens of km²). - The simple, predictable geometry allows **easy mapping** via seismic and even remote sensing. - Associated fracturing **enhances permeability** beyond the matrix value. - Regional seals (e.g., marine shales) drape conformably over anticlines, ensuring **high seal integrity**. - Approximately **~60–65% of world's discovered oil** is in anticlinal traps. *(Sketch worth 3 marks; explanation of dominance worth 4 marks.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b): The mechanism of oil entrapment proceeds through **five sequential stages**: **Stage 1 — Generation (Catagenesis):** Organic-rich source rocks (Type I/II kerogen) are buried to depths of 1000–4500 m. At temperatures of **60°C–150°C**, thermal cracking converts kerogen into liquid hydrocarbons (the "oil window"). **Stage 2 — Primary Migration (Expulsion):** Generated hydrocarbons are expelled from the low-permeability source rock into adjacent permeable carrier beds. Driving forces include **compaction overpressure, thermal expansion, and pressure solution**. **Stage 3 — Secondary Migration:** Hydrocarbons migrate upward along carrier beds due to **buoyancy**: $$F_{buoy} = (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC}) \cdot g \cdot V$$ Migration continues laterally along the carrier bed until a trap is encountered. **Stage 4 — Accumulation:** Hydrocarbons pool at the highest point of the trap closure, displacing formation water downward. The accumulation continues to grow until either the trap is filled to spill point or capillary leakage occurs at the seal-reservoir interface: $$h_{max} = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC})}$$ **Stage 5 — Preservation:** The seal must remain intact against subsequent tectonic, erosional, and biodegradation events. Loss of seal integrity leads to trap failure and oil escape. *(2 marks per stage + 1 mark for the equation = 7 marks total.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **workflow of remote sensing** for petroleum resource development. Discuss the role of spectral anomaly detection in identifying hydrocarbon microseepage. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the **application of multi-spectral, hyperspectral, and SAR remote sensing** in petroleum exploration. Compare their strengths and limitations for the geographic conditions of Kerala. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Remote Sensing Workflow (Block Diagram — 3 marks):** The standard pipeline is: > Satellite/Airborne Data Acquisition → Pre-processing (atmospheric + geometric correction) → Image Enhancement (band ratios, PCA, texture filters) → Lineament Extraction → Spectral Anomaly Detection → Integration with Geophysical Data → Prospect Ranking → Drilling Decision. **Spectral Anomaly Detection — Role (4 marks):** Hydrocarbon microseepage refers to the slow upward leakage of light hydrocarbons (mainly C₁–C₅ alkanes) from a subsurface accumulation to the surface. Although the flux is small, it alters the **geochemistry, mineralogy, and vegetation** of the surface, producing diagnostic spectral signatures: 1. **Bleaching of red beds:** Reduction of iron oxides (hematite) by reducing hydrocarbon gases causes loss of red coloration, detectable in visible-NIR bands. 2. **Carbonate enrichment:** Microbial oxidation of seeping hydrocarbons produces secondary calcite, detectable via SWIR absorption features at ~2.34 µm. 3. **Vegetation stress:** Trees and shrubs over seeps show reduced chlorophyll absorption (red-edge shift toward shorter wavelengths), detectable via hyperspectral indices like NDVI and GRVI. 4. **Magnetic mineral depletion:** Reduction of ferromagnetic minerals (magnetite) by hydrocarbon fluids, detectable via aeromagnetic surveys integrated with remote sensing. These anomalies form the basis of the *Direct Detection Method* of remote sensing exploration. --- #### Model Solution for (b): | Sensor Type | Wavelength | Strength | Limitation | Kerala Suitability | |-------------|-----------|----------|------------|--------------------| | **Multispectral** (Landsat 8/9, Sentinel-2) | VIS–SWIR | Wide coverage, free, regular revisit | Coarse spectral resolution (10–30 m), misses narrow absorption features | Moderate — limited by cloud cover during monsoon | | **Hyperspectral** (AVIRIS, PRISMA, EnMAP) | VIS–SWIR (200+ bands) | Detects narrow diagnostic absorption features (e.g., carbonate, clay, vegetation stress) | Expensive, lower spatial resolution, complex data processing | High — exceptional mineralogical discrimination | | **SAR (Radar)** (Sentinel-1, ALOS-PALSAR) | Microwave (C-band, L-band) | Cloud-penetrating, day/night operation, sensitive to surface roughness and structural features | Speckle noise, complex interpretation | Very High — ideal for tropical monsoon climate with persistent cloud cover | **Kerala-Specific Discussion:** Kerala's Western Ghats region is one of the most cloud-covered terrains in India, with 6–8 months of dense cloud cover annually. This makes **optical multispectral and hyperspectral sensors unreliable** during monsoons. **SAR sensors (Sentinel-1, NISAR) are therefore the optimal choice** for year-round structural mapping. For dry-season surveys (December–March), hyperspectral airborne surveys (e.g., AVIRIS-NG, which has been flown over India) can provide unparalleled mineralogical detail relevant to onshore sedimentary basin studies (e.g., the Kerala-Konkan basin). *(3 marks for table; 4 marks for Kerala-specific discussion.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing source rock with reservoir rock** — students frequently mix these up. Source rock *generates* hydrocarbons; reservoir rock *stores* them. Cap rock is *impermeable*, not porous. > 2. **Stating "anticline" without specifying seal** — a structural closure alone is not a trap; the impermeable seal is essential. > 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — density in kg/m³, permeability in Darcy or m², API in °API. > 4. **Treating remote sensing as direct detection** — remote sensing detects *surface indicators*; only seismic/drill-core confirms *subsurface* hydrocarbon presence. > 5. **Ignoring cloud-cover issues in Kerala** — recommending Landsat-only workflows without acknowledging monsoon limitations loses marks in application questions. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Petroleum Trap = Source Rock + Migration Pathway + Reservoir Rock + Cap Rock + Trap Geometry** (all five required). - **Structural traps (~65% world reserves)** include anticline, fault, salt dome, and fracture traps. - **Stratigraphic traps** include lenticular, unconformity, reef, and tar-mat seals. - **Combination traps** integrate both structural and stratigraphic elements. - **Buoyancy force density:** $F_{buoy}/V = (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC}) \cdot g$ — always positive for HC; drives upward migration. - **Capillary seal capacity:** $h_{max} = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{w} - \rho_{HC})}$ — limits maximum oil column height. - **API gravity** classifies crude as light ($> 31.1°$API), medium (22.3–31.1°API), or heavy ($< 22.3°$API). - **Reservoir quality benchmarks:** $\phi \geq 8\%$, $k \geq 1$ mD minimum for commercial reservoir. - **Remote sensing sensors for petroleum:** Multispectral (Landsat, Sentinel-2), Hyperspectral (AVIRIS, PRISMA), SAR (Sentinel-1). - **Direct indicators** of HC microseepage: red-bed bleaching, carbonate enrichment, vegetation stress (red-edge shift), magnetic mineral depletion. - **Indirect indicators:** lineaments, circular features, fault patterns, anticlinal curvature. - **Kerala-specific best sensor:** SAR (cloud-penetrating); hyperspectral (dry season only). - **Oil window:** 60–150°C, 1000–4500 m depth. - **Densities to remember:** Water 1000–1200 kg/m³; Oil 700–950 kg/m³; Gas 0.1–400 kg/m³. - **Refinery linkage:** API gravity and sulfur content measured upstream guide downstream refinery crude selection — a direct bridge from exploration to PEICT631 instrumentation domain.

topics.title: Basin and Exploration Strategies, The Model Approach to Exploration Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basin and Exploration Strategies, The Model Approach to Exploration Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sedimentary Basin — Formal Definition

A **sedimentary basin** is a depression in the Earth's crust, of regional extent, in which a significant thickness of sedimentary rocks has accumulated over geological time. These basins are the primary locations where hydrocarbon generation, migration, and accumulation occur, making them the fundamental unit of study in petroleum exploration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A sedimentary basin is defined as a region where the rate of sediment accumulation exceeds the rate of subsidence, allowing long-term preservation of organic-rich strata capable of generating hydrocarbons under thermal maturation.

Mathematically, the **basin subsidence rate** $(S)$ over geological time can be expressed as:

$$S(t) = \frac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$$

where:
- $S(t)$ = subsidence rate in meters per million years (m/My)
- $\Delta h$ = change in basement depth (meters)
- $\Delta t$ = time interval (million years)

## 1.2 Exploration Strategy — Formal Definition

An **exploration strategy** is a systematic, phased, and economically-optimized plan designed to identify, evaluate, and delineate potential hydrocarbon accumulations within a sedimentary basin. It integrates geological, geophysical, geochemical, and engineering data to minimize financial risk and maximize the probability of commercial discovery.

## 1.3 The Model Approach to Exploration Strategy — Definition

The **Model Approach** is a probabilistic, data-driven methodology where a "play" or "prospect" is conceptualized as a set of interdependent geological variables (reservoir, source, seal, trap, migration, timing). Each variable is assigned a probability of success, and the overall exploration risk is computed as the product of conditional probabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Imagine you are a treasure hunter looking for buried gold across a vast countryside (the *basin*). Instead of randomly digging, you:
> 1. **Study the geology** (where ancient rivers might have deposited gold — analogous to *source rock deposition*).
> 2. **Use aerial maps and metal detectors** (analogous to *seismic surveys* and *well logging instruments*).
> 3. **Identify the most promising spots** (analogous to *prospect generation*).
> 4. **Estimate the chance of finding gold at each spot** (the *probability of success*, $P$).
> 5. **Plan your digging in stages** — cheapest surveys first, drilling last (the *phased exploration strategy*).
>
> The **Model Approach** simply means: instead of guessing, you build a mathematical model of the treasure location using probabilities, just like an actuary calculates insurance risk.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board Examiner Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | Industrial Context |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Play** | A family of prospects sharing common geological attributes | Regional exploration unit |
| **Prospect** | A specific mappable trap potential for hydrocarbons | Drilling target |
| **Lead** | An unverified geological anomaly requiring further work | Initial exploration indicator |
| **Trap** | A geometric arrangement of rocks sealing hydrocarbons | Reservoir containment |
| **Reservoir** | Porous, permeable rock capable of storing fluids | Hydrocarbon storage |
| **Seal** | Impermeable rock preventing fluid escape | Cap rock |
| **Source Rock** | Organic-rich rock capable of generating hydrocarbons | Kerogen-bearing strata |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sedimentary Basins

Sedimentary basins are classified based on their **tectonic origin**, which fundamentally controls their geometry, thermal history, and hydrocarbon potential.

### 2.1.1 Types of Basins

1. **Divergent (Extensional) Basins**
   - Formed by lithospheric stretching and rifting.
   - Examples: North Sea, Red Sea, Gulf of Suez.
   - High geothermal gradients → favorable for oil and gas generation.

2. **Convergent (Compressional) Basins**
   - Associated with subduction zones and fold belts.
   - Examples: Persian Gulf foreland basin, Zagros Basin.
   - Characterized by folded traps and thrust structures.

3. **Strike-Slip Basins**
   - Formed along transform fault systems.
   - Examples: San Andreas fault basins, Vienna Basin.
   - Often narrow, deep, and rapidly subsiding.

4. **Intracratonic Basins**
   - Located within stable continental interiors.
   - Examples: Michigan Basin, Paris Basin.
   - Slow subsidence, mature source rocks.

5. **Passive Margin Basins**
   - Located along continental edges not at active plate boundaries.
   - Examples: Atlantic Coastal margin, Western Australian shelf.
   - Thick sedimentary wedges with good reservoir potential.

## 2.2 The Model Approach — Detailed Framework

The **Model Approach** to exploration strategy was formalized by petroleum geoscientists in the 1970s and is now the industry-standard methodology. It rests on **three pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Play Analysis (Regional Scale)
- Identifies whether the geological conditions for hydrocarbon accumulation exist in a basin.
- Evaluates the presence of **source rock, reservoir rock, seal rock, and timing**.

### Pillar 2: Prospect Generation (Local Scale)
- Uses geophysical data (2D/3D seismic) to identify structural or stratigraphic traps.
- Maps potential drilling targets.

### Pillar 3: Risk and Volume Assessment
- Assigns probabilities to each geological element.
- Estimates hydrocarbon volumes using volumetric equations.

## 2.3 Geological Risk Analysis (GRA) — The Heart of the Model

The **Probability of Success (POS)** for a prospect is calculated as the product of individual conditional probabilities for each essential geological element:

$$P_{success} = P_{source} \times P_{reservoir} \times P_{trap} \times P_{seal} \times P_{migration} \times P_{timing}$$

where each $P_i$ is the marginal probability of the corresponding geological element being present and effective.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all critical formulas required for KTU University Exam questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | $P_{success} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Probability of geological success | $0 \le P_i \le 1$ |
| 2 | $V = 7758 \times A \times h \times \phi \times (1 - S_w) / B_o$ | Hydrocarbon pore volume (STOIIP) | Barrels of oil |
| 3 | $V_g = 43560 \times A \times h \times \phi \times (1 - S_w) / B_g$ | Gas initially in place | Standard cubic feet (scf) |
| 4 | $GR = \frac{V_p}{V_s} = \frac{\phi}{1 - \phi}$ | Velocity ratio (geophysical) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_{o} = \frac{\sum C_i P_i}{\sum C_i}$ | Expected monetary value (weighted risk) | Monetary units |
| 6 | $EMV = (P_s \times V_s) - (P_f \times C_f)$ | Expected Monetary Value | Currency |
| 7 | $\rho = \frac{M}{V}$ | Bulk density of source rock | g/cm³ |
| 8 | $TIIP = A \times h \times \phi$ | Total initial hydrocarbon pore volume | Reservoir volume units |
| 9 | $R = \frac{V_p}{V_b}$ | Porosity ratio | $0 < R < 1$ |
| 10 | $TOC(\%) = \frac{M_{organic}}{M_{rock}} \times 100$ | Total organic carbon content | Percentage by weight |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Notation:**
> - $A$ = Area of trap (acres or m²)
> - $h$ = Net pay thickness (ft or m)
> - $\phi$ = Porosity (fraction)
> - $S_w$ = Water saturation (fraction)
> - $B_o$ = Oil formation volume factor (rb/stb)
> - $B_g$ = Gas formation volume factor (rcf/scf)
> - $P_s, P_f$ = Probabilities of success and failure
> - $V_s, C_f$ = Value of success and cost of failure

## 2.5 Phases of Exploration Strategy (Real-World Workflow)

The exploration process is structured into **sequential phases** with progressive capital commitment:

1. **Regional Reconnaissance (Low Cost)**
   - Basin identification using gravity, magnetic, and satellite data.
   - Capital expenditure: minimal (₹ crores).

2. **Geophysical Surveys (Moderate Cost)**
   - 2D seismic acquisition and processing.
   - Capital expenditure: substantial (₹ tens of crores).

3. **Detailed Prospect Evaluation (High Cost)**
   - 3D seismic interpretation, AVO analysis, inversion.
   - Capital expenditure: significant.

4. **Exploratory Drilling (Very High Cost)**
   - Drilling wildcat wells to test prospects.
   - Capital expenditure: extremely high (₹ hundreds of crores).

5. **Appraisal and Delineation (Highest Cost)**
   - Confirming discovery, estimating reserves, planning development.
   - Capital expenditure: massive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Industrial Reality:** Each progressive phase costs roughly **5–10× more** than the previous one. Hence the strategic importance of rigorous geological and geophysical analysis before drilling — a single offshore well can cost **USD 100+ million**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Probability of Success Formula

**Starting Point:** We consider a prospect requires **n independent geological elements** (typically n = 4 to 6) to be present for hydrocarbon accumulation.

**Step 1 — Define Marginal Probability**

The marginal probability $P_i$ of each element (source, reservoir, trap, seal, etc.) being present is a value between 0 and 1, assigned by the exploration geologist based on available data.

$$0 \le P_i \le 1 \quad \text{for} \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Step 2 — Assume Independence**

For the simplest model, we assume the geological elements are **statistically independent** events. This means the probability of all elements coexisting is the product of their individual probabilities.

$$P_{all} = P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3 \times \cdots \times P_n$$

**Step 3 — Express as Product Notation**

$$\boxed{P_{success} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Logarithmic Transformation for Analysis**

Taking natural logarithms (useful for risk analysis and sensitivity studies):

$$\ln(P_{success}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln(P_i)$$

This linear form allows easy identification of which element most reduces overall success probability (largest negative $\ln P_i$ contributes most).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Geological Risk Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**

An exploration team evaluates a prospect with the following marginal probabilities for each geological element:
- Source rock present: $P_1 = 0.7$
- Reservoir rock present: $P_2 = 0.6$
- Trap geometry exists: $P_3 = 0.5$
- Seal rock effective: $P_4 = 0.8$
- Hydrocarbon migration occurred: $P_5 = 0.5$
- Timing favorable: $P_6 = 0.9$

**Calculate:**
(a) The overall probability of success.
(b) Which element contributes most to the risk?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the Probability Formula**

$$P_{success} = P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3 \times P_4 \times P_5 \times P_6$$

$$P_{success} = 0.7 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 0.9$$

**Step 2 — Multiply Sequentially**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{success} &= 0.7 \times 0.6 \\
&= 0.42 \\
&= 0.42 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.21 \\
&= 0.21 \times 0.8 \\
&= 0.168 \\
&= 0.168 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.084 \\
&= 0.084 \times 0.9 \\
&= 0.0756
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — State the Final Answer**

$$\boxed{P_{success} \approx 0.0756 \text{ or } 7.56\%}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Highest Risk Element**

Compute $\ln P_i$ for each element:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(0.7) &= -0.357 \\
\ln(0.6) &= -0.511 \\
\ln(0.5) &= -0.693 \\
\ln(0.8) &= -0.223 \\
\ln(0.5) &= -0.693 \\
\ln(0.9) &= -0.105
\end{aligned}$$

The most negative values are $-0.693$ for **trap geometry** and **migration** (both at 0.5). These are the **highest-risk elements** requiring additional geological or geophysical investigation.

## 3.3 Derivation: Expected Monetary Value (EMV)

The **Expected Monetary Value** balances potential reward against risk:

**Step 1 — Define Success Scenario Value $V_s$**

If the prospect succeeds (contains commercial hydrocarbons), the company gains the **net present value of the discovery** (estimated recoverable reserves × price − costs).

$$V_s = \text{NPV}_{discovery}$$

**Step 2 — Define Failure Scenario Cost $C_f$**

If the prospect fails (dry well), the company loses the **dry hole cost** (drilling + testing expenditure with zero revenue).

$$C_f = \text{Dry hole cost}$$

**Step 3 — Combine Using Probabilities**

$$\text{EMV} = (P_s \times V_s) - (P_f \times C_f)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $P_f = 1 - P_s$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV} &= P_s \cdot V_s - (1 - P_s) \cdot C_f \\
&= P_s \cdot V_s - C_f + P_s \cdot C_f \\
&= P_s(V_s + C_f) - C_f
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EMV} = P_s \cdot V_s - (1 - P_s) \cdot C_f}$$

**Decision Rule:** A prospect should be drilled if **EMV > 0**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Volumetric Reserve Estimation (STOIIP)

The **Stock Tank Oil Initially In Place (STOIIP)** is calculated as:

$$\text{STOIIP} = 7758 \times A \times h \times \phi \times (1 - S_w) / B_o$$

**Step 1 — Define Rock Volume**

$$V_{rock} = A \times h \quad \text{(acre-ft)}$$

**Step 2 — Define Hydrocarbon Pore Volume (HCPV)**

$$V_{HCPV} = V_{rock} \times \phi \times (1 - S_w)$$

**Step 3 — Convert Reservoir Conditions to Stock Tank Conditions**

Oil at reservoir conditions occupies a larger volume than at surface. The **formation volume factor** $B_o$ converts reservoir barrels (rb) to stock tank barrels (stb).

$$V_{STO} = \frac{V_{HCPV}}{B_o}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Unit Conversion Constant**

The constant **7758** converts acre-ft × porosity (fraction) × hydrocarbon saturation × 1/$B_o$ to stock tank barrels.

$$\boxed{\text{STOIIP} = 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_o}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Basin Type vs. Hydrocarbon Potential

| Basin Type | Tectonic Setting | Thermal Regime | Hydrocarbon Potential | Example |
|------------|------------------|----------------|----------------------|---------|
| Rift | Extensional | High heat flow | Excellent for oil & gas | North Sea |
| Foreland | Compressional | Moderate | Giant gas fields | Persian Gulf |
| Strike-slip | Transform | Variable | Complex traps | San Joaquin |
| Intracratonic | Stable interior | Low | Mostly gas | Michigan |
| Passive margin | Stable edge | Low-moderate | Proven plays | NW Shelf Australia |
| Back-arc | Subduction-related | Variable | Variable | Andaman |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Petroleum Exploration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Basin Identification"] --> B["Regional Geological Study"]
    B --> C["Geophysical Survey 2D Seismic"]
    C --> D["Prospect Generation"]
    D --> E["3D Seismic Acquisition"]
    E --> F["Geological Risk Analysis"]
    F --> G["Probability of Success Calculation"]
    G --> H{"Drillable Prospect?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Exploratory Drilling"]
    H -- No --> J["Re-evaluate or Abandon"]
    I --> K["Discovery Evaluation"]
    K --> L{"Commercial?"}
    L -- Yes --> M["Appraisal and Development"]
    L -- No --> N["Plug and Abandon"]
    M --> O["Production"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart maps the **phased exploration strategy** from basin-scale reconnaissance to production. Each decision gate (rhombus shape) acts as a filter, reducing risk progressively while escalating capital commitment.

## 4.2 Schematic — The Model Approach to Exploration Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Source Rock Probability"] --> M["Combined Success Probability"]
    P2["Reservoir Rock Probability"] --> M
    P3["Trap Geometry Probability"] --> M
    P4["Seal Rock Probability"] --> M
    P5["Migration Probability"] --> M
    P6["Timing Probability"] --> M
    M --> R["Risk Assessment Output"]
    R --> EMV["Expected Monetary Value"]
    EMV --> DR["Drill or Drop Decision"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each independent geological factor feeds into the multiplicative success probability. The Model Approach uses this **bottom-up risk composition** to guide investment decisions.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Basin Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["Tectonic Setting Analysis"] --> B2["Stratigraphic Framework"]
    B2 --> B3["Source Rock Evaluation"]
    B2 --> B4["Reservoir Characterization"]
    B2 --> B5["Seal Integrity Analysis"]
    B3 --> B6["Play Concept Definition"]
    B4 --> B6
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7["Prospect Inventory"]
    B7 --> B8["Risk-weighted Ranking"]
    B8 --> B9["Drilling Candidate Selection"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Drilling Investment Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Estimated STOIIP Greater Than Threshold?"] -->|"Yes"| Q2["EMV Greater Than Zero?"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| Drop["Drop Prospect"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| Drill["Proceed with Drilling"]
    Q2 -->|"No"| Drop
    Drill --> Outcome["Success or Failure"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define the term "Sedimentary Basin" and list any four types of basins based on tectonic origin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sedimentary basin** is a depression in the Earth's crust where sediments accumulate over geological time, forming thick sequences of sedimentary rocks that may host hydrocarbon accumulations.

Four types of basins based on tectonic origin:
1. **Divergent (Extensional) Basins** — formed by lithospheric stretching, e.g., North Sea.
2. **Convergent (Compressional) Basins** — formed at convergent plate boundaries, e.g., Zagros Basin.
3. **Strike-slip Basins** — formed along transform faults, e.g., San Andreas system.
4. **Intracratonic Basins** — formed within stable continental interiors, e.g., Michigan Basin.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for the four types.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`
**What is meant by the "Model Approach" in petroleum exploration strategy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Model Approach** is a probabilistic, data-driven exploration methodology that conceptualizes a prospect as a set of interdependent geological elements (source, reservoir, trap, seal, migration, timing). Each element is assigned a marginal probability, and the overall success probability is computed as their product. This allows exploration teams to quantitatively rank prospects, manage risk, and allocate capital efficiently across a portfolio of drilling opportunities.

*(Award full 3 marks for a concise definition + identification of the probabilistic nature.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Students must answer **ONE full question** (either Option A or Option B) in the exam. Each option carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **OPTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the classification of sedimentary basins with suitable examples. Discuss the geological characteristics that make a basin prospective for hydrocarbon exploration.

**Model Answer:**

A sedimentary basin is classified based on its tectonic setting, which controls subsidence, sedimentation patterns, heat flow, and ultimately hydrocarbon potential.

**1. Divergent (Extensional) Basins**
- Formed by lithospheric stretching and crustal thinning.
- Initial rift stage produces lacustrine source rocks.
- Drift stage produces marine shales as source rocks.
- High geothermal gradients favor early thermal maturation.
- **Examples:** North Sea Basin, Gulf of Suez, Cambay Basin (India).

**2. Convergent (Compressional) Basins**
- Formed at convergent plate margins (subduction/collision).
- Foreland basins develop adjacent to fold-thrust belts.
- Contain large structural traps (anticlines, fault blocks).
- **Examples:** Persian Gulf Foreland Basin, Zagros Basin.

**3. Strike-slip Basins**
- Formed along transform fault systems.
- Localized rapid subsidence → deep basin centers.
- Often contain excellent source rocks due to restricted circulation.
- **Examples:** Los Angeles Basin, Dead Sea Basin.

**4. Intracratonic Basins**
- Located within stable continental interiors.
- Slow, long-term subsidence over hundreds of millions of years.
- Mature source rocks; predominantly gas-prone.
- **Examples:** Michigan Basin, Paris Basin, Vindhyan Basin.

**5. Passive Margin Basins**
- Located along passive continental margins.
- Receive enormous sediment supply from continental rivers.
- Excellent reservoirs in deltaic and shallow-marine sandstones.
- **Examples:** Gulf of Mexico, NW Shelf of Australia.

**Prospectivity Criteria:**
- Presence of organic-rich source rock (TOC > 1%).
- Mature source rock (vitrinite reflectance $R_o$ between 0.6% and 1.3%).
- Quality reservoir rock (porosity > 10%, permeability > 1 mD).
- Effective seal rock (capillary entry pressure high).
- Favorable migration pathway and timing.

*(Award 7 marks: 1 for introduction + 1.2 each for five basin types + 1 for prospectivity criteria.)*

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** The marginal probabilities for a prospect are: Source rock = 0.8, Reservoir = 0.7, Trap = 0.6, Seal = 0.9, Migration = 0.5. Calculate the overall probability of success. If the dry hole cost is ₹50 crores and the expected success value is ₹500 crores, determine whether the prospect should be drilled using the EMV criterion.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Overall Probability of Success**

$$P_s = P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3 \times P_4 \times P_5$$

$$P_s = 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.6 \times 0.9 \times 0.5$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_s &= 0.8 \times 0.7 \\
&= 0.56 \\
&= 0.56 \times 0.6 \\
&= 0.336 \\
&= 0.336 \times 0.9 \\
&= 0.3024 \\
&= 0.3024 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.1512
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P_s = 0.1512 \text{ or } 15.12\%}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Probability of Failure**

$$P_f = 1 - P_s = 1 - 0.1512 = 0.8488$$

**Step 3: Calculate Expected Monetary Value (EMV)**

$$\text{EMV} = (P_s \times V_s) - (P_f \times C_f)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV} &= (0.1512 \times 500) - (0.8488 \times 50) \\
&= 75.6 - 42.44 \\
&= 33.16 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EMV} = +₹33.16 \text{ crores}}$$

**Step 4: Decision**

Since **EMV > 0**, the prospect is **economically viable** and should be **drilled**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often forget to convert percentages to decimals — wrong by a factor of 100.
> - Some confuse $P_f$ with $P_s$ — must use $1 - P_s$ for failure probability.
> - Always state the decision explicitly (drill / drop) — examiners allocate marks for the conclusion.
> - In EMV calculations, ensure both $V_s$ and $C_f$ are in the same currency units before substitution.

---

### **OPTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, describe the phased exploration strategy used in petroleum exploration. Discuss the role of geophysical methods in each phase.

**Model Answer:**

Petroleum exploration follows a **phased approach**, with capital commitment escalating at each stage.

**Phase 1: Reconnaissance (Regional Scale)**
- Activities: Remote sensing, gravity and magnetic surveys, geological mapping.
- Cost: Low (₹ crores).
- Output: Identification of prospective basins.

**Phase 2: 2D Seismic Surveys**
- Activities: Acquisition of 2D seismic lines across the basin.
- Cost: Moderate (₹ tens of crores).
- Output: Identification of leads and large structural trends.

**Phase 3: Detailed Prospect Evaluation**
- Activities: 3D seismic surveys, AVO analysis, seismic inversion.
- Cost: High (₹ hundreds of crores).
- Output: Mapped prospects with volumetric and risk estimates.

**Phase 4: Exploratory Drilling**
- Activities: Drilling wildcat exploration wells.
- Cost: Very high (₹ hundreds of crores per well, especially offshore).
- Output: Discovery or dry hole.

**Phase 5: Appraisal**
- Activities: Drilling appraisal wells, well testing, reservoir simulation.
- Cost: Massive.
- Output: Confirmed reserves and development plan.

**Role of Geophysical Methods:**

| Phase | Geophysical Method | Purpose |
|-------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | Gravity, Magnetics | Basin delineation |
| 2 | 2D Seismic | Regional mapping |
| 3 | 3D Seismic, AVO, Inversion | Prospect definition |
| 4 | LWD, Mud Logging | Real-time drilling decisions |
| 5 | 4D Seismic, Production Logging | Reservoir monitoring |

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for the flowchart description + 2 for phases + 2 for geophysical role + 1 for conclusion.)*

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Derive the STOIIP (Stock Tank Oil Initially In Place) formula for an oil reservoir. Given a prospect with area = 500 acres, net pay thickness = 50 ft, porosity = 20%, water saturation = 30%, and oil formation volume factor $B_o = 1.25$ rb/stb, calculate the STOIIP.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Bulk Rock Volume**

The bulk rock volume is the geometric product of the trap area and net pay thickness:

$$V_{rock} = A \times h \quad \text{(acre-ft)}$$

**Step 2: Define Pore Volume**

Multiplying bulk volume by porosity gives the pore volume:

$$V_{pore} = V_{rock} \times \phi \quad \text{(acre-ft)}$$

**Step 3: Define Hydrocarbon Pore Volume**

Subtracting the water-filled pore volume from total pore volume yields HCPV:

$$V_{HCPV} = V_{pore} \times (1 - S_w) \quad \text{(acre-ft)}$$

**Step 4: Convert Reservoir Volume to Surface Conditions**

The **formation volume factor** $B_o$ converts reservoir barrels to stock tank barrels. Thus:

$$V_{STO} = \frac{V_{HCPV}}{B_o}$$

**Step 5: Apply Unit Conversion**

One acre-foot equals 7758 barrels. Therefore:

$$\text{STOIIP} = 7758 \times A \times h \times \phi \times (1 - S_w) / B_o$$

**Step 6: Substitute the Given Values**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{STOIIP} &= 7758 \times 500 \times 50 \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.30) / 1.25 \\
&= 7758 \times 500 \times 50 \times 0.20 \times 0.70 / 1.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Compute Sequentially**

$$\begin{aligned}
7758 \times 500 &= 3,879,000 \\
3,879,000 \times 50 &= 193,950,000 \\
193,950,000 \times 0.20 &= 38,790,000 \\
38,790,000 \times 0.70 &= 27,153,000 \\
27,153,000 / 1.25 &= 21,722,400
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{STOIIP} = 21,722,400 \text{ stock tank barrels} \approx 21.72 \text{ MMstb}}$$

*(Award 7 marks: 3 for derivation + 1 for formula + 1 for substitution + 2 for calculation.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit consistency trap:** Always keep $A$ in acres, $h$ in ft → result is barrels. If SI units are used, a different constant applies.
> - **Confusing porosity $\phi$ with permeability $k$** — these are different properties.
> - **Forgetting $B_o$** in the denominator — examiners specifically check for this.
> - **$S_w$ is water saturation**, not oil saturation. Oil saturation $S_o = 1 - S_w$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- **Sedimentary Basin:** A depression where sediment accumulates; classified by tectonic origin.
- **Five Basin Types:** Divergent, Convergent, Strike-slip, Intracratonic, Passive Margin — each has unique hydrocarbon potential.
- **Exploration Strategy:** Phased approach from regional reconnaissance → appraisal drilling.
- **Model Approach:** Probabilistic method using independent geological variables.
- **Probability of Success:** $P_s = \prod P_i$ — multiplication of all marginal probabilities.
- **EMV Formula:** $\text{EMV} = P_s V_s - (1 - P_s) C_f$ — drill if EMV > 0.
- **STOIIP Formula:** $7758 \times A \times h \times \phi \times (1 - S_w) / B_o$ — convert to MMstb by dividing by $10^6$.
- **Highest Risk Element:** The one with the smallest $P_i$ (largest negative $\ln P_i$).
- **Phase Cost Escalation:** Each phase costs roughly 5–10× the previous one.
- **Risk Reduction:** Risk decreases with each exploration phase; information value increases.
- **TOC and $R_o$:** Key source rock maturity indicators ($R_o$ = 0.6–1.3% for oil window).
- **Geophysical Tools:** Gravity, magnetic, 2D/3D seismic, AVO, inversion, well logging.
- **Capital Intensity:** Drilling an offshore well can exceed USD 100 million — making risk analysis critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depositional Systems Such as Marine, Non-Marine, Coastal, Shelf, Carbonate Evaporites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Depositional Systems Such as Marine, Non-Marine, Coastal, Shelf, Carbonate Evaporites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# DEPOSITIONAL SYSTEMS: FOUNDATIONS FOR RESERVOIR CHARACTERIZATION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depositional System (Formal KTU Definition):** A three-dimensional assemblage of lithofacies that are genetically linked by active (contemporary) sedimentary processes and environments, as defined by **Fisher & McGowan (1967)** and refined by **Dalrymple (2010)**. In petroleum engineering, a depositional system is the genetic framework that controls reservoir geometry, porosity-permeability distribution, and heterogeneity — directly impacting hydrocarbon recovery and instrument-based downhole logging interpretation.

In simple terms, a **depositional system** is a *natural factory* that manufactures sedimentary rocks. The "raw materials" (grains, ions, organic matter) enter the factory, the "machinery" (water, wind, gravity, chemistry) processes them, and the "finished product" (sandstone, shale, limestone, evaporite) exits as a potential hydrocarbon reservoir or seal.

## 1.2 The Five Major Depositional Realms

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 Highlight:** Students must master **five** depositional classifications: **Marine, Non-Marine, Coastal, Shelf, and Carbonate-Evaporite**. Each governs distinct reservoir properties that refinery instrumentation must later characterize.

### Real-World Analogy

Imagine five different **kitchen recipes** that produce five different types of bread:
- **Marine recipe** = deep, slow-cooked, uniform texture
- **Non-Marine recipe** = fast, variable, unpredictable texture
- **Coastal recipe** = layered, sandwiched, mixed ingredients
- **Shelf recipe** = thin, sheet-like, finely ground
- **Carbonate-Evaporite recipe** = chemically precipitated, crystalline

Each "bread" has different porosity (holes) and permeability (connected holes) — exactly what refinery instruments measure!

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Sea-level reference** | **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** | Datum for marine/non-marine boundary |
| **Average continental shelf slope** | **0.1° – 0.5°** | Boundary geometry |
| **Average shelf break depth** | **~120 – 200 m** | Shelf-edge definition |
| **Salinity threshold (marine)** | **35 ‰ (parts per thousand)** | Marine vs. brackish classification |
| **Evaporite precipitation order** | **Calcite → Gypsum → Halite → Sylvite** | Carnallite |
| **Compaction factor (sandstone)** | **~25 – 30 % porosity loss** per km burial | Subsidence calculations |
| **Standard temperature gradient** | **25 – 30 °C/km** | Reservoir diagenesis |

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview: The Depositional Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sediment Source] --> B{Transport Medium}
    B -->|Water - Marine| C[Marine System]
    B -->|Water - River/Fluvial| D[Non-Marine System]
    B -->|Wave/Tide - Coastal| E[Coastal System]
    B -->|Low Energy - Shelf| F[Shelf System]
    B -->|Chemical Precipitation| G[Carbonate-Evaporite]
    C --> H[Reservoir Rock]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> I[Seal/Cap Rock]
    G --> H
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Refinery Instrumentation Context:** Each depositional system creates a unique **fingerprint** in well logs (Gamma Ray, SP, Resistivity, Sonic, NMR, Density). Recognizing the depositional origin from these log signatures is a fundamental skill for petroleum/reservoir engineers.
