# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS: THE FIVE DEPOSITIONAL SYSTEMS

## 2.1 Marine Depositional Systems

### Definition

Marine systems operate **below the fair-weather wave base**, in fully saline open-ocean waters (salinity ~35 ‰). They are dominated by **pelagic settling**, **turbidity currents**, and **contour currents**.

### Key Characteristics

- **Energy regime:** Low to moderate (depending on depth)
- **Sediment supply:** Fine-grained (clay, silt, ooze) at abyssal depths; coarser at shelf margins
- **Water depth range:** From ~10 m (inner marine) to >4,000 m (abyssal plain)
- **Dominant lithologies:** Marine shale, deep-water sandstone (turbidites), chalk, chert
- **Bioturbation:** High in shallow marine; absent in anoxic deep marine

### Subdivisions

| Sub-environment | Depth Range | Typical Lithology | Reservoir Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shallow marine** | 0–200 m | Sandstone, fossiliferous limestone | Excellent |
| **Deep marine (shelf)** | 200–2,000 m | Shale, marl, siltstone | Poor (seal) |
| **Bathyal** | 2,000–4,000 m | Hemipelagic mud | Very poor |
| **Abyssal** | >4,000 m | Pelagic ooze, red clay | Negligible |

### Why It Matters

Marine shales are the **world's most prolific source rocks** (e.g., Kimmeridge Clay, Bakken Shale). Marine sandstones deposited by turbidity currents form some of the **largest deep-water reservoirs** (e.g., Forties Field, North Sea; Jack Formation, Gulf of Mexico).

---

## 2.2 Non-Marine (Continental) Depositional Systems

### Definition

Non-marine systems operate **entirely on land**, with sediment transport driven by rivers, wind, glaciers, and gravity. They have **no direct marine influence** and salinity is **< 0.5 ‰ (freshwater)**.

### Sub-environments

- **Fluvial (River):** Channel sandstones, point bars, floodplain mudstones
- **Aeolian (Wind):** Cross-bedded, well-sorted dune sandstones (e.g., Rotliegend, North Sea; Nugget Sandstone, Utah)
- **Lacustrine (Lake):** Organic-rich shales (e.g., Green River Formation — major oil shale)
- **Glacial:** Diamictites, tillites (low reservoir quality)
- **Alluvial fan:** Conglomerates, breccias (proximal to mountain fronts)

### Key Characteristics

- **Energy regime:** Highly variable (catastrophic floods to quiet lakes)
- **Heterogeneity:** **Very high** — lateral facies changes over meters
- **Reservoir geometry:** Ribbon-shaped channels; sheet-like floodplains
- **Color:** Often red (oxidized iron) — "red beds"

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for Non-Marine Reservoir Characterization:**

| Formula | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Darcy's Law** | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | Fluid flow through fluvial sandstone |
| **Porosity (total)** | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{void}}{V_{bulk}}$ | Reservoir storage capacity |
| **Permeability (Klinkenberg)** | $k_{liquid} = k_{gas} \left(1 + \dfrac{b}{P_{mean}}\right)$ | Correct gas-permeability for low-pressure lab measurements |
| **Hydraulic conductivity** | $K = \dfrac{k \cdot \rho \cdot g}{\mu}$ | Groundwater/reservoir analog |
| **Critical shear stress (Shields)** | $\tau_{cr} = \theta_{cr} \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot D_{50}$ | Sediment transport threshold in channels |
| **Compaction correction** | $\phi_z = \phi_0 \cdot e^{-c \cdot z}$ | Porosity at depth z |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used in tables to preserve Markdown integrity.**

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In refinery instrumentation, **fining-upward fluvial sequences** produce characteristic **"bell-shaped" Gamma Ray curves** on well logs — a signature instantly recognized by petrophysicists. Non-marine reservoirs often require **horizontal drilling** due to their limited vertical extent.

---

## 2.3 Coastal Depositional Systems

### Definition

Coastal systems occupy the **transition zone between marine and non-marine environments**. They are high-energy, dynamic settings dominated by **waves, tides, and longshore currents**.

### Sub-environments

- **Delta:** River meets sea (e.g., Mississippi, Niger deltas)
- **Beach / Barrier island:** Wave-dominated sands
- **Tidal flat:** Mixed sand-mud with tidal rhythmites
- **Lagoon:** Restricted circulation, often evaporitic
- **Estuary:** Tide-dominated, funnel-shaped

### Why Coastal Systems Are Critically Important

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coastal sandstones host ~40 % of the world's giant oil fields** because they combine:
> 1. Excellent reservoir quality (well-sorted, wave-worked sand)
> 2. Lateral continuity (sheet-like geometry)
> 3. Frequent juxtaposition with marine shales (seals)
> 4. Proximity to mature source rocks

### KTU Formula: Wave Base Equation

The **fair-weather wave base (FWWB)** defines the lower limit of continuous wave reworking:

$$d_{FWWB} = \dfrac{L_w}{2}$$

where the deep-water wavelength is:

$$L_w = \dfrac{g \cdot T^2}{2\pi}$$

with **g = 9.81 m/s²** (gravitational acceleration) and **T = wave period (s)**.

For a typical ocean wave with T = 8 s:

$$L_w = \dfrac{9.81 \times 8^2}{2\pi} = \dfrac{628.32}{6.2832} \approx 100 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{FWWB} = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ m}$$

This defines the typical depth limit of coastal reworking.

---

## 2.4 Shelf (Continental Shelf) Depositional Systems

### Definition

The continental shelf is the **submerged margin of a continent**, extending from the shoreline to the **shelf break** (typically at 120–200 m depth, average ~130 m globally). It is a **low-gradient** (0.1°–0.5°) submerged platform.

### Characteristics

- **Sediment sources:** Rivers, longshore drift, biogenic production, aeolian dust
- **Sediment type:** Sand (inner shelf) → Silt → Mud (outer shelf)
- **Energy:** Decreases offshore; storm-dominated inner shelf
- **Reservoirs:** Inner-shelf sands; seals = outer-shelf shales

### Sediment Accumulation Rate

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Shelf sedimentation rate can be modeled as:

$$R_{acc} = \dfrac{Q_s}{A_{shelf}} \cdot (1 - P_{escape})$$

where:
- $R_{acc}$ = accumulation rate (mm/yr)
- $Q_s$ = suspended sediment input (kg/yr)
- $A_{shelf}$ = shelf area (m²)
- $P_{escape}$ = proportion bypassing shelf to deep water

**Typical values:** $R_{acc}$ ranges from **1 mm/yr** (low-input margins) to **>50 mm/yr** (high-input deltas like the Ganges-Brahmaputra).

### Engineering Significance

Shelf shales are the **primary seals** for many offshore reservoirs. Well-log interpretation on the shelf relies on **SP (Spontaneous Potential)** curves to distinguish sand from shale — a direct application of depositional knowledge in instrumentation.

---

## 2.5 Carbonate-Evaporite Depositional Systems

### Definition

Carbonate-evaporite systems form in **warm, shallow, restricted marine basins** where evaporation exceeds inflow. Carbonates precipitate **organically and inorganically**, while evaporites form by **sequential chemical precipitation**.

### The Classic Carbonate Platforms

| Platform Type | Geometry | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Rimmed shelf** | Continuous reef barrier at edge | Great Barrier Reef (modern) |
| **Open shelf** | No rim; gradual slope | Bahama Banks |
| **Isolated platform** | Surrounded by deep water | Atolls (e.g., Maldives) |
| **Epeiric platform** | Vast, shallow epicontinental sea | Cretaceous Western Interior Seaway |

### The Evaporite Precipitation Sequence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporites precipitate in a strict order as salinity increases:**

$$\text{Calcite (CaCO}_3\text{)} \rightarrow \text{Gypsum (CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O)} \rightarrow \text{Halite (NaCl)} \rightarrow \text{Sylvite (KCl)} \rightarrow \text{Carnallite (KCl·MgCl}_2\text{·6H}_2\text{O)}$$

This is the **"Chebotarev Sequence"** — students must memorize this order for KTU exams.

### Reservoir Properties of Carbonates vs. Clastics

| Property | Carbonate | Clastic (Sandstone) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary porosity** | Common (reefs, grainstones) | Common |
| **Secondary porosity** | **Very common** (vugs, fractures) | Rare |
| **Porosity range** | 5 – 40 % | 10 – 35 % |
| **Permeability** | Often unpredictable | More predictable |
| **Diagenesis sensitivity** | **Extremely high** | Moderate |

### The Lucia Petrophysical Class (Carbonate Reservoir Quality)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fracture Porosity Estimation:**

$$\phi_f = \dfrac{w \cdot \sum L_i}{V_{bulk}}$$

where **w** = average fracture aperture, **$L_i$** = individual fracture lengths.

**Dolomitization equation** (a key diagenetic process for enhancing porosity):

$$2 \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{Mg}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{CaMg(CO}_3\text{)}_2 + \text{Ca}^{2+}$$

Dolomitization commonly **increases porosity by 12–13 %** because dolomite crystals are denser and more compact than calcite.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Carbonate-evaporite successions host **supergiant fields** such as:
- **Arab-D Reservoir** (Saudi Arabia — carbonate ramp)
- **Kupferschiefer** (Central Europe — Permian)
- **Zechstein** (North Sea — evaporite seals)

In refinery instrumentation, **NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance) logging** is particularly valuable in carbonates because it can distinguish **free-fluid porosity** (vugs) from **bound-fluid porosity** (capillary-held water) — a critical distinction for production planning.

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Porosity Loss During Burial Compaction

This derivation is **essential for KTU problems** involving reservoir quality prediction at depth.

### Statement

For a sandstone undergoing mechanical compaction, the porosity at depth $z$ follows an exponential decay law known as the **Athy (1930) Equation**:

$$\phi(z) = \phi_0 \cdot e^{-c \cdot z}$$

where:
- $\phi(z)$ = porosity at depth $z$
- $\phi_0$ = surface (depositional) porosity
- $c$ = compaction coefficient (per meter)
- $z$ = burial depth (m)

### Derivation

Starting from the fundamental relationship of compaction (volume conservation of solid grains):

$$\text{Volume of solids} = V_s = \text{constant}$$

Let the bulk volume at depth $z$ be $V_b(z)$, and the void volume be $V_v(z)$. The porosity is defined as:

$$\phi(z) = \dfrac{V_v(z)}{V_b(z)}$$

Since $V_s = V_b(z) \cdot (1 - \phi(z))$, we have:

$$V_b(z) = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - \phi(z)}$$

The change in bulk volume with depth $dz$ is due to mechanical rearrangement of grains and pore-fluid expulsion:

$$\dfrac{dV_b}{dz} = -c \cdot V_b$$

Separating variables:

$$\dfrac{dV_b}{V_b} = -c \, dz$$

Integrating from surface ($z = 0$, $V_b = V_{b,0}$) to depth $z$:

$$\int_{V_{b,0}}^{V_b(z)} \dfrac{dV_b}{V_b} = -c \int_0^z dz$$

This yields:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{V_b(z)}{V_{b,0}}\right) = -c \cdot z$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\dfrac{V_b(z)}{V_{b,0}} = e^{-c \cdot z}$$

Substituting the porosity relationship:

$$\dfrac{1 - \phi_0}{1 - \phi(z)} = e^{-c \cdot z}$$

For small compaction where $\phi(z) \approx \phi_0 \cdot (1 - c \cdot z)$ (linear approximation), we recover the simpler **Athy form**:

$$\phi(z) \approx \phi_0 \cdot e^{-c \cdot z}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Style)

A marine sandstone is deposited with surface porosity $\phi_0 = 0.40$ and compaction coefficient $c = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{-1}$. Calculate porosity at 2,000 m burial depth.

**Solution:**

$$\phi(2000) = 0.40 \times e^{-(4 \times 10^{-4}) \times 2000}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$-(4 \times 10^{-4}) \times 2000 = -0.8$$

Then:

$$\phi(2000) = 0.40 \times e^{-0.8} = 0.40 \times 0.4493$$

$$\phi(2000) = 0.1797 \approx 18.0 \%$$

**Interpretation:** Porosity drops from **40 %** at surface to **18 %** at 2 km depth — a **55 % reduction**, demonstrating why deep marine sandstones often require diagenetic enhancement (e.g., dissolution) to remain economic reservoirs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Evaporite Thickness from Brine Concentration

### Statement

The thickness of an evaporite deposit depends on the original brine volume, the concentration ratio, and the residual pore volume retained in the crystal framework.

### Setup

For halite (NaCl) precipitation from seawater:
- Initial seawater volume: $V_0$
- Salinity: $S_0 = 35 \text{ g/kg}$ (35 ‰)
- Concentration at halite saturation: $S_h \approx 270 \text{ g/kg}$
- Brine density at saturation: $\rho_h \approx 1,260 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Halite density: $\rho_{NaCl} = 2,165 \text{ kg/m}^3$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Mass of NaCl precipitated per kg of initial seawater:

$$m_{NaCl} = \dfrac{(S_h - S_0)}{1 - S_h/1000} \cdot V_0 \cdot \rho_{seawater} \cdot \dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ g}}$$

For typical seawater with $\rho_{sw} = 1,025 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$m_{NaCl} \approx 0.27 \cdot V_0 \cdot 1025 \approx 277 \cdot V_0 \text{ (kg)}$$

**Step 2:** Volume of solid halite deposited:

$$V_{halite} = \dfrac{m_{NaCl}}{\rho_{NaCl}} = \dfrac{277 \cdot V_0}{2165}$$

$$V_{halite} \approx 0.128 \cdot V_0 \text{ (m}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Average compacted thickness over an area $A$:

$$h_{halite} = \dfrac{V_{halite}}{A} = \dfrac{0.128 \cdot V_0}{A}$$

**Numerical Example:**
A restricted marine basin with initial volume $V_0 = 10 \text{ km}^3 = 10^{10} \text{ m}^3$ and area $A = 1,000 \text{ km}^2 = 10^9 \text{ m}^2$:

$$h_{halite} = \dfrac{0.128 \times 10^{10}}{10^9} = 1.28 \text{ m}$$

So a **10 km³** brine body produces only **~1.3 m** of halite over the basin — explaining why thick evaporite successions (e.g., **1,000+ m Zechstein halite**) require **repeated cycles** of flooding and evaporation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Reservoir Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This matrix is the single most important comparative tool. Memorize it.

| Parameter | Marine | Non-Marine | Coastal | Shelf | Carbonate-Evaporite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical porosity** | 15–25 % | 15–30 % | 20–35 % | 5–20 % | 10–30 % (carbonate); 0–5 % (evaporite seal) |
| **Typical permeability** | 10–500 mD | 50–2,000 mD | 100–3,000 mD | 1–50 mD | 1–1,000 mD (variable) |
| **Reservoir geometry** | Sheet/turbidite lobe | Ribbon (channel) | Sheet/barrier | Sheet | Lens/platform-wide |
| **Heterogeneity** | Moderate | **Very high** | Low | Low | **Very high** |
| **Source rock association** | **Excellent** (marine shale) | Variable | Excellent (juxtaposed) | Good | Good (in platform interior) |
| **Diagenetic sensitivity** | Moderate | Low | Low | Low | **Extremely high** |
| **Common well-log signature** | Cylindrical GR | Bell/funnel-shaped GR | Cylindrical, low GR | Serrated GR | Variable; porosity-density crossover |
| **Recovery factor (typical)** | 30–40 % | 20–35 % | 35–50 % | 25–40 % | 20–45 % |

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** — *3 Marks*

> **Q:** Differentiate between **Marine** and **Non-Marine** depositional systems. List two diagnostic lithological features of each.

**Model Answer:**

**Marine Depositional System:**
- Operates in fully saline open-ocean waters (salinity ~35 ‰)
- Sediment dominated by fine-grained pelagic material, fossils, and turbidite sands
- Two diagnostic features: (1) **Glauconite** mineralogy (green pellets), and (2) **abundant marine microfossils** (foraminifera, coccoliths, diatoms)

**Non-Marine Depositional System:**
- Operates on continental landmasses with freshwater (<0.5 ‰)
- Sediment is highly variable (clay to boulder), often red-colored due to oxidation
- Two diagnostic features: (1) **Plant remains / coal seams**, and (2) **red mudstones with desiccation cracks** and vertebrate fossils

---

### Question 2

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** — *3 Marks*

> **Q:** State the **evaporite precipitation sequence** in order of increasing salinity. Why is this sequence important for petroleum exploration?

**Model Answer:**

**Sequence (in increasing salinity):**

$$\text{Calcite} \rightarrow \text{Gypsum} \rightarrow \text{Anhydrite} \rightarrow \text{Halite} \rightarrow \text{Sylvite} \rightarrow \text{Carnallite}$$

(Anhydrite and gypsum are often considered interchangeable depending on temperature; gypsum < ~60 °C, anhydrite > ~60 °C.)

**Importance in Petroleum Exploration:**
1. **Evaporites act as world-class seals** for hydrocarbon traps due to their extremely low permeability (<0.01 mD) and plastic deformation behavior
2. The presence of **halite-bearing sequences** indicates restricted marine basins, which are favorable for **organic matter preservation** (anoxic bottom waters)
3. **Evaporite-capped carbonate reservoirs** form prolific plays globally (Arabian Gulf, North Sea Zechstein, Siberian Platform)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Coastal and Shelf Depositional Systems

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** — *14 Marks*

> **Q (a)** Describe the **Coastal depositional system** in detail. With neat sketches, explain the formation, characteristics, and reservoir potential of **(i) Deltaic** and **(ii) Barrier Island** sub-environments. *7 Marks*

> **Q (b)** Discuss the **Continental Shelf depositional system**. Explain the factors controlling sedimentation on the shelf. How do shelf shales contribute to petroleum systems? *7 Marks*

#### Model Answer to (a)

**Coastal Depositional System — Definition:**
A coastal system occupies the **land-sea transition zone** and is characterized by high-energy wave, tidal, and fluvial processes. It produces laterally continuous, well-sorted sand bodies with excellent reservoir quality.

**(i) Deltaic Sub-environment:**
- **Formation:** A delta forms where a river enters a standing body of water (sea or lake) and its sediment load is deposited as the flow decelerates.
- **Three classical components (after Fisher, 1969):**
  1. **Delta plain** — topset; fluvial channels, marshes, swamps
  2. **Delta front** — foreset; the most active sand deposition; distributary mouth bars
  3. **Prodelta** — bottomset; fine-grained shales
- **Log signature:** Funnel-shaped GR profile (cleaning-upward), reflecting progradational stacking
- **Reservoir potential:** Delta-front sandstones are excellent reservoirs, e.g., **Mahakam Delta (Indonesia), Niger Delta**

**(ii) Barrier Island Sub-environment:**
- **Formation:** Wave-built, elongate sand ridges parallel to the coastline, separated from the mainland by a lagoon
- **Characteristics:** Well-sorted, fine-to-medium quartz sand; high lateral continuity (km-scale); often capped by aeolian dunes
- **Log signature:** Cylindrical (blocky) GR profile
- **Reservoir potential:** **Heavy oil and gas** in many basins (e.g., Athabasca tar sands; heavy oil of coastal Brazil)

#### Model Answer to (b)

**Continental Shelf System:**
- The continental shelf extends from **shoreline (0 m)** to **shelf break (~130 m)**, with slope of **0.1°–0.5°**
- Sediments grade from **sand (inner shelf)** → **silt (middle shelf)** → **mud (outer shelf)**

**Factors Controlling Shelf Sedimentation:**

| Factor | Influence |
|---|---|
| **Climatic regime** | Determines fluvial input and sea-ice extent |
| **Tectonic setting** | Width, subsidence, and tilt of shelf |
| **Sea-level fluctuations** | Eustatic transgressions/regressions shift facies belts |
| **Oceanographic regime** | Tides, storms, currents (e.g., Gulf Stream) redistribute sediment |
| **Biological productivity** | Carbonate-dominated shelves in low-clastic settings |

**Role of Shelf Shales in Petroleum Systems:**
1. **Source Rock Function:** Outer-shelf shales deposited under **upwelling zones** are organically rich (Type II kerogen)
2. **Seal/Cap Rock Function:** Thick, laterally continuous shelf shales form effective seals for underlying reservoirs
3. **Migration Pathway:** Shelf shales act as **carrier beds** for hydrocarbons migrating updip
4. **Overpressure Generation:** Rapid burial of low-permeability shelf shales generates **abnormal formation pressures**, a key consideration for safe drilling

---

### Question B — Carbonate-Evaporite Depositional Systems

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** — *14 Marks*

> **Q (a)** With neat diagrams, describe the **four major types of carbonate platforms**. Explain how platform geometry controls facies distribution. *7 Marks*

> **Q (b)** Discuss the **Evaporite depositional system**. Derive the relationship between original brine volume and deposited halite thickness, and explain the petroleum significance of evaporite successions. *7 Marks*

#### Model Answer to (a)

**Four Major Carbonate Platforms:**

1. **Rimmed Shelf:** A continuous **reef barrier** at the platform margin protects a low-energy interior lagoon. The reef acts as a wave-break. *Example: Great Barrier Reef (modern analog).*

2. **Open (Unrimmed) Shelf:** No reef rim; facies grade gradually from shoreline to deep basin. Energy decreases offshore. *Example: Bahama Banks edges.*

3. **Isolated Platform (Atoll/Guyot):** A platform entirely surrounded by deep water, often atop a subsiding volcanic edifice. *Example: Maldives, Marshall Islands.*

4. **Epeiric Platform:** A vast, shallow, epicontinental sea covering continental interiors. Facies belts are broad and facies changes are gradual. *Example: Cretaceous Western Interior Seaway.*

**Platform Geometry → Facies Control:**

| Position | Energy | Dominant Facies |
|---|---|---|
| Platform margin (rim) | **Very high** | Reef, grainstone, oolite |
| Platform interior (lagoon) | Low–moderate | Mudstone, wackestone |
| Leeward margin | Variable | Skeletal packstone |
| Toe-of-slope | Low | Allochthonous debris flows |

Platform geometry dictates **grain size, sorting, and porosity distribution**, which in turn governs reservoir quality.

#### Model Answer to (b)

**Evaporite Depositional System:**

Evaporites form in basins where **evaporation exceeds inflow**, causing progressive concentration of dissolved ions. The classical setting is a **sabkha** (supratidal flat) or a **restricted marine basin** (e.g., Mediterranean during the Messinian Salinity Crisis).

**Derivation of Halite Thickness:**

Starting from a brine volume $V_0$ with seawater salinity $S_0 = 35 \text{ g/kg}$. At halite saturation:

$$S_{sat} \approx 270 \text{ g/kg}, \quad \rho_{sat} \approx 1,260 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Mass of NaCl precipitated:

$$m_{NaCl} = (S_{sat} - S_0) \cdot V_0 \cdot \rho_{sw}$$

For $V_0 = 10^9 \text{ m}^3$ (1 km³ of brine):

$$m_{NaCl} = (270 - 35) \times 10^9 \times 1025 = 2.41 \times 10^{14} \text{ kg}$$

Volume of solid halite:

$$V_{halite} = \dfrac{m_{NaCl}}{\rho_{NaCl}} = \dfrac{2.41 \times 10^{14}}{2165} = 1.11 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^3$$

For a basin area $A = 10^6 \text{ m}^2$ (1 km²):

$$h_{halite} = \dfrac{V_{halite}}{A} = \dfrac{1.11 \times 10^{11}}{10^6} = 1.11 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}$$

(This is theoretical — in reality, only a fraction of brine reaches halite saturation, and most basins require **multiple cycles** of flooding.)

**Petroleum Significance of Evaporites:**

1. **Effective Seals:** Halite has permeability <0.01 mD; effective seals for 60 % of world's giant fields
2. **Trap Formation:** Salt diapirs create structural traps (e.g., Gulf of Mexico, Iranian fields)
3. **Source Rock Preservation:** Underlying anoxic shales (e.g., **Kupferschiefer**) are world-class source rocks
4. **Reservoir Diagenesis:** Evaporite-derived brines drive dolomitization, enhancing carbonate porosity

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **DO NOT confuse "shelf" with "shallow marine."** The shelf is a specific geomorphologic province; shallow marine is a depth-energy category.
> 2. **Evaporite sequence order is FIXED.** Writing gypsum before calcite loses full marks.
> 3. **Porosity values must specify "primary" vs. "secondary"** for carbonates — generic statements lose marks.
> 4. **Always state units** in numerical answers (e.g., mD for permeability, ‰ for salinity).
> 5. **Reservoir ≠ Source Rock ≠ Seal** — students frequently confuse these three fundamental petroleum system elements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 Depositional Systems:**

- ✅ **Five depositional systems:** Marine, Non-Marine, Coastal, Shelf, Carbonate-Evaporite
- ✅ **Marine salinity:** ~35 ‰; **Non-marine:** <0.5 ‰
- ✅ **Shelf break depth:** ~130 m (global average)
- ✅ **Evaporite sequence:** Calcite → Gypsum → Halite → Sylvite → Carnallite
- ✅ **Coastal sandstones** host ~40 % of giant oil fields
- ✅ **Carbonate porosity** is highly susceptible to **secondary enhancement** (vugs, fractures, dolomitization)
- ✅ **Athy compaction equation:** $\phi(z) = \phi_0 \cdot e^{-c \cdot z}$
- ✅ **Wave base:** $d_{FWWB} = L_w / 2$, with $L_w = gT^2 / 2\pi$
- ✅ **Reservoir geometries:** Marine = sheet/turbidite lobe; Non-marine = ribbon; Coastal = sheet/bar; Shelf = sheet; Carbonate = lens/platform-wide
- ✅ **Key instruments for reservoir characterization:** Gamma Ray (lithology), SP (permeability), Resistivity (hydrocarbons), Density + Neutron (porosity), Sonic (mechanical properties), NMR (fluid typing)
- ✅ **Carbonate diagenesis** is **the single most important control** on carbonate reservoir quality — memorize the dolomitization equation:

$$2\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{Mg}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{CaMg(CO}_3\text{)}_2 + \text{Ca}^{2+}$$

- ✅ **Fluvial channels** show **bell-shaped** GR; **Deltaic/mouth-bar** show **funnel-shaped**; **Beach/barrier** show **cylindrical** GR — this is the most tested log-shape question in KTU exams

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basin Evolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basin Evolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 1 : BASIN EVOLUTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Sedimentary Basin** is a long-lived, regional-scale depression in the Earth's crust that accumulates thick sequences of sediments over geological time. **Basin Evolution** refers to the complete geological life-cycle of such a basin, encompassing its tectonic birth, subsidence, sedimentary filling, thermal maturation, and ultimate destruction or preservation. The study quantifies the dynamic interplay between **lithospheric stretching, thermal relaxation, isostatic compensation, and sediment loading** through time.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a heavy wooden dining table that develops a small dent when you press down on it with your palm. The dent fills with whatever spills on the table (sediment). Now imagine the Earth’s crust is that table, tectonic forces are your palm, and the dent is the **basin**. Over millions of years, the dent may widen (stretching), sink deeper as it cools (thermal subsidence), get filled with sand and mud (sedimentation), and eventually harden into rock that may trap oil, gas, or water.

In a **refinery context**, basin evolution matters because **the quality, API gravity, sulfur content, and paraffinicity of crude oil** depend entirely on the basin in which it matured. A deltaic, anoxic basin (like the Bombay High) generates waxy, low-sulfur crude, while a carbonate-evaporitic platform generates lighter, paraffinic crude.

### 1.2 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Density of Mantle (ρ_m):** **3300 kg/m³**
- **Density of Crust (ρ_c):** **2800 kg/m³**
- **Density of Sediment (ρ_s):** **2300–2500 kg/m³**
- **Density of Water (ρ_w):** **1000 kg/m³** (or 1030 kg/m³ for seawater)
- **Thermal Diffusivity (κ):** **8 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s** (≈ 25.2 km²/Myr)
- **Initial Crustal Thickness (y_c0):** **~35 km** (continental) / **~6 km** (oceanic)
- **Geothermal Gradient:** **25–30 °C/km** (typical continental)
- **Lithospheric Mantle Thickness (a):** **~125 km**
- **Stretching Factor (β):** **dimensionless**, typically **1.1 to 5.0**
- **Subsidence Rate (V):** **m/Myr** (meters per million years)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The student is expected to correlate **basin tectonics** with the **bulk properties of the produced hydrocarbon**, since this dictates the choice of downstream refining operations (atmospheric/vacuum distillation, hydrotreating, catalytic cracking, etc.).

### 1.3 Why Basin Evolution Matters for Refinery Engineers

| Basin Type | Typical Crude Type | Refinery Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Rift / Lacustrine** | Waxy, low sulfur, high N | Cold-flow issues, hydrodenitrogenation |
| **Passive Margin** | Paraffinic, moderate sulfur | Standard distillation profile |
| **Foreland** | Mixed, gas-condensate prone | LPG recovery units required |
| **Deltaic** | Biodegraded, heavy, high S | Extensive desulfurization, coking |
| **Carbonate Platform** | Light, sweet, low metal | Simple topping refinery suffices |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Wilson Cycle & Basin Life Cycle

A basin's life mirrors the **Wilson Cycle** of ocean opening and closing. The complete evolutionary sequence is:

1. **Intra-cratonic Sag Stage** — Slow, broad subsidence over a stable craton (e.g., Michigan Basin). Driven by mantle cooling, low sediment influx.
2. **Rifting / Extensional Stage** — Lithosphere stretches and thins; normal faulting creates half-grabens; volcanic activity; rapid initial subsidence.
3. **Drift / Passive Margin Stage** — Rifting ceases, new ocean forms, margins cool and subside thermally over 100–200 Myr.
4. **Foreland / Flexural Stage** — Continent–continent collision loads the lithosphere, causing a flexural moat (e.g., Persian Gulf foreland).
5. **Inversion / Destruction Stage** — Reactivation of faults in compression; basin uplift and partial erosion.
6. **Preservation Stage** — Burial deepens the basin, preserving hydrocarbon accumulations.

## 2.2 Fundamental Mechanisms of Subsidence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Truth:** Total Subsidence = **Tectonic Subsidence** + **Sediment-Load Subsidence** + **Compactional Subsidence**

### 2.2.1 Tectonic (Driving) Subsidence
Caused by the basin-forming mechanism itself, before sediment is added. Two principal drivers in the McKenzie model:
- **Initial (Fault-Bounded) Subsidence (S_i):** Instantaneous isostatic response to crustal/lithospheric thinning.
- **Thermal Subsidence (S_t):** Slow, long-wavelength subsidence as the hot, thinned lithosphere cools and contracts.

### 2.2.2 Sediment-Load Subsidence
Additional subsidence caused by the **weight of accumulating sediments** (Airy isostasy). The sediment-filled basin subsides further than the empty basin by the ratio of densities.

### 2.2.3 Compactional Subsidence
Mechanical and chemical compaction of buried sediments reduces porosity, expels pore water, and thins the stratigraphic column, creating accommodation for more sediment.

## 2.3 Basin Classification (After Ingersoll & Busby, 1995)

| Genetic Class | Tectonic Setting | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Rift Basin** | Extensional, intra-plate | North Sea, Gulf of Suez |
| **Passive Margin** | Post-rift thermal sag | Atlantic margin of Brazil |
| **Active Margin (Trench)** | Subduction-related | Peru–Chile Trench |
| **Foreland Basin** | Collision flexure | Persian Gulf, Alberta |
| **Strike-Slip / Pull-Apart** | Transtensional | Los Angeles, Dead Sea |
| **Cratonic Sag** | Broad epeirogenic | Michigan, Paris Basin |
| **Back-Arc** | Behind volcanic arc | Sea of Japan |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are given in **SI units** unless otherwise noted. The **stretching factor β** is the master variable linking all basin parameters.

| # | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{y_{c0}}{y_c}$ | dimensionless | Stretching factor (crustal) |
| 2 | $y_L = a - (a - y_{c0})/\beta$ | km | Lithospheric mantle thickness after stretching |
| 3 | $S_i = \dfrac{y_{c0}\,(1 - 1/\beta)\,(\rho_m - \rho_c)}{\rho_m} - \dfrac{\Delta y_L\,(\rho_m - \rho_a)}{\rho_m}$ | m | Initial (syn-rift) subsidence |
| 4 | $S_t(t) = E_0\, \dfrac{\beta}{\pi}\,\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{\beta}\right)\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)\,y_{c0}$ | m | Thermal subsidence at time *t* |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{a^2}{\pi^2 \kappa}$ | Myr | Thermal time constant (~62.8 Myr for *a* = 125 km) |
| 6 | $S_{\text{total}} = S_i\,(1 - \phi) + S_t$ | m | Airy-corrected tectonic subsidence |
| 7 | $S_{\text{sed}} = S_{\text{tect}}\,\dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_w}{\rho_m - \rho_w}$ | m | Sediment-load subsidence (Airy) |
| 8 | $T(t) = T_s + \dfrac{q_m\,y_L}{\kappa}\,\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{y_L}{2\sqrt{\kappa t}}\right)$ | °C | Temperature at depth, time *t* |
| 9 | $Q(t) = K\,\dfrac{T_m - T_s}{y_L(t)}$ | mW/m² | Surface heat flow |
| 10 | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{\text{oil}}} - 131.5$ | °API | API gravity of crude (refining-critical) |

**Boundary Conditions Used:**

- At $t = 0$ (immediately after rifting): heat flow is maximum, lithosphere is thinnest.
- At $t \to \infty$: $S_t$ asymptotically approaches the maximum thermal subsidence, equal to the initial subsidence when $\rho_w = 0$.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**

- Formulae (1)–(7) are the **backbone of basin modeling software** (Petromod, BasinMod, Trinity). Refinery planners use the **heat-flow history (Eq. 8–9)** to predict *when* and *where* source rocks entered the **oil window** (60–120 °C) — this controls the **API gravity, gas-oil ratio, and sulfur content** of the in-place crude, which in turn dictates the entire downstream refinery configuration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: McKenzie's Uniform Stretching Model (1978)

The McKenzie model is **the most heavily examined derivation** in petroleum geology. It is **non-negotiable for a KTU 14-mark question**.

### Step 1 — Define the Stretching Factor β

> **Logical Premise:** When the lithosphere stretches uniformly by a factor β, the crustal thickness reduces from $y_{c0}$ to $y_c$, and the lithospheric mantle thins from $(a - y_{c0})$ to $(a - y_{c0})/\beta$.

$$
\beta = \frac{y_{c0}}{y_c}
$$

**Explanation:** If $\beta = 1$, no stretching occurred. If $\beta = 2$, the crust is halved in thickness (e.g., 35 km → 17.5 km). β is the *master input* to every subsequent equation.

### Step 2 — Lithospheric Mantle Thickness After Stretching

Since the lithospheric mantle also thins by β, its new thickness is:

$$
y_L = a - \frac{a - y_{c0}}{\beta}
$$

**Explanation:** The total lithospheric column is *a* km tall. We subtract the stretched lithospheric mantle thickness. This is the hot, buoyant column that will later cool and subside.

### Step 3 — Apply Airy Isostasy for Initial Subsidence

> **Logical Premise:** Immediately after rifting, the thinned lithosphere is replaced by hot asthenosphere of density $\rho_m$. The surface must drop to maintain mass balance.

The initial subsidence is the difference between the un-stretched and stretched columns, weighted by density contrasts:

$$
S_i = y_{c0}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\frac{\rho_m - \rho_c}{\rho_m} - \Delta y_L \frac{\rho_m - \rho_a}{\rho_m}
$$

Assuming the asthenosphere has density $\rho_m$ and using Airy isostasy, this simplifies (with $\rho_a = \rho_m$ at the surface) to:

$$
S_i = \frac{(\rho_m - \rho_c)\,y_{c0}}{\rho_m}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

**Explanation:** This is the *instantaneous, fault-controlled* subsidence. For β = 2 and typical crustal density 2800 kg/m³, mantle 3300 kg/m³, the result is ~3.2 km of initial subsidence — enough to flood the rift and deposit marine sediments.

### Step 4 — Derive the Thermal Time Constant τ

> **Logical Premise:** Cooling of the lithosphere follows the 1-D heat equation. The longest-wavelength eigenmode dominates and has decay time τ.

The thermal diffusivity equation is:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \kappa \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}
$$

The boundary conditions are $T(0, t) = T_s$ (surface) and $T(a, t) = T_m$ (base of lithosphere). The dominant eigenmode gives:

$$
\tau = \frac{a^2}{\pi^2 \kappa}
$$

**Numerical evaluation** with $a = 125{,}000$ m and $\kappa = 8 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s:

$$
\tau = \frac{(1.25 \times 10^5)^2}{\pi^2 \times 8 \times 10^{-7}}
= \frac{1.5625 \times 10^{10}}{7.896 \times 10^{-6}}
\approx 1.98 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}
$$

Converting to Myr ($1$ Myr $= 3.156 \times 10^{13}$ s):

$$
\tau \approx 62.8 \text{ Myr}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the characteristic time over which the lithosphere loses ~63 % of its excess heat. It controls the **rate** of post-rift thermal subsidence.

### Step 5 — Derive the Thermal Subsidence Equation

The total heat content in the stretched lithospheric column (at $t = 0$) is reduced because the column is thinner. The deficit in heat content decays exponentially:

$$
S_t(t) = E_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

where $E_0$ is the total subsidence possible at $t \to \infty$:

$$
E_0 = \frac{4\,a\,\rho_m\,\alpha\,T_m}{\pi^2 (\rho_m - \rho_w)}\left(\frac{\beta}{\pi}\sin\frac{\pi}{\beta}\right)
$$

Substituting and simplifying (full expansion follows):

$$
S_t(t) = \frac{4\,a\,\rho_m\,\alpha\,T_m}{\pi^2 (\rho_m - \rho_w)}\left(\frac{\beta}{\pi}\sin\frac{\pi}{\beta}\right)\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

**Explanation:** $\alpha$ is the thermal expansion coefficient ($\sim 3.2 \times 10^{-5}$ /K). The term $\left(\frac{\beta}{\pi}\sin\frac{\pi}{\beta}\right)$ is approximately 1 for $\beta = 1$ and approaches 0 as $\beta \to \infty$.

### Step 6 — Total Tectonic Subsidence (Backstripping Form)

$$
S_{\text{tect}} = S_i + S_t
$$

### Step 7 — Apply Sediment-Load Correction (Airy Backstripping)

Sediments filling the basin push it down further. Remove the sediment load to get the **tectonic** (water-loaded) subsidence:

$$
S_{\text{sed-loaded}} = S_{\text{tect}}\,\frac{\rho_m - \rho_s}{\rho_m - \rho_w}
$$

Or, equivalently, to backstrip (remove) the sediment effect:

$$
S_{\text{tect, true}} = S_{\text{observed}}\,\frac{\rho_m - \rho_w}{\rho_m - \rho_s}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Crustal vs. Lithospheric Stretching

| Property | Pure Crustal Stretching | Pure Lithospheric Stretching |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta_c$ | 1.5 to 3.0 | 1.5 to 3.0 |
| $\beta_l$ | 1.0 (no mantle stretch) | = $\beta_c$ |
| Heat-flow anomaly | None | Large, prolonged |
| Volcanism | Minor / absent | Significant (e.g., Afar) |
| Subsidence pattern | Steep, brief | Initial + long thermal tail |
| Hydrocarbon potential | Limited source-rock maturation | Excellent, mature source rocks |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** A passive margin basin has $\beta = 2.0$, $y_{c0} = 35$ km, $a = 125$ km, $\rho_m = 3300$ kg/m³, $\rho_c = 2800$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1030$ kg/m³, $\alpha = 3.2 \times 10^{-5}$ /K, $T_m = 1330$ °C, $t = 50$ Myr.

**Find:** (a) Initial subsidence $S_i$, (b) Thermal subsidence $S_t$, (c) Total tectonic subsidence.

### (a) Initial Subsidence

$$
S_i = \frac{(3300 - 2800) \times 35{,}000}{3300}\left(1 - \frac{1}{2.0}\right)
$$

$$
S_i = \frac{500 \times 35{,}000}{3300} \times 0.5 = 5303 \times 0.5 = 2651.5 \text{ m}
$$

### (b) Thermal Subsidence at t = 50 Myr

$$
E_0 = \frac{4 \times 125{,}000 \times 3300 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 1330}{\pi^2 \times (3300 - 1030)} \times \left(\frac{2.0}{\pi}\sin\frac{\pi}{2.0}\right)
$$

Numerator: $4 \times 125{,}000 \times 3300 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 1330 = 7.0248 \times 10^{7}$ (units: K·kg/m²)

Denominator: $\pi^2 \times 2270 \approx 22{,}408$

Sine term: $\frac{2.0}{\pi}\sin(1.5708) = \frac{2.0}{\pi} \times 1.0 = 0.6366$

$$
E_0 = \frac{7.0248 \times 10^7}{22{,}408} \times 0.6366 \approx 3135 \times 0.6366 \approx 1995.5 \text{ m}
$$

At $t = 50$ Myr, $\tau = 62.8$ Myr, so $e^{-50/62.8} = e^{-0.796} \approx 0.4512$:

$$
S_t = 1995.5 \times (1 - 0.4512) = 1995.5 \times 0.5488 \approx 1095.2 \text{ m}
$$

### (c) Total Tectonic Subsidence

$$
S_{\text{tect}} = 2651.5 + 1095.2 \approx 3746.7 \text{ m}
$$

> **Final Answer:** The basin has tectonically subsided approximately **3.75 km** — a typical value for a moderately stretched passive-margin basin. This is the *water-loaded* basement depth, before sediment loading adds another ~1.5–2.0 km of fill-driven subsidence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wilson Cycle of Basin Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Cratonic Sag Stage"] --> B["Rifting and Extension"]
    B --> C["Continental Breakup and Ocean Opening"]
    C --> D["Passive Margin Thermal Subsidence"]
    D --> E["Foreland Flexure on Plate Convergence"]
    E --> F["Collision and Orogeny"]
    F --> G["Inversion and Basin Destruction"]
    G --> H["Preservation and Hydrocarbon Maturation"]
    H --> A

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    style B fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f,color:#000
    style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
    style D fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
    style E fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000
    style F fill:#f1948a,stroke:#641e16,color:#000
    style G fill:#abebc6,stroke:#145a32,color:#000
    style H fill:#f8c471,stroke:#7e5109,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Basin Subsidence Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Tectonic Input: Beta Stretching Factor"] --> M1["McKenzie Initial Subsidence Calculator"]
    INP --> M2["Thermal Subsidence Module"]
    INP --> M3["Sediment Load Compensation Module"]

    M1 --> SUM["Subsidence Summation Block"]
    M2 --> SUM
    M3 --> SUM

    SUM --> DEC["Decompaction Corrector"]
    DEC --> OUT1["Paleo Water Depth Output"]
    SUM --> OUT2["Total Accommodation Space Output"]

    OUT2 --> RM["Refinery Mapping Module"]
    RM --> RM1["Crude API Gravity Predictor"]
    RM --> RM2["Sulfur Content Estimator"]
    RM --> RM3["Gas Oil Ratio Profiler"]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Stretching Basin (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph section1["Pre Rift Lithospheric Column"]
        A1["Sediment Cover"]:::cover
        A2["Crust Layer 35 km thick"]:::crust
        A3["Lithospheric Mantle 90 km"]:::mantle
        A4["Asthenosphere Hot Ductile"]:::asth
    end

    subgraph section2["Syn Rift Active Stretching"]
        B1["Half Graben Filled with Lacustrine Sediment"]:::synsed
        B2["Thinned Crust 18 km"]:::crust2
        B3["Stretched Lithospheric Mantle 45 km"]:::mantle2
        B4["Hot Asthenosphere Rising"]:::asth2
        B5["Normal Fault Plane"]:::fault
    end

    subgraph section3["Post Rift Thermal Subsidence"]
        C1["Marine Shale Deposition"]:::post
        C2["Cooled Thinned Crust 18 km"]:::crust3
        C3["Cooled Lithospheric Mantle Recovering"]:::mantle3
        C4["Stable Asthenosphere"]:::asth3
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B4
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C4

    classDef cover fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#7e5109,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Decompaction and Backstripping Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Present Day Stratigraphic Column"] --> B["Apply Porosity Depth Curves to Each Layer"]
    B --> C["Decompact Each Layer Sequentially"]
    C --> D["Remove Sediment Load for Each Step"]
    D --> E["Reconstruct Tectonic Subsidence Curve"]
    E --> F["Forward Model Thermal History"]
    F --> G["Calculate Maturation Indices Vitrinite Reflectance Ro"]
    G --> H["Predict Hydrocarbon Phase Window"]
    H --> I["Refinery Feedstock Characterization Output"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a sedimentary basin. List any four genetic types of basins with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A sedimentary basin is a long-lived, regional-scale depression of the Earth's crust that accumulates thick sedimentary sequences over geological time (1 mark). Four genetic types:
1. **Rift Basin** — extensional; e.g., North Sea (0.5)
2. **Passive Margin Basin** — post-rift thermal sag; e.g., Atlantic margin of Brazil (0.5)
3. **Foreland Basin** — flexural moat from collision; e.g., Persian Gulf (0.5)
4. **Strike-Slip / Pull-Apart Basin** — transtensional; e.g., Los Angeles Basin (0.5)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the "stretching factor β" in the McKenzie model? How does it relate to crustal thinning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
β is the dimensionless ratio of the original crustal thickness to the thickness after stretching: $\beta = y_{c0}/y_c$ (1.5 marks). It quantifies the amount of extensional deformation; if β = 2, the crust is halved in thickness. β controls the magnitude of both the initial (syn-rift) and the long-term thermal subsidence of the basin (1.5 marks).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) Derive the McKenzie (1978) uniform stretching model for the initial (syn-rift) subsidence of an extensional basin. List the key assumptions of the model.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) With suitable sketch, explain the Wilson Cycle and the complete evolutionary sequence of a sedimentary basin. Discuss the relevance of basin evolution in determining the bulk properties of crude oil delivered to a refinery.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Uniform stretching of crust and lithospheric mantle.
2. Instantaneous stretching at t = 0.
3. Airy isostasy — local compensation.
4. Two-layer lithosphere (crust + lithospheric mantle).
5. No vertical heat loss during stretching.

**Derivation steps (6 marks):**

Step 1 — Define β. The crustal thickness reduces from $y_{c0}$ to $y_{c0}/\beta$:

$$
y_c = \frac{y_{c0}}{\beta}
$$

Step 2 — The lithospheric mantle also thins by β, so its new thickness is $(a - y_{c0})/\beta$. The new total lithospheric column is:

$$
y_L = a - \frac{a - y_{c0}}{\beta}
$$

Step 3 — Apply Airy isostasy. Before rifting, the column of height *a* with average density $\rho_l$ floats on the asthenosphere of density $\rho_m$. After rifting, the thinned column (mostly replaced by hot asthenosphere) is denser and sinks.

The mass balance for a column gives the initial subsidence $S_i$:

$$
S_i = y_{c0}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\frac{\rho_m - \rho_c}{\rho_m} - \frac{a - y_{c0}}{\beta}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\frac{\rho_m - \rho_m}{\rho_m}
$$

The second term is zero (since lithospheric mantle is replaced by asthenosphere of the same density). With water filling the void of density $\rho_w$:

$$
S_i = y_{c0}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\frac{\rho_m - \rho_c}{\rho_m} + \Delta y_L \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_m} \cdot (\text{water correction})
$$

For an *air-filled* basin: $S_i = y_{c0}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\dfrac{\rho_m - \rho_c}{\rho_m}$ **(1.5 marks for final form)**.

For a *water-filled* basin: $S_i = y_{c0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\dfrac{\rho_m - \rho_c}{\rho_m} - \Delta y_L \dfrac{\rho_m - \rho_w}{\rho_m}$ **(1 mark for full form)**.

**(b) Wilson Cycle & Refinery Relevance (7 marks):**

**Sketch (2 marks):** Show the eight-stage sequence: cratonic sag → rifting → breakup → passive margin → foreland → collision → inversion → preservation (Mermaid figure 4.1 can be referenced).

**Description (3 marks):** Each stage creates distinct sedimentary architecture. Rift basins (lacustrine) yield waxy crude; passive margins (marine shales) yield paraffinic crude; forelands (mixed) yield gas-condensate. Thermal maturation, controlled by burial and heat flow through the Wilson Cycle, determines the API gravity, sulfur content, and gas-oil ratio of the produced crude.

**Refinery relevance (2 marks):** Lighter, sweeter crudes (e.g., Brent) require simple topping refineries; heavier, sour crudes (e.g., Maya, Athabasca) demand **vacuum distillation, coking, hydrodesulfurization, and SDA (solvent deasphalting)**. Refinery capital expenditure of ~$10,000–25,000 per barrel/day is heavily dependent on basin type. Therefore, basin evolution is a **strategic input** to refinery design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a) Explain the mechanism of thermal subsidence in a passive margin basin. Derive the expression for the thermal time constant and discuss its geological significance.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the process of backstripping. How is it used to separate tectonic subsidence from sediment-load subsidence? Illustrate with the Airy isostasy model.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Thermal subsidence derivation (7 marks):**

After instantaneous rifting, the thinned lithospheric column is hotter than the surrounding unstretched lithosphere. Heat diffuses upward, the column cools, contracts, and the basin subsides further **(1.5 marks)**.

The 1-D heat equation governs the temperature evolution:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \kappa \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}
$$

With BCs $T(0,t) = T_s$ and $T(a,t) = T_m$, the solution decomposes into Fourier modes. The dominant, longest-wavelength mode has decay time **(2 marks)**:

$$
\tau = \frac{a^2}{\pi^2 \kappa}
$$

**Numerical evaluation (1 mark):**

$$
\tau = \frac{(1.25 \times 10^5)^2}{\pi^2 \times 8 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.98 \times 10^{15} \text{ s} \approx 62.8 \text{ Myr}
$$

The thermal subsidence follows **(1.5 marks)**:

$$
S_t(t) = E_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

**Geological significance (1 mark):**
τ ≈ 60–80 Myr controls the time-scale over which the passive margin subsides. After ~3τ (≈ 200 Myr), subsidence virtually ceases. This explains why mature passive margins (e.g., eastern US coast) are now shelf-platform settings rather than deep basins.

**(b) Backstripping (7 marks):**

**Concept (1.5 marks):** Backstripping is the inverse procedure: starting from the present-day stratigraphic column, we sequentially remove (strip) each sedimentary layer, decompact the underlying layers, and remove the isostatic sediment load to recover the **tectonic subsidence history** of the basement.

**Steps (3 marks):**
1. Assign each layer its lithology, thickness, and porosity-depth law.
2. Decompact: $\phi(z) = \phi_0\, e^{-z/c}$ where *c* is a compaction constant.
3. Remove the layer's mass: $\Delta S_{\text{load}} = t_s \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_w}{\rho_m - \rho_w}$.
4. Re-calculate the new basement depth after each removal.

**Airy isostasy formulation (2.5 marks):**
The total observed subsidence is corrected:

$$
S_{\text{tect}}(t) = S_{\text{obs}}(t) \cdot \frac{\rho_m - \rho_w}{\rho_m - \rho_s(t)}
$$

Here, $\rho_s(t)$ is the depth-averaged sediment density at time *t*, accounting for compaction. For $\rho_m = 3300$, $\rho_s = 2300$, $\rho_w = 1030$ kg/m³, the correction factor is $\approx 1.49$. Thus, the *true* tectonic subsidence is ~49% larger than the *observed* sediment thickness.

**Significance:** The recovered tectonic subsidence curve is fitted to McKenzie's model to extract β and τ — the fundamental basin parameters used to predict thermal maturity, hydrocarbon phase, and crude quality for refinery planning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing crustal β with lithospheric β** — McKenzie's full model uses *two* β values. In "uniform" stretching, they are equal. In "two-layer" stretching, they differ. **Loses ~2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting the water-load correction** — Students write $S_i$ for an air-filled basin, but real basins are water-filled. The full expression must include $\rho_w$. **Loses ~1.5 marks.**
> 3. **Sign error in $e^{-t/\tau}$** — Subsidence *grows* with time, so the bracket is $(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, *not* $e^{-t/\tau}$. **Loses ~1 mark.**
> 4. **Omitting units** — Always carry units through the derivation. τ must end in **Myr** (not seconds).
> 5. **Sketch missing arrowheads / scale** — In the Wilson Cycle question, a sketch without tectonic arrows (extension / compression) is treated as a half-mark question. **Loses ~1 mark.**
> 6. **Mixing the geothermal gradient (25 °C/km) with heat flow (60 mW/m²)** — They are *related* but not the same. Heat flow = thermal conductivity × gradient.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sedimentary basin** = regional crustal depression accumulating thick sediments over geological time.
- **Basin evolution** spans the **Wilson Cycle** in 8 stages: cratonic sag → rift → breakup → passive margin → foreland → collision → inversion → preservation.
- **McKenzie's uniform stretching model** (1978) decomposes subsidence into **(i)** initial (syn-rift, fault-controlled) and **(ii)** thermal (post-rift, exponentially decaying)**.
- The **stretching factor** $\beta = y_{c0}/y_c$ is the master parameter. Typical values: **1.1 to 5.0**.
- **Initial subsidence** is *instantaneous* and *fault-bounded*; **thermal subsidence** is *gradual* and *regional*.
- **Thermal time constant** $\tau = a^2/(\pi^2 \kappa) \approx$ **62.8 Myr** for a 125 km thick lithosphere.
- **Total tectonic subsidence** = $S_i + S_t$ — then corrected for sediment load using Airy isostasy.
- **Backstripping** is the *inverse* procedure that recovers tectonic subsidence from the present stratigraphic column.
- **Density values to memorize:** $\rho_m = 3300$, $\rho_c = 2800$, $\rho_s = 2300$–$2500$, $\rho_w = 1000$–$1030$ kg/m³.
- **Refinery link:** Basin type → crude API, sulfur, gas-oil ratio → refinery configuration (topping vs. complex). Refinery CapEx varies from **~$10,000/bpd (simple)** to **>$25,000/bpd (complex coker)**.
- **Equations to memorize cold:** Eq. (1) β, Eq. (2) $y_L$, Eq. (3) $S_i$, Eq. (4) $S_t$, Eq. (5) τ, Eq. (7) sediment-load correction.
- **A common trick in numerical questions:** give the *time elapsed since rifting* and ask for the *fractional thermal subsidence* completed, which is $1 - e^{-t/\tau}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sedimentation and Plate Tectonics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sedimentation and Plate Tectonics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sedimentation — Formal Definition
**Sedimentation** is the geological and physical process by which solid particles (sediments) suspended in a fluid medium (water, air, or ice) settle out and accumulate at the base of the fluid column due to the influence of gravity, forming layered deposits known as **sedimentary strata**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** Sedimentation is the entire suite of processes — *weathering, erosion, transport, deposition, lithification* — that produces sedimentary rocks. In engineering contexts, sedimentation is a critical parameter for **reservoir sedimentation analysis, clarifier design, tailings management, and petroleum source-rock maturation studies**.

### 1.2 Plate Tectonics — Formal Definition
**Plate Tectonics** is the unifying geological theory stating that the Earth's outer rigid shell (**lithosphere**) is fragmented into a mosaic of large plates (e.g., Pacific, Eurasian, Indian, African) and smaller microplates, which move relative to one another atop the ductile **asthenosphere** at rates of **1–10 cm/year**, driven primarily by mantle convection, slab pull, and ridge push forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Plate tectonics is the *unifying paradigm* of Earth Sciences. It explains the distribution of earthquakes, volcanism, mountain building, ore deposits, and hydrocarbon basins — all of which are foundational to exploration geophysics and petroleum geology.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

**Sedimentation Analogy — "The Tea Cup Test":**
Imagine stirring tea with sugar at the bottom. When you stop stirring, the heavier sugar grains settle *first* (larger/denser particles), followed by finer suspended tea particles. This is exactly how a river delta is built — coarse sand drops first near the river mouth, while fine clay is carried far out to sea. The **heavier and larger the particle, the faster it falls** — this is the essence of Stokes' Law.

**Plate Tectonics Analogy — "The Boiling Pot of Soup":**
Picture a large pot of thick soup (mantle) being heated from below. As hot soup rises, cooler soup sinks — these are **convection currents**. The thin, brittle "skin" that forms on top (lithosphere) is broken into large "rafts" (plates) that are dragged along by the circulating soup below. When two rafts collide, mountains rise; when they pull apart, rift valleys form; when they slide past each other, earthquakes occur.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Gravitational acceleration (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of water (ρw)** = **1000 kg/m³**
- **Density of quartz (ρq)** = **2650 kg/m³**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C (μ)** = **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- **Average continental crust density** = **2.7 g/cm³**
- **Average oceanic crust density** = **3.0 g/cm³**
- **Mantle density** = **3.3 g/cm³**
- **Average plate velocity** = **1–10 cm/year** (mid-ocean ridges spread at ~2–10 cm/yr)
- **Earth's radius** = **6371 km**
- **Lithosphere thickness** = **0–100 km** (oceanic: ~50–100 km; continental: ~100–200 km)
- **Asthenosphere depth** = **100–350 km**

### 1.5 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping
> [!NOTE]
> This topic primarily maps to **CO1** of the KTU 2024 syllabus: *"Understand the fundamental geological processes governing sedimentary basin formation and tectonic framework."* It addresses Bloom's cognitive levels: **Remember (L1)**, **Understand (L2)**, and **Apply (L3)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sedimentary Process — Stepwise Logic

The formation of sedimentary rocks follows a **four-stage pipeline**:

1. **Weathering (Source Preparation)**
   - Physical weathering: thermal expansion, freeze–thaw, abrasion
   - Chemical weathering: hydrolysis, oxidation, carbonation
   - Biological weathering: lichen, root wedging
   - Output: *clastic grains, dissolved ions, and organic matter*

2. **Erosion & Transport (The Conveyor)**
   - Agents: water (rivers, ocean currents), wind, ice (glaciers), gravity (mass wasting)
   - Transport modes: *traction, saltation, suspension, solution*
   - Sorting improves with distance and transport energy

3. **Deposition (The Settling Event)**
   - Velocity drop → carrying capacity drops → particles settle according to **Stokes' Law**
   - Coarse → high energy (gravel, sand); Fine → low energy (silt, clay)
   - Environments: fluvial, lacustrine, deltaic, shallow marine, deep marine

4. **Lithification (Rock Conversion)**
   - **Compaction** + **Cementation** (silica, calcite, iron oxides)
   - Result: *sandstone, shale, limestone, conglomerate*

### 2.2 Stokes' Law — The Heart of Sedimentation Engineering

For a small spherical particle settling under gravity in a viscous fluid at low Reynolds number (Re < 1):

$$v = \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot r^2}{\mu}$$

Where:
- $v$ = settling (terminal) velocity (m/s)
- $\rho_p$ = density of the particle (kg/m³)
- $\rho_f$ = density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $r$ = radius of the particle (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s or kg/m·s)

**Key Insights:**
- $v \propto r^2$ → doubling particle size **quadruples** settling velocity
- $v \propto (\rho_p - \rho_f)$ → heavier particles settle faster
- $v \propto 1/\mu$ → warmer (less viscous) water reduces settling velocity
- Valid only for **laminar flow** (Re < 1)

### 2.3 Settling Regimes (Beyond Stokes' Law)

| Regime | Reynolds Number | Applicable Law | Description |
|--------|----------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Stokes (Laminar)** | Re < 1 | $v = \frac{2 g r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}$ | Smooth settling, viscosity dominates |
| **Allen (Transition)** | 1 < Re < 10⁴ | $v = \frac{0.153 g^{0.71} r^{1.14} (\rho_p - \rho_f)^{0.71}}{\mu^{0.43} \rho_f^{0.29}}$ | Intermediate flow |
| **Newton (Turbulent)** | Re > 10⁴ | $v = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot d \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | Inertia dominates |

### 2.4 Plate Tectonics — The Driving Forces

**Three Primary Driving Mechanisms:**

1. **Mantle Convection (Heat Engine)**
   - Hot, less-dense mantle rises at mid-ocean ridges
   - Cool, dense mantle sinks at subduction zones
   - Heat source: ~46 TW from radiogenic decay and primordial heat

2. **Slab Pull (Primary Driver — ~70% of force)**
   - Dense, cold subducting slabs pull plates downward
   - Operates at convergent boundaries (e.g., Nazca → South America)

3. **Ridge Push (~20% of force)**
   - Elevated mid-ocean ridges push plates apart due to gravity
   - Operates at divergent boundaries (e.g., Mid-Atlantic Ridge)

4. **Basal Drag (Minor — ~10%)**
   - Friction between asthenosphere and lithosphere

### 2.5 Types of Plate Boundaries — Engineering Importance

| Boundary Type | Relative Motion | Surface Expression | Example | Engineering Relevance |
|---------------|-----------------|-------------------|---------|----------------------|
| **Divergent** | Plates move apart | Mid-ocean ridges, rift valleys, basaltic volcanism | Mid-Atlantic Ridge, East African Rift | Seafloor hydrothermal ore deposits, geothermal energy |
| **Convergent (Ocean–Ocean)** | Subduction | Island arcs, deep trenches | Mariana Trench, Aleutians | Tsunamis, seismic hazard |
| **Convergent (Ocean–Continent)** | Subduction | Volcanic arc, mountain belt | Andes (Nazca → S. America) | Cu–Au–Ag porphyry deposits |
| **Convergent (Continent–Continent)** | Collision | Fold mountains, suture zones | Himalayas (India → Eurasia) | Crustal thickening, hydrocarbon traps |
| **Transform** | Lateral sliding | Strike-slip faults, shallow earthquakes | San Andreas Fault, Anatolian Fault | Earthquake engineering, pipeline route planning |

### 2.6 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Equation | Application / Use |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Stokes' Law** | $v = \frac{2 g r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}$ | Settling velocity in clarifiers, deltas |
| 2 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \frac{2 \rho_f v r}{\mu}$ | Flow regime check |
| 3 | **Newton's Law (Turbulent)** | $v = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{g d (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | Coarse particle settling |
| 4 | **Heat Flow (Fourier)** | $q = k \cdot \frac{dT}{dz}$ | Geothermal gradient analysis |
| 5 | **Spreading Rate** | $V_s = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ | Plate velocity from magnetic anomalies |
| 6 | **Crustal Age from Distance** | $t = \frac{d}{V_s}$ | Dating oceanic crust |
| 7 | **Isostasy (Airy)** | $h = \frac{\rho_m - \rho_c}{\rho_m} \cdot H$ | Crustal root depth |
| 8 | **Paleomagnetic Inclination** | $\tan I = 2 \tan \lambda$ | Reconstructing past latitudes |
| 9 | **Continental Drift Velocity** | $V = \frac{D}{T}$ | Wegener-style reconstruction |
| 10 | **Subduction Angle** | $\sin \alpha = \frac{L_s}{L_h}$ | Slab dip from earthquake foci |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Refinery/Industrial Engineering:** Sediment accumulation in process vessels, storage tanks, and cooling towers reduces efficiency, causes corrosion, and triggers shutdowns. Stokes' Law and settling velocity calculations are used to design **sediment traps, settling chambers, and hydrocyclones** in petrochemical plants.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Geology:** Sedimentary basins (formed by plate tectonics) host **~99% of hydrocarbon reserves**. Understanding subsidence history, source-rock deposition, and trap formation is critical.
- **Civil Engineering:** Sedimentation rates in reservoirs affect dam design (e.g., Tarbela Dam loses ~6% storage/year to silt).
- **Seismic Hazard Zonation:** Plate boundary maps guide building codes in Japan, Chile, California.
- **Mining:** Sedimentary placer deposits (gold, diamonds, tin) concentrate via sedimentation principles.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Stokes' Law is the basis for **wastewater treatment clarifiers** in refineries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Law (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

A spherical particle settling in a viscous fluid at terminal velocity is acted upon by **three forces**:

**Step 1: Identify the Forces**

- **Weight of particle (W)** acts downward: $W = m \cdot g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_p \cdot g$

- **Buoyant force (F_B)** acts upward: $F_B = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f \cdot g$

- **Viscous drag (F_D)** acts upward: $F_D = 6 \pi \mu r v$ (Stokes' drag formula, valid for Re < 1)

**Step 2: Apply Terminal Velocity Condition**

At terminal velocity, the net force = 0 (no acceleration):

$$W = F_B + F_D$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_p \cdot g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f \cdot g + 6 \pi \mu r v$$

**Step 3: Rearrange**

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_p \cdot g - \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f \cdot g = 6 \pi \mu r v$$

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) = 6 \pi \mu r v$$

**Step 4: Isolate v**

$$v = \frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{6 \pi \mu r}$$

$$v = \frac{4 \pi r^3 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \pi \mu r}$$

$$v = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}$$

**Step 5: Final Stokes' Law**

$$\boxed{v = \frac{2 g r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Stokes' Law

> **KTU University Exam - June 2023 Type Problem**

**Problem:** A spherical quartz particle of radius **0.1 mm** settles in water at 20°C. Calculate the terminal settling velocity and verify the Reynolds number for laminar flow assumption. Given: $\rho_p = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert units.
$r = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$

**Step 2:** Apply Stokes' Law.

$$v = \frac{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-4})^2 \cdot (2650 - 1000)}{9 \cdot 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v = \frac{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 1650}{9.018 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v = \frac{3.2373 \times 10^{-4}}{9.018 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v = 0.0359 \text{ m/s} = 3.59 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 3:** Verify Reynolds number.

$$Re = \frac{2 \rho_f v r}{\mu} = \frac{2 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.0359 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-4}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{7.18 \times 10^{-3}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.16$$

**Step 4:** Validation.
Since $Re = 7.16 > 1$, the flow regime is **transitional (Allen regime)**, not laminar. The Stokes' Law gives an *approximate* but biased result. We should use Allen's law for higher accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Students *must* verify Re < 1 for Stokes' Law validity. Failing to do so will cost **2 marks** in ESE.

### 3.3 Derivation of Age of Oceanic Crust from Spreading Rate

**Problem Setup:** Magnetic anomaly stripes are observed at distances of **5 km, 10 km, 20 km, and 40 km** from a mid-ocean ridge axis. If the spreading rate is **5 cm/year**, calculate the age of crust at each distance.

**Step 1:** Convert distance to cm: 1 km = 10⁵ cm
- $d_1 = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ cm}$
- $d_2 = 10 \times 10^5 \text{ cm}$
- $d_3 = 20 \times 10^5 \text{ cm}$
- $d_4 = 40 \times 10^5 \text{ cm}$

**Step 2:** Apply $t = d / V_s$:

$$t_1 = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{5} = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ years} = 0.1 \text{ Ma}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{10 \times 10^5}{5} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ years} = 0.2 \text{ Ma}$$

$$t_3 = \frac{20 \times 10^5}{5} = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ years} = 0.4 \text{ Ma}$$

$$t_4 = \frac{40 \times 10^5}{5} = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ years} = 0.8 \text{ Ma}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation: The age–distance relationship is **linear** in this symmetric spreading model, confirming Hess's seafloor spreading hypothesis.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sediment vs. Tectonic Indicators

| Indicator | Sedimentary Environment | Plate Tectonic Setting |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Thick shale sequences | Deep marine basin | Passive margin / back-arc |
| Thick carbonate platform | Shallow tropical sea | Stable craton interior |
| Ophiolite sequences | Ancient oceanic crust | Convergent boundary (suture) |
| Flysch–Molasse | Trench fill → foreland basin | Continent–continent collision |
| Pillow basalts | Submarine eruption | Mid-ocean ridge / back-arc |
| Granitic batholiths | Plutonic emplacement | Andean-type margin |
| Mélange | Chaotic tectonic mixture | Accretionary prism / subduction |
| Evaporites | Restricted arid basin | Rifting stage (Red Sea type) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Sediment Settling System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspension Inlet] --> B[Sedimentation Tank]
    B --> C[Laminar Flow Region]
    C --> D1[Coarse Particles Settle Fast - Stokes Regime]
    C --> D2[Medium Particles Settle - Allen Regime]
    C --> D3[Fine Particles Settle Slow - Requires Flocculation]
    D1 --> E[Sludge Bed]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Compaction and Lithification]
    F --> G[Sedimentary Rock Formation]
    B --> H[Clarified Water Outlet]
    H --> I[Filtration Unit]
    I --> J[Process Water Reuse]
```

### 4.2 Plate Tectonic Boundary Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Divergent["DIVERGENT BOUNDARY"]
        D1[Mid-Ocean Ridge Upwelling]
        D2[New Crust Formation]
        D3[Rift Valley]
        D1 --> D2
        D2 --> D3
    end
    subgraph Convergent["CONVERGENT BOUNDARY"]
        C1[Subducting Plate]
        C2[Trench]
        C3[Volcanic Arc]
        C4[Mountain Belt]
        C1 --> C2
        C2 --> C3
        C3 --> C4
    end
    subgraph Transform["TRANSFORM BOUNDARY"]
        T1[Strike Slip Fault]
        T2[Shallow Earthquakes]
        T1 --> T2
    end
    subgraph Mantle["MANTLE CONVECTION DRIVER"]
        M1[Hot Mantle Rises]
        M2[Heat from Core]
        M3[Cool Mantle Sinks]
        M2 --> M1
        M1 --> M3
    end
    Mantle --> Divergent
    Mantle --> Convergent
    Divergent --> Transform
    Transform --> Convergent
```

### 4.3 Sediment Transport and Deposition Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Rock - Parent Material] --> B[Weathering]
    B --> B1[Physical Weathering]
    B --> B2[Chemical Weathering]
    B --> B3[Biological Weathering]
    B1 --> C[Erosion]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Transport Agents]
    D --> D1[Rivers and Streams]
    D --> D2[Wind]
    D --> D3[Glaciers]
    D --> D4[Ocean Currents]
    D1 --> E[Deposition by Stokes Law]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Sedimentary Environment]
    F --> F1[Continental]
    F --> F2[Transitional]
    F --> F3[Marine]
    F1 --> G[Lithification]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Sedimentary Rock]
```

### 4.4 Global Tectonic Driving Force Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Source["HEAT SOURCE"]
        HS1[Primordial Heat]
        HS2[Radiogenic Decay]
        HS3[Core Cooling]
    end
    Source --> MC[Mantle Convection Cell]
    MC --> SP[Slab Pull Force]
    MC --> RP[Ridge Push Force]
    MC --> BD[Basal Drag Force]
    SP --> PM[Plate Motion]
    RP --> PM
    BD --> PM
    PM --> PB[Plate Boundary Interactions]
    PB --> RES[Earthquakes]
    PB --> VOL[Volcanism]
    PB --> MTN[Mountain Building]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define sedimentation. State Stokes' Law and explain its significance in petroleum engineering.*

**Model Answer:**

Sedimentation is the geological process by which suspended solid particles settle out of a fluid medium under gravity and accumulate as layered deposits.

Stokes' Law states that the terminal settling velocity $v$ of a spherical particle in a viscous fluid at low Reynolds number is:

$$v = \frac{2 g r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}$$

**Significance in Petroleum Engineering:**
- Predicts **settling rates of cuttings** in drilling mud (critical for hole cleaning)
- Designs **solid control equipment** (shale shakers, centrifuges, hydrocyclones)
- Calculates **reservoir sedimentation rates** to forecast production decline
- Models **tank bottom sludge** accumulation in crude storage tanks
- *(3 marks — full answer expected)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List the three types of plate boundaries with one example each. Which type is responsible for the formation of the Himalayas?*

**Model Answer:**

| Boundary Type | Motion | Example |
|--------------|--------|---------|
| **Divergent** | Plates move apart | Mid-Atlantic Ridge |
| **Convergent** | Plates collide | Nazca–South America (Andes) |
| **Transform** | Plates slide laterally | San Andreas Fault |

The **Himalayas** were formed by a **Convergent (Continent–Continent) Collision** boundary between the **Indian Plate** and the **Eurasian Plate**, beginning ~50 million years ago and continuing today. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Sedimentation Focus**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive Stokes' Law for a spherical particle settling in a viscous fluid. State the assumptions and limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A spherical particle of diameter **0.2 mm** and density **2700 kg/m³** settles in a fluid of density **1100 kg/m³** and viscosity **1.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**. Calculate the terminal velocity and check whether Stokes' Law is applicable. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
The derivation is provided in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student should present:
- Force diagram (Weight, Buoyancy, Drag) — *1 mark*
- Equilibrium at terminal velocity — *1 mark*
- Substitution of formulas: $W = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_p g$, $F_B = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f g$, $F_D = 6 \pi \mu r v$ — *2 marks*
- Algebraic simplification — *1 mark*
- Final boxed equation — *1 mark*
- Assumptions (spherical, rigid, smooth particle; laminar flow; infinite medium) and limitations (Re < 1 only; not for non-Newtonian fluids) — *1 mark*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Given data:
- $d = 0.2 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow r = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $\rho_p = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 2:** Apply Stokes' Law:

$$v = \frac{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-4})^2 \cdot (2700 - 1100)}{9 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v = \frac{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 1600}{1.35 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v = \frac{3.1392 \times 10^{-4}}{1.35 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v = 0.02325 \text{ m/s} = 2.325 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{2 \cdot 1100 \cdot 0.02325 \cdot 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{5.115 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.41$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion: Since $Re = 3.41 > 1$, the flow is in the **transitional (Allen) regime**, and Stokes' Law is **not strictly applicable**. However, it provides a reasonable first approximation. *(7 marks complete)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Plate Tectonics Focus**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the theory of Plate Tectonics. Discuss the three types of plate boundaries with neat diagrams and suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mid-ocean ridge has symmetric magnetic anomalies at distances of **20 km, 60 km, and 100 km** from the ridge axis. The half-spreading rate is **3 cm/year**. Determine the age of the oceanic crust at each location. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory and Boundaries (7 marks):**

- **Introduction (2 marks):** Plate Tectonics unifies the earlier concepts of *Continental Drift* (Wegener, 1912) and *Seafloor Spreading* (Hess, 1962). The lithosphere is divided into ~15 major plates that move over the asthenosphere at 1–10 cm/yr.

- **Three Boundary Types (5 marks):**
  - **Divergent** — Plates separate, magma upwells, new crust forms (e.g., Mid-Atlantic Ridge, East African Rift)
  - **Convergent** — Plates collide; subduction forms deep trenches; arc volcanism and mountain building (e.g., Andes, Himalayas, Japan arc)
  - **Transform** — Plates slide horizontally past each other; shallow earthquakes along strike-slip faults (e.g., San Andreas Fault, Alpine Fault of New Zealand)
  - Each boundary should be illustrated with a **schematic cross-section** (Mermaid diagrams in Section 4 can be referenced)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Use $t = d / V_s$ with $V_s = 3 \text{ cm/yr}$

**Step 2:** Convert distances to cm:
- $d_1 = 20 \text{ km} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}$
- $d_2 = 60 \text{ km} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}$
- $d_3 = 100 \text{ km} = 1 \times 10^7 \text{ cm}$

**Step 3:** Calculate ages:

$$t_1 = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{3} = 6.67 \times 10^5 \text{ yr} \approx 0.67 \text{ Ma}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{3} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ yr} = 2.0 \text{ Ma}$$

$$t_3 = \frac{1 \times 10^7}{3} = 3.33 \times 10^6 \text{ yr} \approx 3.33 \text{ Ma}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: Age increases linearly with distance, confirming symmetric seafloor spreading. *(7 marks complete)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors** — Converting mm to m incorrectly (1 mm = 10⁻³ m, NOT 10⁻² m). This is the #1 reason for full-mark loss in Stokes' Law problems.
> 2. **Skipping Re Verification** — Always check $Re < 1$ when applying Stokes' Law. Failure to verify costs **2 marks** in ESE.
> 3. **Forgetting Assumptions** — In derivation questions, examiners allocate **1 mark specifically for stating assumptions** (laminar flow, spherical particle, homogeneous fluid, no particle interactions).
> 4. **Confusing Slab Pull with Ridge Push** — Students often swap their definitions. Remember: **Slab Pull = dense sinking slab; Ridge Push = elevated ridge spreading outward**.
> 5. **Half-Spreading vs Full-Spreading Rate** — In seafloor spreading problems, check whether the rate is *half* (one side only) or *full* (both sides combined). Misinterpreting this gives wrong ages.
> 6. **Diagram Orientation** — In convergent boundary diagrams, the **subducting plate must be clearly labeled with arrows** pointing downward into the mantle. Vague arrows lose 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Day KTU Prep):**

**Sedimentation:**
- ☐ Sedimentation = Weathering → Erosion → Transport → Deposition → Lithification
- ☐ **Stokes' Law:** $v = \frac{2 g r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}$ (valid for Re < 1)
- ☐ Settling velocity is **proportional to r²** — the most-tested relationship
- ☐ **Stokes regime** (laminar, Re < 1), **Allen regime** (transitional, 1 < Re < 10⁴), **Newton regime** (turbulent, Re > 10⁴)
- ☐ **Clastic** sediments = broken fragments; **Chemical** sediments = precipitates; **Biogenic** sediments = shells/skeletons
- ☐ **Sorting** improves with transport distance and energy

**Plate Tectonics:**
- ☐ Lithosphere (~100 km thick, rigid) floats on asthenosphere (ductile, partially molten)
- ☐ **Three driving forces:** Slab Pull (~70%), Ridge Push (~20%), Basal Drag (~10%)
- ☐ **Three boundary types:** Divergent, Convergent, Transform — *know the motion, the feature, and one example each*
- ☐ **Himalayas** = Continent–Continent Convergent; **Andes** = Ocean–Continent Convergent; **Mid-Atlantic Ridge** = Divergent
