- ☐ **Seafloor spreading** confirmed by symmetric magnetic anomalies and age–distance linearity
- ☐ Plate velocity range: **1–10 cm/year** (Hawaii hotspot moves ~8 cm/yr)
- ☐ **Asthenosphere** is mechanically weak; **lithosphere** is mechanically strong
- ☐ **Hotspots** (e.g., Hawaii, Yellowstone) are intraplate volcanic features from deep mantle plumes

**Integration & Engineering Applications:**
- ☐ ~99% of hydrocarbon reserves occur in **sedimentary basins** formed by plate tectonic processes
- ☐ Refinery instrumentation includes **sediment probes, level sensors, and density meters** that rely on sedimentation principles
- ☐ Reservoir **lithology and trap geometry** are direct products of tectonic framework and depositional history
- ☐ Seismic hazard analysis requires understanding of **plate boundary kinematics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Important Unit Operations: Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Important Unit Operations: Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Drying** is a unit operation in petroleum refining and chemical process industries in which a volatile liquid (usually water) is removed from a solid material by thermal evaporation, into a gas/vapor phase, facilitated by heat transfer to the wet solid and mass transfer of vapor away from it. In the context of refinery instrumentation, drying is critically important for:
- Crude oil dehydration (removing emulsified water and salt)
- Catalyst drying and regeneration
- Lube oil finishing
- Polymer and petrochemical intermediate drying

Mathematically, drying is a coupled **heat and mass transfer** operation governed by simultaneous diffusion of moisture from inside the solid to the surface, followed by convective/conductive removal at the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT631 Module 2):** Drying is treated as one of the "Important Unit Operations" in refining. The instrumentation of dryers (temperature, humidity, flow, and weight sensors) is a key focus for measurement and control.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you have just washed a thick cotton towel. The water drips off easily at first (free water), then the surface feels dry but the inside is damp (bound water). To remove the last bit, you need heat (sun or dryer) and airflow to carry moisture away. Industrial drying is **exactly this process** — but on a refinery scale.

- **Wet solid** = Refinery feedstock (e.g., wet catalyst, crude emulsion)
- **Heat source** = Steam coils, hot gas, or flue gas
- **Carrier gas** = Air, nitrogen, or flue gas
- **Removed vapor** = Water (in most refinery dryers)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Drying
- **Moisture Content (X)** = kg water / kg dry solid (dry basis, **dimensionless**)
- **Moisture Content (% wet basis)** = kg water / kg wet solid × 100
- **Equilibrium Moisture Content (X*)** = Minimum moisture a solid retains when exposed to given air conditions
- **Free Moisture Content (X − X*)** = Moisture removable by drying at given conditions
- **Critical Moisture Content (Xc)** = Point at which constant rate drying ends
- **Standard Air Drying Conditions:** **1 atm, 25 °C, 1.16 kg/m³** density, **0.018 kg water/kg dry air** saturated

> [!NOTE]
> In refinery drying, **dry basis moisture** is preferred over wet basis because dry solid mass is invariant during drying, making material balances simpler.

### 1.4 Classification of Dryers (Refinery-Relevant)
| Dryer Type | Refinery Application | Heat Transfer Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Rotary Dryer | Catalyst, salt drying | Convection |
| Fluidized Bed Dryer | Polymer granules | Convection |
| Spray Dryer | Lube oil additives | Convection |
| Tray Dryer | Lab / batch catalyst | Conduction + Convection |
| Flash Dryer | Slurries, fines | Convection |
| Pneumatic Dryer | Crushed catalyst | Convection |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Drying — Two Fundamental Periods

Drying kinetics of a wet solid universally follow a two-period mechanism:

#### (A) Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP)
- Surface remains **completely saturated** with free moisture.
- Rate is governed solely by **external mass/heat transfer** between gas and solid surface.
- All heat supplied is used as **latent heat of vaporization**.
- Drying rate is constant → linear moisture loss vs time.

#### (B) Falling Rate Drying Period (FRDP)
Begins at **critical moisture content (Xc)**. The surface starts to dry out, forming a dry patch. Moisture must now diffuse from the interior to the surface — internal mass transfer controls. Drying rate decreases continuously. Often has two sub-stages:
- **First falling rate period:** Partially wetted surface, plane of evaporation recedes inward.
- **Second falling rate period:** Internal diffusion-controlled; moisture movement via liquid and vapor diffusion.

### 2.2 The Drying Rate Curve
A plot of **drying rate N (kg water / m²·h)** vs **moisture content X** is the master curve. Key landmarks:
- **AB** → Initial adjustment (transient)
- **BC** → Constant Rate Period (CRP)
- **CD** → First Falling Rate Period
- **DE** → Second Falling Rate Period
- **E** → Equilibrium moisture content X*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **constant rate** period is dominant for **non-porous, free-moisture** solids (e.g., wet sand, catalyst pellets in initial stages). The **falling rate** period dominates for **porous, hygroscopic** solids (e.g., zeolites, molecular sieves, polymer beads).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $X = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$ | kg/kg dry solid | Invariant reference |
| 2 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $X_w = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | % | Common in industry reports |
| 3 | Conversion dry↔wet basis | $X = \dfrac{X_w}{1 - X_w}$ | kg/kg | Algebraic conversion |
| 4 | Free moisture content | $X_{FM} = X - X^*$ | kg/kg | Drivable portion |
| 5 | Drying rate (general) | $N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | kg/(m²·h) | Definition |
| 6 | Constant rate period rate | $N_c = \dfrac{h_s (T_g - T_s)}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/(m²·h) | Surface temp = wet bulb |
| 7 | Mass transfer form (CRDP) | $N_c = k_y (Y_s - Y_g)$ | kg/(m²·h) | Equiv. to heat form |
| 8 | Time of drying — CRP | $t_1 = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}$ | h | Linear regime |
| 9 | Time of drying — FRDP | $t_2 = \dfrac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \dfrac{dX}{f(X)}$ | h | f(X) = N(X)/N_c |
| 10 | Total drying time | $t_{total} = t_1 + t_2$ | h | Sum of periods |
| 11 | Heat required (sensible+latent) | $Q = m_s [c_{ps}(1 + X_{avg}) \Delta T + \lambda X_{removed}]$ | kJ | Pre-heating + vaporization |
| 12 | Latent heat of water (25 °C) | $\lambda_s \approx 2,442$ | kJ/kg | At wet bulb temp |
| 13 | Equilibrium humidity | $Y^* = 0.622 \dfrac{P_w}{P - P_w}$ | kg water/kg dry air | Psychrometric |
| 14 | Humidity ratio difference | $\Delta Y = Y_s - Y_g$ | kg/kg | Driving force |
| 15 | Material balance (air) | $L (Y_2 - Y_1) = m_s (X_1 - X_2)$ | kg/h | Cross-flow dryers |

Where:
- $m_s$ = mass of bone-dry solid (kg)
- $A$ = drying surface area (m²)
- $h_s$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_g, T_s$ = gas (dry bulb) and surface (wet bulb) temperatures (°C or K)
- $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of vaporization at $T_s$ (kJ/kg)
- $k_y$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·h·ΔY)
- $L$ = mass flow rate of dry air (kg/h)
- $P$ = total pressure (Pa or atm), $P_w$ = partial pressure of water vapor (Pa)

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Refinery Engineering
- **Crude Dehydration Trains:** Three-phase separators + electrostatic dehydrators reduce water from crude to **< 0.5 vol%** before atmospheric distillation. Drying rate sensors (capacitance type) monitor in real time.
- **FCC Catalyst Regeneration:** Hot flue gas (650–750 °C) strips coke and adsorbed hydrocarbons — a high-temperature drying/regeneration operation. Oxygen, CO, and temperature analyzers are key.
- **Lube Oil Vacuum Drying:** Reduces moisture in lube base oil to **< 50 ppm** to prevent foaming and hydrolysis.
- **Polymer Drying (Petrochemicals):** Removes residual monomers and moisture from polyethylene/PP pellets before extrusion; **dew point meters** monitor air quality.
- **Molecular Sieve Drying of Natural Gas:** Adsorptive drying to pipeline spec **< 1 lb H₂O/MMSCF**; controlled by ΔT across beds and outlet moisture analyzers.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, instrumentation questions on drying frequently test your ability to identify **which sensor measures what** (e.g., IR hygrometer for outlet moisture, RTD for bed temperature, orifice meter for air flow).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time of Drying in the Constant Rate Period

**Starting point** — definition of drying rate N:

$$
N \;=\; -\dfrac{m_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{dt}
$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables to isolate $dt$:

$$
dt \;=\; -\dfrac{m_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{N}
$$

**Step 2:** In the constant rate period, $N$ is independent of $X$, i.e., $N = N_c = $ constant. Substitute:

$$
dt \;=\; -\dfrac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c}\,dX
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from initial moisture $X_1$ (at $t = 0$) to critical moisture $X_c$ (at $t = t_1$):

$$
\int_{0}^{t_1} dt \;=\; -\dfrac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$
t_1 \;=\; -\dfrac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \bigl[\,X_c - X_1\,\bigr]
$$

**Step 5:** Since $X_1 > X_c$ (we are drying from wet to dry), the negative sign flips:

$$
\boxed{\,t_1 \;=\; \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical formula** universally tested in KTU for batch/convective dryers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Drying Rate in Terms of Mass Transfer Coefficient

In the constant rate period, the surface is at wet-bulb temperature $T_s$. Vapor leaves by convection at a rate proportional to the humidity driving force $(Y_s - Y_g)$:

$$
N_c \;=\; k_y \,(Y_s - Y_g)
$$

where:
- $Y_s$ = humidity of air saturated at surface temperature $T_s$ (kg water/kg dry air)
- $Y_g$ = humidity of the bulk gas (kg water/kg dry air)
- $k_y$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg water / m² · h · ΔY)

Using the **Chilton–Colburn analogy** between heat and mass transfer, the heat-transfer form is:

$$
N_c \;=\; \dfrac{h_s}{\lambda_s}\,(T_g - T_s)
$$

**Step-by-step conversion** (textual logic):
- Heat transferred per unit area per hour = $h_s (T_g - T_s)$ in W/m²
- Convert to kJ/(m²·h) by multiplying by 3.6
- All this heat is used as latent heat, so divide by $\lambda_s$ (kJ/kg) to get kg water/(m²·h)
- Result matches the mass-transfer form, confirming both represent the same physical process

$$
\boxed{\,N_c \;=\; k_y (Y_s - Y_g) \;=\; \dfrac{h_s (T_g - T_s)}{\lambda_s}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Time of Drying in the Falling Rate Period (Linear Approximation)

In the simplest engineering model, the falling rate period is **assumed linear** with X, so:

$$
N(X) \;=\; N_c \cdot \left(\dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}\right)
$$

**Step 1:** Write the integral form of drying time:

$$
t_2 \;=\; \dfrac{m_s}{A} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \dfrac{dX}{N(X)}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the linear $N(X)$:

$$
t_2 \;=\; \dfrac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X - X^*}\,dX
$$

**Step 3:** Pull out the constant and integrate:

$$
t_2 \;=\; \dfrac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the standard logarithmic integral:

$$
t_2 \;=\; \dfrac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \bigl[\,\ln(X - X^*)\,\bigr]_{X_2}^{X_c}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply limits:

$$
t_2 \;=\; \dfrac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \left[\ln(X_c - X^*) - \ln(X_2 - X^*)\right]
$$

**Step 6:** Combine using log identity $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$:

$$
\boxed{\,t_2 \;=\; \dfrac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\,}
$$

**Total batch drying time:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{total} \;=\; \dfrac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c}\Bigl[(X_1 - X_c) + (X_c - X^*)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\Bigr]\,}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Refinery Catalyst Drying)

> A batch of wet **FCC catalyst** (10 kg bone-dry mass, surface area 0.5 m²) is dried in a tray dryer. Initial moisture $X_1 = 0.40$ kg/kg, critical $X_c = 0.18$ kg/kg, final $X_2 = 0.04$ kg/kg, equilibrium $X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg. Air conditions give $N_c = 1.5$ kg/(m²·h). Calculate the total drying time.

**Given:**
- $m_s = 10$ kg
- $A = 0.5$ m²
- $X_1 = 0.40$, $X_c = 0.18$, $X_2 = 0.04$, $X^* = 0.02$
- $N_c = 1.5$ kg/(m²·h)

**Step 1 — Constant rate time:**

$$
t_1 = \dfrac{10 \times (0.40 - 0.18)}{0.5 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{10 \times 0.22}{0.75} = \dfrac{2.20}{0.75} = 2.933 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 2 — Falling rate time:**

$$
t_2 = \dfrac{10 \times (0.18 - 0.02)}{0.5 \times 1.5} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.18 - 0.02}{0.04 - 0.02}\right) = \dfrac{10 \times 0.16}{0.75} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.16}{0.02}\right)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1.6}{0.75} \cdot \ln(8) = 2.133 \times 2.0794 = 4.436 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 3 — Total time:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{total} = 2.933 + 4.436 = 7.37 \text{ h} \approx 7 \text{ h } 22 \text{ min}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The falling rate period is **longer** than the constant rate period, which is typical for porous refinery solids. Increasing $N_c$ (e.g., hotter air, higher velocity) reduces both periods but is limited by thermal degradation temperature of the catalyst.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Drying Operation with Instrumentation

The figure below shows a **batch tray dryer** with the key instrumentation points. Every sensor maps to a measurable physical variable critical to KTU-level questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIR1["AIR INLET"] --> FILT1["FILTER AND PREHEATER"]
    FILT1 --> HTR1["STEAM COIL HEATER"]
    HTR1 --> FAN1["BLOWER AND DUCT"]
    FAN1 --> PLENUM1["AIR PLENUM CHAMBER"]
    PLENUM1 --> TRAY1["WET TRAY FCC CATALYST"]
    TRAY1 --> MOIST1["MOIST AIR EXHAUST"]
    MOIST1 --> STACK1["STACK TO ATMOSPHERE"]

    T1["RTD T1 INLET AIR TEMP"] -.-> HTR1
    T2["RTD T2 PLENUM TEMP"] -.-> PLENUM1
    T3["THERMOCOUPLE T3 BED TEMP"] -.-> TRAY1
    H1["CAPACITIVE HUMIDITY SENSOR H1 INLET"] -.-> FILT1
    H2["IR HYGROMETER H2 OUTLET"] -.-> MOIST1
    W1["LOAD CELL W1 TRAY WEIGHT"] -.-> TRAY1
    F1["ORIFICE METER F1 AIR FLOW"] -.-> FAN1
    P1["DP CELL P1 PLENUM PRESSURE"] -.-> PLENUM1

    HTR1 --> CTRL1["PID CONTROLLER"]
    FAN1 --> CTRL1
    T1 --> CTRL1
    F1 --> CTRL1
    H2 --> CTRL1
    CTRL1 --> HMI1["DCS HMI AND ALARMS"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Temperature loop (T1, T2, T3):** Maintains air and bed temperature within safe window.
- **Humidity loop (H1, H2):** H2 (outlet IR hygrometer) is the **key controlled variable** — when H2 approaches inlet H1, drying is essentially complete.
- **Flow loop (F1):** Air velocity affects $h_s$ and hence $N_c$.
- **Weight loop (W1):** Load cell provides direct, real-time moisture mass balance check.

---

### 4.2 Drying Rate Curve Map (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["WET SOLID X1"] --> A2["SURFACE EVAPORATION ONLY"]
    A2 --> A3["CONSTANT RATE PERIOD BC"]
    A3 --> A4["CRITICAL POINT Xc SURFACE STARTS DRYING"]
    A4 --> A5["FIRST FALLING RATE CD PARTIAL DRY SURFACE"]
    A5 --> A6["SECOND FALLING RATE DE INTERNAL DIFFUSION"]
    A6 --> A7["DRY SOLID X2 APPROACHES X EQUILIBRIUM"]
    A7 --> A8["DRYING COMPLETE"]

    CTRL2["CONTROLLING RESISTANCE"] --> A2
    CTRL2 --> A5
    CTRL2 --> A6

    A2 -. EXTERNAL MASS TRANSFER .-> CTRL2
    A5 -. MIXED INTERNAL AND EXTERNAL .-> CTRL2
    A6 -. INTERNAL DIFFUSION .-> CTRL2
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Checklist (Verified Above)
- Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (`AIR1`, `TRAY1`, `CTRL1`) — no reserved words.
- Labels contain only uppercase text, hyphens, and spaces — no `**` bold, no `*` italic, no pipes `|`.
- All quotes are balanced.
- All arrows are explicit `--> ` or `-.-> ` (dotted for sensor feedback, as is standard).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Equilibrium Moisture Content" of a solid. How is it significant in drying operations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$ is the moisture retained by a solid when it is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air at a given temperature and humidity. It represents the **lower limit** to which the solid can be dried under those conditions.
- **(1 mark) Distinction from free moisture:** Moisture above $X^*$ is called *free moisture* and is removable by drying. Moisture below $X^*$ is *bound moisture* and is held by the solid's internal structure.
- **(1 mark) Significance:** (i) Sets the **theoretical minimum** achievable moisture for given air conditions. (ii) Determines **drying cost and feasibility** — drying to below $X^*$ is impossible without changing air conditions (e.g., lowering humidity, raising temperature). (iii) Critical in refinery solids like molecular sieves and catalysts.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the constant rate drying period and the falling rate drying period.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Constant Rate:** Surface remains saturated with free moisture; drying rate is **constant** and determined by **external heat/mass transfer** between hot gas and wet surface; surface temperature ≈ wet-bulb temperature of gas.
- **(1 mark) Falling Rate:** Begins at critical moisture content $X_c$; surface starts to dry out; rate **decreases** continuously; controlled by **internal moisture diffusion** to the surface.
- **(1 mark) Engineering implication:** Constant rate period is energy-efficient (all heat → vaporization). Falling rate requires **more time for less moisture removal** and may need higher air temperatures to compensate, important for porous refinery solids like FCC catalyst.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **two periods of drying** and the concept of **critical moisture content**. Discuss what factors affect the critical moisture content in a refinery drying operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet solid of 50 kg (bone-dry basis) is dried from an initial moisture content of 0.35 to a final moisture content of 0.05 kg water/kg dry solid. The equilibrium moisture content is 0.02 kg/kg and the critical moisture content is 0.15 kg/kg. The drying surface area is 1.0 m² and the constant drying rate is 2.0 kg/(m²·h). Assuming the falling rate period is linear with moisture content, calculate the **total drying time**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Sketch (block-level representation — KTU boards accept this):**
- Draw a graph of **drying rate N vs moisture content X** with labeled points A, B (initial adjustment), B–C (constant rate), C (critical), C–D (first falling), D–E (second falling), E at $X^*$. *(2 marks for the sketch)*

**Explanation of two periods (3 marks):**
- **Constant rate period (B–C):** Entire surface is wet; mass transfer resistance is in the gas film; surface temperature = wet-bulb; rate $N_c = h_s(T_g - T_s)/\lambda_s = k_y(Y_s - Y_g)$.
- **Falling rate period (C–E):** Surface becomes partially dry; rate falls as dry patches grow; resistance shifts to internal moisture movement; surface temperature rises toward gas temperature.

**Critical moisture content $X_c$ — definition and factors (2 marks):**
- $X_c$ is the moisture content at which the **constant rate period ends** and falling rate begins.
- **Factors:** (i) Thickness of solid (thicker → higher $X_c$); (ii) Initial moisture distribution (less uniform → lower $X_c$); (iii) Solidity/pore structure (more porous → lower $X_c$); (iv) External drying conditions (higher $T_g$ or velocity → lower $X_c$ because surface dries faster than interior can feed it); (v) Particle size.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Given:** $m_s = 50$ kg, $A = 1.0$ m², $X_1 = 0.35$, $X_2 = 0.05$, $X_c = 0.15$, $X^* = 0.02$, $N_c = 2.0$ kg/(m²·h).

**Step 1 — Constant rate period time:**

$$
t_1 = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \dfrac{50 \times (0.35 - 0.15)}{1.0 \times 2.0} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.20}{2.0} = \dfrac{10}{2.0} = 5.0 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 2 — Falling rate period time (linear FRDP):**

$$
t_2 = \dfrac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{50 \times (0.15 - 0.02)}{1.0 \times 2.0} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.15 - 0.02}{0.05 - 0.02}\right)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{50 \times 0.13}{2.0} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.13}{0.03}\right) = \dfrac{6.5}{2.0} \cdot \ln(4.333)
$$

$$
= 3.25 \times 1.466 = 4.765 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 3 — Total drying time:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{total} = t_1 + t_2 = 5.0 + 4.765 = 9.77 \text{ h} \approx 9 \text{ h } 46 \text{ min}\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** The falling rate period is nearly as long as the constant rate period — typical for porous refinery solids.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of industrial dryers** with at least one refinery-relevant example for each class. Highlight the key **instrumentation** used to monitor and control them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **time of drying in the constant rate period** for a batch dryer. A refinery batch tray dryer has 20 kg of dry catalyst with surface area 0.8 m². Air at 80 °C and 10% relative humidity passes over trays. Initial moisture is 0.50, critical is 0.20, and final is 0.05 kg/kg dry solid. The wet-bulb temperature is 30 °C, and the convective heat transfer coefficient is 35 W/m²·K. Calculate the **constant rate drying time**. Use $\lambda_s$ at 30 °C ≈ 2,430 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Classification (5 marks):**
1. **Direct (Convection) Dryers —** Hot gas contacts wet solid. *Refinery example:* Rotary dryer for FCC catalyst. *Instruments:* Inlet/outlet RTDs, IR hygrometer, orifice flow meter.
2. **Indirect (Conduction) Dryers —** Heat transferred through a metal wall; no direct gas–solid contact. *Refinery example:* Steam-tube rotary dryer for salt/sludge. *Instruments:* Steam pressure transmitter, surface thermocouple.
3. **Radiation Dryers —** Infrared or microwave radiation supplies heat. *Refinery example:* IR preheater for polymer pellets. *Instruments:* Radiant flux pyrometer, surface pyrometer.
4. **Dielectric Dryers —** High-frequency electric field heats water molecules. *Refinery example:* RF dryer for specialty chemicals. *Instruments:* RF power meter, temperature probes.
5. **Vacuum Dryers —** Operate under reduced pressure, lower boiling point. *Refinery example:* Vacuum lube oil dryer. *Instruments:* Absolute pressure transmitter, vacuum gauge, condenser temperature.

**Instrumentation summary (2 marks):** Every dryer monitors (i) **Inlet/outlet air temperature** (RTD/thermocouple), (ii) **Humidity** (capacitance sensor / IR hygrometer / psychrometer), (iii) **Air flow** (orifice, venturi, or thermal mass flow meter), (iv) **Solids temperature** (thermocouple in bed), (v) **Product weight/moisture** (load cell or NIR moisture analyzer).

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Derivation (already shown in §3.1, restate concisely for 3 marks):**

Starting from $N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ and integrating over the constant rate period from $X_1$ to $X_c$ with $N = N_c$ constant:

$$
\boxed{\,t_1 = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} \quad \text{where} \quad N_c = \dfrac{h_s (T_g - T_s)}{\lambda_s}\,}
$$

**Numerical calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:** $m_s = 20$ kg, $A = 0.8$ m², $T_g = 80$ °C, $T_s = 30$ °C, $h_s = 35$ W/m²·K, $\lambda_s = 2,430$ kJ/kg, $X_1 = 0.50$, $X_c = 0.20$, $X_2 = 0.05$ kg/kg.

**Step 1 — Compute constant drying rate $N_c$:**

$$
N_c = \dfrac{35 \times (80 - 30)}{2{,}430 \times 10^3 / 3{,}600}
$$

**Convert units carefully:** $h_s$ in W/m²·K = J/(s·m²·K). To get kJ/(h·m²), multiply by 3.6.

$$
N_c = \dfrac{35 \times 50 \times 3.6}{2{,}430} = \dfrac{6{,}300}{2{,}430} = 2.593 \text{ kg/(m²·h)}
$$

**Step 2 — Time of drying in constant rate period:**

$$
t_1 = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \dfrac{20 \times (0.50 - 0.20)}{0.8 \times 2.593}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{20 \times 0.30}{2.074} = \dfrac{6.0}{2.074} = 2.893 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 3 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_1 \approx 2.89 \text{ h} \approx 2 \text{ h } 53 \text{ min}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):** A very common mistake in this question is **forgetting to convert W to kW or to multiply by 3600/1000 = 3.6** when going from W/m²·K to kJ/(h·m²·K). If you use $h_s$ directly without conversion, you get $N_c$ in kg/(m²·s), which when put into a formula expecting h gives wrong units. **Always write units at every step in your solution — KTU examiners award 1–2 grace marks for clear unit handling.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Drying (PEICT631 Module 2)**

- ✅ **Drying = coupled heat + mass transfer** — heat drives liquid→vapor; mass transfer removes vapor via gas.
- ✅ **Moisture content (dry basis)** $X = m_w / m_s$ is the standard KTU formulation; remember the conversion $X = X_w / (1 - X_w)$.
- ✅ **Two periods:** Constant Rate (external-controlled) → Falling Rate (internal diffusion-controlled), separated at **critical moisture $X_c$**.
- ✅ **Drying rate formulas:**
  $N_c = h_s(T_g - T_s)/\lambda_s = k_y(Y_s - Y_g)$
  $t_1 = m_s(X_1 - X_c)/(A \cdot N_c)$
  $t_2 = [m_s(X_c - X^*)/(A \cdot N_c)] \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$
- ✅ **Equilibrium moisture $X^*$** = thermodynamic floor; free moisture = $X - X^*$.
- ✅ **Refinery applications:** crude dehydration, FCC catalyst regeneration, lube vacuum drying, polymer pellet drying, molecular sieve natural gas drying.
- ✅ **Key instruments:** RTD/thermocouple (temperature), capacitance/IR hygrometer (humidity), orifice/venturi (flow), load cell/NIR (weight/moisture), DP cell (pressure).
- ✅ **Numerical pitfalls:** Unit conversions (W→kJ/h via ×3.6), natural log arguments must be **positive** (so $X_2 > X^*$), and falling rate time is computed between $X_c$ and $X_2$ — not $X_1$.
- ✅ **Linear falling rate assumption** is the KTU default; the integral form using $f(X)$ is for general cases.
- ✅ **Energy:** All supplied heat in CRP goes to vaporization; in FRDP some goes to sensible heating of solid — important for energy balance in refinery dryers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Refinery Instrumentation**, **Separation** refers to the engineered set of unit operations and their associated control, measurement, and analytical instrumentation systems used to partition a hydrocarbon feedstock (typically crude oil or its intermediate streams) into two or more distinct product fractions based on differences in **physical properties** (boiling point, volatility, polarity, molecular size) or **chemical affinity** (solubility, reactivity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Separation processes in a refinery are the physical means by which mixed hydrocarbon streams are divided into marketable products (LPG, naphtha, kerosene, diesel, lube oil, residue), and **instrumentation** ensures this separation is achieved within the tightest possible purity, yield, energy, and safety envelopes.

The primary separation processes in a refinery are:

| S.No | Separation Process | Governing Property |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Atmospheric Distillation** | Boiling point (≈ 35 °C – 400 °C) |
| 2 | **Vacuum Distillation** | Boiling point under reduced pressure (≈ 1 mmHg – 100 mmHg) |
| 3 | **Solvent Extraction** | Differential solubility |
| 4 | **Absorption / Stripping** | Selective gas-liquid contact |
| 5 | **Adsorption** | Surface affinity (e.g., molecular sieves) |
| 6 | **Desalting** | Density / electrostatic coalescence |
| 7 | **Membrane Separation** | Size and permeability |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Laundry Sorting Conveyor"**
> Imagine a conveyor belt carrying a mixed pile of white shirts, blue jeans, red towels, and silk sarees. A separation system is simply a series of smart "gates" that direct each item to its own basket based on one distinguishing trait (color, weight, fabric type). In a refinery, the conveyor is the **crude distillation unit (CDU)**, the gates are the **distillation trays / packing**, and the smart sensors that decide "which gate opens when" are the **temperature, pressure, flow, and composition transmitters**. Without those sensors, every shirt would end up in the same basket — that is, we'd only get tar, not gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel.

**Key Physical Constants / Metrics to remember:**
- Atmospheric pressure reference: **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)**
- Standard temperature for VLE data: **15.6 °C (60 °F)** — API standard
- Typical CDU column top pressure: **1.0 – 2.5 bar (g)**
- Typical Vacuum column top pressure: **10 – 50 mmHg (≈ 1.33 – 6.67 kPa)**
- API gravity of crude: **10 °API (heavy) to 45 °API (light)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Distillation Column — A Structured Analytical Breakdown

A **Continuous Distillation Column** is the workhorse of refinery separation. Let us break down the operational concept, the underlying physical chemistry, and the instrumentation rationale step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)**
At every tray, the rising vapour and the descending liquid establish a thermodynamic equilibrium defined by Raoult's Law and Dalton's Law. The relative volatility $\alpha_{ij}$ between any two components $i$ (light key) and $j$ (heavy key) decides how *easily* they can be separated.

**Step 2 — Mass Transfer Across Trays**
Vapour bubbles through the liquid on each tray, transferring the lighter (more volatile) component preferentially into the vapour phase, while the heavier component is dragged downward by the liquid. This is the **stage-wise contact**.

**Step 3 — Internal Reflux**
The condensed overhead vapour is split into two streams:
- **Distillate (D)** — the top product
- **Reflux (L)** — returned to the top tray to enrich the vapour rising up the column.

**Step 4 — Reboiler / Reboiled Vapor**
At the column base, the **reboiler** vaporizes part of the bottom liquid (B) to provide the vapour flow V rising up the column. The vapour rate V and liquid rate L define the internal traffic on every tray.

**Step 5 — Instrumentation Justification**
Because **tray temperature is a near-direct indicator of tray composition** (at fixed column pressure), a small perturbation in product spec can be detected by RTDs/thermocouples on a *sensitive tray* (usually 4–6 trays below the top), and the controller can manipulate **reflux flow** to drive temperature back to setpoint — thereby restoring product purity.

**Step 6 — Energy-Mass Coupling**
Distillation is the largest energy consumer in a refinery (≈ **30 – 50 %** of total process energy). Hence, advanced control strategies (e.g., **Dual Composition Control, Inferential Control via Soft Sensors, MPC**) are central to KTU-level refinery instrumentation questions.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Variable Definition | Units |
|---|------------------|------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $y_i = \dfrac{\alpha_{ij} \cdot x_i}{1 + (\alpha_{ij} - 1)x_i}$ | **Relative Volatility (VLE)** — $x_i$ = mol frac. liquid, $y_i$ = mol frac. vapour | dimensionless |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{\text{moles of liquid formed}}{\text{moles of feed}}$ | **Thermal condition of feed** (q-line slope parameter) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$ | **Minimum Reflux Ratio** (Underwood) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R = 1.2 \, R_{\min} \text{ to } 1.5 \, R_{\min}$ | **Operating Reflux Ratio** (heuristic for design) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\log\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\log \alpha_{avg}}$ | **Minimum number of stages** (Fenske equation) | stages |
| 6 | $N = N_{\min} \cdot \dfrac{R + 1}{R - R_{\min}}$ | **Actual number of stages** (Gilliland correlation — graphical) | stages |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{ideal stages}}{\text{actual stages}}$ | **Overall Tray Efficiency (Murphree/Voverall)** | dimensionless (0.4 – 0.8 typical) |
| 8 | $L = R \cdot D$ | **Reflux flow** (R = reflux ratio, D = distillate rate) | kmol/h |
| 9 | $V = L + D = (R+1) \cdot D$ | **Vapour boil-up rate** | kmol/h |
| 10 | $F = D + B$ | **Overall mass balance** | kmol/h |
| 11 | $F z_F = D x_D + B x_W$ | **Component mass balance** (light key) | kmol/h |
| 12 | $q\text{-line}: \quad y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | **Feed line equation** (McCabe-Thiele construction) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - \Delta P_{steam ejector}$ | **Vacuum column top pressure** | kPa or mmHg |
| 14 | $\Delta P_{tray} = K \cdot \rho_V \cdot v_s^2$ | **Dry tray pressure drop** (instrument: dP cell across column) | kPa |
| 15 | $\text{API Gravity} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | **API gravity from specific gravity** | °API |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These 15 equations form the analytical skeleton of *every* KTU ESE problem on distillation / separation. In a real plant, these equations are embedded inside **Aspen HYSYS / UniSim** simulations, and the instrumentation engineer uses the *disturbance propagation dynamics* derived from them to tune cascade, override, and MPC controllers.

### 2.3 Refinery Separation — Hierarchy of Control Loops

| Loop Level | Variable Controlled | Manipulated Variable | Typical Sensor |
|------------|-------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Regulatory (L1) | Tray Temperature | Reflux Flow | RTD (Pt-100) |
| Regulatory (L1) | Column Top Pressure | Condenser Cooling Water / Vapor Bypass | Pressure Transmitter |
| Regulatory (L1) | Reboiler Level | Bottom Product Flow | DPT / DP Cell |
| Regulatory (L1) | Accumulator Level | Distillate Flow | DPT |
| Regulatory (L1) | Reboiler Steam Flow | Steam Control Valve | Orifice + DPT |
| Supervisory (L2) | Top Product Composition (xD) | Tray-Temp Setpoint | Refractometer / NIR / GC |
| Supervisory (L2) | Bottom Product Composition (xW) | Reboiler Steam Setpoint | Viscometer / GC |
| Advanced (L3) | Energy Minimization | Feed Pre-heat, Reflux Ratio | MPC / APC |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Component Mass Balance Around a Distillation Column

**Given:** A continuous-feed distillation column separating a binary mixture of A (light) and B (heavy).

**Assumptions:**
- Steady state
- Negligible heat loss
- Constant molar overflow (CMO)
- Saturated liquid feed

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance**

Total material entering the column must equal total material leaving:

$$F = D + B$$

This means: every kmol/h of feed becomes 1 kmol/h of total products.

**Step 2 — Component Mass Balance (for the light key, A)**

The light component A entering with the feed is distributed between the distillate and the bottoms:

$$F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + B \cdot x_W$$

**Step 3 — Solve for One Unknown Stream**

From Step 1 we have $B = F - D$. Substituting into Step 2:

$$F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + (F - D) \cdot x_W$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Rearrangement**

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + F \cdot x_W - D \cdot x_W$$

Collect the $D$ terms on one side:

$$F \cdot z_F - F \cdot x_W = D \cdot x_D - D \cdot x_W$$

$$F \cdot (z_F - x_W) = D \cdot (x_D - x_W)$$

**Step 5 — Final Distillate Rate Formula**

$$\boxed{D = F \cdot \dfrac{z_F - x_W}{x_D - x_W}}$$

**Step 6 — Final Bottoms Rate Formula**

By symmetry (or by using $B = F - D$):

$$\boxed{B = F \cdot \dfrac{x_D - z_F}{x_D - x_W}}$$

**Numerical Check (sanity example):** $F = 100$ kmol/h, $z_F = 0.50$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_W = 0.05$:

$$D = 100 \cdot \dfrac{0.50 - 0.05}{0.95 - 0.05} = 100 \cdot \dfrac{0.45}{0.90} = 50 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$B = 100 \cdot \dfrac{0.95 - 0.50}{0.95 - 0.05} = 100 \cdot \dfrac{0.45}{0.90} = 50 \text{ kmol/h}$$

This is correct because the feed is equimolar and the products are symmetric. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Reflux Ratio (Underwood Equation for Saturated Liquid Feed)

**Given:** A binary feed with $z_F$ = 0.55, $x_D$ = 0.95, $x_W$ = 0.05, relative volatility $\alpha = 2.5$, saturated liquid feed (q = 1).

**Step 1 — The q-line slope for saturated liquid feed (q = 1):**

$$y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$$

As $q \to 1$, the q-line becomes **vertical** at $x = z_F$. The intersection of the q-line with the equilibrium curve gives the **q-point**: $(z_F, y_q)$.

**Step 2 — Calculate $y_q$ using VLE:**

$$y_q = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot z_F}{1 + (\alpha - 1) z_F} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.55}{1 + (1.5)(0.55)} = \dfrac{1.375}{1.825} = 0.7534$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Rectifying Line Through $(x_D, x_D)$ and $(x_W, y_q)$ to find the y-intercept:**

Rectifying line equation:

$$y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$$

At the q-point $(x = z_F = 0.55,\; y = y_q = 0.7534)$:

$$0.7534 = \dfrac{R}{R+1}(0.55) + \dfrac{0.95}{R+1}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $(R+1)$:**

$$0.7534(R+1) = 0.55 R + 0.95$$

$$0.7534 R + 0.7534 = 0.55 R + 0.95$$

$$(0.7534 - 0.55) R = 0.95 - 0.7534$$

$$0.2034 \, R = 0.1966$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R_{\min}$:**

$$\boxed{R_{\min} = 0.967 \approx 0.97}$$

**Step 6 — Operating Reflux Ratio (design rule of thumb):**

$$R_{op} = 1.3 \times R_{\min} = 1.3 \times 0.967 = 1.257$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Separation Techniques (KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | Distillation | Solvent Extraction | Absorption | Adsorption | Membrane |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Driving Property | Boiling point difference | Solubility difference | Gas-liquid affinity | Surface polarity | Size/permeability |
| Energy Intensity | Very High | Medium | Low–Medium | Low | Lowest |
| Typical Refinery Application | CDU, VDU, FCC main column | Lube oil refining, BTX | Sulfur recovery (DEA/MDEA) | Naphtha dearomatization, drying | H2 recovery, N2/CO2 separation |
| Primary Sensor | Tray RTD | Interface level DPT | Gas analyzer (IR) | Bed dP + breakthrough GC | Permeate flow + composition |
| Control Strategy | Tray Temp → Reflux Flow | Solvent-to-Feed ratio | Lean solvent flow | Cycle time (PSA) | Transmembrane pressure |
| Capital Cost | Very High | High | Medium | Medium | Low–Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Distillation Column Instrumentation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Crude Feed F] --> B[Feed Preheater]
    B --> C[CDU Main Column]
    C --> D[Overhead Vapor Line]
    D --> E[Condenser]
    E --> F[Reflux Drum Accumulator]
    F --> G[Distillate Product D]
    F --> H[Reflux Pump]
    H --> I[Top Tray of Column]
    C --> J[Reboiler]
    J --> K[Steam Supply]
    J --> L[Bottom Product B]
    C --> M[Sidestream Draw]

    T1[RTD Tray 5] --> C
    T2[RTD Tray 15] --> C
    T3[RTD Tray 25] --> C
    P1[Pressure Transmitter Top] --> C
    P2[Pressure Transmitter Bottom] --> C
    L1[DP Cell Reboiler Level] --> J
    L2[DP Cell Accumulator Level] --> F
    F1[Orifice Reflux Flow] --> H
    F2[Orifice Steam Flow] --> K
    AX1[Refractometer Top] --> G
    AX2[GC Sidestream] --> M

    TC[Temperature Controller TRC-101] --> F1
    PC[Pressure Controller PRC-102] --> E
    LC1[Level Controller LRC-103] --> L
    LC2[Level Controller LRC-104] --> G
    FC[Flow Controller FRC-105] --> K

    T1 -.-> TC
    P1 -.-> PC
    L1 -.-> LC1
    L2 -.-> LC2
    F1 -.-> FC

    DCS[DCS / APC Layer MPC] --> TC
    DCS --> PC
    DCS --> LC1
    DCS --> LC2
    DCS --> FC
    AX1 -.-> DCS
    AX2 -.-> DCS
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Cascade Control for Reflux

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Top Product Spec Setpoint] --> AC[Analyzer Controller AC-201]
    AC -->|resets| TC[Temperature Controller TC-202]
    TC -->|manipulates| FC[Reflux Flow Controller FC-203]
    FC -->|valve signal| CV[Reflux Control Valve]
    CV -->|changes| RF[Reflux Flow L]
    RF -->|affects| TT[Top Tray Temperature T]
    TT --> TC
    AX[Online NIR Analyzer] --> AC
    TT -->|PV feedback| TC
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Typical Disturbance Propagation in a CDU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[Disturbance: Feed Composition Change] --> R1[Top Tray T rises]
    D2[Disturbance: Feed Temperature Drop] --> R2[Reboiler steam demand up]
    D3[Disturbance: Cooling Water Failure] --> R3[Column P surges]
    R1 --> C1[TC increases reflux flow]
    R2 --> C2[FC opens steam valve]
    R3 --> C3[PC bypasses vapor to flare]
    C1 --> S[Stable product spec restored]
    C2 --> S
    C3 --> S
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Diagram Scope:** The mermaid diagrams above model the **functional signal flow and control loop architecture** of a refinery distillation column. Physical pictorial representations of the column internals (trays, weirs, downcomers, reboiler bundles) are not possible in mermaid syntax; in a real engineering project, these are produced using P&ID software such as **AutoCAD Plant 3D, AVEVA E3D, or SmartPlant P&ID**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**"With a neat sketch, explain the working of an Atmospheric Distillation Unit. List the key instrumentation used in the column."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Working (2 marks):** Crude oil preheated in a furnace enters the **Atmospheric Distillation Column (CDU)** near the flash zone. Inside the column, vapours rise through a series of **trays (or packing)**; as they ascend, they cool progressively, condensing at heights corresponding to their boiling ranges. Liquid collected on each tray is withdrawn as side-streams (naphtha, kerosene, diesel, AGO). The non-vaporised residue leaves the bottom as **long residue**. The overhead vapour is condensed in the overhead condenser, collected in the reflux drum, and split into **reflux** (returned to top tray) and **distillate (naphtha)**. Heat input to the column is provided by the **reboiler**.
- **Key Instrumentation (1 mark):** Tray temperature RTDs (Pt-100), Top & bottom pressure transmitters, Reflux drum level DPT, Reflux flow orifice + FC, Reboiler steam flow FC, Bottom level DPT, Side-stream on-line analyser (NIR/refractometer).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"Explain the principle of solvent extraction as a refinery separation technique. Give two examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Principle (2 marks):** Solvent extraction separates components of a liquid mixture based on **differential solubility in a chosen solvent**. When the feed is intimately contacted with an *immiscible* solvent in a **mixer-settler** or **extraction column**, the more soluble component preferentially transfers into the solvent phase (extract), leaving the less soluble component in the raffinate phase. The two phases are then separated by gravity in a settler. The solvent is recovered (e.g., by distillation) and recycled.
- **Two Examples (1 mark):** (i) **Lube oil refining** — furfural or NMP solvent removes aromatics from paraffinic lube distillate; (ii) **BTX extraction** — sulfolane solvent extracts benzene, toluene, and xylene from reformate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Module 2 Choice 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **control strategies used in a binary distillation column**. Explain the **dual composition control** scheme and the role of a **sensitive tray**.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A refinery feeds a binary mixture of benzene and toluene to a distillation column at $F = 100$ kmol/h, $z_F = 0.45$ (mol fraction benzene). The top product is required at $x_D = 0.95$ and the bottom at $x_W = 0.05$. Calculate:
- (i) The distillate and bottom product flow rates
- (ii) The minimum number of stages using Fenske's equation, given the average relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Dual Composition Control & Sensitive Tray — 7 marks**

A binary distillation column has **two** quality spec variables (top and bottom composition) and effectively **five** manipulated variables (D, B, V, L, V̄). Two degrees of freedom are typically consumed for inventory control (D → accumulator level; B → reboiler level), leaving three manipulated variables for the two composition loops and the column pressure loop.

**Five standard control configurations** (Wood & Berry, 1968; KTU syllabus):

| Configuration | Top Composition (xD) | Bottom Composition (xW) | Pressure |
|---------------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| **Configuration A (Direct Material Balance)** | D | V | P → Condenser duty |
| **Configuration B (Indirect Material Balance)** | D | Q (Steam) | P → Condenser duty |
| **Configuration C** | L | V | P → Condenser |
| **Configuration D** | L | Q (Steam) | P → Condenser |
| **Configuration E (Energy Balance)** | L/D ratio | Q | P → Vapour bypass |

**Dual Composition Control** uses **two composition analysers** (refractometer / NIR / GC) — one on the top product line and one on the bottom — each driving a separate composition controller. The **sensitive tray** is identified by "step-testing" the column: a small pulse change is given in reflux (or reboiler steam) and the response of all tray temperatures is observed. The tray with the **largest, sharpest, and most rapid temperature change** is the *sensitive tray*. Its temperature is used as the **inferential variable for composition** (a *soft sensor*) to avoid analyser delay. The sensitive tray typically lies **4–6 trays below the top** for a typical 30–40 tray column.

*Block diagram covered in SECTION 4 (mermaid figure 4.1).*

*(7 marks: 3 for configuration listing, 2 for sensitive tray, 2 for block diagram description)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $F = 100$ kmol/h
- $z_F = 0.45$
- $x_D = 0.95$
- $x_W = 0.05$
- $\alpha_{avg} = 2.47$

**Step 1 — Distillate Flow Rate (1 mark):**

$$D = F \cdot \dfrac{z_F - x_W}{x_D - x_W} = 100 \cdot \dfrac{0.45 - 0.05}{0.95 - 0.05} = 100 \cdot \dfrac{0.40}{0.90}$$

$$\boxed{D = 44.44 \text{ kmol/h}}$$

**Step 2 — Bottom Product Flow Rate (1 mark):**

$$B = F - D = 100 - 44.44$$

$$\boxed{B = 55.56 \text{ kmol/h}}$$

**Step 3 — Fenske Equation for Minimum Stages (5 marks):**

$$N_{\min} = \dfrac{\log\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\log \alpha_{avg}}$$

**Compute the bracket term:**

$$\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} = \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.0$$

$$\dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W} = \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.0$$

$$\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W} = 19.0 \times 19.0 = 361.0$$

**Compute the log:**

$$\log_{10}(361.0) = 2.5575$$

$$\log_{10}(2.47) = 0.3927$$

**Final result:**

$$N_{\min} = \dfrac{2.5575}{0.3927} = 6.51$$

$$\boxed{N_{\min} \approx 6.5 \text{ stages} \; \rightarrow \; 7 \text{ stages (round up)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** A very common mistake students make is to use **natural log (ln)** in Fenske's equation while the log of the *relative volatility* is implicitly base-10. Always explicitly write $\log_{10}$ to be safe, OR use $\ln/\ln$ consistently — but the **standard KTU form uses log base 10.** Also, students forget to convert 6.5 to 7 actual stages because we cannot have a half-stage in a real column.

---

### **Question B — [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Module 2 Choice 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss why vacuum is used, and explain the role of **steam ejectors** and **barometric condensers** in achieving and maintaining column vacuum.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A refinery uses a **solvent extraction unit** to extract aromatics from a lube distillate. The feed contains 30 wt% aromatics. The solvent-to-feed ratio (S/F) is 2.5. After one equilibrium stage, the extract contains 90 wt% aromatics and the raffinate contains 5 wt% aromatics. Calculate:
- (i) The masses of extract and raffinate per 100 kg of feed.
- (ii) The distribution coefficient $\beta$ (mass fraction aromatics in extract ÷ mass fraction aromatics in raffinate).

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Vacuum Distillation Unit — 7 marks**

A **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)** is the second major separation step in a refinery, processing the **long residue** from the bottom of the CDU. At atmospheric pressure, heating the long residue above ≈ 400 °C would cause **thermal cracking** (C–C bond breaking) — degrading product quality and fouling furnace tubes. Operating the column at very low pressure (≈ 1–50 mmHg) **reduces the boiling point of every component** so that separation can be achieved at temperatures below 350 °C.

**Working Principle:**
1. Long residue from the CDU is heated in a **vacuum furnace** to ≈ 380–400 °C and flashed into the **vacuum column** at the flash zone.
2. Steam is injected at the bottom (**stripping steam**, ≈ 5–15 kg per 100 kg feed) to reduce the partial pressure of hydrocarbons and assist vaporization.
3. Vapours rise through the column; vacuum gas oil (VGO), lube distillate, and other fractions are drawn off as side-streams.
4. Unconverted residue (bitumen or pitch) is withdrawn from the bottom.

**Achieving the Vacuum — Steam Ejectors & Barometric Condensers:**

- **Steam Ejector (Jet Ejector):** A converging–diverging nozzle in which high-pressure motive steam entrains the non-condensable gases from the column, compressing and discharging them to atmosphere. Multiple stages (typically 2 or 3) are arranged in series to reach very low absolute pressures.
- **Barometric Condenser:** A direct-contact condenser in which the overhead vapours are sprayed with cold water, condensing water vapour and heavy hydrocarbons. The condensate falls down a **barometric leg** (≈ 10 m water column) into a hot well by gravity, providing a liquid seal that prevents air ingress. This combination — ejector + barometric condenser — can achieve absolute pressures as low as **1–5 mmHg**.

**Key Instrumentation in VDU:**
- Absolute pressure transmitter at the top (range: 0–100 mmHg, capacitance type)
- Thermocouples on trays and furnace tubes
- Steam flow orifices
- dP cells across the ejector stages
- Bottom level DPT (heavy, viscous — use **displacer-type** or **guided-wave radar**)

*(7 marks: 2 for working, 2 for vacuum mechanism, 1 for diagram, 2 for instrumentation)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given (per 100 kg feed):**
- Aromatics in feed: 30 kg
- Non-aromatics in feed: 70 kg
- $S/F = 2.5$ → Solvent = 250 kg
- Extract aromatics: 90 wt% → Extract non-aromatics: 10 wt%
- Raffinate aromatics: 5 wt% → Raffinate non-aromatics: 95 wt%

**Step 1 — Set up equations (1 mark):**

Let $E$ = mass of extract, $R$ = mass of raffinate.

Aromatics balance: $30 = 0.90 E + 0.05 R$ ...(i)
Total mass balance (feed + solvent): $100 + 250 = 350 = E + R$ ...(ii)

**Step 2 — Solve the simultaneous equations (3 marks):**

From (ii): $R = 350 - E$. Substitute into (i):

$$30 = 0.90 E + 0.05(350 - E)$$

$$30 = 0.90 E + 17.5 - 0.05 E$$

$$30 - 17.5 = 0.85 E$$

$$12.5 = 0.85 E$$

$$\boxed{E = 14.71 \text{ kg}}$$

$$R = 350 - 14.71 = 335.29 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Check (1 mark):**
- Aromatics in extract: $0.90 \times 14.71 = 13.24$ kg
- Aromatics in raffinate: $0.05 \times 335.29 = 16.76$ kg
- Total: $13.24 + 16.76 = 30.00$ kg ✓

**Step 4 — Distribution Coefficient (2 marks):**

$$\beta = \dfrac{\text{mass fraction aromatics in extract}}{\text{mass fraction aromatics in raffinate}} = \dfrac{0.90}{0.05}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = 18}$$

A high $\beta$ value indicates the solvent has strong selectivity for the aromatic component.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students commonly forget to **include the solvent mass** in the total material balance when solvent is added externally. Always write the total balance as: $\text{Feed} + \text{Solvent in} = \text{Extract} + \text{Raffinate}$. Also, for full marks, the *units* (kg) must be specified in the final answer, not just the number.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Marks-Loss Zones)

> [!WARNING]
> **Five Top Mark-Loss Areas in "Separation" Questions:**
> 1. **Missing units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per answer without proper units.
> 2. **Confusing "stages" with "trays"** — Fenske gives ideal stages; a partial reboiler counts as 1 stage, and the number of *trays* is $N - 1$ (if no condenser counts as a stage) or $N - 2$ (if total condenser also counts).
> 3. **Using ln instead of log₁₀** in Fenske without converting both numerator and denominator.
> 4. **Forgetting the solvent mass** in the total balance of an extraction column.
> 5. **Writing "pressure transmitter" instead of "absolute pressure transmitter"** for VDU tops — KTU expects you to know the vacuum column uses absolute-type instruments with a special low-range capsule.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Distillation** is the *primary* refinery separation technique, governed by **Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium** and characterized by **relative volatility $\alpha$**.
- ⭐ The **operating reflux ratio $R$** is typically **1.2 – 1.5 times $R_{\min}$** for economically optimal design.
- ⭐ **Fenske** → $N_{\min}$, **Underwood** → $R_{\min}$, **Gilliland** → actual stages $N$, **Erbar-Maddox** → feed-stage location.
- ⭐ **McCabe-Thiele** is the graphical stepping method for binary columns; the **q-line** slope depends on feed thermal condition (q = 1 for saturated liquid, q = 0 for saturated vapour).
- ⭐ The **sensitive tray** is the *inferential composition sensor* — its RTD signal replaces the slow online analyser.
- ⭐ **Vacuum Distillation** uses **steam ejectors + barometric condensers** to reach **1–50 mmHg**; uses *absolute* pressure instruments and *displacer/radar* level instruments for viscous bottoms.
- ⭐ **Solvent Extraction** is governed by the **distribution coefficient $\beta$**; a high $\beta$ means fewer stages.
- ⭐ **Five Wood-Berry configurations** (A through E) are the canonical distillation control structures — know them by name.
- ⭐ **Instrumentation hierarchy:** Regulatory loops (PID) → Supervisory (composition) → Advanced (MPC).
- ⭐ **Material balance** must include solvent in extraction; **Energy balance** drives reboiler sizing in distillation.
- ⭐ **Tray efficiency $\eta$** typically 0.4–0.8; **dP per tray** is used as a fouling indicator.
- ⭐ The **API gravity** formula connects specific gravity to the conventional refinery density metric.
- ⭐ **Cascade control** (Analyzer → Temperature → Flow) is the *workhorse* of distillation composition control.
- ⭐ **Online analysers** in refineries: **NIR, Refractometer, GC, Viscometer, Flash Point Analyser** — each suited to a different stream.
- ⭐ **MPC (Model Predictive Control)** in refineries is dominated by vendors like **Honeywell RMPCT, Aspen DMCplus, ABB 800xA APC** — the "advanced" tier in KTU's control hierarchy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Transfer** is the branch of engineering thermodynamics and process instrumentation that deals with the generation, use, conversion, and exchange of **thermal energy** between physical systems. It is governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, which states that heat spontaneously flows from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature until thermal equilibrium is reached.

In the context of **Refinery Instrumentation (PEICT631)**, heat transfer is the foundational principle behind the operation of all temperature-measuring devices, furnace control systems, distillation column reboilers, condensers, and catalytic cracking reactors. Without a rigorous understanding of heat transfer modes, the design and calibration of refinery temperature sensors and control loops become impossible.

The three fundamental modes of heat transfer are:

1. **Conduction** — Transfer of heat through a stationary medium (solid, liquid, or gas) by direct molecular contact and energy transfer between adjacent molecules.
2. **Convection** — Transfer of heat between a surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas) through the combined effects of conduction and bulk fluid motion.
3. **Radiation** — Transfer of heat in the form of electromagnetic waves (photons) that does not require a medium and can occur through vacuum.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are holding a metal spoon in a hot cup of tea.
> - The **handle of the spoon** getting hot after a few minutes is **Conduction** — heat travels through the metal atoms by vibration and electron transfer.
> - The **steam rising from the tea** carrying heat away is **Convection** — the hot fluid (steam) physically moves, carrying thermal energy with it.
> - The **warmth you feel on your face** when standing near a hot furnace (without touching it) is **Radiation** — electromagnetic infrared waves traveling through the air to warm your skin.

In a **petroleum refinery**, all three modes occur simultaneously:
- Heat conducts through furnace tubes.
- Hot crude oil convects through heat exchanger shells.
- Molten salt and catalytic reactors radiate heat to surrounding equipment.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are essential for refinery heat transfer calculations:

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | **$5.67 \times 10^{-8}$** | $\text{W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$ |
| Thermal Conductivity of Copper | $k_{Cu}$ | **401** | $\text{W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$ |
| Thermal Conductivity of Insulator (Asbestos) | $k_{ins}$ | **0.17** | $\text{W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$ |
| Emissivity of Polished Aluminum | $\varepsilon$ | **0.05** | Dimensionless |
| Emissivity of Black Body | $\varepsilon$ | **1.00** | Dimensionless |
| Standard Reference Temperature | $T_{ref}$ | **273.15** | $\text{K}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mode 1: Conduction — Fourier's Law

**Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** is the cornerstone equation of conductive heat transfer. It states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous material is proportional to the negative gradient of temperature and the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the direction of heat flow.

### Mathematical Statement (1-D Steady State)

$$q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- $q$ = Rate of heat transfer in **Watts (W)**
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of the material in **$\text{W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$**
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow in **$\text{m}^2$**
- $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = Temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow in **$\text{K/m}$**
- The negative sign indicates heat flows from high to low temperature.

### Why and How of Conduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in Refineries:** The thickness of refractory lining in a **cracking furnace**, the insulation of a **steam tracing line**, and the thermal resistance of a **thermowell** are all designed using Fourier's law.

- In **solids**, conduction occurs via lattice vibrations (phonons) and free electron migration.
- In **liquids and gases**, conduction occurs primarily through molecular collisions.
- **Steady-state conduction** occurs when temperature at any point does not change with time.
- **Transient conduction** occurs during startup/shutdown of refinery units (e.g., reactor warm-up).

## 2.2 Mode 2: Convection — Newton's Law of Cooling

**Newton's Law of Cooling** describes the convective heat transfer between a surface and a moving fluid.

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Where:
- $h$ = Convective heat transfer coefficient in **$\text{W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K})$**
- $A$ = Surface area in **$\text{m}^2$**
- $T_s$ = Surface temperature in **$\text{K}$**
- $T_\infty$ = Bulk fluid temperature (free stream) in **$\text{K}$**

### Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient Ranges

| Process | Typical $h$ value $(\text{W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K}))$ |
|---------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| Natural Convection (Air) | $5$ to $25$ |
| Forced Convection (Air) | $10$ to $200$ |
| Forced Convection (Water) | $50$ to $20{,}000$ |
| Boiling Water | $2{,}500$ to $100{,}000$ |
| Condensing Steam | $5{,}000$ to $100{,}000$ |

## 2.3 Mode 3: Radiation — Stefan-Boltzmann Law

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law** governs thermal radiation between surfaces. Unlike conduction and convection, radiation does not require a medium.

$$q = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity of the surface ($0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **$5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$**
- $A$ = Surface area in **$\text{m}^2$**
- $T_s$ = Absolute temperature of the emitting surface in **$\text{K}$**
- $T_{sur}$ = Absolute temperature of the surroundings in **$\text{K}$**

> [!NOTE]
> **Kirchhoff's Law** states that at thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity: $\varepsilon = \alpha$. A perfect blackbody has $\varepsilon = 1$; a perfect reflector has $\varepsilon = 0$.

## 2.4 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)

In refinery heat exchangers, heat must pass through multiple resistances (convective on hot side, conductive through wall, convective on cold side). These resistances are combined into a single **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient**:

$$\frac{1}{U \cdot A} = \frac{1}{h_i \cdot A_i} + \frac{\Delta x}{k \cdot A} + \frac{1}{h_o \cdot A_o}$$

For a thin-walled tube where $A_i \approx A_o \approx A$:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\Delta x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

The overall heat transfer rate is then:

$$q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

Where $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ is the **Log Mean Temperature Difference**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE and Module test paper on heat transfer.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Parameter |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Fourier's Law (Conduction) | $q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$ in $\text{W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$ |
| 2 | Heat Conduction (Plane Wall) | $q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | $L$ is wall thickness |
| 3 | Heat Conduction (Cylindrical) | $q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | For pipes and tubes |
| 4 | Thermal Resistance (Conduction) | $R_{cond} = \frac{L}{kA}$ | Analogous to electrical resistance |
| 5 | Thermal Resistance (Convection) | $R_{conv} = \frac{1}{hA}$ | Combined with conduction in series |
| 6 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$ depends on flow regime |
| 7 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| 8 | LMTD (Parallel Flow) | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ |
| 9 | LMTD (Counter Flow) | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ |
| 10 | Overall Heat Transfer | $q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $U$ in $\text{W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K})$ |
| 11 | Critical Insulation Radius | $r_{cr} = \frac{k}{h_o}$ | For cylindrical insulation |
| 12 | Biot Number | $Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k}$ | Lumped system analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility in Refineries:** These formulas drive the design of **crude preheat trains**, **atmospheric and vacuum distillation column reboilers**, **catalytic cracker feed heaters**, and **LNG regasification exchangers**. A petroleum refinery typically contains hundreds of heat exchangers, and even a 5% improvement in overall heat transfer efficiency can save millions of dollars annually.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Heat Conduction Through a Plane Wall

**Problem Statement:** A flat furnace wall of thickness $L = 0.25 \text{ m}$ has an inner surface temperature of $T_1 = 800 \text{ K}$ and an outer surface temperature of $T_2 = 450 \text{ K}$. The wall is made of fireclay brick with thermal conductivity $k = 1.2 \text{ W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$. The wall area is $A = 4 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate the rate of heat loss through the wall.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation

Fourier's Law for one-dimensional, steady-state conduction through a plane wall:

$$q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

### Step 2: Integrate the Differential Equation

For a plane wall with constant thermal conductivity, the temperature gradient is linear:

$$\frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}$$

Substituting back:

$$q = -k \cdot A \cdot \left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}\right)$$

The negative sign cancels because $(T_2 - T_1)$ is negative:

$$q = k \cdot A \cdot \frac{T_1 - T_2}{L}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

We have:
- $k = 1.2 \text{ W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$
- $A = 4 \text{ m}^2$
- $T_1 = 800 \text{ K}$
- $T_2 = 450 \text{ K}$
- $L = 0.25 \text{ m}$

$$q = (1.2) \cdot (4) \cdot \frac{800 - 450}{0.25}$$

### Step 4: Compute Numerator and Denominator

Numerator of temperature term:

$$800 - 450 = 350 \text{ K}$$

Temperature gradient:

$$\frac{350}{0.25} = 1400 \text{ K/m}$$

### Step 5: Final Multiplication

$$q = 1.2 \times 4 \times 1400$$

$$q = 4.8 \times 1400$$

$$q = 6720 \text{ W}$$

### Step 6: Result and Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $q = 6720 \text{ W} = 6.72 \text{ kW}$

This means the furnace wall loses 6.72 kW of heat to the surroundings by conduction. To maintain furnace temperature, the burners must supply at least this much additional heat.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Loss by Radiation from a Refinery Heater Stack

**Problem Statement:** A vertical cylindrical heater stack has a surface temperature of $T_s = 600 \text{ K}$ and is surrounded by ambient air at $T_\infty = 300 \text{ K}$. The stack has an emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.85$ and a surface area $A = 12 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate the net radiative heat loss from the stack.

### Step 1: State the Stefan-Boltzmann Equation

$$q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

### Step 2: Compute $T_s^4$

$$T_s^4 = (600)^4$$

$$T_s^4 = 600 \times 600 \times 600 \times 600$$

$$600^2 = 360{,}000$$

$$600^4 = 360{,}000 \times 360{,}000$$

$$360{,}000 \times 360{,}000 = 1.296 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

### Step 3: Compute $T_{sur}^4$

$$T_{sur}^4 = (300)^4$$

$$300^2 = 90{,}000$$

$$300^4 = 90{,}000 \times 90{,}000 = 8.1 \times 10^9 \text{ K}^4$$

### Step 4: Compute Temperature Difference

$$T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{11} - 8.1 \times 10^9$$

$$1.296 \times 10^{11} = 129.6 \times 10^9$$

$$129.6 \times 10^9 - 8.1 \times 10^9 = 121.5 \times 10^9$$

$$T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4 = 1.215 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

### Step 5: Multiply by Other Parameters

$$q_{rad} = (0.85) \cdot (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (12) \cdot (1.215 \times 10^{11})$$

Group constants:

$$q_{rad} = 0.85 \times 12 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.215 \times 10^{11}$$

$$0.85 \times 12 = 10.2$$

$$5.67 \times 1.215 = 6.889$$

$$10^{-8} \times 10^{11} = 10^{3}$$

### Step 6: Final Computation

$$q_{rad} = 10.2 \times 6.889 \times 10^3$$

$$10.2 \times 6.889 = 70.27$$

$$q_{rad} = 70.27 \times 10^3 = 70{,}270 \text{ W}$$

### Step 7: Result and Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $q_{rad} \approx 70.27 \text{ kW}$

This significant radiative heat loss explains why refinery heater stacks are often equipped with **external insulation cladding** and **high-emissivity coatings** to either retain or dissipate heat as required.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Heat Transfer Modes

| Property | Conduction | Convection | Radiation |
|----------|------------|------------|-----------|
| **Medium Required** | Yes (solid, liquid, gas) | Yes (fluid only) | No (can occur in vacuum) |
| **Governing Law** | Fourier's Law | Newton's Law of Cooling | Stefan-Boltzmann Law |
| **Driving Force** | Temperature gradient | Temperature difference | $(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ |
| **Key Parameter** | Thermal conductivity $k$ | Heat transfer coefficient $h$ | Emissivity $\varepsilon$ |
| **Mechanism** | Molecular/electron collisions | Bulk fluid motion + diffusion | Electromagnetic wave emission |
| **Temperature Dependence** | Linear in $\Delta T$ | Linear in $\Delta T$ | Quartic in $T$ |
| **Dominant at High T** | Less dominant | Moderate | **Highly dominant** |
| **Dominant in Solids** | **Highly dominant** | None | Minor |
| **Refinery Example** | Refractory wall | Cooler/condenser | Fired heater tubes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
