# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Thermal Conductivity** and state its SI unit. Explain how it varies with temperature for (a) metals, (b) gases.

**Model Answer:**

**Thermal Conductivity** is defined as the ability of a material to conduct heat. It is numerically equal to the rate of heat transfer per unit cross-sectional area per unit temperature gradient. SI unit: **$\text{W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$**.

- **For metals:** Thermal conductivity **decreases** with increase in temperature because increased lattice vibrations scatter free electrons, impeding heat flow. Example: $k_{Cu}$ at $300 \text{ K} \approx 401 \text{ W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$; at $800 \text{ K}$ it drops to approximately $340 \text{ W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$.
- **For gases:** Thermal conductivity **increases** with temperature because gas molecules have higher kinetic energy and transfer momentum more efficiently at elevated temperatures.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** of thermal radiation. Why is emissivity always less than 1 for real bodies?

**Model Answer:**

Stefan-Boltzmann Law states that the radiant energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a blackbody is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

For a real (gray) body:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

Where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4$.

**Emissivity is less than 1 for real bodies** because no real surface emits as much radiation as a perfect blackbody at the same temperature. Real surfaces absorb and re-emit only a fraction of incident radiation due to:
1. Surface roughness and geometric imperfections.
2. Material composition (e.g., polished metals have $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$).
3. Wavelength-dependent absorption characteristics.

Only a hypothetical **blackbody** has $\varepsilon = 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** (a) Derive the expression for **steady-state heat conduction through a cylindrical pipe wall** with inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, length $L$, and thermal conductivity $k$. (b) A steam pipeline of outer diameter $15 \text{ cm}$ and inner diameter $12 \text{ cm}$ carries steam at $T_1 = 450 \text{ K}$. The outside surface temperature is $T_2 = 380 \text{ K}$. The thermal conductivity of the pipe material is $k = 50 \text{ W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$. Calculate the **heat loss per metre length** of the pipe.

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

For radial heat conduction through a cylinder, Fourier's law in cylindrical coordinates gives:

$$q = -k \cdot A(r) \cdot \frac{dT}{dr}$$

Where the cross-sectional area at radius $r$ is:

$$A(r) = 2\pi r L$$

Therefore:

$$q = -k \cdot (2\pi r L) \cdot \frac{dT}{dr}$$

Rearranging and integrating from $r_1$ to $r_2$:

$$q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$q \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)$$

Hence the radial heat conduction equation:

$$q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Inner diameter $d_1 = 12 \text{ cm}$, so $r_1 = 6 \text{ cm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- Outer diameter $d_2 = 15 \text{ cm}$, so $r_2 = 7.5 \text{ cm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $T_1 = 450 \text{ K}$, $T_2 = 380 \text{ K}$
- $k = 50 \text{ W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$
- $L = 1 \text{ m}$ (per metre length)

**Step 1: Compute the log ratio**

$$\ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{0.075}{0.06}\right) = \ln(1.25)$$

$$\ln(1.25) = 0.2231$$

**Step 2: Compute temperature difference**

$$T_1 - T_2 = 450 - 380 = 70 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator**

$$2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2) = 2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 1 \times 70$$

$$2 \times 3.14159 \times 50 \times 70 = 21{,}991.1$$

**Step 4: Final Division**

$$q = \frac{21{,}991.1}{0.2231} = 98{,}572 \text{ W/m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $q \approx 98.57 \text{ kW}$ per metre length of pipeline.

This is the radial conductive heat loss through the pipe wall. In a real refinery, this loss is mitigated by adding insulation (lagging) of low thermal conductivity.

---

### OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks)

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** (a) Define the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**. Derive its expression for heat transfer through a composite cylindrical wall with convective boundaries. (b) In a refinery **shell-and-tube heat exchanger**, hot oil enters at $T_{h,i} = 420 \text{ K}$ and leaves at $T_{h,o} = 380 \text{ K}$. Cooling water enters at $T_{c,i} = 300 \text{ K}$ and leaves at $T_{c,o} = 340 \text{ K}$. If $U = 500 \text{ W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K})$ and the heat transfer area is $A = 25 \text{ m}^2$, determine the **rate of heat transfer** using the **LMTD method** for a **counter-flow** configuration.

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is defined as the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance per unit area between two fluid streams separated by a solid wall.

For a composite cylindrical wall with convective resistances on both sides:

$$\frac{1}{U \cdot A} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$

Each term represents:
- $\frac{1}{h_i A_i}$ = convective resistance on inner (hot) side
- $\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ = conductive resistance of the tube wall
- $\frac{1}{h_o A_o}$ = convective resistance on outer (cold) side

The rate of heat transfer is then:

$$q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

This equation allows us to treat a complex multi-resistance heat transfer problem as a single overall resistance problem.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify Inlet/Outlet Temperatures for Counter-Flow**

For counter-flow configuration:
- Hot fluid inlet: $T_{h,i} = 420 \text{ K}$
- Cold fluid outlet: $T_{c,o} = 340 \text{ K}$
- Cold fluid inlet: $T_{c,i} = 300 \text{ K}$
- Hot fluid outlet: $T_{h,o} = 380 \text{ K}$

**Step 2: Compute Temperature Differences at Both Ends**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 420 - 340 = 80 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 380 - 300 = 80 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Compute LMTD**

Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the LMTD equals the common value (degenerate case):

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{80 - 80}{\ln(1)} = \frac{0}{0}$$

Apply L'Hôpital's rule: when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, LMTD reduces to:

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 80 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4: Apply Overall Heat Transfer Equation**

$$q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

$$q = 500 \times 25 \times 80$$

**Step 5: Final Computation**

$$q = 500 \times 2000 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $q = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ MW}$

This represents the rate at which thermal energy is transferred from hot oil to cooling water in the heat exchanger.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Temperature Unit Errors:** Always convert °C to Kelvin (add 273.15) when applying Stefan-Boltzmann law. Mixing Celsius in the $T^4$ term is the **#1 cause of wrong answers**.
>
> 2. **LMTD Sign Convention:** In counter-flow vs parallel flow, the temperature differences $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are defined differently. Drawing the heat exchanger schematic first prevents confusion.
>
> 3. **Lumped vs Distributed Resistance:** Forgetting the $\ln(r_2/r_1)$ factor in cylindrical conduction. Students often mistakenly use plane wall formula for pipes, losing **3–4 marks**.
>
> 4. **Critical Insulation Radius:** A common conceptual trap — adding insulation to a small pipe may *increase* heat loss below the critical radius $r_{cr} = k/h_o$. Always check.
>
> 5. **Emissivity vs Absorptivity:** Many students confuse these. Remember Kirchhoff's law: $\varepsilon = \alpha$ at thermal equilibrium.
>
> 6. **Boundary Conditions:** In derivation problems, clearly state the boundary conditions ($T = T_1$ at $r = r_1$, $T = T_2$ at $r = r_2$) before integration. KTU examiners award **2 marks** specifically for correctly stated boundary conditions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Heat Transfer (Module 2)**

- [x] **Three modes** of heat transfer: **Conduction, Convection, Radiation** — must be identified in any scenario-based question.
- [x] **Fourier's Law** $q = -kA(dT/dx)$ is the master equation for conduction; $k$ is the material property.
- [x] **Plane wall:** $q = kA(T_1 - T_2)/L$ (linear temperature profile).
- [x] **Cylindrical wall:** $q = 2\pi kL(T_1 - T_2)/\ln(r_2/r_1)$ (logarithmic profile).
- [x] **Newton's Law of Cooling** $q = hA\Delta T$ governs convection; $h$ depends on flow type.
- [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** $q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ governs radiation; temperatures **must be in Kelvin**.
- [x] **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** combines convective and conductive resistances in series; $1/U$ is sum of $1/h$ terms.
- [x] **LMTD** is the effective temperature driving force for heat exchangers; L'Hôpital's rule applies when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$.
- [x] **Counter-flow** heat exchangers have higher LMTD than **parallel-flow** for the same inlet/outlet temperatures.
- [x] **Biot Number** $Bi = hL_c/k$ indicates whether lumped capacitance analysis is valid (lumped valid if $Bi < 0.1$).
- [x] **Critical Insulation Radius** $r_{cr} = k/h_o$ — important for refinery pipe insulation design.
- [x] **Refinery Applications:** Furnace walls, crude preheat trains, distillation reboilers, condensers, catalytic cracker feed heaters all rely on these principles.
- [x] **Temperature Conversion:** Always convert to Kelvin before any radiation calculation: $T(\text{K}) = T(°\text{C}) + 273.15$.
- [x] **Units Check:** $q$ in W, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, $k$ in $\text{W}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{K})$, $h$ in $\text{W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K})$, $U$ in $\text{W}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K})$.
- [x] **Emissivity values:** Polished metal (low, ~0.05), oxidized metal (moderate, ~0.5–0.8), blackbody (1.0).
- [x] **Thermal Resistance Analogy:** Treat heat flow like electrical current; thermal resistance in series adds up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question gives you two fluids and asks for the heat exchanger duty, the **standard 3-step approach** is: (1) Draw the schematic with hot/cold streams marked, (2) Compute LMTD carefully, (3) Apply $q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$. Marks are awarded for **each** correct step. Practice this template — it covers ~60% of Part B questions on heat transfer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Distillation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Distillation** is a physical separation process that exploits the difference in **volatility (relative volatility, $\alpha$)** of components in a liquid mixture. In a petroleum refinery, distillation is the *primary unit operation* responsible for separating crude oil into commercially valuable fractions such as Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel, Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO), and Reduced Crude Oil (RCO). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Distillation* in the context of Refinery Instrumentation refers to the **measurement, monitoring, and control** of the distillation column (tower) — including the precise regulation of temperature, pressure, reflux, reboiler heat duty, feed composition, and product draw-off rates — to achieve the desired product specifications (e.g., ASTM D86 95% point, ASTM D1160 vacuum 95% point). In refinery parlance, two types of distillation columns dominate: | Column Type | Operating Pressure | Function | |---|---|---| | **Atmospheric Distillation Column (ADC)** | Slightly above atmospheric (~ 1.0 – 1.5 kg/cm²) | Primary separation of crude into light and middle distillates | | **Vacuum Distillation Column (VDC)** | Reduced pressure (10 – 50 mmHg absolute) | Separation of heavy residue without thermal cracking | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling-Pot" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a tall vertical kitchen pot filled with a mixture of water, oil, and alcohol. As you heat the bottom, the most **volatile (lowest boiling point)** component — alcohol — evaporates first. The vapours rise, encounter a cool surface, condense, and drip out at the top as a *light product*. The middle component — water — partially vaporizes and condenses on trays partway up. The heaviest — oil — stays at the bottom as a *bottom product*. Now scale this kitchen pot up to **50 metres tall**, **8 metres in diameter**, **30 – 50 bubble-cap/sieve trays**, processing **200,000 barrels per day** at **400 °C** — that is a refinery distillation column. ## 1.3 The Distillation Column — A Refinery Workhorse A typical **Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU)** consists of the following integrated sub-units: - **Desalter:** Removes dissolved salts (NaCl, MgCl₂) from crude to prevent downstream corrosion and fouling. - **Preheat Train (Heat Exchanger Network / PUM)** – Uses hot product streams to pre-heat incoming crude. - **Furnace (Heater):** Raises the crude to its **pseudo-critical temperature** (≈ 350 – 400 °C) before it flashes in the column. - **Distillation Tower:** Houses trays or packing where vapour-liquid mass transfer occurs. - **Pumparound (PA) Circuits:** Internal circulation streams withdrawn from below a tray, cooled, and returned to the column to remove heat and provide internal reflux. - **Overhead System:** Condenser, reflux drum, reflux pump, and product rundown coolers. - **Sidestream Strippers:** Steam-stripping columns (e.g., kerosene stripper, diesel stripper) to control the front-end volatility of side draws. - **Vacuum System (for VDU):** Steam ejectors, surface condensers, and hot wells to maintain the reduced column pressure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Refinery Focus:** The instrumentation engineer is *not* designing the column — they are designing the **control loops** that make it operate safely, efficiently, and on-specification. A modern distillation column can have **200 – 400 instruments** (sensors, controllers, valves) integrated through a **Distributed Control System (DCS)** such as Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV, or Yokogawa CENTUM. ## 1.4 Key Process Variables to be Measured & Controlled For a typical distillation column, the following are the **primary controlled variables (CVs)** and **manipulated variables (MVs)**: - **Controlled Variables (CVs):** Top temperature (or inferred top composition), bottom temperature, product draw rates, column pressure, reflux drum level, sump level, overhead receiver temperature. - **Manipulated Variables (MVs):** Reflux flow, reboiler steam flow, top product draw, side-draw flow, bottoms flow, pumparound duty, column pressure (via overhead vapour bypass to compressor or vent). - **Disturbance Variables (DVs):** Feed composition, feed temperature, feed flow rate, ambient conditions, cooling water temperature. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) At the heart of every distillation column is the principle of **Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)**. For a binary mixture at a given pressure, the equilibrium relationship between the vapour phase mole fraction ($y_i$) and the liquid phase mole fraction ($x_i$) is given by **Raoult's Law** (ideal systems): $$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$$ where: - $P_i$ = Partial pressure of component $i$ in vapour - $x_i$ = Mole fraction of $i$ in liquid - $P_i^{sat}(T)$ = Saturation (vapour) pressure of pure $i$ at temperature $T$ Total pressure: $P = \sum_i P_i$. For a binary mixture (A is more volatile): $$y_A = \frac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T)}{P}$$ ## 2.2 Relative Volatility ($\alpha$) The **relative volatility** is the *dimensionless number* that quantifies how easily one component can be separated from another: $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat} / P}{P_B^{sat} / P} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$ where $K_i = y_i / x_i$ is the **K-value** (equilibrium constant). > [!NOTE] > **Rule of thumb:** Separation is **easy** when $\alpha > 1.5$; **moderately difficult** for $1.05 < \alpha < 1.5$; **extremely difficult / requires extractive or azeotropic distillation** when $\alpha < 1.05$. In crude oil, adjacent true-boiling-point (TBP) cuts have $\alpha$ values between **1.01 and 1.10**, which is why refinery columns are tall (40 – 50 m) and have many trays (30 – 50). ## 2.3 The McCabe-Thiele Method — Stage-by-Stage Design For binary distillation column design, the **McCabe-Thiele method** graphically determines the **Number of Theoretical Stages (N)** required. The operating lines are: **Rectifying Section (above feed):** $$y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_n + \frac{D}{V} x_D$$ where: - $L$ = Liquid molar flow rate down the column (reflux + liquid from feed) - $V$ = Vapour molar flow rate up the column ($L + D$) - $D$ = Distillate (top product) molar flow rate - $x_D$ = Distillate composition **Stripping Section (below feed):** $$y_{n+1} = \frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} x_n - \frac{B}{\bar{V}} x_B$$ The **Reflux Ratio** $R = L/D$ and the **Minimum Reflux Ratio** $R_m$ are critical design parameters: $$R_m = \frac{x_D - y_F^*}{y_F^* - x_F}$$ where $y_F^*$ is the vapour composition in equilibrium with the feed liquid composition $x_F$. ## 2.4 Fenske, Underwood & Gilliland — Quick Estimate Equations For multi-component separations (which is what refinery columns really are), the **Fenske equation** gives the *minimum number of stages at total reflux*: $$N_m = \frac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_D \left( \frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}} \right)_B \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{LK,HK}}$$ The **Underwood equation** estimates the **minimum reflux ratio** $R_m$: $$\sum_i \frac{q \cdot z_i}{V_{min} - q \cdot z_i / (K_i - 1)} = 1$$ The **Gilliland correlation** then links $N$ and $R$ to the actual operating point via the empirical parameters $X$ and $Y$. ## 2.5 The Distillation Column as a Control System From the **control engineering** perspective, the distillation column is a **multi-input multi-output (MIMO)**, highly interactive, non-linear process with significant **dead time (transport delay)** and **inverse response**. ### 2.5.1 The Five Classic Loops Every refinery distillation column has, at a minimum, these **five regulatory control loops**: 1. **Column Pressure Control** — Manipulates the overhead vapour bypass to the compressor or the coolant flow to the condenser. 2. **Reflux Drum Level Control** — Manipulates the **top product draw rate** (D). 3. **Sump (Reboiler) Level Control** — Manipulates the **bottom product draw rate** (B). 4. **Overhead Receiver Temperature / Composition Control** — Manipulates the **reflux flow rate** (R). 5. **Column Temperature Profile Control** — Manipulates the **reboiler steam flow** (Q_R) or the pumparound duty. ### 2.5.2 Advanced Control Strategies | Strategy | Function | When Used | |---|---|---| | **Cascade Control** | Inner loop for flow/pressure; outer loop for temperature/composition | When there is a secondary, faster variable to measure | | **Feedforward Control** | Anticipates feed disturbances using feed flow & temperature measurement | When feed flow varies frequently | | **Ratio Control** | Maintains R/D ratio constant | To handle throughput changes | | **Inferential Composition Control** | Estimates product composition from tray temperatures using ASTM correlations | When online analyzers are too slow/expensive | | **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** — e.g., DMC, RMPCT | Solves a dynamic optimization problem over a prediction horizon | Modern large-scale refineries (standard practice) | ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $y_i = \dfrac{x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$ | Raoult's Law (binary VLE) | | 2 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B}$ | Relative volatility (dimensionless) | | 3 | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | Reflux ratio | | 4 | $R_m = \dfrac{x_D - y_F^*}{y_F^* - x_F}$ | Minimum reflux ratio (McCabe-Thiele) | | 5 | $q = \dfrac{\text{moles of saturated liquid formed per mole of feed}}{\text{total moles of feed}}$ | Feed thermal condition (q-line slope) | | 6 | $N_m = \dfrac{\ln \left[\left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B\right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{LK,HK}}$ | Fenske – minimum stages at total reflux | | 7 | $A = \dfrac{R - R_m}{R + 1}$ | Gilliland correlation – X-axis parameter | | 8 | $Y = \dfrac{N - N_m}{N + 1}$ | Gilliland correlation – Y-axis parameter | | 9 | $T_{reflux} = T_{top} + \Delta T_{subcool}$ | Reflux temperature relationship | | 10 | $\dot{Q}_{reboiler} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Reboiler duty (LMTD = log mean temperature difference) | | 11 | $\tau_p = \dfrac{M_{hold-up}}{F_{out}}$ | Process time constant (level loop) | | 12 | $G_{PID}(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right)$ | PID controller transfer function | | 13 | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p K_c}$ | Steady-state offset (proportional only) | | 14 | $\text{ASTM 95\%} \approx f(T_{tray})$ | Inferential composition (linear correlation) | | 15 | $\text{Weir loading} = \dfrac{L}{\ell_w \cdot h_{weir}^{3/2}}$ | Tray hydraulic design check | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize formulas **1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 12, 13**. These appear almost every cycle. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Distillation control is the *highest-value* application of process control in the hydrocarbon industry. A 1% improvement in distillation energy efficiency at a 200,000 BPD refinery saves roughly **₹ 15 – 25 crore per year** in fuel consumption. Advanced control via MPC routinely yields **3 – 5% throughput increase**, **5 – 10% energy reduction**, and pushes products closer to **specification limits**, which is the difference between profit and loss in modern refining. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Rectifying Section Operating Line ### 3.1.1 Statement Derive the equation of the operating line for the **rectifying section** of a distillation column from first principles, using a steady-state mass balance. ### 3.1.2 Setup — Define the Control Volume Consider the **upper portion** of the distillation column, bounded below by tray $n$ and above by the condenser. Let the streams crossing the control volume be: - Vapour rising from tray $n$ into tray $n+1$ → flow rate $V$ (mol/s), composition $y_n$ - Liquid descending from tray $n+1$ into tray $n$ → flow rate $L$ (mol/s), composition $x_{n+1}$ - Distillate leaving the condenser → flow rate $D$ (mol/s), composition $x_D$ ### 3.1.3 Total Mass Balance (Steady State) At steady state, accumulation = 0: $$V = L + D$$ ### 3.1.4 Component Mass Balance (for the more-volatile component) The total moles of light key entering the CV per unit time equals the total moles leaving: $$V \cdot y_n = L \cdot x_{n+1} + D \cdot x_D$$ ### 3.1.5 Substitution for V From the total mass balance, $V = L + D$. Substituting: $$(L + D) \cdot y_n = L \cdot x_{n+1} + D \cdot x_D$$ ### 3.1.6 Solve for $y_n$ Rearrange to isolate $y_n$: $$L \cdot y_n + D \cdot y_n = L \cdot x_{n+1} + D \cdot x_D$$ $$L \cdot (y_n - x_{n+1}) = D \cdot (x_D - y_n)$$ Divide both sides by $V = L + D$: $$y_n = \frac{L}{V} x_{n+1} + \frac{D}{V} x_D$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** The **rectifying operating line** is a straight line on the McCabe-Thiele $y$–$x$ diagram with: > - Slope = $\dfrac{L}{V} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}$ > - $y$-intercept = $\dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ > - It passes through the point $(x_D, x_D)$ on the diagonal $y=x$. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Fenske Equation (Minimum Stages at Total Reflux) ### 3.2.1 Statement Show that, at total reflux ($D = 0$, $L = V$), the minimum number of theoretical stages required to separate a binary mixture of light key (LK) and heavy key (HK) between distillate and bottoms compositions is: $$N_m = \frac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_D \left( \frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}} \right)_B \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{LK,HK}}$$ ### 3.2.2 At Total Reflux When $D = 0$, the operating lines coincide with the diagonal $y = x$. Therefore, on every tray, the **vapour leaving stage $n$** is in equilibrium with the **liquid leaving stage $n$** AND is the same composition as the liquid entering stage $n$: $$y_n = x_{n+1}, \quad y_n = K_{LK} \cdot x_n^{(LK)}$$ ### 3.2.3 Equilibrium Relationship for Each Component By definition of $K$-value: $$\frac{y_{LK}}{y_{HK}} = \alpha_{LK,HK} \cdot \frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}$$ ### 3.2.4 Recursion Across Trays Since $y_n = x_{n+1}$ at total reflux, the ratio $\beta_n = \dfrac{x_n^{LK}}{x_n^{HK}}$ evolves down the column as: $$\beta_{n+1} = \frac{x_{n+1}^{LK}}{x_{n+1}^{HK}} = \frac{y_n^{LK}}{y_n^{HK}} = \alpha_{LK,HK} \cdot \frac{x_n^{LK}}{x_n^{HK}} = \alpha_{LK,HK} \cdot \beta_n$$ This gives a geometric progression: $$\beta_{n+1} = (\alpha_{LK,HK})^n \cdot \beta_0$$ ### 3.2.5 Apply Boundary Conditions Top of column: $\beta_{top} = \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D$ Bottom of column: $\beta_{bottom} = \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B$ (note: invert!) After $N_m$ stages: $$\frac{\beta_{top}}{\beta_{bottom}} = (\alpha_{LK,HK})^{N_m}$$ ### 3.2.6 Solve for $N_m$ Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$N_m \cdot \ln \alpha_{LK,HK} = \ln \left[\left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \cdot \left(\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B\right]$$ $$\boxed{N_m = \frac{\ln \left[\left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B\right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{LK,HK}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Takeaway:** $N_m$ depends **only** on the relative volatility $\alpha$ and the desired separation sharpness — *not* on the reflux ratio. It is the **theoretical lower bound** on the number of stages. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Minimum Reflux Ratio $R_m$ (Underwood Approximation for Saturated Liquid Feed) ### 3.3.1 Setup At $R_m$, an infinite number of stages is required; the operating line intersects the equilibrium curve at the **pinch point** at the feed stage. ### 3.3.2 Feed Condition For a **saturated liquid feed** ($q = 1$): - $L$ below feed = $L_{above} + F$ (feed adds to liquid) - $V$ below feed = $V_{above}$ (no vapour in saturated liquid feed) - $V_{min} = L_{min} + D$ (rectifying mass balance) - $R_m = L_{min} / D$ ### 3.3.3 Pinch Point Condition The intersection point of the operating line and the equilibrium curve is on the 45° line at the feed composition $(x_F, y_F)$ when the feed is at its bubble point. Solving the rectifying operating line evaluated at this point: $$y_F = \frac{R_m}{R_m + 1} x_F + \frac{x_D}{R_m + 1}$$ Rearrange to isolate $R_m$: $$y_F (R_m + 1) = R_m x_F + x_D$$ $$R_m (y_F - x_F) = x_D - y_F$$ $$\boxed{R_m = \frac{x_D - y_F}{y_F - x_F}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** This is the **special case** for saturated liquid feed. The general Underwood equation handles sub-cooled, two-phase, and superheated feeds via the $q$-line. ## 3.4 Worked Example — $N_m$ for an Naphtha-Kerosene Separation > **Problem:** A refinery needs to separate a binary feed into distillate with 98 mol% light key (LK = $n$-C₉) and bottoms with 2 mol% LK. The heavy key (HK = $n$-C₁₂) saturation pressures at column pressure give $\alpha = 2.5$. Calculate $N_m$. **Solution:** $$\left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D = \frac{0.98}{0.02} = 49$$ $$\left(\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B = \frac{0.98}{0.02} = 49$$ $$N_m = \frac{\ln(49 \times 49)}{\ln(2.5)} = \frac{\ln(2401)}{\ln(2.5)} = \frac{7.783}{0.9163} = 8.49 \approx 8.5 \text{ stages}$$ With a typical **Murphree vapour efficiency** $E_{MV} = 0.7$, the actual number of trays is: $$N_{actual} = \frac{N_m}{E_{MV}} = \frac{8.5}{0.7} \approx 12.1 \approx 13 \text{ trays}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Working-Style Tip:** Always show *every* numerical substitution and the units. Examiners award full marks only when the substitution is clearly written. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Refinery Distillation Column ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crude Oil Feed

[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Relative Volatility. Why is it important in distillation column design? **Model Answer:** **Relative Volatility ($\alpha$)** is defined as the ratio of the $K$-values (or saturation pressures) of two components in a mixture: $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$ It is a **dimensionless number** that quantifies the ease of separation between components A and B. Its importance in distillation design is threefold: 1. **Separation feasibility:** If $\alpha = 1$, separation by ordinary distillation is impossible; if $\alpha > 1$, separation is possible; the larger $\alpha$, the easier the separation. 2. **Number of stages:** Fenske's equation shows $N_m$ decreases logarithmically as $\alpha$ increases. 3. **Reflux requirement:** Underwood's equation shows $R_m$ decreases sharply as $\alpha$ increases. > **[Marks: Definition = 1, Three points of importance = 2. Total 3 marks.]** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between Reflux Ratio ($R$) and Minimum Reflux Ratio ($R_m$). Why do refineries operate at $R = 1.2\,R_m$ to $1.5\,R_m$? **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Reflux Ratio $R = L/D$ | Minimum Reflux Ratio $R_m$ | |---|---|---| | Definition | Operating liquid returned to column per unit distillate | Theoretical minimum below which separation is impossible | | Number of stages | Finite, decreases as $R$ increases | **Infinite** number of stages required | | Operating cost | High (more reboiler + condenser duty) | Lowest theoretical reboiler duty | | Use | Real, designed operating point | Reference for design via Gilliland correlation | Refineries do **not** operate at $R = R_m$ because that would require an **infinitely tall column**, which is physically impossible. They also avoid $R = \infty$ (total reflux) because that produces **zero throughput**. The optimum is the **total annualized cost minimum**, which empirically falls at $R = 1.2\,R_m$ to $1.5\,R_m$ for refinery columns. > **[Marks: Tabular distinction = 2, Justification of 1.2–1.5 Rm = 1. Total 3 marks.]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. There is an **internal choice** — the student answers either **Question A** *or* **Question B** in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **function of a Distillation Column** in a refinery. List the **five classical control loops** with their manipulated variables. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Derive the **rectifying section operating line equation** of a binary distillation column from first principles. Show how the slope of this line is related to the **reflux ratio** $R$. **[7 Marks]** ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Five Loops [7 Marks] A **refinery distillation column** is a vertical, cylindrical, trayed or packed vessel that separates a multi-component hydrocarbon feed (e.g., desalted crude oil) into multiple product streams of differing boiling ranges through **vapour-liquid mass transfer** on internal trays. **Block Diagram (textual representation):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────┐ Crude Feed (F) ───► │ Furnace (Heater) │ └────────────┬─────────────┘ │ Hot Crude ▼ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ Distillation Tower │ │ (Trays 1 to N) │ └──┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──┘ │ │ │ │ Overhead │ Kero │ Diese│ AGO │ Bottom (RCO) ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ Naphtha Kero Diesel AGO RCO (Top) Side 1 Side 2 Side 3 (Bott.) ``` **The Five Classical Control Loops:** | Loop | Controlled Variable (CV) | Manipulated Variable (MV) | Typical Sensor | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Column Pressure (PT) | Condenser vent / vapour bypass valve | Pressure Transmitter | | 2 | Reflux Drum Level (LT) | Top product draw flow ($D$) | Level Transmitter / Dp cell | | 3 | Sump (Reboiler) Level (LT) | Bottom product draw flow ($B$) | Level Transmitter / Dp cell | | 4 | Tray Temperature (TT) near top | Reflux flow ($L$ or $R$) | RTD / Thermocouple | | 5 | Tray Temperature (TT) near bottom | Reboiler steam flow / fuel gas | RTD / Thermocouple | > **[Marks: Block diagram = 2, Naming loops and CVs = 3, Identifying MVs = 2. Total 7 marks.]** #### Part (b) — Derivation of Rectifying Operating Line [7 Marks] (See Section 3.1 for the full derivation.) **Summary of the Derivation Steps:** 1. **Draw control volume** around the top section of the column, bounded by tray $n$, tray $n+1$, and the condenser. **[1 mark]** 2. **Total mass balance** at steady state: $V = L + D$. **[1 mark]** 3. **Component mass balance**: $V \cdot y_n = L \cdot x_{n+1} + D \cdot x_D$. **[1 mark]** 4. **Substitute** $V = L + D$. **[1 mark]** 5. **Solve for $y_n$:** $y_n = \dfrac{L}{V} x_{n+1} + \dfrac{D}{V} x_D$. **[1 mark]** 6. **Express in terms of $R$:** Since $R = L/D$ and $V = L + D = (R+1)D$: $$y_n = \frac{R}{R+1} x_{n+1} + \frac{1}{R+1} x_D$$ Therefore the **slope** of the rectifying operating line is $\dfrac{R}{R+1}$ and the **y-intercept** is $\dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$. **[2 marks]** > **Key Conclusion:** As $R \to \infty$ (total reflux), slope $\to 1$ (line coincides with 45° diagonal). As $R \to R_m$, the line just touches the equilibrium curve at the pinch point. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** What is **Inferential Composition Control** in a refinery distillation column? Why is it preferred over direct on-line analyzers? Explain the working principle with a block diagram. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A saturated liquid feed containing **60 mol% methanol** and **40 mol% water** is to be distilled at 1 atm to give a distillate of **95 mol% methanol** and bottoms of **5 mol% methanol**. The feed is **100 kmol/h**. The relative volatility $\alpha = 3.5$. Calculate: (i) The **minimum number of stages** $N_m$ using the Fenske equation. (ii) The **minimum reflux ratio** $R_m$. **[7 Marks]** ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — Inferential Composition Control [7 Marks] **Definition:** Inferential composition control is a strategy in which the **product composition** is *not measured directly* (which would require a slow chromatograph, NIR analyzer, or boiling-point analyzer) but is **inferred** from a fast, reliable secondary measurement — typically a **tray temperature** near the top or bottom of the column. The tray temperature is correlated to composition via **ASTM distillation correlations** (e.g., ASTM D86 95% point). **Why preferred over on-line analyzers:** | Criterion | Direct Analyzer | Inferential (Temperature) | |---|---|---| | Response time | 3 – 20 min (chromatograph) | 5 – 30 seconds (RTD) | | Capital cost | ₹ 50 L – 2 Cr per stream | Fraction of the cost | | Maintenance | High; carrier gas, calibration | Low; solid-state sensor | | Reliability | Moderate (moving parts, sample system) | High (no sampling lag) | | Suitability for control | Poor (dead time dominates) | Excellent | **Block Diagram:** ``` [Column Tray T] → [Temperature TT-101] → [Master TC-101] → [Slave FC-102] → [Reflux Valve] (setpoint) (flow loop) ``` **[Marks: Definition = 1, Block diagram = 2, Comparison table = 2, Working explanation = 2. Total 7 marks.]** #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks] **Given:** - $x_F = 0.60$ (methanol, saturated liquid feed) - $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$ - $\alpha = 3.5$, $F = 100$ kmol/h - Saturated liquid feed → $q = 1$, $y_F = x_F$ (in the simplified Underwood pinch) **(i) Minimum Number of Stages — Fenske Equation: [3 Marks]** $$N_m = \frac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \right) \left( \frac{1 - x_B}{x_B} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha}$$ $$N_m = \frac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \left( \frac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \right]}{\ln 3.5}$$ $$N_m = \frac{\ln(19 \times 19)}{\ln 3.5} = \frac{\ln(361)}{\ln 3.5} = \frac{5.886}{1.2528} = 4.70 \approx 5 \text{ stages}$$ **(ii) Minimum Reflux Ratio: [4 Marks]** For saturated liquid feed, the pinch is at the feed plate and: $$R_m = \frac{x_D - y_F}{y_F - x_F}$$ But for saturated liquid feed, $y_F$ equals the vapour composition in equilibrium with $x_F$: $$y_F = \frac{\alpha \cdot x_F}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_F} = \frac{3.5 \times 0.60}{1 + 2.5 \times 0.60} = \frac{2.10}{2.50} = 0.84$$ $$R_m = \frac{0.95 - 0.84}{0.84 - 0.60} = \frac{0.11}{0.24} = 0.458$$ $$\boxed{N_m \approx 5 \text{ stages}, \quad R_m \approx 0.46}$$ > **[Marks: Fenske substitution = 2, Fenske answer = 1; Underwood-equilibrium step = 2, Final Rm = 2. Total 7 marks.]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Refinery Instrumentation Exams:** > 1. **Mixing up symbols:** $L$ (liquid downflow, mol/s) vs. $\bar{L}$ (stripping-section liquid) — wrong subscript loses **1 mark**. > 2. **Forgetting the $q$-line:** The $q$-line is essential in McCabe-Thiele; omitting it on the diagram loses **2 marks**. > 3. **Confusing $K$-value and relative volatility:** $K_i = y_i/x_i$ (component-specific), $\alpha$ = ratio of $K$'s (binary property). > 4. **Forgetting saturation pressure dependence on temperature:** Raoult's Law is at a *given* $T$; if the problem doesn't specify $T$, students often write $P_i$ without $P^{sat}$ — loses **1 mark**. > 5. **In the numerical question:** The Underwood pinch is on the **$q$-line intersecting the equilibrium curve**, *not* at $y_F = x_F$ (this is only true for saturated liquid feed). > 6. **In control loop diagrams:** Forgetting the *direction* of the cascade arrow (master → slave, never slave → master) loses **1 mark** on a 14-mark question. > 7. **Not specifying the units** in a numerical answer (e.g., "$N_m = 5$" instead of "$N_m = 5$ theoretical stages"). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Distillation** is the workhorse of every refinery — primary separation of crude oil into commercial fractions. - [x] Two main types: **Atmospheric Distillation Column (ADC)** and **Vacuum Distillation Column (VDC)**. - [x] Core governing principle: **Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)** described by **Raoult's Law** and **Relative Volatility** $\alpha$. - [x] **Reflux Ratio** $R = L/D$ — the single most important operating parameter. - [x] **Minimum Reflux Ratio** $R_m$ — lower bound; corresponds to infinite stages. - [x] **Fenske Equation** → $N_m$ (minimum stages at total reflux). - [x] **Underwood Equation** → $R_m$ for multi-component feeds with general $q$. - [x] **Gilliland Correlation** → links $N$ and $R$ at the actual operating point. - [x] **Five classical control loops** are mandatory in every column: Pressure, Drum Level, Sump Level, Top Temperature, Bottom Temperature. - [x] **Cascade control** = master–slave architecture; **Feedforward** = anticipates feed disturbance. - [x] **Inferential control** = infer composition from tray temperature (fast, cheap, reliable). - [x] **MPC (DMC, RMPCT)** is standard in modern refineries. - [x] **Reflux ratio** affects both **operating cost** (energy) and **capital cost** (number of trays). Optimum at $R \approx 1.2\,R_m$ to $1.5\,R_m$. - [x] KTU frequently tests: Fenske, McCabe-Thiele operating lines, five-loop identification, and inferential control block diagrams. --- > **End of Module 2 — Distillation Notes** > *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech PEICT631 — Refinary Instrumentation.*

topics.title: Thermal Cracking, Catalytic Cracking, Catalytic Reforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Cracking, Catalytic Cracking, Catalytic Reforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Cracking? (The Foundation Concept)

In petroleum refining, **cracking** is the process by which large, heavy, and complex hydrocarbon molecules (with high molecular weight and low commercial value — e.g., gas oils, vacuum residues, heavy naphtha) are broken down into smaller, lighter, and more valuable molecules (e.g., gasoline, LPG, olefins, diesel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cracking is the "molecular demolition" step of a refinery.** The refinery cannot sell heavy fuel oil at gasoline prices — so the refiner chemically breaks the heavy molecules into lighter, more valuable ones.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a long railway train (heavy, slow, carrying low-value cargo) being uncoupled at certain cars by applying heat (thermal) or a chemical scissor (catalyst). The smaller trains that result are faster, more agile, and carry premium cargo. The "uncoupling station" is the **cracker reactor**.

- Without cracking: Crude oil → mostly heavy fuel oil, wax, asphalt (low value)
- With cracking: Crude oil → gasoline + diesel + LPG + petrochemical feedstocks (high value)

The **average molecular weight** of the product is *lower* than the feed, and the **API gravity** (a measure of how light a petroleum liquid is) is *higher*.

---

### 1.2 Thermal Cracking

**Formal Definition:**
Thermal cracking is a refinery process in which saturated hydrocarbons are decomposed (C–C bond scission) into smaller molecules by the action of **heat alone**, without the use of a catalyst. It proceeds through a **free-radical chain mechanism** and produces olefins, gasolines, gas oils, and residues depending on severity.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a stretched rubber band on a hot summer afternoon — the heat (thermal energy) is enough to snap the polymer chain. Similarly, when a long-chain hydrocarbon is heated above its decomposition temperature, the weakest C–C bonds break first, fragmenting the molecule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating envelope (classical):** Temperature = **450 °C – 750 °C**, Pressure = **10 – 70 bar** (high pressure for residue, low pressure for olefin production via steam cracking).

**Key characteristics:**
- Bond breaking is **non-selective** — many parallel reactions occur.
- Produces significant **olefinic (unsaturated) products** — useful for petrochemicals.
- Operates without catalyst, so it tolerates **sulfur, nitrogen, metals, and asphaltenes**.
- Used to crack **vacuum residue, deasphalted oil, atmospheric residue, and gas oils**.

---

### 1.3 Catalytic Cracking

**Formal Definition:**
Catalytic cracking is the refinery conversion process in which heavy hydrocarbon fractions (typically vacuum gas oil, VGO) are broken into lighter products (gasoline, LPG, light olefins) by contact with a **solid acidic catalyst** (silica-alumina or modern zeolites such as **ZSM-5, USY, FAU**) at moderate temperatures and near-atmospheric pressure. The mechanism is **ionic (carbocation)**, giving much higher selectivity to gasoline than thermal cracking.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a **lock and key** — a heavy hydrocarbon molecule (key) approaches the acidic sites of the zeolite (lock) and the chemical reaction is "facilitated" rather than forced. The catalyst lowers the activation energy, so lower temperatures suffice, and the products are *branched and aromatic* (which burn smoothly in engines) rather than *straight and olefinic*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating envelope:** Temperature = **480 °C – 540 °C**, Pressure = **1 – 3 bar (gauge)** — close to atmospheric! Residence time = **1 – 4 seconds** in a modern **riser reactor**.

**Why use a catalyst?**
- Higher gasoline yield (≈ **70–80 %** vs. **50–60 %** in thermal).
- Higher **Research Octane Number (RON ≈ 90–95)** in gasoline product.
- Lower operating temperature → less fuel for furnace.
- Suppresses coke (carbonaceous deposit) formation somewhat (though coke is still formed and must be burned off in a **regenerator**).

---

### 1.4 Catalytic Reforming

**Formal Definition:**
Catalytic reforming is a refinery process that **upgrades low-octane naphtha** (C₆–C₁₁, containing paraffins and naphthenes) into **high-octane reformate** (rich in aromatics and isoparaffins) by passing the feed over a **bifunctional catalyst** (platinum or platinum–rhenium on chlorided alumina) at elevated temperature and low hydrogen pressure. The process simultaneously produces a large quantity of **hydrogen gas** as a by-product, which is critical for hydrotreating and hydrocracking units.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Take a plain, dull piece of iron and heat it in a furnace with a special coating — it transforms into shiny stainless steel. Similarly, low-value straight-chain paraffins and naphthenes are "re-arranged" by the reforming catalyst into **aromatic rings** (benzene, toluene, xylene — BTX) and **branched isomers** that resist engine knock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating envelope:** Temperature = **450 °C – 525 °C**, Pressure = **3.5 – 35 bar** (modern units operate at the **low end, ~ 3.5–7 bar**). Hydrogen-rich atmosphere (H₂/Hydrocarbon molar ratio ≈ **3–6**).

**The four principal reforming reactions:**
1. **Dehydrogenation of naphthenes → aromatics** (the dominant, highly endothermic reaction — produces most of the H₂).
2. **Isomerization of n-paraffins → isoparaffins** (mildly exothermic).
3. **Dehydrocyclization of paraffins → aromatics** (highly endothermic).
4. **Hydrocracking of paraffins → lighter products** (exothermic, undesirable — minimized by controlling severity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reforming is the principal source of refinery hydrogen.** A typical reformer producing reformate of RON 98 can co-produce **150–300 Nm³ H₂ per m³ of naphtha feed**. This hydrogen is piped to hydrodesulfurization (HDS), hydrotreating, and hydrocracking units.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermal Cracking — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Feedstock preparation**
Heavy feedstock (atmospheric or vacuum residue) is mixed with **recycle streams** and preheated in a convection section of a fired heater.

**Step 2 — Vaporization and reaction in the soaking coil**
The feed enters a **tubular heater (furnace)** where it reaches **450–750 °C**. The hydrocarbon molecules absorb thermal energy, vibrations of the C–C bonds exceed the bond dissociation energy, and **homolytic bond scission** occurs. The reaction is **first-order** with respect to hydrocarbon concentration and roughly follows Arrhenius kinetics.

$$ k = A \, e^{-E_a / (RT)} $$

where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹), $E_a$ is the activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ is the universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**, and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

**Step 3 — Free-radical chain mechanism (Rice–Herzfeld)**
A hydrocarbon RH is thermally cleaved into two free radicals:

$$ \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} $$

These radicals undergo **β-scission** (breaking the bond β to the radical), producing an **α-olefin** and a smaller radical:

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{–CH}_2^{\bullet} $$

The chain continues until two radicals combine (termination step).

**Step 4 — Quenching**
The hot cracked vapours are rapidly **quenched** (heat exchange) to stop secondary reactions that would otherwise over-crack the gasoline into gas.

**Step 5 — Fractionation and product recovery**
The quenched product is sent to an **atmospheric fractionator** to separate: unconverted residue (recycled), gas oil, gasoline, and gases.

**Severity control** is done by manipulating:
- **Coil outlet temperature (COT)** — higher COT → more gasoline, more gas.
- **Soaking time** — longer time → deeper conversion.
- **Pressure** — higher pressure suppresses vaporization but suppresses secondary cracking of gasoline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is pressure high in thermal cracking of residues?**
> High pressure keeps more material in the liquid phase (suppresses gas formation) and gives more **time for liquid-phase cracking** in the soaking drum.

---

### 2.2 Catalytic Cracking — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Feed vaporization and catalyst contact**
Preheated VGO is sprayed as fine droplets into a **riser reactor** where it contacts hot, regenerated catalyst particles (≈ **650–730 °C catalyst temperature**). The catalyst vaporizes the feed instantly.

**Step 2 — Carbocation (carbonium ion) mechanism**
At the acidic sites of the zeolite (Brønsted acid sites, Si–OH–Al), a hydrocarbon gains a proton:

$$ \text{RH} + \text{H}^+(\text{cat}) \rightarrow \text{R}^+ + \text{H}_2(\text{cat}) $$

The carbocation undergoes **β-scission** to form a smaller carbocation and an olefin. The smaller carbocation can abstract a hydride from another molecule, propagating the chain.

**Step 3 — Product formation**
The mixture of cracked hydrocarbons is disengaged from the catalyst in **cyclones** at the top of the riser, sent to a **main fractionator**, and separated into: dry gas, LPG, gasoline (naphtha), light cycle oil (LCO), heavy cycle oil (HCO), slurry oil, and **coke deposited on catalyst**.

**Step 4 — Catalyst regeneration (the heart of FCC)**
The coke-laden catalyst (called **spent catalyst**) is stripped with steam to recover entrained hydrocarbons, then flows to the **regenerator** where it meets air. Coke is burned off as:

$$ \text{C (coke)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

The regenerated catalyst (≈ **< 0.05 wt % coke**) returns to the riser, completing the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **The regenerator also functions as the energy source for the process.** The heat released by burning coke (≈ **3.5–4.5 MJ per kg of catalyst**) provides the energy needed to vaporize the feed and maintain reactor temperature.

**Step 5 — Critical control variables**
- **Reactor (riser) outlet temperature (ROT):** **510–540 °C** — directly determines conversion.
- **Catalyst-to-oil ratio (C/O):** typically **5–8** — high C/O → high conversion.
- **Feed preheat temperature:** **200–350 °C**.
- **Regenerator temperature:** **680–730 °C** — limited by catalyst hydrothermal stability.

---

### 2.3 Catalytic Reforming — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Feed preparation**
Naphtha (C₆–C₁₁, RON ≈ 40–60) is hydrotreated to remove **sulfur and nitrogen**, which are **permanent poisons** to the platinum catalyst. Sulfur levels must be reduced to **< 0.5 ppm** before the reformer.

**Step 2 — Mixed-phase catalytic reaction**
The clean naphtha + recycle hydrogen enters a series of **adiabatic reactors** (3 or 4 in series), each containing a fixed bed of catalyst. Modern units use either:
- **Semi-regenerative (SR)** — periodic shut-down for catalyst regeneration.
- **Continuous catalyst regeneration (CCR)** — catalyst circulates continuously (UOP Platforming™ with CCR) — gives the highest yield at the lowest pressure.

**Step 3 — The four reforming reactions**

| # | Reaction | Type | Thermodynamics | Effect on RON |
|---|----------|------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Naphthene → Aromatic + 3 H₂ | Dehydrogenation | Endothermic (ΔH ≈ +205 kJ/mol), favored by low P, high T | **+** large |
| 2 | n-Paraffin → i-Paraffin | Isomerization | Mildly exothermic (ΔH ≈ –5 kJ/mol) | **+** moderate |
| 3 | n-Paraffin → Aromatic + 4 H₂ | Dehydrocyclization | Highly endothermic (ΔH ≈ +260 kJ/mol) | **+** large |
| 4 | Paraffin → smaller paraffin + gas | Hydrocracking | Exothermic (ΔH ≈ –40 kJ/mol) | – (loss) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reaction 1 is the dominant H₂-producing step.** Because it is endothermic and increases moles, **high temperature and low pressure** shift equilibrium strongly toward aromatics and H₂. This is precisely why modern reformers operate at the **lowest pressure economically viable (≈ 3.5 bar)**.

**Step 4 — Product separation**
Reactor effluent is cooled, sent to a **separator** to recover recycle H₂, then to a **stabilizer column** to remove dissolved light gases (C₁–C₄). The bottom product is the **reformate** (RON ≈ 95–102).

**Step 5 — Net hydrogen export**
H₂ from the separator (after compression) is exported to HDS, hydrocracker, and isomerization units. This makes reforming the **hydrogen engine of the modern refinery**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Unit |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Arrhenius rate | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}$ | Rate constant $k$ (s⁻¹) |
| Conversion | $X = \dfrac{F_{\text{in}} - F_{\text{out}}}{F_{\text{in}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Selectivity | $S_i = \dfrac{Y_i}{X}$ | Product $i$ per unit conversion |
| Yield | $Y_i = S_i \cdot X$ | Mass or vol basis |
| API gravity | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{\text{rel}}} - 131.5$ | $\rho_{\text{rel}}$ at 15.6 °C |
| H₂ balance | $\text{H}_2$ produced (Nm³) = feed vol (m³) $\times$ (1.5 – 3) × 100 | Typical reformer |
| Coke on catalyst (FCC) | $\Delta C = $ wt % coke burned in regenerator | 0.6 – 1.2 wt % typical |
| Regenerator heat | $Q_{\text{regen}} = m_{\text{cat}} \cdot \Delta C \cdot \Delta H_{\text{coke}}$ | kW |
| RON gain | $\Delta \text{RON} = \text{RON}_{\text{prod}} - \text{RON}_{\text{feed}}$ | Usually 40 – 60 units |
| H₂/Hc molar ratio | $r = \dfrac{n_{\text{H}_2}}{n_{\text{HC}}}$ | 3 – 6 typical |
| Equilibrium constant (R1) | $K_p = \dfrac{p_{\text{Ar}} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2}^3}{p_{\text{Naphthene}}}$ | Increases with T, decreases with P |
| C/O ratio (FCC) | $\dfrac{W_{\text{cat}}}{W_{\text{oil}}}$ | 5 – 8 typical |
| Severity | $\text{Severity} \propto f(T, t)$ | Higher T or t → more conversion |
| Activation energy (thermal) | $E_a \approx 200 - 280$ kJ/mol | C–C scission |
| Activation energy (catalytic) | $E_a \approx 80 - 140$ kJ/mol | With acid catalyst |
| Mass balance | $F_{\text{feed}} = F_{\text{gas}} + F_{\text{gasoline}} + F_{\text{resid}} + F_{\text{coke}}$ | Closure of cracker |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip (KTU 2024):** For comparison questions, memorize the **three operating envelopes** (T, P, catalyst state) and **product slates** — this is the most frequently asked pattern.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal cracking** → still used in **delayed coking** and **visbreaking** units (the workhorses for upgrading vacuum residue in Indian refineries such as BPCL-Kochi, IOCL-Panipat).
- **Catalytic cracking (FCC)** → produces the **largest volume of gasoline** in a modern refinery; integrated with petrochemicals (propylene → polypropylene).
- **Catalytic reforming** → produces **high-octane gasoline blendstock + hydrogen**; the hydrogen keeps the hydrocracker running, the hydrocracker keeps the FCC feed clean — a tightly coupled ecosystem.
- Together these three units convert a barrel of crude into **~ 90 % transportation fuels** vs. ~ 40 % by simple distillation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Cracking Conversion as a Function of Severity

For a first-order thermal cracking reaction in a plug-flow reactor (the soaking coil), the **design equation** is:

$$ \tau = -\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \dfrac{dC_A}{-r_A} $$

Since the rate is first order in the cracking of heavy species, $-r_A = k \cdot C_A$, we obtain:

$$ \tau = \int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} -\dfrac{dC_A}{k \cdot C_A} = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) $$

In terms of **conversion** $X = 1 - C_A / C_{A0}$, so $C_A = C_{A0} (1 - X)$, giving:

$$ \tau = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - X}\right) $$

Rearranging for conversion:

$$ X = 1 - e^{-k\tau} $$

Substituting the Arrhenius expression $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)}$:

$$ \boxed{\, X = 1 - \exp\!\left[ -A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)} \cdot \tau \right] \,} $$

**Reading this result:**
- Higher temperature $T$ → larger exponent inside → **higher conversion**.
- Longer residence time $\tau$ → larger exponent → **higher conversion**.
- Higher activation energy $E_a$ → the rate is more sensitive to temperature (typical of thermal cracking ≈ **200–280 kJ/mol**).

**Numerical example (typical thermal cracker):**
Let $A = 1.0 \times 10^{13}$ s⁻¹, $E_a = 230 \times 10^3$ J/mol, $T = 973$ K (700 °C), $\tau = 30$ s.

$$ \dfrac{E_a}{RT} = \dfrac{230\,000}{8.314 \cdot 973} = 28.43 $$

$$ k = 1.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot e^{-28.43} = 1.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot 4.5 \times 10^{-13} \approx 4.5 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

$$ X = 1 - e^{-(4.5)(30)} = 1 - e^{-135} \approx 1.0 $$

(Reaction is essentially complete — that is why the soaking coil is short.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Pressure on Catalytic Reforming Equilibrium

The dominant reforming reaction is:

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12} \,(\text{naphthene}) \rightleftharpoons \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \,(\text{aromatic}) + 3\,\text{H}_2 $$

The equilibrium constant (in terms of partial pressures, ideal gas):

$$ K_p = \dfrac{p_{\text{Ar}} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2}^3}{p_{\text{Naph}}} $$

For an initial pressure $P_0$ and a basis of 1 mole of naphthene with extent of reaction $\xi$:

- $p_{\text{Naph}} = \dfrac{1 - \xi}{1 + 3\xi} \cdot P_0$
- $p_{\text{Ar}} = \dfrac{\xi}{1 + 3\xi} \cdot P_0$
- $p_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{3\xi}{1 + 3\xi} \cdot P_0$

Substituting:

$$ K_p = \dfrac{\left[\dfrac{\xi}{1+3\xi} P_0\right] \cdot \left[\dfrac{3\xi}{1+3\xi} P_0\right]^3}{\left[\dfrac{1-\xi}{1+3\xi} P_0\right]} = \dfrac{27\,\xi^4}{(1-\xi)(1+3\xi)^3} \cdot P_0^3 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The pressure dependence is $K_p \propto P_0^3$.** Reducing $P_0$ from 35 bar to 3.5 bar (a factor of 10) **decreases $K_p$ requirement by a factor of 1000** — but $K_p$ itself is fixed by temperature. Therefore $\xi$ (conversion) must **increase dramatically** to compensate. This is precisely why **low-pressure reforming is so attractive**.

Solving the equation at fixed $T$ and varying $P_0$ shows that the equilibrium conversion to aromatics at $T = 500$ °C increases from **~ 60 % at 35 bar** to **> 95 % at 3.5 bar**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Most Important for KTU)

| Parameter | Thermal Cracking | Catalytic Cracking (FCC) | Catalytic Reforming |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Catalyst** | None | Zeolite (USY, ZSM-5) | Pt / Pt-Re on Cl-Al₂O₃ |
| **Temperature** | 450 – 750 °C | 480 – 540 °C | 450 – 525 °C |
| **Pressure** | 10 – 70 bar | 1 – 3 bar | 3.5 – 35 bar |
| **Residence time** | 10 – 100 s | 1 – 4 s | 1 – 2 h (in SR); minutes in CCR |
| **Mechanism** | Free radical (homolytic) | Carbocation (heterolytic) | Bifunctional (metal + acid) |
| **Phase of feed** | Liquid + vapor | Vapor | Vapor + H₂ |
| **Feed** | Atmospheric/vacuum residue, gas oil | Vacuum gas oil, atmospheric residue | Naphtha (C₆–C₁₁) |
| **Main product** | Gasoline, gas oil, olefins | Gasoline, LPG, propylene | High-octane reformate, H₂ |
| **Gasoline yield** | 50 – 60 % | 70 – 80 % | 85 – 90 % (reformate) |
| **RON of gasoline** | 60 – 70 | 90 – 95 | 95 – 102 |
| **Coke formation** | Low | High — needs regenerator | Negligible |
| **By-product H₂** | None (some) | None | **150 – 300 Nm³/m³ feed** |
| **Severity parameter** | COT, soaking time | Riser T, C/O | WAIT, T, P, H₂/HC |
| **Selectivity to olefins** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Investment** | Moderate | Very high | High |
| **Energy input** | External fired heater | Heat from coke burn-off | External fired heater (CCR) |
| **Co-product with high value** | α-olefins, residue for coker | Propylene (petrochem grade) | Hydrogen + BTX |
| **Modern trend** | Being phased out for FCC/FCC + coker | Dominant gasoline producer | Mandatory for H₂ supply |
| **Indian refinery presence** | Panipat, Bina (delayed coker) | Almost all PSU refineries | All major PSU refineries |
| **Major licensors** | None (proprietary) | UOP, KBR, Axens, Shell | UOP, Axens, ExxonMobil |

> [!NOTE]
> **In a KTU answer, this matrix is worth 14 marks if drawn neatly with explanation of each row.** Examiners reward clarity, layout, and the use of engineering units.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: Thermal Cracking (Delayed Coker / Visbreaker variant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF[Feed Residue] --> nodeP1[Feed Preheater]
    nodeP1 --> nodeH[Fired Heater<br/>450 - 750 C]
    nodeH --> nodeS[Soaking Drum / Coke Drum]
    nodeS --> nodeQ[Quench Heat Exchanger]
    nodeQ --> nodeFR[Atmospheric Fractionator]
    nodeFR --> nodeG1[Gas and LPG]
    nodeFR --> nodeG2[Cracked Naphtha]
    nodeFR --> nodeG3[Gas Oil Recycle]
    nodeFR --> nodeG4[Heavy Residue Recycle]
    nodeG3 --> nodeP1
    nodeG4 --> nodeP1
    nodeS -. coke .-> nodeC[Coke Pit or Calciner]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The feed is heated in a fired furnace, "soaked" in a drum where thermal cracking proceeds in the liquid phase, then quenched and fractionated. Heavy products are recycled. The coke that forms in the coke drum is the by-product of the visbreaker/delayed-coker variant.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Flow: Catalytic Cracking (FCC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeVGO[Feed VGO Preheater] --> nodeR[Riser Reactor<br/>510 - 540 C]
    nodeRCAT[Regenerated Catalyst<br/>650 - 730 C] --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeST[Stripper<br/>Steam Stripping]
    nodeST --> nodeSC[Spent Catalyst]
    nodeST --> nodeMF[Main Fractionator]
    nodeSC --> nodeREG[Regenerator<br/>Air + Coke Burn Off]
    nodeREG --> nodeRCAT
    nodeMF --> nodeP1[Wet Gas Compressor]
    nodeMF --> nodeP2[Absorber / Debutanizer]
    nodeMF --> nodeP3[Gasoline Product]
    nodeMF --> nodeP4[LCO]
    nodeMF --> nodeP5[Slurry Oil]
    nodeREG -. flue gas .-> nodeEC[CO Boiler / ESP]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The two circulating loops** (catalyst loop and oil loop) are the defining feature of an FCC. The catalyst carries heat from the regenerator to the riser and coke from the riser back to the regenerator. This is the **"heat carrier" function** of the catalyst.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Flow: Catalytic Reforming (CCR Platforming)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeNAPH[Hydrotreated Naphtha] --> nodeH1[Heater 1<br/>Reactor 1 Inlet]
    nodeH1 --> nodeRX1[Reactor 1<br/>Dehydrogenation]
    nodeRX1 --> nodeH2[Heater 2]
    nodeH2 --> nodeRX2[Reactor 2<br/>Dehydrocyclization]
    nodeRX2 --> nodeH3[Heater 3]
    nodeH3 --> nodeRX3[Reactor 3<br/>Isomerization + Dehydrogenation]
    nodeRX3 --> nodeSEP[HP Separator]
    nodeSEP --> nodeH2REC[Recycle H2 Compressor]
    nodeSEP --> nodeSTAB[Stabilizer Column]
    nodeSTAB --> nodeRG[Reflux Drum and Off Gas]
    nodeSTAB --> nodeREF[Reformate Product<br/>RON 95 to 102]
    nodeRX3 -. catalyst .-> nodeCCR[CCR Regenerator]
    nodeCCR -. regenerated catalyst .-> nodeRX1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration) loop is what makes low-pressure reforming feasible.** Spent catalyst is continuously withdrawn, regenerated (coke burned off in chlorine-controlled air), and returned to the top reactor — without shutting the unit down.

---

### 4.4 Process Hierarchy Map — Refinery Position of These Units

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CRUDE[Crude Oil Distillation<br/>Atmospheric and Vacuum]
    NAPH1[Straight Run Naphtha]
    KERO[Kerosene and Diesel]
    VGO[Vacuum Gas Oil]
    RES[Vacuum Residue]
    HT1[Hydrotreating]
    REFOR[Reforming]
    FCC[Fluid Catalytic Cracking]
    DC[Delayed Coker / Visbreaker]
    HCK[Hydrocracker]
    POOL[Gasoline and Diesel Pool]
    LPG[LPG Pool]
    HCEM[Petrochemical Olefins]

    CRUDE --> NAPH1
    CRUDE --> KERO
    CRUDE --> VGO
    CRUDE --> RES
    NAPH1 --> HT1 --> REFOR --> POOL
    VGO --> FCC --> POOL
    VGO --> HCK --> POOL
    RES --> DC --> POOL
    DC -. coke .-> FUEL[Refinery Fuel]
    FCC --> LPG
    FCC --> HCEM
    REFOR -. H2 .-> HT1
    REFOR -. H2 .-> HCK
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reforming is the heart of the hydrogen network.** Its H₂ side-stream supports hydrotreating and hydrocracking, which in turn produce clean feed for FCC and reformer. The three units discussed in this module are deeply **interlocked** — never think of them in isolation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the major difference between thermal cracking and catalytic cracking with respect to mechanism and product distribution.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Thermal Cracking | Catalytic Cracking |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Mechanism | Free-radical (homolytic) | Carbocation (heterolytic) |
| Temperature | 450–750 °C | 480–540 °C |
| Pressure | 10–70 bar | 1–3 bar |
| Selectivity | Non-selective, more olefins | Selective to gasoline + iso-paraffins |
| Gasoline RON | 60–70 | 90–95 |

(1 mark mechanism, 1 mark product distribution, 1 mark operating condition contrast.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the four principal reactions occurring in a catalytic reformer and state which one is the major hydrogen-producing reaction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Dehydrogenation of naphthenes to aromatics — **major H₂ producer** (1 mark).
2. Isomerization of n-paraffins to iso-paraffins (0.5 mark).
3. Dehydrocyclization of paraffins to aromatics (0.5 mark).
4. Hydrocracking of paraffins to lighter products (0.5 mark).
5. Identification of the dehydrogenation of naphthenes as the dominant H₂-producing step (0.5 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students commonly **confuse "thermal cracking" with "steam cracking"**. Steam cracking is for olefins (ethylene, propylene), thermal cracking is for liquid fuels (gasoline, gas oil).
> 2. Students frequently **omit the catalyst regeneration step** in FCC diagrams — this is a 4-mark loss in any 14-marker asking for a process flow.
> 3. In reforming, students forget the **two functions of the catalyst** (metal = dehydrogenation, acid = isomerization) — this is worth **2 marks** in a typical question.
> 4. Always state **units with numbers** in derivations — the KTU 2024 scheme awards marks for **dimensional awareness**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 style]*

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **process flow of a Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) unit**. State the function of each major equipment and explain why the regenerator is essential.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare thermal cracking and catalytic cracking under the following heads: (i) operating temperature and pressure, (ii) mechanism, (iii) catalyst used, (iv) gasoline yield and octane number, (v) main products, (vi) coke formation, and (vii) any one specific industrial process.

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Process flow of FCC unit (7 marks):**

1. **Feed preheater (1 mark):** Preheated VGO is introduced at the bottom of the riser.
2. **Riser reactor (1.5 marks):** VGO + hot regenerated catalyst flow co-currently upward. Endothermic cracking occurs; residence time 1–4 s. Riser outlet T = 510–540 °C.
3. **Disengager + cyclones (0.5 mark):** Vapour products are separated from spent catalyst.
4. **Stripper (0.5 mark):** Steam stripping removes entrained hydrocarbons from spent catalyst.
5. **Main fractionator (1 mark):** Cracked vapours are separated into dry gas, LPG, gasoline, LCO, HCO, and slurry.
6. **Regenerator (2 marks):** Spent catalyst (with ≈ 0.6–1.2 wt % coke) is fluidized with air. Coke is burned to CO₂, releasing ≈ 3.5–4.5 MJ per kg catalyst. This heat is carried back by the regenerated catalyst to the riser.
7. **Catalyst circulation loop (0.5 mark):** Maintained by the pressure balance between regenerator (higher) and riser (lower), typically with a slide valve or standpipe.

**Why regenerator is essential:**
- The catalyst loses activity as coke deposits on its acidic sites (1 mark).
- Without regeneration, conversion would drop within minutes (1 mark).

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):** Present the table from **Section 3.3** (Analytical Comparison Matrix) — that single table covers all seven sub-parts (i) to (vii) for **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **process and reactions in a catalytic reformer**. With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the Continuous Catalyst Regeneration (CCR) concept and state its advantages over semi-regenerative reforming.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **effect of operating pressure on equilibrium conversion** of naphthene to aromatic in catalytic reforming. Use the result to justify why modern reformers operate at the lowest possible pressure (≈ 3.5 bar).

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Process and reactions in catalytic reformer (7 marks):**

1. **Feed preparation (1 mark):** Naphtha is hydrotreated to < 0.5 ppm S and < 0.5 ppm N to prevent Pt poisoning.
2. **Process flow (1.5 marks):** Hydrotreated naphtha + recycle H₂ is heated in a furnace, then passed through 3 (SR) or 4 (CCR) adiabatic reactors in series. The reaction is endothermic, so inter-stage furnaces reheat the stream.
3. **Four reactions (2.5 marks):** State the four reactions from Section 2.3 (1 mark for listing, 1.5 marks for explaining that **Reaction 1** — dehydrogenation of naphthenes — dominates H₂ production and is endothermic and favored by low pressure).
4. **CCR concept (1 mark):** Spent catalyst is continuously withdrawn from the last reactor, lifted to the regenerator, regenerated in chlorine-controlled air, and returned to the top reactor.
5. **Advantages of CCR (1 mark):**
   - Lower operating pressure (3.5 bar) possible → higher aromatic yield.
   - Higher reformate RON (≈ 100–102).
   - No unit shutdown for regeneration → higher on-stream factor (> 95 %).
   - Higher H₂ production.

**(b) Derivation of pressure effect (7 marks):**

1. Write the reaction: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12} \rightleftharpoons \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 3\,\text{H}_2$ (0.5 mark).
2. Define $K_p$ in terms of partial pressures (1 mark).
3. Express $p_i$ in terms of extent of reaction $\xi$ and $P_0$ (1.5 marks).
4. Substitute to obtain:
$$ K_p = \dfrac{27 \xi^4}{(1-\xi)(1+3\xi)^3} \cdot P_0^3 $$
(2 marks)
5. Rearrange to obtain conversion as a function of $P_0$:
$$ \xi^4 = \dfrac{K_p}{27 P_0^3} \cdot (1-\xi)(1+3\xi)^3 $$
(1 mark)
6. **Justification (1 mark):** Since $K_p$ is constant at a given T, decreasing $P_0$ increases the required $\xi$ (conversion). Hence reformers operate at the **lowest pressure** practically feasible (≈ 3.5 bar) to maximize aromatics and H₂ yield.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cracking** = breaking large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller ones via heat (thermal) or catalyst (catalytic).
- **Thermal cracking** = **free radical** mechanism, 450–750 °C, **no catalyst**, tolerates dirty feed (residues), produces olefins + gasoline.
- **Catalytic cracking (FCC)** = **carbocation** mechanism, 480–540 °C, **zeolite catalyst**, near-atmospheric pressure, riser + regenerator, gasoline RON 90–95, propylene as petrochemical co-product.
- **Catalytic reforming** = **bifunctional Pt catalyst**, converts low-octane naphtha to high-octane reformate (RON 95–102), produces **refinery hydrogen** as by-product.
- **Regenerator** in FCC is the "heart" — it burns coke and supplies heat to the riser.
- **CCR** in reforming is what enables **low-pressure operation** (≈ 3.5 bar) and hence high aromatic yield.
- **Modern trend:** thermal cracking is largely replaced by FCC + delayed coker combinations; reforming is the H₂ engine of the integrated refinery.
- **Energy considerations:** FCC is largely **autothermal** (coke combustion); thermal cracking and reforming are **fired-heater based**.
- **Critical numbers to remember for KTU:**
  - Thermal cracking T = **450–750 °C**, P = **10–70 bar**
  - FCC T = **480–540 °C**, P = **1–3 bar**, C/O = **5–8**
  - Reforming T = **450–525 °C**, P = **3.5–35 bar**, RON gain ≈ **40–60 units**
  - H₂ from reforming ≈ **150–300 Nm³/m³ feed**
  - Coke on FCC catalyst: **0.6–1.2 wt %** (regenerated to < 0.05 wt %)
  - Activation energy, thermal ≈ **200–280 kJ/mol**, catalytic ≈ **80–140 kJ/mol**
- **Equilibrium rule for reforming:** High T, low P → more aromatics + more H₂.
- **Order of severity** (T, residence time): thermal > reforming > FCC (in terms of severity of the conditions).
- **Order of selectivity** to gasoline (octane): reforming > FCC > thermal.
- **Two loops of FCC:** catalyst loop (riser → stripper → regenerator → riser) and oil loop (feed → riser → fractionator → products).
- **Always** mention **units** in numerical answers and **always** include a **diagram** in 14-mark questions — KTU 2024 examiners reward structured presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydro Cracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydro Cracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrocracking** is a high-pressure, catalytic petroleum refining process that combines two fundamental chemical operations — **catalytic cracking** (breaking large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller ones) and **hydrogenation** (adding hydrogen to saturate the broken fragments) — in the presence of a **bifunctional catalyst** and excess molecular hydrogen (H₂). The net effect is the conversion of heavy, low-value feedstocks (vacuum gas oil, deasphalted oil, coker gas oil, and sometimes residues) into high-value, saturated, low-sulfur middle distillates such as **diesel, jet fuel (ATF), and naphtha**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hydrocracking belongs to the family of **secondary conversion processes** in a refinery. It is classified as a **hydrogen-addition process**, fundamentally distinct from catalytic cracking (FCC), which is a **carbon-rejection process**.

The process operates in the pressure range of **80 bar to 200 bar** and temperatures between **300 °C and 450 °C**, employing hydrogen partial pressures in the range of **70 bar to 175 bar**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Surgical Dismantling" Metaphor

Imagine you have a **giant LEGO castle** built from dark-colored blocks (representing a heavy hydrocarbon molecule with lots of C–C bonds and aromatic rings). Two things are wrong with this castle:
1. It is **too big and heavy** to be useful (like vacuum gas oil).
2. It has **unstable joints** that would corrode or break easily (sulfur, nitrogen, olefins, aromatics).

**Hydrocracking is like a master craftsman who:**

- **Carefully dismantles the castle** into smaller, more useful pieces (the *cracking* part — driven by the acidic sites of the catalyst).
- **Simultaneously welds reinforcement rods (hydrogen atoms)** at every broken joint to make the new pieces **strong, stable, and saturated** (the *hydrogenation* part — driven by the metallic sites of the catalyst).

The end result is a neat collection of **small, clean, stable, premium-grade blocks** — perfect gasoline, jet fuel, and diesel molecules.

## 1.3 Why Hydrocracking is Crucial in a Modern Refinery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Refinery Drivers for Hydrocracking:**
> 1. **Fuel-quality mandates** — Euro-VI / BS-VI diesel requires **< 10 ppm sulfur** and very high cetane number. Only deep hydroprocessing can achieve this.
> 2. **Yield optimization** — Maximizes middle distillate yield (diesel + jet) which has higher margin than fuel oil.
> 3. **Feedstock flexibility** — Can process difficult feeds (VGO, DAO, coker GO) that FCC cannot handle cleanly.
> 4. **Zero olefins, zero resid** — Products are 100 % saturated, eliminating gum formation and instability.

## 1.4 Governing Physical & Process Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor Pressure | 80 – 200 | **bar** |
| Reactor Temperature | 300 – 450 | **°C** |
| H₂ Partial Pressure | 70 – 175 | **bar** |
| H₂ / Oil Ratio (chemical) | 1,000 – 2,500 | **Nm³/m³** |
| LHSV | 0.5 – 2.0 | **hr⁻¹** |
| Conversion per Pass | 40 – 80 | **% vol** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Hydrocracking — The "Dual-Function" Mechanism

Hydrocracking catalysts are **bifunctional**, meaning they have two distinct active sites operating synergistically:

### A. Acidic Function (Solid Acid — e.g., amorphous silica-alumina or zeolite Y)
- Performs **C–C bond scission** via classical **carbenium-ion (carbocation) chemistry**.
- The acid site donates a proton (H⁺) to an olefin or aromatic, generating a carbocation.
- Beta-scission of the carbocation breaks the long carbon chain.
- Skeletal isomerization also occurs on these sites, improving the **cold-flow properties** of diesel.

### B. Metallic Function (Noble / Sulfided Base Metal — e.g., Ni-Mo, Ni-W, Pt, Pd on acidic support)
- Performs **hydrogenation** and **hydrogenolysis**.
- Activates H₂ into reactive surface H-atoms.
- Saturates olefins immediately after they form, preventing coke deposition.
- Removes heteroatoms (S, N, O) as H₂S, NH₃, and H₂O.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Synergy Principle:** The metal sites must be close enough to the acid sites so that olefins produced on acid sites are **hydrogenated before they polymerize into coke**. This is why catalyst manufacturers carefully balance metal loading and acid strength.

## 2.2 Net Reaction Stoichiometry

A simplified representation for a generic heavy hydrocarbon feed $C_nH_m$ with sulfur and nitrogen heteroatoms:

$$C_nH_m S_x N_y + (a + x + 1.5y)\, H_2 \longrightarrow \text{Products}\,(C_pH_q) + x\, H_2S + y\, NH_3$$

Where:
- $n, p$ are the carbon numbers of feed and product ($p < n$).
- $m, q$ are the hydrogen numbers.
- $a$ is the moles of H₂ consumed purely for **saturation and cracking**.

The H₂ consumption typically ranges from **200 to 400 Nm³ H₂ per m³ of feed** depending on feed quality and conversion severity.

## 2.3 Process Flow Variants

### (a) Single-Stage Once-Through
- One reactor, one separator.
- Total liquid product goes to fractionation.
- Lower capital cost; **unconverted oil (UCO)** is blended or sold as FO.

### (b) Single-Stage with Recycle
- UCO from the fractionator bottom is recycled back to the reactor.
- Achieves **100 % conversion** of feed; maximizes middle distillate yield.

### (c) Two-Stage (Series Flow)
- **Stage 1** removes S and N (severe hydrotreating).
- **Stage 2** performs the actual cracking over a more acidic, zeolite-based catalyst.
- Used for **maximum diesel selectivity** and **highest product quality**.

## 2.4 Critical Process Variables (Instrumentation Context)

| Variable | Effect on Process | Sensor / Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactor Temperature** | Higher T → ↑ conversion but ↑ coking | Multiple RTDs, TC in catalyst beds |
| **H₂ Partial Pressure** | Higher P → ↑ saturation, ↓ coke | Pressure transmitters, dp cells |
| **LHSV** | Lower LHSV → ↑ conversion, ↑ residence time | Feed flow transmitter (coriolis) |
