| **H₂ / Oil Ratio** | Higher ratio → ↑ catalyst life | Gas flow + oil flow meters |
| **Recycle / Make-up H₂** | Controls H₂ partial pressure | Compressor surge control |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **must-know** for the KTU examination. Master the symbols and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{feed}}}{V_{\text{cat}}}$ | Liquid Hourly Space Velocity | hr⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{m_{\text{feed}}}{m_{\text{cat}} \cdot t}$ | Weight Hourly Space Velocity | hr⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\text{Conversion} = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed}} - V_{\text{UCO}}}{V_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % Conversion of 343 °C⁺ fraction | % |
| 4 | $\text{H}_2\text{/Oil} = \dfrac{Q_{H_2,\,Nm^3/h}}{Q_{\text{oil},\,m^3/h}}$ | Hydrogen-to-oil volumetric ratio | Nm³/m³ |
| 5 | $\text{Carbon Efficiency} = \dfrac{\sum C_{\text{products}}}{C_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Carbon balance closure check | % |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{cat}} = \dfrac{\text{kg feed cracked}}{\text{kg catalyst lifetime}}$ | Catalyst utilization index | kg/kg |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \approx -50 \text{ to } -150$ | Exothermic heat of reaction per kg feed | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $P_{H_2} = y_{H_2} \cdot P_{\text{total}}$ | Hydrogen partial pressure | bar |
| 9 | $\text{Cetane Improvement} = CN_{\text{product}} - CN_{\text{feed}}$ | Cetane number gain | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Selectivity}_{D} = \dfrac{Y_{\text{diesel}}}{Y_{\text{total liquid}}}$ | Diesel selectivity | % |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrocracking units are the **most expensive single unit** in a modern refinery (capex: 1000–2000 USD per annual barrel). They are also among the **most instrument-intensive**, with typical units having **1500–3000 I/O points** including:
- **Distributed Control System (DCS)** for temperature profile control across catalyst beds (quench control).
- **Advanced Process Control (APC)** models for yield and conversion optimization.
- **Online analyzers** — NIR for product distillation, sulfur analyzers, density meters.
- **Catalyst protection** — high-pressure letdown valves with anti-coking trim.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU exams, always connect the **chemistry** with the **instrumentation need** — e.g., *exothermicity* → *need for inter-bed quench temperature control*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion & Yield Relationship

A hydrocracker feed has total volume $V_F$. The volume of unconverted oil (UCO, boiling above 343 °C) is $V_U$. The volume of liquid product boiling below 343 °C is $V_L$. The mass balance over the reactor gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_F &= V_U + V_L + V_G
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $V_G$ is the volume of light gases (C1–C4) lost to fuel gas. The volumetric conversion is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{343^+} &= \frac{V_F - V_U}{V_F} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{V_L + V_G}{V_F} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**The liquid yield** (the most economically important metric) is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_L &= \frac{V_L}{V_F} \times 100 \\
&= C_{343^+} - Y_G
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example
A hydrocracker processes **10,000 m³/day** of VGO. The UCO volume is **3,200 m³/day**, and the light gas yield is **5.5 % vol on feed**. Find: (a) Conversion, (b) Liquid yield, (c) Diesel selectivity if diesel volume in product is **4,800 m³/day**.

**Step (a) — Conversion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{343^+} &= \frac{10{,}000 - 3{,}200}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6{,}800}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 68\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Liquid yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_L &= 68 - 5.5 = 62.5\,\% \text{ vol on feed}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Diesel selectivity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_D &= \frac{4{,}800}{6{,}200} \times 100 \\
&= 77.42\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: The denominator in selectivity is the **total liquid product = 6,200 m³/day** (i.e., $10{,}000 - 3{,}200 - 5.5\%\text{ of } 10{,}000 = 6{,}200$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LHSV and Catalyst Volume Relationship

The Liquid Hourly Space Velocity is defined as the reciprocal of residence time $\tau$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHSV} &= \frac{Q_{feed}}{V_{cat}} = \frac{1}{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $Q_{feed}$ is the volumetric feed rate at standard conditions (m³/hr) and $V_{cat}$ is the settled volume of catalyst in the reactor (m³).

**Deriving reactor catalyst volume requirement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cat} &= \frac{Q_{feed}}{\text{LHSV}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example
A feed rate of **400 m³/hr** is processed at LHSV = **1.2 hr⁻¹**. Find catalyst volume and daily feed throughput per m³ of catalyst.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cat} &= \frac{400}{1.2} = 333.33\,m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

In a fixed-bed reactor with catalyst bulk density $\rho_b = 750\,\text{kg/m}^3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{cat} &= V_{cat} \times \rho_b = 333.33 \times 750 = 250{,}000\, \text{kg} = 250\, \text{tons}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion note:** Real industrial reactors achieve LHSV of 0.5–2.0; lower LHSV means **deeper conversion** but **larger, more expensive reactor**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrogen Consumption Calculation

For a feed of density $\rho_F$ and mean molecular formula $C_nH_m$, the stoichiometric H₂ requirement to reach product $C_pH_q$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_2 &= \frac{m_F}{M_F} \cdot \left( \frac{q}{p} - \frac{m}{n} \right) \times \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(kmol H}_2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, engineers use the empirical **H₂ consumption correlation** based on feed API gravity, sulfur, and conversion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{2,\,cons} &= 0.0154 \cdot C_{343^+} + 1.2 \cdot S_{\text{feed}} + 0.05 \cdot N_{\text{feed}} \quad \text{(wt \% on feed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example
A VGO feed has: 65 % vol 343 °C⁺ conversion, 2.0 wt % sulfur, 800 wppm nitrogen. Find H₂ consumption in **Nm³/m³ of feed** (assume feed density = 0.92 g/cm³).

**Step 1 — Calculate wt % H₂ consumed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{2,\,cons} &= (0.0154 \times 65) + (1.2 \times 2.0) + (0.05 \times 0.08) \\
&= 1.001 + 2.4 + 0.004 \\
&= 3.405\, \text{wt \% on feed}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volumetric basis (Nm³/m³):**

Using $1\,\text{kg H}_2$ occupies **11.2 Nm³** at STP, and 1 m³ of feed weighs **920 kg**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{2,\,Nm^3/m^3} &= \frac{3.405}{100} \times 920 \times 11.2 \\
&= 350.86\,\text{Nm}^3\text{H}_2 \text{ per m}^3\text{ feed}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This matches the **typical industrial range of 200–400 Nm³/m³**, validating the correlation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Material Balance Across the Reactor (5-Component Balance)

For an industrial mass balance, the hydrocracker is modeled as **5 lumps**: feed, diesel, kerosene/naphtha, UCO, and light gases.

| Component | In (kg/h) | Out (kg/h) | Conversion Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| VGO Feed | 100,000 | — | → All products |
| Diesel | 0 | 48,000 | Selective from VGO |
| Naphtha + Kero | 0 | 17,000 | From cracking |
| UCO | 0 | 32,000 | Unreacted |
| Light Gases (C1–C4) | 0 | 3,000 | Overcracking loss |
| **Total In** | **100,000** | **100,000** | Mass balance closes ✓ |

### Carbon Efficiency Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_C &= \frac{48{,}000 + 17{,}000 + 32{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 97\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The 3 % loss represents **light gases that leave as off-gas** — this is normal and economically accounted for.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

| Variable ↑ | Conversion | Selectivity (Diesel) | Catalyst Life | H₂ Consumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | ↑ Increases | ↓ Shifts to lighter | ↓ Coke | ↑ Slight |
| **Pressure** | ↑ Mild ↑ | ↑ Improves | ↑ Longer | ↑ Higher |
| **H₂ / Oil** | ↑ Mild ↑ | ↑ Better | ↑ Longer | ↑ Higher |
| **LHSV** (↓) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Heavier products | ↓ Mild ↓ | ↑ Higher |
| **Recycle Ratio** | ↑ Increases | ↑ Tunable | ↓ Mild ↓ | ↑ Higher |

> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off Alert:** Higher conversion always sacrifices **catalyst life** and **H₂ economy**. Modern refineries operate at the **economic optimum** (~60–70 % conversion) rather than maximum conversion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrocracker Unit — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a single-stage, recycle hydrocracking unit, showing all major process and instrumentation blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Surge Drum] --> B[Feed Pump P101]
    B --> C[Feed / Effluent Exchanger E101]
    C --> D[Heater F101]
    H2Source[Make-up H2 Compressor K101] --> Mixer1
    RecComp[Recycle Gas Compressor K102] --> Mixer1
    Mixer1{{Mixer M101}} --> D
    D --> R1[Hydrocracking Reactor R101]
    R1 --> Q1[Reactor Bed 1]
    Q1 --> Q2[Quench H2 Injection]
    Q2 --> Q3[Reactor Bed 2]
    Q3 --> Q4[Reactor Bed 3]
    Q4 --> E1[Hot High Pressure Separator V101]
    E1 --> Gas1[Recycle Gas to K102]
    E1 --> L1[Hot LP Separator V102]
    L1 --> L2[Stripper T101]
    L2 --> Frac[Fractionator T102]
    Frac --> P1[Naphtha Product Tank]
    Frac --> P2[Kero / ATF Product Tank]
    Frac --> P3[Diesel Product Tank]
    Frac --> UCO[Unconverted Oil Recycle or FO]
```

## 4.2 Instrumentation & Control Loop Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **closed-loop control architecture** of a hydrocracker reactor — a critical concept for Refinery Instrumentation students.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ReactorAssembly[Reactor R101 with 3 Catalyst Beds]
        TC1[Temperature TT101 in Bed 1] --> Bed1
        TC2[Temperature TT102 in Bed 2] --> Bed2
        TC3[Temperature TT103 in Bed 3] --> Bed3
    end
    Bed1 --> QuenchNode{{Quench H2 Valve FCV201}}
    QuenchNode --> Bed2
    Bed2 --> QuenchNode2{{Quench H2 Valve FCV202}}
    QuenchNode2 --> Bed3
    DCS[DCS Controller] -->|4-20 mA| QuenchNode
    TC2 -->|Signal| DCS
    TC3 -->|Signal| DCS
    FT[Feed Flow FT101] -->|Signal| DCS
    PT[Reactor Pressure PT101] -->|Signal| DCS
    AT[Sulfur Analyzer AT101 in Product] -->|Signal| DCS
    DCS -->|Display| HMI[Operator HMI / SCADA]
    DCS -->|Trend| Historian[Process Historian]
```

## 4.3 Reaction Network — Bifunctional Catalyst Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Feed[Heavy HC Molecule C20+] --> MetalSite{Metallic Site: H2 Activation}
    MetalSite --> Olef[Olefin Intermediate]
    Olef --> AcidSite{Acidic Site: C-C Scission}
    AcidSite --> Carb[Carbenium Ion]
    Carb --> Frag1[Smaller Fragment A]
    Carb --> Frag2[Smaller Fragment B]
    Frag1 --> MetalSite
    Frag2 --> MetalSite
    MetalSite --> SatA[Saturated Product A]
    MetalSite --> SatB[Saturated Product B]
    MetalSite --> HS[H2S Removal]
    MetalSite --> HN[NH3 Removal]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Note:** Notice the **cyclic nature** — fragments returned to the metal site for hydrogenation prevent coke formation. This is the **key reason** for catalyst life extension.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define hydrocracking. Why is it classified as a "hydrogen addition" process and not a "carbon rejection" process like FCC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Hydrocracking is a catalytic, high-pressure refining process that combines cracking and hydrogenation in the presence of a bifunctional catalyst and excess H₂ to convert heavy hydrocarbons into high-value middle distillates.

**Why hydrogen addition (1 mark):** Hydrocracking **adds hydrogen atoms** to the hydrocarbon fragments produced by cracking, increasing the H/C ratio of the product. The process is **net hydrogen-consuming**.

**Difference from FCC (1 mark):** FCC operates without external hydrogen and **rejects carbon** as coke on the catalyst, reducing the H/C ratio. Hydrocracking uses hydrogen to **saturate and prevent coke**, so the product H/C ratio is *higher* than feed — a true hydrogen-addition process.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is a bifunctional catalyst in hydrocracking? Name the two functions and give an example catalyst for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A bifunctional catalyst (1 mark) possesses two distinct active sites that perform complementary chemical roles:

| Function (1 mark) | Chemical Role | Example (1 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **Acidic Function** | C–C bond scission via carbenium ion mechanism | Amorphous silica-alumina or **Zeolite Y (USY)** |
| **Metallic Function** | Hydrogenation / hydrogenolysis activation | **Ni-Mo / Ni-W sulfides** on acidic support |

The acidic and metallic sites must be in close proximity (typically within nanometers) so that olefins formed on acid sites are immediately hydrogenated on metal sites, preventing polymerization to coke.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the process flow of a single-stage hydrocracker with recycle. Explain the role of each major equipment item. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The single-stage hydrocracker with recycle consists of the following major equipment in series (sketch description + role):

1. **Feed Surge Drum** — provides buffering and steady feed supply; residence time 10–15 min; level control interface.
2. **Feed Pump (centrifugal / reciprocating)** — boosts feed to reactor pressure (~150 bar); flow control loop.
3. **Feed / Effluent Exchanger (E-101)** — recovers heat from hot reactor effluent, preheats cold feed; major energy-recovery device.
4. **Charge Heater (F-101)** — final trim heating to reactor inlet temperature (~330–400 °C); fired heater.
5. **Make-up & Recycle H₂ Compressors (K-101, K-102)** — supply fresh and recycled hydrogen; recycle compressor is **critical** — controls H₂ partial pressure.
6. **Hydrocracking Reactor (R-101)** — fixed-bed, multi-bed vessel containing bifunctional catalyst; inter-bed **quench H₂** controls temperature rise from exothermic reaction.
7. **Hot High-Pressure Separator (V-101)** — separates unreacted H₂ from liquid product at reactor pressure.
8. **Hot Low-Pressure Separator (V-102)** — further separates dissolved gases from liquid.
9. **Stripper (T-101)** — steam-strips H₂S, NH₃, and light ends from the liquid product.
10. **Fractionator (T-102)** — separates product into **naphtha, kerosene (ATF), diesel, and UCO** (recycle or FO blending).

> [!NOTE]
> The **recycle** feature: UCO from fractionator bottom is pumped back and mixed with fresh feed, achieving 100 % conversion of the 343 °C⁺ fraction to distillates.

---

**(b) A hydrocracker unit processes 8,000 m³/day of VGO. The unconverted oil (UCO) volume is 2,400 m³/day. The fractionator yields: naphtha 1,200 m³/day, kerosene 1,600 m³/day, diesel 2,400 m³/day, and light gas yield is 4.5 % vol on feed. Calculate: (i) conversion (ii) total liquid yield (iii) diesel selectivity. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $V_F = 8{,}000\,m^3/\text{day}$
- $V_U = 2{,}400\,m^3/\text{day}$
- $V_{\text{naphtha}} = 1{,}200$, $V_{\text{kero}} = 1{,}600$, $V_{\text{diesel}} = 2{,}400$
- $Y_G = 4.5\,\%$ on feed

**Verification of balance (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{liquid}} &= 1{,}200 + 1{,}600 + 2{,}400 = 5{,}200\,m^3/\text{day} \\
V_G &= 0.045 \times 8{,}000 = 360\,m^3/\text{day} \\
\text{Total out} &= 5{,}200 + 360 + 2{,}400 = 7{,}960 \approx 8{,}000 \quad (\text{check: small rounding})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Conversion (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{343^+} &= \frac{8{,}000 - 2{,}400}{8{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5{,}600}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 70\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total liquid yield (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_L &= 70 - 4.5 = 65.5\,\% \text{ vol on feed} \\
\text{(or)} \quad Y_L &= \frac{5{,}200}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 65\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Diesel selectivity (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_D &= \frac{V_{\text{diesel}}}{V_{\text{liquid product}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2{,}400}{5{,}200} \times 100 \\
&= 46.15\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write $V_L = 8{,}000 - 2{,}400 = 5{,}600$ and forget to subtract gas yield. The correct liquid yield is **5,200 m³/day**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Selectivity denominator must be **total liquid product (5,200)**, not feed (8,000).
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Always state the **basis** of the percentage — *% on feed* vs *% on liquid* vs *% conversion*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the role of the bifunctional catalyst in hydrocracking. Discuss why the balance between acidic and metallic sites is critical for catalyst life. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Bifunctional Catalyst (2 marks):**
A bifunctional hydrocracking catalyst combines two chemically distinct active sites supported on a single pellet:
- **Acidic sites** — typically amorphous silica-alumina or USY zeolite — perform **C–C bond scission** (cracking) via carbenium-ion mechanism.
- **Metallic sites** — typically sulfided Ni-Mo or Ni-W — perform **hydrogenation / dehydrogenation** and **heteroatom removal** (HDS, HDN).

**Why Balance is Critical (5 marks):**

1. **Coke Prevention:** Cracking on acidic sites produces **olefins** as intermediates. If these olefins are not immediately hydrogenated by neighboring metal sites, they **polymerize into coke**, deactivating the catalyst.
2. **Hydrogen Spillover:** Activated H atoms on metal sites "spill over" to acid sites, keeping them clean and proton-rich — prolonging acid-function life.
3. **Feed Diffusivity:** If acid function is too strong (excess zeolite), secondary cracking dominates → **over-cracking** to gases. If metal function dominates (excess metal), only hydrogenation occurs → **insufficient conversion**.
4. **Nitrogen Poisoning:** Strong acid sites adsorb basic nitrogen compounds, blocking active sites. Adequate metal sites (with high HDN activity) convert N to NH₃ first, protecting acid sites.
5. **Sulfur Tolerance:** Modern Ni-W catalysts on Y-zeolite give the **best balance** for diesel-mode hydrocrackers; Pt-Pd on chloride-alumina is preferred for noble-metal applications.

**Industrial Examples:**
- **Maximum middle distillate:** Ni-Mo / USY zeolite
- **Maximum naphtha:** Pt / chlorided alumina

---

**(b) A hydrocracker operates at LHSV = 1.2 hr⁻¹ with a feed rate of 500 m³/hr. The reactor has a settled catalyst bulk density of 780 kg/m³. The H₂ / oil ratio is 1,500 Nm³/m³. Calculate: (i) catalyst volume in reactor (ii) catalyst weight in tons (iii) total H₂ flow in Nm³/hr. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** LHSV = 1.2 hr⁻¹; $Q_F$ = 500 m³/hr; $\rho_b$ = 780 kg/m³; H₂/oil = 1,500 Nm³/m³.

**(i) Catalyst volume (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cat} &= \frac{Q_F}{\text{LHSV}} = \frac{500}{1.2} \\
&= 416.67\,m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Catalyst weight (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{cat} &= V_{cat} \times \rho_b \\
&= 416.67 \times 780 \\
&= 325{,}000\,kg \\
&= 325\, \text{tons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total H₂ flow (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{H_2} &= 1{,}500 \times 500 \\
&= 750{,}000\,Nm^3/hr
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion to kmol/hr (for completeness):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{H_2} &= \frac{750{,}000}{22.414} = 33{,}456\,kmol/hr
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Errors:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse **Nm³/m³** (volumetric H₂ / oil ratio) with **kg/kg** (mass basis). Always quote ratio with units.
> 2. **LHSV** uses **liquid volume at standard (15 °C) conditions** of feed, not at reactor conditions. The reactor operates hot, but LHSV is referenced to cold volume.
> 3. **Bulk density** vs **particle density** — bulk density (with voids) is what engineers use for reactor sizing.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Definition:** Hydrocracking = Cracking + Hydrogenation → saturated middle distillates.
- ✅ **Pressure range:** **80–200 bar** (high pressure distinguishes it from FCC).
- ✅ **Temperature range:** **300–450 °C**.
- ✅ **Catalyst:** **Bifunctional** — Acidic (zeolite) + Metallic (Ni-Mo / Ni-W sulfides).
- ✅ **Feed:** VGO, DAO, Coker GO (NOT atmospheric residue — that needs hydroprocessing).
- ✅ **Products:** Naphtha, Kerosene/ATF, **Diesel** (major), and UCO (recycle or FO).
- ✅ **Key Reactions:** HDS, HDN, HDA (aromatic saturation), and C–C scission.
- ✅ **Process Variants:** Single-stage once-through, Single-stage with recycle, Two-stage series flow.
- ✅ **Instrumentation highlights:** Inter-bed **quench H₂ control**, **reactor temperature profile monitoring**, **H₂ partial pressure control** via recycle compressor, online **NIR / sulfur analyzers**.
- ✅ **Must-memorize formulas:** LHSV = $Q/V_{cat}$, Conversion = $(V_F - V_U)/V_F \times 100$, H₂/Oil ratio, Diesel selectivity.
- ✅ **H₂ consumption** typical: **200–400 Nm³/m³ of feed**.
- ✅ **Sulfur in product:** < 10 ppm (BS-VI / Euro-VI).
- ✅ **Cetane gain:** Hydrocracked diesel has **cetane number 5–15 higher** than straight-run diesel.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always state **units** in your numerical answers. A number without a unit is a guaranteed half-mark loss.
- ✅ **Draw neat PFDs** in the exam — block diagrams with labeled equipment are accepted; you need not draw detailed P&IDs.
- ✅ **Distinguish** between **hydrocracking** (full conversion) and **hydrotreating** (mild, primarily HDS/HDN only).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Chemical Reduction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Chemical Reduction** in the context of refinery operations refers to a class of catalytic hydroprocessing reactions in which petroleum feedstocks are treated with **hydrogen (H₂)** at elevated temperatures and pressures to remove heteroatoms (sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and metals) and to saturate olefinic and aromatic bonds. The dominant industrial embodiment of this principle is **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** and **Hydrotreating**. The generalized stoichiometric representation is: $$\text{R–X} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst, } \Delta, P} \text{R–H} + \text{HX}$$ where **X** denotes a heteroatom-containing functional group (S, N, O, M). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Chemical reduction processes are the cornerstone of modern refinery configurations because they enable compliance with environmental regulations limiting sulfur content in transportation fuels to **<10 ppm** (BS-VI / Euro-VI norms) and **<50 ppm** in marine residual fuels (IMO 2020). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Imagine a piece of jewellery that has tarnished (developed a dark layer of oxide/sulphide compounds). To restore its original lustre, you don't just wipe it — you subject it to a chemical bath (a reducing agent) that **chemically transforms** the tarnish back into the original metal. In an oil refinery, crude oil contains **sulphur, nitrogen, and oxygen** atoms "stitched" into hydrocarbon molecules. Chemical reduction is the refinery's "chemical bath" where **hydrogen gas (the reducing agent)**, under high temperature and pressure, severs these heteroatoms from the hydrocarbons and converts them into gaseous by-products (**H₂S, NH₃, H₂O**) that are easily separated. - The **catalyst** acts like a skilled tailor guiding the reactions. - The **hydrogen** acts like a cleaning agent. - The **reactor** is the workshop where everything happens. - The **instrumentation** ensures temperature, pressure, and flows are precisely maintained. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Even though we call it "reduction", hydrogen gas itself is *oxidized* (its oxidation state increases from **0 to +1** in H₂S or H₂O). What gets reduced is the **carbon** attached to the heteroatom — hence the name "chemical reduction." ## 1.3 Types of Chemical Reduction Processes in Refineries | Process | Primary Objective | Typical Reactor | |---|---|---| | **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** | Remove organic sulfur | Fixed / Trickle Bed | | **Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN)** | Remove organic nitrogen | Fixed / Trickle Bed | | **Hydrodeoxygenation (HDO)** | Remove oxygen (in bio-feeds) | Fixed Bed | | **Hydrodemetallization (HDM)** | Remove Ni, V, Fe | Fixed / Guard Bed | | **Hydrocracking (HC)** | Break C–C bonds with H₂ | Fixed / Ebullated Bed | | **Saturation (olefin/aromatic)** | Stabilize product | Fixed Bed | ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ - **Standard Operating Pressure Range:** $\mathbf{30 - 200 \, bar}$ - **Standard Operating Temperature Range:** $\mathbf{300 - 420 \, ^{\circ}C}$ - **Hydrogen Purity in Recycle Gas:** $\mathbf{>85\% \, vol}$ - **Make-up H₂ Source:** **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** or **Catalytic Partial Oxidation (CPO)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reaction Chemistry of Chemical Reduction ### 2.1.1 Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) Reactions HDS proceeds via two principal pathways — the **direct desulfurization (DDS)** route and the **hydrogenation (HYD)** route. **Thiols (Mercaptans):** $$\text{R–SH} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$$ **Thioethers (Sulfides):** $$\text{R–S–R'} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{R'}–\text{H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$$ **Disulfides:** $$\text{R–S–S–R'} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{R'}–\text{H} + 2\text{H}_2\text{S}$$ **Thiophenes (DDS Pathway):** $$\text{C}_4\text{H}_4\text{S} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_6 + \text{H}_2\text{S} \quad (\text{butadiene intermediate})$$ **Thiophenes (HYD Pathway):** $$\text{C}_4\text{H}_4\text{S} + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$$ **Benzothiophene (BT):** $$\text{C}_8\text{H}_6\text{S} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_8\text{H}_{10} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$$ **Dibenzothiophene (DBT) — Refractory Sulfur:** $$\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_8\text{S} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{10} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \quad (\text{DDS})$$ $$\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_8\text{S} + 5\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{16} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \quad (\text{HYD})$$ > [!NOTE] > **4,6-Dimethyldibenzothiophene (4,6-DMDBT)** is the most refractory sulfur species and dictates deep-HDS catalyst design. ### 2.1.2 Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN) $$\text{R–NH}_2 + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{NH}_3$$ $$\text{Pyridine} + 5\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{Piperidine} \rightarrow \text{Pentane} + \text{NH}_3$$ ### 2.1.3 Olefin and Aromatic Saturation $$\text{R–CH=CH–R'} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–R'}$$ $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}$$ ## 2.2 Why and How — Mechanistic Insights **Step 1 — Adsorption:** The sulfur-containing molecule adsorbs onto an **anion vacancy** of the Co(Ni)-Mo(W)S₂ catalyst via the S-atom (σ-bond). **Step 2 — Hydrogenation:** Adjacent **SH groups** (formed from H₂ dissociation on promoter sites) attack the C–S bond via either **DDS** (direct cleavage) or **HYD** (ring saturation first). **Step 3 — Hydrogenolysis:** The C–S bond breaks, producing a hydrocarbon fragment and H₂S. **Step 4 — Desorption:** H₂S desorbs from the active site, freeing it for the next cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Catalyst Architecture — "Rim-Edge" Model:** Active sites exist at the **rim** (top/bottom edges) and **edge** (side planes) of MoS₂ crystallites. Promoters like **Co and Ni** decorate these edges, creating the dual-function **Co(Ni)–Mo–S** phase that delivers industrial-grade HDS activity. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Equation | Mathematical Form | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{n_{A,0} - n_A}{n_{A,0}}$ | dimensionless | $n_{A,0}$ is inlet sulfur moles | | 2 | **HDS Rate Law (Power Law)** | $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n \cdot C_{H_2}^m$ | $\text{mol} \cdot \text{L}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | Typical $n = 1.5 - 2$ | | 3 | **Arrhenius Equation** | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | $E_a \approx 80 - 150 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ for HDS | | 4 | **Space Velocity (LHSV)** | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed}}}{V_{\text{reactor}}}$ | $\text{h}^{-1}$ | Liquid Hourly Space Velocity | | 5 | **H₂/Oil Ratio** | $\text{HOR} = \dfrac{Q_{H_2}}{Q_{\text{oil}}}$ | $\text{Nm}^3/\text{m}^3$ | Typical: 100–1000 | | 6 | **Sulfur Removal Efficiency** | $\eta_S = \dfrac{S_{\text{in}} - S_{\text{out}}}{S_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | % | Used in KTU numericals | | 7 | **H₂ Consumption** | $\Delta H_2 = \alpha S_{\text{removed}} + \beta N_{\text{removed}} + \gamma O_{\text{removed}}$ | $\text{Nm}^3/\text{m}^3$ | Empirical; $\alpha \approx 8.5$ | | 8 | **Equilibrium Constant** | $K = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right)$ | dimensionless | Governs feasibility | | 9 | **Catalyst Deactivation (Time)** | $k(t) = k_0 \cdot \exp(-\alpha t)$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | $\alpha$ = deactivation constant | | 10 | **Pressure Drop (Ergun)** | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3 d_p^2} v_s + \dfrac{1.75 \rho (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3 d_p} v_s^2$ | $\text{Pa/m}$ | Used for fixed-bed design | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 numericals, the most frequently asked calculations are **(i) Sulfur Removal Efficiency, (ii) H₂ Consumption, and (iii) Arrhenius-based rate constant determination.** Master these three. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Environmental Compliance:** Produces **ultra-low-sulfur diesel (ULSD)** with <10 ppm S. - **Catalyst Protection:** Downstream **FCC and reformer catalysts** are poisoned by S and N — HDS protects them. - **Product Quality:** Improves **cetane number, color stability, and oxidation stability** of diesel. - **Refinery Integration:** Provides **H₂S-rich off-gas** as feedstock to **Claus plants** for sulfur recovery. - **Feedstock Flexibility:** Allows processing of **opportunity crudes** with high TAN, high metals. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sulfur Removal Efficiency from Outlet Composition > **Problem Context:** A naphtha feed containing **2000 ppm S** is processed in an HDS reactor. The product stream is analyzed and shows **40 ppm S**. Compute the sulfur removal efficiency. ### Step 1 — Identify the Given Data $$S_{\text{in}} = 2000 \, \text{ppm}, \quad S_{\text{out}} = 40 \, \text{ppm}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Definition of Removal Efficiency By definition, sulfur removal efficiency is the fractional reduction in sulfur content: $$\eta_S = \dfrac{S_{\text{in}} - S_{\text{out}}}{S_{\text{in}}}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values $$\eta_S = \dfrac{2000 - 40}{2000}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Numerator and Denominator $$\eta_S = \dfrac{1960}{2000}$$ ### Step 5 — Final Result $$\eta_S = 0.98 = 98\%$$ > **Interpretation:** The HDS unit is removing 98% of the inlet sulfur, achieving the BS-VI specification requirement. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Determination of H₂ Consumption from Empirical Correlation > **Problem Context:** A diesel HDS unit processes **100 m³/h** of feed. Sulfur content drops from **12000 ppm** to **50 ppm**. Compute the chemical H₂ consumption using $\alpha = 8.5 \, \text{Nm}^3 \, \text{H}_2 / \text{m}^3 \, \text{feed per wt\% S}$. ### Step 1 — Compute the Sulfur Removed (in wt%) $$\Delta S = S_{\text{in}} - S_{\text{out}} = 12000 - 50 = 11950 \, \text{ppm} = 1.195 \, \text{wt\%}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Empirical H₂ Consumption Formula $$\Delta H_2 = \alpha \cdot \Delta S$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the Values $$\Delta H_2 = 8.5 \times 1.195$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate the Product $$\Delta H_2 = 10.1575 \, \text{Nm}^3 \, \text{H}_2 / \text{m}^3 \, \text{feed}$$ ### Step 5 — Total Hourly H₂ Consumption $$Q_{H_2} = \Delta H_2 \times Q_{\text{feed}} = 10.1575 \times 100 = 1015.75 \, \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$$ > **Final Answer:** The HDS unit consumes approximately **1015.75 Nm³/h of hydrogen** for the desulfurization reaction alone. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Activation Energy from Rate Data > **Problem Context:** A pilot HDS reactor gives the following rate constants at two temperatures: At $T_1 = 340^{\circ}\text{C} = 613 \, \text{K}$, $k_1 = 0.0045 \, \text{s}^{-1}$. At $T_2 = 380^{\circ}\text{C} = 653 \, \text{K}$, $k_2 = 0.0148 \, \text{s}^{-1}$. Compute the activation energy $E_a$. ### Step 1 — Apply the Two-Point Arrhenius Form Taking the natural logarithm of the Arrhenius equation at two temperatures and subtracting: $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Ratio $k_2 / k_1$ $$\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \dfrac{0.0148}{0.0045} = 3.2889$$ ### Step 3 — Take the Natural Logarithm $$\ln(3.2889) = 1.1908$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Reciprocal Temperature Difference $$\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} = \dfrac{1}{653} - \dfrac{1}{613} = 0.001531 - 0.001631 = -1.000 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for $E_a$ $$E_a = -R \cdot \dfrac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$ $$E_a = -8.314 \times \dfrac{1.1908}{-1.000 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$E_a = \dfrac{8.314 \times 1.1908}{1.000 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$E_a = 9.901 \times 10^{4} \, \text{J/mol} \approx 99.0 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ > **Validation:** This value lies within the typical HDS activation energy range of **80–150 kJ/mol**, confirming consistency with literature. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reactor Performance vs Operating Variables | Variable | Effect on HDS Conversion | Effect on H₂ Consumption | Effect on Catalyst Life | |---|---|---|---| | ↑ Temperature | ↑ (up to optimum) | ↑ (cracking side reactions) | ↓ (coking, sintering) | | ↑ Pressure | ↑↑ (H₂ partial pressure) | ↑ | ↑ (suppresses coke) | | ↑ LHSV | ↓ (less contact time) | ↓ | ↑ (mild operation) | | ↑ H₂/Oil Ratio | ↑ | ↑ (recycle cost) | ↑ (suppresses H₂S) | | ↑ Feed Sulfur | ↑ conversion needed | ↑ | ↓ (sulfur poisoning) | | ↑ H₂S Partial Pressure | ↓ (inhibition) | — | ↓ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state the trade-off explicitly — e.g., "Higher pressure improves conversion but increases capital cost of thick-wall reactors." Examiners reward trade-off awareness. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hydrodesulfurization Process with Instrumentation ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Feedstock Storage
Crude / Naphtha / Diesel] --> B[Feed Pump P-101
Centrifugal Pump] B --> C[Feed Preheater E-101
Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger] C --> D[Feed Surge Drum V-101
with Level Transmitter LT-101] D --> E[Reactor Feed Effluent Exchanger E-102] E --> F[Reactor Charge Heater H-101
Fired Heater with TT-101] F --> G[HDS Reactor R-101
Fixed Bed Trickle Reactor] H[H₂ Compressor C-101
with Vibration Monitor] --> G I[Make-up H₂ Header
with FT-101 Flow Transmitter] --> H G --> J[Reactor Effluent Air Cooler AC-101] J --> K[HP Separator V-102
with LT-102 and PT-102] K --> L[LP Separator V-103
with LT-103 and PT-103] L --> M[Recycle Gas Scrubber V-104
Amine Contactor] M --> N[Recycle Gas Compressor C-102] N --> G L --> O[Stripper Column T-101
with TT-102 and PT-104] O --> P[Product Receiver V-105
with Analyzer AT-101] P --> Q[Online Sulfur Analyzer
XRF or UV Fluorescence] P --> R[Finished Product Storage] K --> S[Sour Water Drum V-106] M --> T[H₂S Absorber
Amine System] T --> U[Claus Plant SRU
Sulfur Recovery Unit] G -.TT-103 Bed Temp 1.- G G -.TT-104 Bed Temp 2.- G G -.TT-105 Bed Temp 3.- G classDef reactor fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px classDef instrument fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px classDef vessel fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px classDef analyzer fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px class G reactor class F,K,L instrument class A,D,J,M,O,R,S,T,U vessel class H,P,Q analyzer ``` ## 4.2 Reactor Internal Catalyst Bed Configuration (Quench Zone Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet Distributor
Vapor Liquid Mixing] --> B[Top Bed
Guard Catalyst] B --> C[Quench Interbed

Cold H₂ Injection] E --> F[Middle Bed 2

High Activity HDS] H --> I[Outlet Collector
with PT and TT] J[Temperature Sensor TT-A] -.monitors.- B K[Temperature Sensor TT-B] -.monitors.- D L[Temperature Sensor TT-C] -.monitors.- F M[Temperature Sensor TT-D] -.monitors.- H classDef catalyst fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef quench fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px classDef instrument fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:1px class B,D,F,H catalyst class C,E,G quench class J,K,L,M instrument ``` ## 4.3 Control Loop Architecture for HDS Reactor ```mermaid flowchart TB SP[Setpoint
340 degree C] --> SUM[Comparator / Summer] PV[Process Variable
Reactor Bed Temperature TT] --> TT[Transmitter] TT --> SUM SUM --> CTRL[Temperature Controller TIC-101
PID Algorithm] CTRL --> CV[Control Valve
on Cold H₂ Quench Line] CV --> REACTOR[Reactor Bed Temperature
Maintained at SP] REACTOR -.feedback.- PV classDef ctrl fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef valve fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px classDef sensor fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px class SUM,CTRL ctrl class CV valve class TT,PV,REACTOR sensor ``` > [!NOTE] > **Instrumentation Insight (KTU Focus):** The reactor bed typically has **3 to 5 temperature measurement points** along its height. A rise of more than **10–15°C** across a bed indicates **hotspot formation** and is the primary early-warning signal of catalyst deactivation, channeling, or run-away exothermic reactions. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define Chemical Reduction in the context of refinery operations. List any four major hydroprocessing reactions covered under it.* **Model Answer:** Chemical reduction in refinery operations refers to the catalytic treatment of petroleum feedstocks with **hydrogen (H₂)** at elevated temperatures and pressures to remove heteroatoms and saturate unsaturated bonds. The four major reactions are: 1. **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):** R–S + H₂ → R–H + H₂S 2. **Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN):** R–N + H₂ → R–H + NH₃ 3. **Hydrodeoxygenation (HDO):** R–O + H₂ → R–H + H₂O 4. **Aromatic/Olefin Saturation:** R=CH + H₂ → R–CH₂ > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"chemical reduction"** with **"catalytic cracking"** (which is a thermal decomposition without H₂). Remember: chemical reduction **always requires hydrogen addition**. --- ### Question A.2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the functions of **Cobalt (Co)** and **Nickel (Ni)** as promoters in HDS catalysts.* **Model Answer:** Co and Ni act as **electronic and structural promoters** on the **MoS₂ (or WS₂)** active phase. Their functions are: - They decorate the **edge planes** of MoS₂ crystallites, forming the **Co(Ni)–Mo–S active phase**. - They **donate electrons** to Mo, weakening the Mo–S bond and creating more **anion vacancies** (CUS — Coordinatively Unsaturated Sites) for sulfur adsorption. - **Co–Mo** systems are preferred for **HDS**, while **Ni–Mo** systems offer superior **HDN and aromatic saturation** activity. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question B.1 **or** Question B.2. --- ### Question B.1 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Refinery Instrumentation PEICT631]** **(a)** With the help of a neat labelled process flow diagram, describe the **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) process** for diesel feed. Explain the role of each major equipment item. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **operating variables** that affect HDS performance, with emphasis on temperature, pressure, LHSV, and H₂/oil ratio. Show the trade-offs in tabular form. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to B.1(a) The HDS process for diesel comprises the following sequence: **Step 1 — Feed Pre-treatment:** Diesel feed from storage is pressurized by the charge pump (**P-101**), mixed with **hydrogen-rich recycle gas** and **make-up H₂**, and passed through a **feed preheater (E-101)**. **Step 2 — Heat-up:** The mixed stream enters the **reactor charge heater (H-101)**, a fired heater that raises temperature to the **reaction initiation range of 320–360°C**. **Step 3 — Reaction:** The hot stream enters the **HDS Reactor (R-101)**, a **fixed-bed trickle-flow reactor** packed with **Co-Mo/Al₂O₃ or Ni-Mo/Al₂O₃** catalyst. The major reactions are: - R–S + H₂ → R–H + H₂S - R–N + H₂ → R–H + NH₃ **Step 4 — Effluent Cooling:** Reactor effluent passes through a **feed-effluent heat exchanger (E-102)** to recover energy and then through an **air cooler (AC-101)**. **Step 5 — Separation:** The two-phase effluent enters the **HP Separator (V-102)** where **unreacted H₂ and H₂S** are flashed overhead. Liquid goes to the **LP Separator (V-103)**. **Step 6 — Gas Treatment:** Recycle H₂ is sent to the **amine scrubber (V-104)** to remove H₂S, then recycled via the **recycle compressor (C-102)**. **Step 7 — Product Stripping:** The desulfurized diesel enters the **product stripper (T-101)** to remove dissolved H₂S, and the bottom product is sent to storage after passing through an **online sulfur analyzer (AT-101)**. #### Model Solution to B.1(b) | Variable | Effect on Conversion | Effect on H₂ Consumption | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | **Temperature** | Increases up to ~400°C | Increases | Too high → coking, cracking | | **Pressure** | Strongly increases | Increases | High capex on thick-wall reactor | | **LHSV** | Decreases (lower contact time) | Decreases | Low LHSV → bigger reactor volume | | **H₂/Oil Ratio** | Increases | Increases | High ratio → compressor cost | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that **high H₂S partial pressure inhibits HDS reaction** (product inhibition). Always state this in trade-off tables. --- ### Question B.2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Refinery Instrumentation PEICT631]** **(a)** Derive the **Arrhenius equation** in the form used to compute activation energy from two sets of $(T, k)$ data. Use the result to determine $E_a$ given: $k_1 = 0.005 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 600 \, \text{K}$ and $k_2 = 0.020 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 650 \, \text{K}$. Take $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** List and explain the **major analytical instruments** used to monitor the performance of an HDS unit. Indicate the typical range and measurement principle. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to B.2(a) **Step 1 — Start with the Arrhenius equation in single-point form:** $$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Take natural logarithm on both sides:** $$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$$ **Step 3 — Write the same equation at temperature $T_1$ and $T_2$:** $$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R T_1}$$ $$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R T_2}$$ **Step 4 — Subtract the first from the second to eliminate $\ln A$:** $$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\dfrac{E_a}{R T_2} + \dfrac{E_a}{R T_1}$$ $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $E_a$:** $$E_a = R \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Compute the ratio $k_2/k_1$:** $$\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \dfrac{0.020}{0.005} = 4.0$$ **Step 7 — Take the natural logarithm:** $$\ln(4.0) = 1.3863$$ **Step 8 — Compute the temperature term:** $$\dfrac{T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} = \dfrac{600 \times 650}{650 - 600} = \dfrac{390000}{50} = 7800 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 9 — Final computation:** $$E_a = 8.314 \times 1.3863 \times 7800$$ $$E_a = 89890 \, \text{J/mol} \approx 89.9 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ > **Result:** The activation energy is **≈ 89.9 kJ/mol**, well within the typical HDS range. #### Model Solution to B.2(b) | Instrument | Measurement Principle | Typical Range | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **X-ray Fluorescence (XRF) Sulfur Analyzer** | Excitation of S atoms by X-ray; measurement of fluorescent X-rays | 0.5 ppm – 5 wt% S | Online product sulfur in diesel | | **UV Fluorescence Analyzer** | Combustion → SO₂ → excited SO₂* → fluorescence | 0.1 ppm – 3000 ppm | Trace sulfur in naphtha, ULSD | | **Gas Chromatograph (GC-SCD/FPD)** | Separation + S-selective detector (SCD = Sulfur Chemiluminescence, FPD = Flame Photometric) | ppb to wt% | Speciation of sulfur compounds | | **NDIR H₂S Analyzer** | Infrared absorption at specific wavelength by H₂S | 0 – 500 ppm | Recycle gas H₂S monitoring | | **Thermal Conductivity (TCD) H₂ Analyzer** | Difference in thermal conductivity of gas mixture | 0 – 100% H₂ | H₂ purity in recycle gas | | **pH/Conductivity Analyzer** | Conductivity of scrubbed water | 0 – 14 pH | Amine solution quality | | **Reactor Thermocouples (Type K)** | Seebeck effect | 0 – 1370°C | Bed temperature mapping | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Warning:** Students often write "GC" without specifying the detector (TCD, FID, SCD, FPD). Always specify the **detector type** — examiners explicitly look for it. Marks are lost for vague answers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Chemical Reduction = Catalytic Hydroprocessing with H₂** to remove S, N, O, metals, and saturate olefins/aromatics. - ✅ **HDS is the most industrially dominant** chemical reduction process; target is **<10 ppm S** for BS-VI diesel. - ✅ **Catalyst system:** **Co(Ni)–Mo(W)/Al₂O₃** with the active phase being **Co(Ni)–Mo–S**. - ✅ **Two reaction pathways for thiophenes:** **DDS (Direct Desulfurization)** and **HYD (Hydrogenation)**. - ✅ **4,6-DMDBT** is the **most refractory** sulfur compound and controls deep-HDS catalyst design. - ✅ **Operating window:** T = **300–420°C**, P = **30–200 bar**, LHSV = **0.5–4 h⁻¹**, H₂/Oil = **100–1000 Nm³/m³**. - ✅ **Hydrogen consumption** depends on heteroatom removal, olefin/aromatic saturation, and cracking severity. - ✅ **H₂S inhibits HDS** (product inhibition) — high H₂S partial pressure reduces conversion. - ✅ **Reactor instrumentation** includes multiple bed thermocouples, pressure transmitters, differential pressure gauges, and online sulfur analyzers. - ✅ **Arrhenius activation energy** for HDS lies in the range of **80–150 kJ/mol**. - ✅ **Excess H₂** is recovered in HP/LP separators, scrubbed in an **amine system**, and **recycled** to the reactor. - ✅ **Sulfur balance:** All sulfur removed in HDS ultimately ends up as **H₂S**, which is routed to the **Claus Plant** for elemental sulfur recovery. - ✅ **Hot-spot detection** in the catalyst bed is a **primary KTU instrumentation question** — remember: 3 to 5 thermocouples along bed height, ΔT > 10–15°C indicates deactivation/runaway. - ✅ **XRF** and **UV Fluorescence** are the two principal **online sulfur analyzers** in HDS product streams. - ✅ **Pressure drop (Ergun equation)** is monitored using **differential pressure transmitters (dPT)** across the catalyst bed — increasing ΔP indicates fouling/coking.

topics.title: Polymerisation, Alkylation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polymerisation, Alkylation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymerisation in Refinery Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymerisation (Refinery Definition):** A catalytic conversion process in which light olefinic hydrocarbons (typically C3–C4 mono-olefins such as propylene, n-butenes, and isobutylene) are chemically joined (linked) to form larger hydrocarbon molecules (dimers, trimers, oligomers) in the gasoline boiling range, producing high-octane gasoline blending stock called **Polymer Gasoline** or **Polymerate**.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you have many small LEGO blocks (the monomers — propylene, butylene). Individually, they are too small and too volatile to be useful as fuel. Polymerisation is the process of snapping these blocks together to make longer, heavier chains (dimers, trimers, tetramers). The resulting larger chain has better fuel properties — higher octane number, lower vapour pressure, and better stability — making it an excellent additive for aviation and motor gasoline.

**In a refinery, polymerisation is essentially "upgrading" light, low-value gases into premium gasoline.**

### Key Refinery Feedstocks & Products

| Stream Type | Components | Role |
|-------------|------------|------|
| **Feed** | Propylene, 1-Butene, 2-Butene, Isobutylene | Light olefins (monomers) |
| **Catalyst** | **Solid Phosphoric Acid (SPA)** on kieselguhr | Drives polymerisation |
| **Product** | C6, C7, C8, C9+ iso-olefins | Gasoline blending component |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Refinery polymerisation reactors typically operate at temperatures between **190 °C and 230 °C** and pressures between **30 atm and 60 atm** to maintain the catalyst in a stable liquid-phase/wet condition.

---

## 1.2 Alkylation in Refinery Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alkylation (Refinery Definition):** A catalytic refining process in which iso-paraffins (primarily **isobutane**, i-C4) are chemically combined with light olefins (propylene, 1-butene, 2-butene, isobutylene) in the presence of a strong acid catalyst to form a branched-chain paraffinic hydrocarbon called **Alkylate**, which is a premium, high-octane, low-sensitivity gasoline blending component.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of alkylation as a precise "molecular marriage" where a saturated hydrocarbon (isobutane — the "groom" with no double bonds) is joined to an unsaturated hydrocarbon (an olefin — the "bride" with a double bond). The acid catalyst (H₂SO₄ or HF) acts as a matchmaker, arranging this union to produce a stable, branched saturated product. The resulting "offspring" (alkylate) is non-reactive, high-octane, and ideal for modern spark-ignition engines.

### Key Refinery Feedstocks & Products

| Stream Type | Components | Role |
|-------------|------------|------|
| **Feed (Paraffin)** | Isobutane (i-C4H10) | Saturated backbone |
| **Feed (Olefin)** | C3, C4 olefins | Unsaturated reactant |
| **Catalyst** | **H₂SO₄ (98–99 %)** or **HF (anhydrous)** | Acid catalyst |
| **Product** | C7–C9 branched paraffins (TMP, DMH) | Premium alkylate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics:** For H₂SO₄ alkylation, reactor temperature is maintained at **4–10 °C**; for HF alkylation, it is **25–40 °C**. The critical **Isobutane-to-Olefin (I/O) molar ratio** is held between **8:1 and 15:1** to suppress undesired polymerisation side reactions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerisation — Process Chemistry & Variables

The principal chemical reaction in a refinery catalytic polymerisation unit (using SPA) is:

$$\text{n} \cdot \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_{3n}\text{H}_{6n} \quad (n = 2, 3, 4 \dots)$$

### Step-by-Step Process Logic

- **Step 1 — Feed Preparation:** Cracked gases (FCC off-gas, coker off-gas) are fractionated to isolate a **C3–C4 olefin-rich stream**. Trace impurities like **H₂S, H₂O, and nitrogen bases** must be removed because they poison the SPA catalyst.
- **Step 2 — Pre-heat & Mix:** The dried olefinic feed is mixed with recycle and pre-heated to **180–200 °C** in a feed-effluent heat exchanger (FEHE).
- **Step 3 — Catalytic Reaction:** The feed passes downward through a fixed bed of SPA catalyst pellets. Exothermic heat of reaction is removed by inter-bed cooling (cold olefin injection) or by reactor wall cooling.
- **Step 4 — Product Separation:** Reactor effluent is depressurised, stabilised (de-ethaniser), and debutanised to yield a **C7+ polymer gasoline** of RON ≈ **92–96**.
- **Step 5 — Catalyst Regeneration:** Carbonaceous deposits gradually deactivate the SPA. Hot air regeneration at **350–400 °C** is performed periodically to restore catalyst activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Polymerisation is exothermic:** Forming a new C–C σ-bond releases energy. Removing this heat is the *single most important* instrumentation task in the unit, since temperature excursions above **240 °C** permanently damage the SPA catalyst and cause runaway polymerisation.

### Key Instrumentation Parameters (Polymerisation)

| Variable | Typical Range | Sensor Type | Control Loop Type |
|----------|---------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Reactor Inlet Temperature** | 180–220 °C | RTD / Thermocouple (Type K) | Cascade to fuel gas |
| **Reactor Bed Temperature** | 190–230 °C | Multiple T/Cs at different levels | High-high trip |
| **Reactor Pressure** | 30–60 atm (≈ 30–60 bar) | Bourdon / Strain gauge DP | Pressure controller |
| **Olefin Feed Flow** | 50–500 m³/h | Orifice / Coriolis | Flow controller |
| **Catalyst Bed ΔP** | 0.3–0.8 bar | Differential Pressure (DP) cell | Plugging alarm |
| **Water Wash pH** | 7.0–9.0 | pH analyser | Caustic dosing |

---

## 2.2 Alkylation — Process Chemistry & Variables

The principal net reaction for the alkylation of isobutane with 2-butene is:

$$\text{i-C}_4\text{H}_{10} + \text{C}_4\text{H}_8 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} \;(\text{branched trimethylpentanes})$$

### Step-by-Step Process Logic

- **Step 1 — Feed Drying & Cooling:** Both isobutane and olefin feeds are dried over molecular sieves and cooled. **H₂O above 50 ppm poisons both H₂SO₄ and HF catalysts** and must be rigorously removed.
- **Step 2 — Acid–Hydrocarbon Contact:** The two immiscible liquids are brought into intimate contact in a **contactor reactor** (Stratco horizontal contactor for H₂SO₄; Phillips/UOP riser–reactor for HF). High shear mixing is critical to disperse acid droplets into the hydrocarbon phase.
- **Step 3 — Reaction Control:** Reaction temperature is held extremely tight (± 1 °C). Exothermic heat (~**88 kJ/mol for propene alkylation, ~70 kJ/mol for butene alkylation**) is removed by:
  - H₂SO₄ plant: **Evaporative cooling** of the acid (vacuum flash) or jacket cooling.
  - HF plant: **Tube-side water cooling** with HF on shell side (HF holds less heat).
- **Step 4 — Acid Settling & Recycle:** The acid/hydrocarbon emulsion is sent to a **settler** (acid settler). Acid is decanted, cooled, and recycled. Hydrocarbon goes to the fractionation section.
- **Step 5 — Fractionation:** The de-isobutaniser (DIB) column recovers unreacted isobutane (recycled back) and the butane column separates n-butane (LPG product). The bottom is **alkylate product** (RON ≈ 92–96, MON ≈ 90–94, very low RVP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Alkylation is highly exothermic & corrosive:** The strong acid catalyst (H₂SO₄ or HF) protonates the olefin to form a carbocation intermediate, which then alkylates the isobutane. This ionic mechanism releases substantial heat and the acid environment demands exotic materials of construction (Monel 400, Karbate, Teflon-lined piping).

### Key Instrumentation Parameters (Alkylation)

| Variable | Typical Range | Sensor Type | Why Critical |
|----------|---------------|-------------|--------------|
| **Reactor Temperature** | 4–10 °C (H₂SO₄), 25–40 °C (HF) | RTD with thermowell | Runaway & acid loss |
| **I/O Molar Ratio** | 8:1 to 15:1 | Inferential (flow ratios) | Quality & acid life |
| **Acid Strength** | 90–99 wt % | Conductivity / Density | Catalyst activity |
| **Acid Level in Settler** | 50–70 % of vessel | DP level transmitter | Phase separation |
| **HF Neutraliser pH** | 8–10 | pH + conductivity | **HF leak detection** |
| **Water Spray (HF unit)** | On-demand | Solenoid + flame detector | **Emergency acid mitigation** |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{F_{\text{olefin}}}{F_{\text{isobutane}}}$ | Isobutane-to-Olefin volumetric/molar ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{removed}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{LM}}$ | Heat duty via cooling coil (LMTD method) | kW |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{\text{LM}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K |
| 4 | $P_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \cdot P_{\text{MAWP}}$ | ASME pressure vessel design margin | bar |
| 5 | $T_{\text{set,HH}} = T_{\text{normal}} + 0.5 \cdot (T_{\text{max safe}} - T_{\text{normal}})$ | High-High trip setting (industry rule) | °C |
| 6 | $r_{\text{A}} = k \cdot C_{\text{acid}}^{\alpha} \cdot C_{\text{olefin}}^{\beta} \cdot C_{\text{iC4}}^{\gamma}$ | Alkylation rate law (simplified) | mol/L·s |
| 7 | $\text{RON}_{\text{alkylate}} = 92 + 0.6 \cdot (I/O - 8)$ | Empirical RON vs I/O ratio (linear approx) | — |
| 8 | $m_{\text{acid, lost}} = k_d \cdot v_{\text{feed}} \cdot \rho_{\text{acid}}$ | Acid consumption rate (per pass) | kg/h |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{C7+ out}}}{F_{\text{olefin in}}}$ | Polymerisation conversion efficiency | % |
| 10 | $K_p = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial t}\bigg\vert_{\rho, T}$ | Pressure rise rate (used in relief sizing) | bar/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The LMTD formula and the cascade control configuration derived from the heat removal equation are the foundational tools for designing and tuning **reactor temperature control loops** in both polymerisation and alkylation units.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Required Cooling Duty for a Polymerisation Reactor

Consider a fixed-bed catalytic polymerisation reactor processing **F** kmol/h of propylene feed at conversion **X**, with heat of polymerisation $\Delta H_{rxn} = -100 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (per mole of C=C reacted).

### Step 1 — Define the molar flow of reacted olefin

The rate at which olefin is consumed is given by:

$$n_{\text{reacted}} = F \cdot X$$

where $F$ is in kmol/h and $X$ is fractional conversion (e.g., 0.85).

### Step 2 — Compute the total heat released per hour

The exothermic heat released in one hour is:

$$Q_{\text{released}} = n_{\text{reacted}} \cdot \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert = F \cdot X \cdot 100 \, \text{kJ/mol} \cdot 1000 \text{ mol/kmol}$$

which simplifies to:

$$Q_{\text{released}} = F \cdot X \cdot 10^5 \text{ kJ/h}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to kW (since 1 kW = 3600 kJ/h)

$$Q_{\text{kW}} = \dfrac{F \cdot X \cdot 10^5}{3600} = 27.78 \cdot F \cdot X \;\;\text{kW}$$

### Step 4 — Equate to cooling duty via LMTD method

Using $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{LM}}$, solve for required exchanger area:

$$A = \dfrac{Q_{\text{kW}} \cdot 1000}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{LM}}}$$

where $U$ is overall heat-transfer coefficient in $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and $\Delta T_{\text{LM}}$ is in K.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The factor 27.78 (= 100,000 / 3600) is the standard conversion from kmol/h of reacted olefin into kW of released heat. Students must remember that the heat of polymerisation is a **molar** quantity and must be multiplied by the *number of moles reacted per hour*, not by mass flow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — I/O Ratio in Alkylation from Stream Compositions

Given the combined reactor feed contains:
- $y_i$ = mole fraction of isobutane in the feed
- $y_o$ = mole fraction of olefin in the feed
- $F_T$ = total molar flow (kmol/h)

The I/O ratio is defined as:

$$R_{I/O} = \dfrac{n_{\text{iC4}}}{n_{\text{olefin}}} = \dfrac{F_T \cdot y_i}{F_T \cdot y_o} = \dfrac{y_i}{y_o}$$

If a plant operator wishes to *raise* $R_{I/O}$ from 8 to 12 by adding pure isobutane recycle only, the *additional* isobutane needed is:

$$F_{\text{add}} = F_T \cdot (R_{\text{new}} \cdot y_o - y_i)$$

Substituting $F_T = 100 \text{ kmol/h}, y_i = 0.5, y_o = 0.0625$ (so that current ratio = 8):

$$F_{\text{add}} = 100 \cdot (12 \cdot 0.0625 - 0.5) = 100 \cdot (0.75 - 0.5) = 25 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Result:** The operator must add 25 kmol/h of pure isobutane recycle to shift the operating ratio.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Critical Control Loops Comparison

| Loop Tag | Process Variable | Sensor | Final Control Element | Failure Mode | Action |
|----------|------------------|--------|----------------------|--------------|--------|
| **TIC-101** | Polymer reactor bed T | Multi-point T/C | Reactor FW valve | T > 240 °C | Open steam, vent O₂ |
| **PIC-201** | Alkylation reactor P | Bourdon | Reflux valve | P > 12 bar | Open PSV, dump acid |
| **FICA-301** | Olef. / iC4 ratio | Orifice pair | iC4 recirc. valve | I/O < 5 | Close olefin block valve |
| **LIC-401** | Acid settler level | DP cell | Acid transfer pump | Interface inversion | ESD-1 isolation |
| **AQ-501** | HF acid neutraliser pH | pH probe | Caustic dosing | pH < 7 | Water spray + ESD-1 |
| **TT-HH-601** | HF sight glass T | Infrared | Water deluge | T rise > 5 °C | Activate acid mitigation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** In KTU examination answers, *always* draw the loop block diagram (sensor → controller → I/P → control valve → process) and *explicitly* name the failure mode. Marks are awarded for completeness of the loop, not just naming the variables.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Polymerisation Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Olefin Feed Drum] --> A2[Feed Pre-heater FEHE]
    A2 --> A3[Reactor Inlet Mixer]
    A3 --> A4[SPA Catalyst Bed R-101]
    A4 --> A5[Interbed Quench Injection]
    A5 --> A6[Reactor Effluent Cooler]
    A6 --> A7[De-ethaniser Tower]
    A7 --> A8[Debutaniser Tower]
    A8 --> A9[Polymerate Storage]
    A4 --> A10[Catalyst Regeneration Loop]
    A10 --> A11[Air Blower + Heater]
    A11 --> A4
    A1 --> A12[Caustic Wash Tower]
    A12 --> A1
    A1 --> A13[DCS Control Cabinet]
    A13 --> A4
    A13 --> A7
    A13 --> A8
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — H₂SO₄ Alkylation Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Olefin Feed Drum] --> B2[Olefin Drier]
    B3[Isobutane Feed Drum] --> B4[Isobutane Drier]
    B2 --> B5[Contactor Reactor R-201]
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6[Acid Settler V-202]
    B6 --> B7[Acid Cooler E-203]
    B7 --> B5
    B6 --> B8[De-isobutaniser DIB]
    B8 --> B9[n-Butane Column]
    B9 --> B10[Alkylate Product Tank]
    B6 --> B11[Spent Acid Bleed]
    B11 --> B12[Acid Regeneration Unit]
    B12 --> B7
    B5 --> B13[DCS Alarm and Trip System]
    B13 --> B5
    B13 --> B6
    B6 --> B14[Safety Instrumented System SIS]
    B14 --> B5
```

## 4.3 Cascade Control Architecture for Alkylation Reactor Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Master Setpoint T_reactor] --> CTRL1[Master T Controller TIC-100]
    CTRL1 --> CTRL2[Slave T Controller TIC-101 Cooling Water]
    CTRL2 --> I1[I/P Converter]
    I1 --> V1[Cooling Water Control Valve]
    V1 --> PROC[Alkylation Reactor]
    PROC --> TT1[Reactor T Sensor]
    TT1 --> CTRL1
    PROC --> TT2[Outlet T Sensor]
    TT2 --> CTRL2
    CTRL1 --> ALARM[HH Trip ESD-1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The above cascade arrangement is the *industry-standard* configuration for exothermic alkylation reactors. The master controller (slow) sets the setpoint of the slave controller (fast) which manipulates cooling water flow. This rejects disturbances **before** they reach the reactor temperature itself.

## 4.4 SIS Architecture for HF Alkylation (SIL-3 Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[HF Leak Detector LEL] --> LOGIC[SIS Logic Solver SIL-3]
    D2[Reactor High Pressure PT] --> LOGIC
    D3[Reactor High Temperature TT] --> LOGIC
    D4[Acid Sight Glass Level LT] --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> A1[Emergency Block Valve XV-901]
    LOGIC --> A2[Water Deluge Valve XV-902]
    LOGIC --> A3[HF Vent Scrubber On]
    LOGIC --> ALARM[Operator Annunciation Panel]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** HF alkylation units must meet **IEC 61508 SIL-3** for the acid mitigation system. The logic solver is triple-redundant (2oo3 voting), certified for high-demand mode operation, and physically separate from the basic DCS.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define polymerisation in the context of a refinery. Name the catalyst used in catalytic polymerisation and state the typical operating temperature range.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Polymerisation is a catalytic process in which light olefins (C3–C4) are linked together to form larger hydrocarbon molecules (oligomers) that boil in the gasoline range. The catalyst used is **Solid Phosphoric Acid (SPA)** on kieselguhr support. The reactor typically operates in the range of **190–230 °C** and 30–60 atm. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for catalyst, 1 mark for T range.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the role of the I/O ratio in alkylation? Why must it be maintained high?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **I/O (Isobutane-to-Olefin) ratio** is the molar ratio of isobutane to olefin in the reactor feed. It is maintained high (**8:1 to 15:1**) to (i) suppress olefin–olefin polymerisation side reactions, (ii) improve alkylate octane, and (iii) extend catalyst acid life by reducing acid-soluble oil (ASO) formation. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons.)*

---

## **Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **catalytic polymerisation unit** using SPA catalyst. Explain the role of inter-bed quenching.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain **any four critical instrumentation loops** required for safe operation of the polymerisation reactor. Mention the sensor, the final control element, and the alarm/trip setpoint for each.
**(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Process Description (7 marks)

1. **Feed preparation (1 mark):** Light olefinic C3–C4 stream from FCC/coker is amine-treated for H₂S removal and molecular-sieve dried.
2. **Heating & mixing (1 mark):** Feed mixed with recycle and pre-heated to ~190 °C in a feed–effluent heat exchanger.
3. **Reactor (2 marks):** Vertical downflow fixed-bed reactor packed with SPA pellets. Reaction:
   $$n \cdot \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_{3n}\text{H}_{6n} + \text{heat}$$
4. **Inter-bed quenching (2 marks):** Since the reaction is highly exothermic, the bed is divided into multiple catalyst sections with **cold olefin injection between beds** to remove inter-stage heat. This keeps each bed within the **200–225 °C** safe window, preventing hot spots and SPA dehydration.
5. **Product recovery (1 mark):** Effluent is cooled, depressurised, de-ethanised, and debutanised to yield C7+ polymerate (RON ~93).

#### Part (b) — Four Critical Loops (7 marks, ≈ 1.75 each)

| Loop | Variable | Sensor | FCV | Trip / Alarm |
|------|----------|--------|-----|--------------|
| 1 | **Reactor T** | Type K T/C (multipoint) | Reactor FW coil valve | T > 240 °C → HH trip |
| 2 | **Reactor P** | Bourdon pressure transmitter | Effluent block valve | P > 65 bar → HH trip |
| 3 | **Bed ΔP** | DP cell across bed | — | ΔP > 1.0 bar → plug alarm |
| 4 | **Olefin feed F** | Orifice flowmeter | Feed control valve | F < min → ESD-1 |
| 5 | **Water wash pH** | pH analyser | Caustic dosing valve | pH < 6 → corrosive alarm |

*(Any 4 of the 5 above = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **H₂SO₄ alkylation process** with a flow diagram. Compare its operating conditions with the HF alkylation process.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **safety instrumentation system (SIS) architecture** for an HF alkylation unit. Why is it classified as SIL-3? List any two specific HF detectors used.
**(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — H₂SO₄ Alkylation vs HF (7 marks)

1. **Process flow (4 marks):** Isobutane + olefin → contactor (Stratco) → acid settler → acid recycle/cool → de-isobutaniser → n-butane column → alkylate. Spent acid is bled to regeneration.
2. **Reaction (1 mark):** $\text{i-C}_4\text{H}_{10} + \text{C}_4\text{H}_8 \rightarrow \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$ (TMP, DMH).
3. **Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | H₂SO₄ | HF |
|-----------|-------|-----|
| Temperature | 4–10 °C | 25–40 °C |
| Acid strength | 90–99 % | 85–95 % |
| I/O ratio | 8–10:1 | 12–15:1 |
| Acid regeneration | Required (spent → 90 %) | Not required (re-distilled) |
| Hazard | Corrosive (low toxicity) | **Highly toxic, low boiling (19 °C)** |

#### Part (b) — HF SIS Architecture (7 marks)

1. **Hazard justification (2 marks):** HF boils at **19.5 °C**, forms dense ground-hugging aerosols on release, is highly toxic (IDLH = 30 ppm), and reacts violently with water only in concentrated pools. Hence **SIL-3** is mandated by IEC 61511.
2. **SIS architecture (3 marks):** Triple-redundant 2oo3 logic solver; dedicated sensors (T, P, level, LEL HF); automatic activation of water spray, block valves, vent scrubber.
3. **Two HF detectors (2 marks):** 
   - **Electrochemical HF sensor** (point type)
   - **Tunable Diode Laser Absorption Spectroscopy (TDLAS)** (open-path)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse catalytic polymerisation with polymerisation of plastics.** The KTU paper is about *refinery* polymerisation of light olefins to gasoline — not polyethylene or polypropylene. Writing about Ziegler–Natta catalysts will fetch **zero marks**.
> 2. **Always quote numerical ranges** (T = 4–10 °C for H₂SO₄ alkylation, RON ≈ 92–96, I/O = 8:1–15:1). Examiners reward specific numbers.
> 3. **Never omit the failure mode** when describing an instrument loop. "Reactor T is measured by RTD" alone is 1 mark; adding "with HH trip at 240 °C activating ESD-1" adds another 1 mark.
> 4. **For HF alkylation questions, always mention the water deluge system** — it is a frequent 2-mark sub-part and students lose marks by forgetting it.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Polymerisation** joins C3–C4 olefins using **SPA catalyst** at **190–230 °C / 30–60 atm** to make C7+ polymer gasoline (RON ≈ 93).
- **Alkylation** joins isobutane with C3–C4 olefins using **H₂SO₄ (4–10 °C)** or **HF (25–40 °C)** to make branched C7–C9 alkylate (RON ≈ 94–96, MON ≈ 92).
- The **I/O ratio (8:1–15:1)** is the single most important manipulated variable in alkylation; low ratios cause polymerisation side reactions and acid deactivation.
- **Temperature control** is the *primary* safety concern in both processes — use **cascade control** (master T → slave cooling medium) and **HH trips** with **ESD-1**.
- **HF alkylation** demands a dedicated **SIL-3 SIS** with redundant sensors, water deluge, block valves, and a **vent scrubber (KOH neutralisation)**.
- **Catalyst poisoning** agents: water (>50 ppm) and basic nitrogen compounds (poison both H₂SO₄ and HF). Feeds must be **molecular-sieve dried** and **amine-washed**.
- **Materials of construction:** Carbon steel is NOT acceptable in HF service — use **Monel 400**, **Karbate**, or **Teflon-lined** piping and vessels.
- **Key loop tags to memorise:** TIC (temperature), PIC (pressure), FFC/FIC (flow), LIC (level), AIC (analyser like pH), and **ESD-1** (emergency shutdown level 1).
- **Standard design margin** per ASME: $P_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \cdot P_{\text{MAWP}}$ and $T_{\text{design}} = T_{\text{max}} + 25\,°\text{C}$ (min).

> **End of Module 2 — Polymerisation, Alkylation Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isomerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Isomerisation # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Isomerisation** is a catalytic refinery process that converts low-octane straight-chain hydrocarbons (*n*-paraffins: n-C₄, n-C₅, n-C₆) into their higher-octane branched-chain isomers (*iso*-paraffins: i-C₄, i-C₅, i-C₆) by rearranging the carbon skeleton **without altering the molecular weight or the empirical formula** of the feedstock. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for the Refinery Instrumentation course, isomerisation is classified under **secondary upgrading processes** whose primary objective is the **octane enhancement** of light straight-run naphtha (LSRN) fractions, prior to their blending into the gasoline pool. The general skeletal rearrangement can be represented as: $$ n\text{-paraffin} \; \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst, } \Delta H, \, P} \; \text{iso-paraffin} $$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Furniture Rearrangement" > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like rearranging furniture in a room.** > You have the **same chairs, tables, and desks** (same number of carbon and hydrogen atoms). You are only changing **how they are connected and positioned** (the molecular skeleton). A row of identical chairs (n-butane) becomes an L-shaped cluster (iso-butane). The room's contents stay the same, but the **function** changes — just like in a refinery, the molecular inventory stays constant but the **fuel quality (octane number)** improves dramatically. A student-friendly mental model: | Inventory (Same) | Geometry (Changed) | Output Quality | |------------------|--------------------|----------------| | 4 carbons, 10 hydrogens | Straight chain → Branched | Octane 50 RON → 100 RON | | Same atoms | Re-arranged | Higher knock resistance | --- ## 1.3 Why Isomerisation is Essential — The Refinery Context Modern gasoline specifications (BIS-VI / Euro-VI equivalent) impose **strict octane ceilings** with simultaneous **sulphur and olefin limits**. Isomerisation helps refiners in three engineering-critical ways: 1. **Octane Boosting:** Typical research octane number (RON) uplift is from baseline **55–70 RON** to product **82–93 RON** depending on feed carbon number. 2. **Sulphur-Free:** Unlike catalytic reforming, isomerisation **does not require hydrogen** for many once-through units, though modern recycle units do. 3. **Feedstock Flexibility:** Converts the cheapest, lowest-value light naphtha cuts (n-C₅ / n-C₆) into premium gasoline blendstock. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Constants Used in KTU Problems:** > - **Typical reactor inlet temperature:** 120 °C to 280 °C > - **Reactor pressure range:** 1.5 MPa to 7.0 MPa > - **Hydrogen-to-hydrocarbon molar ratio:** 0.5 : 1 to 4 : 1 > - **Weight hourly space velocity (WHSV):** 1 h⁻¹ to 4 h⁻¹ > - **Standard catalyst:** Platinum on chlorided alumina (Pt/Al₂O₃-Cl) > - **Typical conversion per pass (n-C₅):** 50 % to 75 % --- ## 1.4 Categorisation of Isomerisation Processes (KTU Syllabus Map) For the PEICT631 course, isomerisation processes are broadly classified by: - **Feed carbon number:** C₄ isomerisation (butamer) and C₅/C₆ isomerisation (penex, CKS, TIP / Zeolite-based) - **Reactor type:** Fixed-bed catalytic reactor - **Hydrogen management:** Once-through (non-recycle) vs Recycle (with H₂ recycle loop) - **Catalyst chemistry:** Chloride-promoted Pt/Al₂O₃ vs Zeolite (e.g., H-mordenite) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Chemistry of Isomerisation The reaction is a **skeletal (structural) isomerisation** of paraffin hydrocarbons, catalysed by a bifunctional catalyst that provides both **metallic (dehydrogenation–hydrogenation)** and **acidic (skeletal rearrangement)** sites. ### 2.1.1 Reaction Pathway (Bifunctional Mechanism) Step 1 — **Dehydrogenation** over the metallic site (Pt): $$ n\text{-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{Pt}]{\text{}}\; n\text{-alkene} + \text{H}_2 $$ Step 2 — **Protonation & skeletal rearrangement** over the acidic site (chlorided alumina or zeolite): $$ n\text{-alkene} + \text{H}^+ \;\longrightarrow\; \text{iso-alkyl carbenium ion} $$ Step 3 — **De-protonation / re-hydrogenation** to yield the iso-paraffin: $$ \text{iso-alkyl carbenium ion} + \text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{iso-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + \text{H}^+ $$ The overall **net heat of reaction** is mildly exothermic, releasing approximately: $$ \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \approx -4 \text{ to } -8 \text{ kJ/mol} \; (\text{for C}_5 \text{–C}_6 \text{ isomerisation}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation of the 'Why':** Because the reaction is only **mildly exothermic**, reactor temperature control does **not** require aggressive heat removal — a **modest inter-stage cooling** between catalyst beds in stacked-reactor designs (e.g., UOP Penex) is sufficient. --- ## 2.2 Octane Number — The Key Performance Metric The entire economic and engineering purpose of isomerisation is **octane enhancement**. Research Octane Number (RON) values for typical n- and iso-paraffins are tabulated below. ### Table 2.1 — RON Map of n-Paraffins vs iso-Paraffins | Carbon # | Normal (n-) Form RON | Iso (i-) Form RON | RON Uplift | |----------|----------------------|-------------------|------------| | C₄ (Butane) | 95 | 100 (iso-butane) | +5 | | C₅ (Pentane) | 62 | 92 (iso-pentane) | +30 | | C₆ (Hexane) | 25 | 73 (2,2-dimethylbutane) | +48 | | C₇ (Heptane) | 0 | 42 (one branch) | +42 | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **C₆ → 2,2-dimethylbutane** conversion is the most rewarding single transformation in the entire refinery — yielding a RON uplift of nearly **+48 points**. This is why modern C₅/C₆ isomerisation units are economically dominant. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > All formulae below are framed for direct substitution in KTU 2024 scheme numerical problems.
