Holds negative charge closer to nucleus"] node1 -->|"Decreasing acidity"| node2 node2 -->|"Decreasing acidity"| node3 node3 --> node4 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define acetylene. Write its bond angle and hybridization. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** Acetylene is the simplest alkyne with molecular formula **C₂H₂** and structure **H−C≡C−H**. It is also called **ethyne** in IUPAC nomenclature. Each carbon atom is **sp-hybridized**, forming: - One **σ-bond** with the other carbon (sp–sp overlap) - One **σ-bond** with hydrogen (sp–s overlap) - Two **π-bonds** between the carbons (p–p lateral overlap) The molecule is **linear**, with a **bond angle of 180°** and a C≡C bond length of **120 pm**. The triple bond consists of one σ + two π bonds, with total bond energy of **965 kJ/mol**. --- ### **Q2. Why is acetylene acidic while ethane is not? [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Model Answer:** Acetylene's terminal hydrogen is acidic because the carbon is **sp-hybridized (50% s-character)**, which concentrates bonding electrons close to the carbon nucleus, making the C−H bond highly polarized. The resulting carbanion (HC≡C⁻) is stabilized by **50% s-character**, which holds the negative charge near the nucleus. In ethane, carbon is **sp³-hybridized (25% s-character)**, the C−H bond is much less polarized, and the carbanion (C₂H₅⁻) is poorly stabilized. Experimentally: $$\text{p}K_a(\text{HC}{\equiv}\text{CH}) \approx 25 \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{p}K_a(\text{C}_2\text{H}_6) \approx 50$$ This 25-unit difference corresponds to **10²⁵** times stronger acidity for acetylene, allowing it to react with strong bases like **NaNH₂** to form sodium acetylide. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Mechanism steps MUST be shown with curved-arrow notation** — students who write only reagents without mechanism lose 4–5 marks. > 2. **Naming the catalyst is mandatory** — writing "H₂O addition" without specifying "HgSO₄/H₂SO₄ (Kucherov)" loses 2 marks. > 3. **Distinguish vinyl chloride from vinylidene chloride** — examiners specifically test if students identify *mono-addition* vs *double-addition* products. > 4. **Always write the industrial application** of the derivative — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes Outcome-Based Education (CO mapping). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a) Explain the Kucherov reaction. Discuss its mechanism and the industrial significance of the product. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** The Kucherov reaction (Kucherov's hydration) is the **acid-catalyzed hydration of alkynes** in the presence of **mercuric sulfate (HgSO₄)** and **dilute sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** to form **carbonyl compounds**. **Reaction:** $$\text{HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{HgSO}_4 / \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$$ **Mechanism (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1 — Activation of triple bond:** Hg²⁺ coordinates with π-electrons, polarizing the C≡C bond. **Step 2 — Nucleophilic attack by water:** Water attacks the more substituted carbon, forming an **enol intermediate** (vinyl alcohol). **Step 3 — Proton transfer:** Loss of H⁺ regenerates the catalyst and yields the enol. **Step 4 — Tautomerization:** The unstable enol (CH₂=CH−OH) rearranges to the stable keto form (CH₃−CHO). $$\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHOH} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} \quad \Delta G < 0$$ **Industrial Significance:** - Acetaldehyde is oxidized to **acetic acid**, a key solvent in refinery operations. - Acetic acid is the precursor for **vinyl acetate**, **cellulose acetate**, and **terephthalic acid**. - Historically, the Wacker process and Kucherov reaction together revolutionized acetaldehyde production in the early 20th century. **(b) Describe the preparation, properties, and applications of vinyl chloride from acetylene. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Preparation:** $$\text{HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} + \text{HCl} \xrightarrow{\text{HgCl}_2 / \text{Activated Carbon}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHCl}$$ This is a **Markovnikov addition** — HCl adds such that H goes to the CH carbon already bearing H, and Cl attaches to the other carbon, yielding vinyl chloride. **Mechanism (Summary):** HgCl₂ activates the triple bond; Cl⁻ attacks; proton transfer yields CH₂=CHCl. **Properties:** - Boiling point: **−13.4 °C** (colorless gas at room temperature) - Sweet odor; **highly flammable** (flash point −78 °C) - Slightly soluble in water; miscible with most organic solvents - Polymerizes readily in presence of light/heat/peroxides **Applications:** - **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)** manufacture — pipes, valves, cable insulation, instrument housings. - **Copolymer production** — with vinyl acetate, acrylonitrile, etc. - **Refinery application:** PVC-lined pipes and fittings for corrosive service (HCl, H₂SO₄ streams). - Used as a refrigerant (rare due to toxicity). $$\text{n CH}_2{=}\text{CHCl} \xrightarrow{\text{Radical initiator, } \Delta} [-{\text{CH}_2}{-}\text{CHCl}-]_n \text{ PVC}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a) Discuss the acidity of acetylene. How does it form metal acetylides? Mention two analytical applications. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Acidity of Acetylene:** The terminal C−H bond of acetylene is acidic because the carbon is **sp-hybridized (50% s-character)**. The high s-character holds the bonding electrons close to the carbon nucleus, polarizing the C−H bond. The conjugate base (HC≡C⁻) is stabilized because its negative charge resides in an sp orbital with **50% penetration to the nucleus**. **Comparison:** | Hydrocarbon | Hybridization | s-character | pKₐ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Acetylene | sp | 50% | **25** | | Ethylene | sp² | 33% | 44 | | Ethane | sp³ | 25% | 50 | **Formation of Metal Acetylides:** Acetylene reacts with **strong bases** to form acetylides: $$\text{HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} + \text{NaNH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{HC}{\equiv}\text{C}^{-}\text{Na}^{+} + \text{NH}_3$$ $$\text{HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} + 2 \, [\text{Ag}(\text{NH}_3)_2]\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{AgC}{\equiv}\text{CAg} \downarrow + 4\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Analytical Applications:** 1. **Qualitative detection of terminal alkynes:** Formation of a **white precipitate** of AgC≡CAg (silver acetylide) with Tollens' reagent confirms the presence of a terminal ≡C−H group. *(Caveat: Silver acetylide is explosive when dry — must be destroyed with dilute HNO₃.)* 2. **Calcium carbide test:** Generation of acetylene (detected by odor and burning) when CaC₂ reacts with water confirms the presence of moisture in soils, solvents, and gases — used in **refinery moisture analysis**. **(b) Write notes on: (i) Polymerization of acetylene to benzene, and (ii) Polymerization to polyacetylene. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Trimerization to Benzene (Berthelot's Synthesis):** $$\boxed{3 \text{ HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} \xrightarrow{\text{Red-hot iron tube, 500°C}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \text{ (Benzene)}}$$ This was the **first laboratory synthesis of benzene** by Marcellin Berthelot (1866). The mechanism proceeds through sequential Diels–Alder-type cycloadditions, forming the thermodynamically stable aromatic ring. $$\text{HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} + \text{HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}{-}\text{C}{\equiv}\text{CH} \text{ (vinylacetylene, Nieuwland)}$$ $$\text{Vinylacetylene} + \text{HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6$$ **Industrial Relevance:** Today, benzene is produced mainly by catalytic reforming and cracking, but the Berthelot reaction remains a textbook demonstration of alkyne cyclization. **(ii) Polymerization to Polyacetylene:** $$\boxed{\text{n HC}{\equiv}\text{CH} \xrightarrow{\text{Ziegler-Natta catalyst}} [-{\text{CH}}{=}\text{CH}-]_n \text{ (Polyacetylene)}}$$ Polyacetylene, first polymerized by Shirakawa, Heeger, and MacDiarmid (Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 2000), is a **conjugated polymer** with alternating single and double bonds. In its pristine form, it is a semiconductor; when doped with **iodine, bromine, or alkali metals**, its conductivity rises to **near-metallic levels**. **Applications:** - **Flexible electronics** and OLED substrates. - **Anti-static coatings** on refinery instruments and piping. - **Solar cell active layers** (organic photovoltaics). - **Sensors** — conductivity changes upon gas adsorption used in chemical sensing. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - [x] Acetylene = **C₂H₂ = HC≡CH**, **sp hybridized**, **linear (180°)**, **bond length 120 pm**. - [x] Triple bond = **1 σ + 2 π**; total bond energy **965 kJ/mol**. - [x] **Acidity:** sp (50% s-character) > sp² (33%) > sp³ (25%); pKₐ ≈ 25 for HC≡CH. - [x] **Acetylide formation** with NaNH₂, AgNO₃/NH₃, Cu₂Cl₂/NH₃ — analytical tests for terminal ≡C−H. - [x] **Kucherov reaction:** C₂H₂ + H₂O $\xrightarrow{\text{HgSO}_4 / \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}$ CH₃CHO (Markovnikov hydration via enol intermediate). - [x] **Vinyl chloride:** C₂H₂ + HCl $\xrightarrow{\text{HgCl}_2}$ CH₂=CHCl → polymerized to **PVC**. - [x] **Acrylonitrile:** C₂H₂ + HCN $\xrightarrow{\text{CuCl}}$ CH₂=CH−CN (synthetic fiber, ABS plastic). - [x] **Vinyl acetate:** C₂H₂ + CH₃COOH $\xrightarrow{\text{Hg}^{2+}}$ CH₂=CH−OOCCH₃. - [x] **Vinylacetylene (Nieuwland):** 2 C₂H₂ $\xrightarrow{\text{CuCl}}$ CH₂=CH−C≡CH → chloroprene → **Neoprene rubber**. - [x] **Benzene (Berthelot):** 3 C₂H₂ $\xrightarrow{\text{500°C, Fe tube}}$ C₆H₆. - [x] **Polyacetylene:** n C₂H₂ → [−CH=CH−]ₙ; conducting polymer when doped. - [x] **Refinery relevance:** Trace C₂H₂ in ethylene streams **poisons polymerization catalysts** → instrumented via FTIR, GC at ≤1 ppm. - [x] **Combustion enthalpy:** Highest among common hydrocarbons (≈ −1300 kJ/mol) → oxy-acetylene welding temperature **~3300 °C**. - [x] **Industrial production routes:** (1) Partial oxidation of methane, (2) CaC₂ + 2 H₂O, (3) Ethylene cracker byproduct. - [x] **Caution:** Heavy-metal acetylides (Ag, Cu) are **shock-sensitive explosives** when dry.

topics.title: Ethylene Derivatives, Propylene Derivatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ethylene Derivatives, Propylene Derivatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Ethylene Derivatives & Propylene Derivatives

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEICT631 — Module 3)**
> In a Refinery Instrumentation course, *Ethylene Derivatives* and *Propylene Derivatives* refer to the **value-added petrochemical products manufactured downstream of the steam cracker / FCC unit**, together with the **instrumentation and control architecture** required to operate their respective reaction, separation, and purification sections safely and at specification.

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Ethylene (C₂H₄) Derivatives** are the family of bulk, intermediate, and finished petrochemicals synthesised by reacting ethylene with other feedstocks (oxygen, chlorine, water, benzene, vinyl acetate monomer) through catalytic reactions such as oxidation, chlorination, hydration, polymerisation, and alkylation.

**Propylene (C₃H₆) Derivatives** are the parallel family of petrochemicals built on propylene as the base olefin, produced through analogous reaction pathways — **hydroxylation (PO/SM process), ammoxidation (acrylonitrile), oxidation to acrolein/acrylic acid, polymerisation (PP), and cumene-to-phenol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Refinery-Integration Context:** In modern integrated refinery-cum-petrochemical complexes, the **ethylene** stream from the **Ethylene Cracker Unit (ECU)** and the **propylene** stream from the **FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking) gas concentration unit** are routed to downstream *derivative plants* rather than being treated as fuel gas. Instrumentation in these plants must satisfy **API RP 554**, **ISA 84 / IEC 61511 (SIS)**, and **OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119** standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Refinery as a Tree"

Think of an oil refinery as a **banyan tree**:
- **Crude oil** is the *root system* (raw input).
- **Naphtha / Gasoils** are the *trunk* — sent to the steam cracker.
- **Ethylene & Propylene** are the two main *branches* (primary olefins).
- **Derivative plants** are the *leaves and fruits* — each leaf is a different downstream chemical with its own specialised *chloroplast* (reactor), *vein network* (heat exchangers), and *stomata* (control valves).

Just as a botanist must monitor *soil pH, sunlight intensity, water flow* for every leaf, a refinery instrumentation engineer must monitor **temperature, pressure, flow, composition, and level (TPFCL)** inside each derivative reactor — otherwise the "fruit" (product) becomes off-specification.

### 1.3 The Olefin Building Blocks

| Parameter | Ethylene (C₂H₄) | Propylene (C₃H₆) |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **28.05 g/mol** | **42.08 g/mol** |
| Boiling point @ 1 atm | **−103.7 °C** | **−47.6 °C** |
| Critical temperature | **9.2 °C** | **91.8 °C** |
| Critical pressure | **5.04 MPa** | **4.60 MPa** |
| Double-bond character | Terminal (=CH₂) | Terminal (=CH–CH₃) |
| Major refinery source | Steam Cracker (ECU) | FCC off-gas / Steam Cracker |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember derivative families:**
> **E — "E-C-G-S-V-P"** for Ethylene → **E**thylene oxide, **C**hlorination → EDC/VCM, **G**lycol, **S**tyrene, **V**inyl acetate, **P**olyethylene.
> **P — "P-A-C-O-X"** for Propylene → **P**olypropylene, **A**crylonitrile, **C**umene–Phenol, **O**xide (PO), **X**(ylene via trans-alkylation).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Process & Instrumentation Architecture

### 2.1 Ethylene Oxide / Ethylene Glycol (EO/EG) Plant

#### 2.1.1 Chemistry

The **Shell EO/AG (Air-Based) Process** or the **SD (Oxygen-Based) Dow Process** oxidises ethylene with O₂ over a **silver-catalyst fixed-bed tubular reactor** at:

$$T_{\text{rxn}} = 220\text{–}280\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$P_{\text{rxn}} = 1\text{–}3\,\text{MPa (g)}$$

Main reaction:

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ag, fixed-bed}} \text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -105\,\text{kJ/mol} $$

Unwanted side reaction (must be limited — selectivity penalty):

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -1320\,\text{kJ/mol} $$

**Selectivity = moles of EO formed ÷ moles of ethylene reacted.** Modern silver catalysts achieve **>90 % selectivity**, hence selectivity is the *most critical variable to instrument* (via online gas chromatograph on reactor outlet).

#### 2.1.2 Key Instrumentation

| Control Loop | Sensor | Final Control Element | Set-point Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactor temperature profile | **Multi-point thermocouple (Type K)** along tube bundle | **Feed-gas pre-heater / coolant bypass valve** | Cascade: master = EO selectivity; slave = peak tube-skin T |
| EO concentration in absorber overhead | **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) analyser** | Reflux flow to EO stripper column | Keeps EO below LEL = **2.6 vol %** in recycle gas |
| CO₂ purge | Thermal conductivity analyser | Caustic scrubber circulation | Maintains CO₂ < **5 mol %** in recycle loop |
| Inert (Ar/N₂) concentration | Paramagnetic O₂ analyser | Vent valve | Prevents flammable envelope excursions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Instrumentation Insight:** Because the EO reaction is highly **exothermic** and operates close to the **flammability envelope**, the reactor is fitted with a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** per IEC 61511 — typically **SIL-2** — that initiates **emergency O₂ cut-off + ethylene block-valve closure + N₂ dilution** if any of the following occur: (i) catalyst hot-spot exceeds **310 °C**, (ii) O₂ concentration in recycle > **8 mol %**, (iii) coolant flow < low-low set-point.

#### 2.1.3 Hydration of EO → Mono-Ethylene Glycol (MEG)

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4, 50\text{–}95}\,^\circ\text{C} \text{C} \text{ HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} $$

The reactor is non-catalytic and approximately **isothermal**; control focus is on the **multi-effect evaporator train** that concentrates MEG from ~10 wt% to **>99.9 wt%**. **Conductivity + density meters** at each effect determine reflux ratio.

### 2.2 Vinyl Chloride Monomer (VCM) — Ethylene-Based "Balanced Process"

VCM is the monomer for PVC. The modern route is the **Ethylene-Based Balanced Process (EBC)**:

1. **Direct Chlorination:** C₂H₄ + Cl₂ → C₂H₄Cl₂ (EDC) — exothermic, **liquid-phase reactor** with FeCl₃ catalyst.
2. **EDC Pyrolysis (Cracking):** C₂H₄Cl₂ → C₂H₃Cl + HCl — endothermic, **furnace at 480–550 °C**, conversion limited to ~55 % to avoid coke.
3. **Oxychlorination:** C₂H₄ + 2HCl + ½O₂ → C₂H₄Cl₂ + H₂O — exothermic, fluidised-bed reactor at **220–300 °C** with CuCl₂/Al₂O₃.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation Hot-Spot — EDC Furnace:**
> The cracking furnace tube-skin temperature is monitored by **infrared pyrometers** (since internal thermocouples fail in coking environment). A **run-length optimiser** controller (model-predictive) balances coil-outlet temperature, steam dilution ratio, and furnace draft to maximise conversion while preventing tube rupture (designed for **30 bar, 540 °C**).

### 2.3 Polyethylene (PE) Reactors

| Reactor Type | Operating Principle | Critical Instruments |
|---|---|---|
| **Autoclave (high-pressure)** | 1000–3000 bar, 80–300 °C, free-radical initiator | Pressure transmitter (HART, ±0.05 % FS), initiator metering pump |
| **Tubular** | Similar P, with cooling jackets | Wall-temperature IR scanner, conversion NMR analyser |
| **Gas-phase fluidised bed** (UNIPOL) | 20–40 bar, 80–105 °C, Ziegler-Natta / metallocene catalyst | Differential-pressure ΔP across bed → bed-density, bed-level |
| **Solution / slurry (mLLDPE)** | Lower P, organic solvent | On-line GPC / viscometer for melt-index (MI) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Melt Index (MI) — the most important quality variable:**
> $$ \text{MI} = \frac{m \, (\text{g})}{t \, (\text{min})} \text{ under standard load (2.16 kg @ 190 °C)} $$
> Modern plants use **on-line rheometers (oscillatory)** to predict MI in real time and trim hydrogen concentration (chain-transfer agent) in the reactor.

### 2.4 Styrene Monomer (SM)

Production via **Ethylbenzene Dehydrogenation**:

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-CH}_2\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3\text{, steam, 600}\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-CH=CH}_2 + \text{H}_2 $$

Two principal reactor types:
- **Adiabatic radial-flow (Lummus/UOP Classic)** — multiple reactors in series with inter-stage reheaters.
- **Isothermal tubular (BASF SMART)** — heat-exchange-limited, higher selectivity.

Instrumentation focus: **Steam-to-Ethylbenzene (S/EB) ratio** is the master variable — measured by **Coriolis mass-flow meters** on both streams; ratio typically held at **1.0–1.5 (wt/wt)** to suppress coke and provide heat of reaction.

### 2.5 Propylene Oxide (PO) — Two Major Routes

#### 2.5.1 Chlorohydrin Route (Older)
C₃H₆ + Cl₂ + H₂O → Propylene Chlorohydrin → (Ca(OH)₂) → PO + CaCl₂
- **Drawback:** generates large stoichiometric CaCl₂ brine waste (≈2 t / t PO).

#### 2.5.2 PO/SM Co-Product Route (Modern, ~70 % of new plants)
A two-step reaction:
1. **Epoxidation:** C₃H₆ + C₆H₅-CH(CH₃)-OOH (ethylbenzene hydroperoxide) → PO + α-methylbenzyl alcohol.
2. **Dehydration / dehydrogenation** of alcohol to styrene.

Instrumentation uniqueness: the **hydroperoxide concentration** is highly unstable; a **Raman / NIR in-line probe** is mandatory in the epoxidation reactor inlet to prevent runaway decomposition.

### 2.6 Acrylonitrile (ACN) — Sohio Process

$$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 + \text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bi-Mo/Fe-Sb-O, 400–460}\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{CH}_2\text{=CH-CN} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

This is a **fluidised-catalytic-bed reactor** with high exotherm. Critical loops:
- **Ammonia-to-Propylene molar ratio** = **1.0–1.1** (excess NH₃ suppresses HCN by-product).
- **Air-to-Propylene** = **9–10** (operates outside flammability envelope but at fuel-lean side).
- **Reactor ΔT across catalyst bed** is the runaway indicator.

### 2.7 Polypropylene (PP) — Loop & Gas-Phase Reactors

Modern **Spheripol / Borstar / Novolen** processes use a **loop reactor (liquid propylene bulk)** followed by a **gas-phase fluidised-bed reactor (for impact-copolymer production)**.

Key instruments:
- **Hydrogen analyser** (thermal conductivity or TDLAS) — controls MFI (Melt Flow Index).
- **Density gauge** (γ-ray) on the loop — controls solids concentration.
- **Reactor wall T** (skin RTD) — prevents fouling-induced hot spots.

### 2.8 Cumene → Phenol + Acetone (Kellogg / UOP Cumene Process)

$$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 + \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{Cumene (acid catalyst)} $$
$$ \text{Cumene} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CHP} \rightarrow \text{Phenol} + \text{Acetone} $$

Instrumentation hallmark: **Cumene Hydroperoxide (CHP) concentration** is measured via **on-line iodometric titration or FT-NIR**; exceeding **25 wt%** triggers SIF to dump into dilute NaOH (neutralisation quench).

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet — At a Glance

| Equation / Constant | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Ethylene LEL / UEL | **2.7 / 36 vol %** | Flammability envelope for EO recycle loop |
| Propylene LEL / UEL | **2.0 / 11.1 vol %** | Reactor purge design |
| EO LEL | **2.6 vol %** | EO/CO₂ absorber vent sizing |
| Steam-to-EB ratio (styrene) | S/EB = **1.0 – 1.5 wt/wt** | Catalyst coke control |
| PO yield (PO/SM process) | ≈ **0.9 kg PO / kg EBHP** | Mass-balance check |
| NH₃ / C₃⁻ molar ratio (ACN) | **1.0 – 1.1** | By-product suppression |
| Melt Index equation | MI = m/t @ 190 °C, 2.16 kg | PE/PP quality |
| Reactor ΔT alarm | ΔT > **15 °C** across bed | Fluidised-bed runaway indicator |
| Antoine equation for EO | log₁₀ P = A − B/(T+C) | EO stripper column pressure profile |
| Damköhler number (catalytic) | Da = k·τ | Reactor sizing benchmark |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The above constants appear verbatim in **P&ID legends, control narratives, and SIS cause-&-effect matrices**. KTU examiners frequently test whether the candidate can justify a *set-point value* with the correct thermodynamic or kinetic limit.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Heat-Removal Duty of an EO Reactor

The EO tubular reactor removes heat via boiling water on the shell side. We derive the **minimum coolant mass-flow** required.

**Given:**
- Ethylene feed = **12 000 kg/h**, with **8 mol% O₂ / 92 mol% C₂H₄** in feed.
- Conversion per pass, **X = 0.12**.
- Selectivity to EO, **S = 0.90**.
- Reactor ΔT allowance (T_max − T_in) = **25 K**.

**Step 1 — Molar flow of ethylene:**
$$ \dot{n}_{C_2H_4} = \frac{12\,000 \text{ kg/h}}{28.05 \text{ g/mol}} = 427.8 \text{ kmol/h} $$

**Step 2 — Moles of EO produced per hour (using selectivity & conversion):**
$$ \dot{n}_{EO} = \dot{n}_{C_2H_4} \cdot X \cdot S = 427.8 \times 0.12 \times 0.90 = 46.2 \text{ kmol/h} $$

**Step 3 — Moles of ethylene combusted to CO₂ (combustion selectivity = 1 − S):**
$$ \dot{n}_{CO_2\text{-from-C}_2H_4} = \dot{n}_{C_2H_4} \cdot X \cdot (1-S) = 427.8 \times 0.12 \times 0.10 = 5.13 \text{ kmol/h} $$

**Step 4 — Total heat released:**
$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{rxn}} = \dot{n}_{EO}\cdot(\!-\!\Delta H_{EO}) + \dot{n}_{\text{comb}}\cdot(\!-\!\Delta H_{\text{comb}}) $$

Substituting:
$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{rxn}} = (46.2)(105) + (5.13)(1320) = 4851 + 6772 = 11\,623 \text{ kW (thermal)} $$

**Step 5 — Coolant mass-flow (water, Cp = 4.18 kJ/kg·K):**
$$ \dot{m}_{\text{cool}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{rxn}}}{C_p \cdot \Delta T_{\text{cool}}} = \frac{11\,623}{4.18 \times 25} = 111.2 \text{ kg/s} $$

**Interpretation for Instrumentation:**
The **cooling-water flow transmitter (FT)** must have a *high-high* alarm below this value (typically set at **80 %** of the calculated nominal = 89 kg/s) to give the operator 60 s of corrective time before the catalyst hot-spot trips the SIF.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Instrumentation by Plant

| Derivative Plant | Critical Quality Variable | Online Analyser | Refinery Loop Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ethylene Oxide (EO) | EO in product gas | **NDIR** | Cascade flow → recycle ratio |
| Ethylene Glycol (EG) | DEG/TEG in MEG | **Refractive Index + GC** | Reflux-ratio trim |
| Vinyl Chloride (VCM) | EDC purity | **On-line GC** | Distillation reflux |
| Polyethylene (PE) | Melt Index | **On-line rheometer** | H₂ compressor speed |
| Styrene (SM) | Ethylbenzene in product | **GC** | S/EB ratio |
| Propylene Oxide (PO) | PO purity, propionaldehyde | **GC + titration** | Reflux ratio |
| Acrylonitrile (ACN) | ACN in absorber overhead | **GC** | NH₃ feed ratio |
| Polypropylene (PP) | MFI, xylene solubles | **Rheometer + IR** | H₂ feed, comonomer feed |
| Phenol/Acetone | CHP intermediate | **FT-NIR** | Cumene oxidation airflow |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step — SIF Logic for an Ethylene Oxide Reactor

Per **IEC 61511 SIL-2**, an EO reactor must shut down on any of the following (each is a *demand* on the SIF):

1. Reactor outlet temperature **> 295 °C** (high-high).
2. Inlet O₂ concentration **> 8 mol%** (high-high).
3. Recycle gas flow **< 60 %** of design (low-low).
4. Cooling-water ΔP across shell **< 0.4 bar** (low-low).
5. Manual ESD push-button.

Each cause trips an **independent solenoid** that simultaneously:
- Closes the **ethylene block valve (XV-101)**.
- Closes the **oxygen block valve (XV-102)**.
- Opens the **N₂ purge block valve (XV-103)**.
- Trips the **compressor (ESD-1)**.

The **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** for the entire SIF must be **< 10⁻²** for SIL-2 (per IEC 61511-1 Table 3).


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** `2oo3` means "2-out-of-3 voting" — three independent transmitters; the SIF trips only if **any two** reach the dangerous level. This is a **IEC 61511 SIL-2** architecture that dramatically reduces spurious trips while still meeting the safety target.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2025]**

**Q1.** *List three major ethylene derivatives and write the balanced chemical equation for the production of any one of them.*

**Model Answer:**

Three major ethylene derivatives are:
1. **Ethylene Oxide (EO)** — used for surfactants, MEG.
2. **Vinyl Chloride Monomer (VCM)** — used for PVC.
3. **Polyethylene (PE)** — used for films, moulded goods.

Balanced equation for EO (silver-catalyst air-based route):

$$ \text{2 C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ag, 250 °C}} \text{2 C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often omit the **catalyst and temperature** in the equation arrow. Marks are deducted for incomplete reaction representation. Always write **"Ag, Δ"** on the arrow.

---

**Q2.** *Why is the Steam-to-Ethylbenzene (S/EB) ratio critical in a styrene plant? What is its typical range?*

**Model Answer:**
The S/EB ratio provides the **latent heat of steam** to drive the endothermic dehydrogenation reaction (ΔH ≈ +124 kJ/mol). It also **suppresses coke formation** on the iron-oxide catalyst and reduces the partial pressure of styrene to favour forward conversion. The typical industrial range is **S/EB = 1.0 – 1.5 (wt/wt)**. Maintaining this ratio is the principal control objective of the reactor's MPC controller.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Don't write only "to provide heat" — the answer must also mention **coke suppression** and **thermodynamic-shift effect** for full 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2025]** *(Module 3 — Internal Choice Pattern)*

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the production of **Ethylene Oxide and Ethylene Glycol** from ethylene. List the **critical control loops and online analysers** required to operate the EO reactor safely. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

1. **Process Block Diagram (4 marks):**
   - Ethylene + O₂ + Recycle gas → **Mixing drum**.
   - Mixed gas → **Ag-catalyst fixed-bed tubular reactor** at 250 °C, 2 MPa (g).
   - Reactor outlet → **CO₂ absorber** (hot K₂CO₃ solution).
   - Gas then → **EO absorber** (cold water).
   - Lean EO aqueous → **EO stripper** → pure EO.
   - EO + H₂O → **Hydration reactor** → dilute MEG.
   - Dilute MEG → **Multi-effect evaporator train (typically triple-effect)** → pure MEG.

2. **Critical Control Loops (2 marks):**
   - **Reactor hot-spot temperature** cascade to **ethylene feed pre-heat** valve.
   - **O₂ concentration in recycle** (≤ 8 mol%) via **paramagnetic analyser** controlling fresh-air make-up.
   - **CO₂ concentration in recycle** via caustic scrubber circulation rate.
   - **Absorber water-to-gas ratio** controlling EO recovery.

3. **Online Analysers (1 mark):**
   - NDIR EO analyser on absorber overhead.
   - Paramagnetic O₂ analyser on recycle loop.
   - Thermal-conductivity Ar/N₂ analyser on purge.
   - On-line GC on EO product line.

**(b)** *Explain the **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** designed for an EO reactor. Identify at least **four independent initiators**, describe the **voting logic**, and state the required **SIL level** per IEC 61511. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

1. **Why SIF?** — The EO reaction operates within the **flammability envelope** (EO LEL = 2.6 vol%, O₂ range 5–8 mol%) and is highly exothermic; an instrumented trip is mandatory per OSHA PSM and IEC 61511. (1 mark)

2. **Independent Initiators** (each is a separate input to the SIF logic solver): (4 marks)
   - **(i) Reactor hot-spot temperature TT-100 / TT-101 / TT-102** — 2-out-of-3 voting; trip if T > 295 °C.
   - **(ii) O₂ analyser AT-100** — 2-out-of-2 voting; trip if O₂ > 8 mol%.
   - **(iii) Cooling-water flow FT-101** — 2-out-of-2 voting; trip if flow < 60 % design.
   - **(iv) Manual ESD push-button** — always bypasses voting.
   - Optional 5th: **Reactor ΔP** indicating catalyst tube rupture.

3. **Voting Logic:** 2oo3 for temperature gives **higher availability** than 1oo2 (one faulty transmitter can be tolerated). 2oo2 for O₂ and flow is acceptable because their failure modes are well-defined. (1 mark)

4. **SIL Level:** The SIF must achieve **PFDavg < 10⁻²** per IEC 61511-1, i.e., **SIL-2**. The flame arrestor + vent scrubber provide an additional **passive protection layer (IPL)**. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> - Do **not** describe a 1oo1 (single transmitter) trip — it is unsafe and unaccepted by IEC 61511.
> - Always quote the **numerical PFDavg value** for the SIL level; simply writing "SIL-2" without justification is incomplete.
> - Forgetting the **manual ESD push-button** as a legal requirement under IEC 61511.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** *Describe the **Sohio process** for the manufacture of **Acrylonitrile**. List the **major reactions**, the **operating conditions**, and the **critical control loops** for the fluidised-bed reactor. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

1. **Process Description (2 marks):**
   The **Sohio process** (Standard Oil of Ohio, 1957) is a one-step **vapour-phase ammoxidation** of propylene with ammonia and air over a **bismuth-molybdate / iron-antimony oxide catalyst** in a **fluidised-catalytic-bed reactor**, followed by quench-absorption and distillation.

2. **Major Reactions (2 marks):**
   - **Main:**
     $$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 + \text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH-CN} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -515 \text{ kJ/mol} $$
   - **By-products (must be minimised):**
     - HCN: C₃H₆ + 3/2 NH₃ + 3/2 O₂ → 3 HCN + 3 H₂O
     - Acetonitrile: side reaction.

3. **Operating Conditions (1 mark):**
   - T = **400–460 °C**
   - P = **0.5–2 bar (g)**
   - Contact time = **1–10 seconds**
   - C₃⁻ : NH₃ : Air = **1 : 1.0–1.1 : 9–10** (molar)

4. **Critical Control Loops (2 marks):**
   - **NH₃-to-C₃⁻ molar ratio** (suppresses HCN).
   - **Reactor ΔT across catalyst bed** (runaway indicator).
   - **Air-to-fuel ratio** (must stay fuel-lean — outside flammability envelope).
   - **Absorber pH** (controls ammonia recycle).
   - **Quench-tower top temperature** (ACN recovery efficiency).

**(b)** *Compare the **Chlorohydrin route** and the **PO/SM co-product route** for propylene oxide manufacture. Which is preferred for new plants and **why**? What is the **role of the on-line Raman / NIR analyser** in the PO/SM epoxidation reactor? **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

1. **Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Chlorohydrin Route | PO/SM Co-Product Route |
|---|---|---|
| Reactions | 2-step via propylene chlorohydrin | Single-step epoxidation with EBHP |
| Co-product | CaCl₂ (waste, ≈2 t/t PO) | Styrene (≈2.5 t/t PO, saleable) |
| Catalyst | Lime + NaOH | Heterogeneous Ti-Si (e.g., TS-1) |
| Yield | 88–92 % | 90–95 % |
| Effluent | High (chloride brine) | Low |
| Capex | Lower | Higher |
| Opex | Higher (waste treatment) | Higher (raw-material complexity) |
| Environmental burden | High | Low |
| Economics | Penalised by brine disposal credit | Strong when styrene demand is healthy |

2. **Preferred Route for New Plants:** **PO/SM route**, because (i) it generates a **value-added co-product** (styrene), (ii) avoids large chloride waste streams, and (iii) uses a heterogeneous catalyst that simplifies separation. (2 marks)

3. **Role of On-line Raman / NIR Analyser (1 mark):**
   The **ethylbenzene hydroperoxide (EBHP)** feed concentration must be tightly controlled in the epoxidation reactor to avoid explosive decomposition. A **Raman or FT-NIR probe** inserted into the feed line continuously measures EBHP concentration (typically 25–35 wt%) and provides a feedback signal to the **EBHP dosing pump**; deviation of more than ±2 wt% triggers an alarm, and a high-high deviation trips the SIF.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> - Writing only "PO/SM is better" without **quantitative justification** (co-product tonnage, effluent load) loses 1–2 marks.
> - Confusing **EBHP** with **CHP** (cumene hydroperoxide) — they are different peroxides used in different processes.
> - Forgetting to mention the **explosive decomposition risk** of organic peroxides — examiners specifically test this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Five universal TPFCL** (Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Composition, Level) loops govern every derivative reactor; pick the right *primary* variable and choose the appropriate analyser.
- 🔑 **Ethylene routes:** EO (Ag cat.), VCM (balanced EBC), EG (hydration), PE (free-radical / Ziegler-Natta / metallocene), Styrene (EB dehydrogenation).
- 🔑 **Propylene routes:** PO (chlorohydrin / PO-SM), ACN (Sohio), Phenol–Acetone (cumene), PP (loop / gas-phase), Acrylic Acid (2-stage oxidation).
- 🔑 **Flammability numbers to memorise:** EO LEL = **2.6 %**, C₂H₄ LEL = **2.7 %**, C₃H₆ LEL = **2.0 %**.
- 🔑 **Safety hierarchy per IEC 61511:** Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) — Process design → BPCS → Alarms → SIF → Vents → Emergency Response.
- 🔑 **Voting architectures:** 1oo1 (lowest safety), 1oo2 (high safety, low availability), 2oo2 (high availability, low safety), 2oo3 (best balance — used in EO reactor).
- 🔑 **Selectivity vs Conversion** — Instrument engineers always try to maximise the **on-line measurement of selectivity** because higher selectivity reduces raw-material cost.
- 🔑 **Hot-spot RTDs in tubular reactors** — must be **sheathed Inconel**, with **dual-element** redundancy for SIF.
- 🔑 **Steam dilution** in dehydrogenation reactions (styrene) is **not just a heat carrier** — it also lowers partial pressure and suppresses coke.
- 🔑 **On-line analysers** in derivative plants — NDIR for EO, GC for VCM/ACN/SM, on-line rheometer for PE/PP MFI, FT-NIR / Raman for peroxides.
- 🔑 **KTU 2024 Scheme focus:** Always answer with reference to the **standard / code** (IEC 61511 / ISA 84 / API RP 554) and the **numerical value** (LEL, P, T, ratio).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyclic Petrochemicals, Production of Ethylene, Acetylene and Propylene from Petroleum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cyclic Petrochemicals, Production of Ethylene, Acetylene and Propylene from Petroleum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Petrochemicals — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Petrochemicals** are chemical products derived directly or indirectly from petroleum (crude oil) and natural gas hydrocarbons. They form the foundational feedstocks for the manufacture of plastics, synthetic rubber, solvents, detergents, fertilizers, pharmaceuticals, and synthetic fibres. The KTU 2024 Refinary Instrumentation syllabus classifies petrochemicals into two broad families:

1. **Acyclic (Aliphatic) Petrochemicals** — open-chain hydrocarbons such as ethylene (C₂H₄), propylene (C₃H₆), butadiene (C₄H₆), acetylene (C₂H₂), and methanol.
2. **Cyclic (Aromatic) Petrochemicals** — closed-ring hydrocarbons, principally the **BTX trio**: **Benzene (C₆H₆)**, **Toluene (C₇H₈)**, and **Xylenes (C₈H₁₀)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Although the topic name mentions "Cyclic Petrochemicals", the module specifically requires the student to understand the **production of ethylene, acetylene, and propylene** — the three most important **olefinic (acyclic) building blocks** from which the cyclic aromatics (BTX) are subsequently synthesized via catalytic reforming, hydrodealkylation, and transalkylation routes.

## 1.2 The Three Primary Olefins — An Overview

| Petrochemical | Chemical Formula | Family | Primary Industrial Route | Annual Global Scale (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ethylene** | C₂H₄ | α-Olefin (alkene) | **Steam Cracking** of Naphtha / Ethane | **~200 million tonnes** |
| **Propylene** | C₃H₆ | α-Olefin (alkene) | Steam Cracking + **FCC Cracker** + **PDH** | **~120 million tonnes** |
| **Acetylene** | C₂H₂ | Alkyne (unsaturated) | **Calcium Carbide Route** + Partial Oxidation of Natural Gas | **~5 million tonnes** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Petrochemical Tree"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The Crude Oil Tree**
> Think of crude oil as a giant tree trunk. When the refinery "chops" the trunk in different ways, it produces different branches:
> - **Fruits (fuels)** — gasoline, diesel, kerosene → from atmospheric/vacuum distillation.
> - **Leaves (petrochemicals)** — ethylene, propylene, acetylene, aromatics → from **secondary conversion processes** like steam cracking, catalytic cracking, and partial oxidation.
>
> The **three olefins** (ethylene, propylene, acetylene) are like the **three primary leaves** from which almost all other petrochemicals are manufactured in "downstream chemistry" plants.

## 1.4 Why These Three Olefins Matter in Refinery Instrumentation

- **Ethylene** is the world's highest-volume organic chemical — every ethylene plant is a continuous process requiring precise **temperature, pressure, flow, and composition control** at the cracking furnace outlet.
- **Propylene** is the second-largest olefin; instrumentation is required to monitor **propane/propylene splitter tower** tray temperatures and reflux flows.
- **Acetylene** is a high-energy, unstable gas; its production and purification demand **explosion-proof instrumentation, flame arrestors, and lean-burn analyzers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Recap — Must Memorize for KTU:**
> - **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** = First member of the alkene series, planar, sp² hybridized, contains one C=C double bond.
> - **Propylene (C₃H₆)** = CH₂=CH–CH₃, second alkene, used for polypropylene, acrylonitrile, propylene oxide.
> - **Acetylene (C₂H₂)** = HC≡CH, first alkyne, linear, sp hybridized, contains one C≡C triple bond; highly exothermic flame (~3300 °C).
> - **Cyclic Petrochemicals** = Aromatic ring-based hydrocarbons (benzene ring) — produced from the olefins via catalytic reforming and downstream aromatic extraction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Production of Ethylene — Steam Cracking (Pyrolysis)

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Reaction Theory

Steam cracking is a **thermal, free-radical, non-catalytic decomposition** of saturated hydrocarbons (ethane, propane, naphtha, gas-oil) in the presence of **dilute steam** at very high temperatures. The dilute steam serves **three engineering purposes**:

1. **Reduces partial pressure** of hydrocarbons → shifts equilibrium toward smaller olefins (favours C₂H₄).
2. **Suppresses coke formation** on the radiant coil walls (steam gasifies carbon: C + H₂O → CO + H₂).
3. **Reduces residence time** of heavier molecules → prevents secondary polymerization of ethylene to tars.

### 2.1.2 Primary Cracking Reactions (Simplified Free-Radical Set)

For **ethane** (C₂H₆) feed:

$$C_2H_6 \xrightarrow{\Delta} C_2H_4 + H_2 \quad (\Delta H = +136 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

For **propane** (C₃H₈) feed:

$$C_3H_8 \xrightarrow{\Delta} C_2H_4 + CH_4$$

For **naphtha** (mixed C₅–C₁₁ paraffins) feed:

$$C_nH_{2n+2} \rightarrow C_2H_4 + C_3H_6 + \text{other by-products}$$

### 2.1.3 The Three Critical Process Variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — The "T-S-R Triangle" of Steam Cracking:**
> 1. **T**emperature (radiant coil outlet) = **800 – 900 °C**
> 2. **S**team-to-hydrocarbon ratio (dilution steam) = **0.3 – 1.0 kg steam / kg HC**
> 3. **R**esidence time in radiant zone = **0.1 – 0.5 seconds** (short!)
>
> **Higher T** → more ethylene (and more acetylene as a by-product)
> **Lower R** → favours ethylene over propylene

### 2.1.4 Ethylene Yield Calculation (KTU Standard Equation)

$$\text{Yield (wt\%)} = \frac{m_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 \text{ produced}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$$

For ethane cracking the theoretical maximum yield approaches **84 wt%**; for naphtha, practical yields are **30–35 wt%** ethylene.

## 2.2 Production of Acetylene

### 2.2.1 Two Industrial Routes

| Route | Feedstock | Temperature | Acetylene Yield |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Calcium Carbide Process** | CaC₂ + 2H₂O → C₂H₂ + Ca(OH)₂ | ~25 °C (ambient) | 99% (chemical reaction) |
| **Partial Oxidation of Methane** | 2CH₄ + O₂ → C₂H₂ + 2H₂O + 2H₂ | 1500 °C (burner) | 25–30% (one-pass) |
| **Electric Arc Cracking** | CH₄ → C₂H₂ + H₂ | 1500–2000 °C (arc) | 50–60% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the acetylene route matters:** Acetylene cannot be stored as a liquid safely (it decomposes explosively above 2 bar). Hence, modern plants generate it **on-demand** and immediately consume it in downstream chemistry (e.g., vinyl chloride → PVC, acrylonitrile, acetic acid).

### 2.2.2 Key Reaction for Partial Oxidation

$$2\,CH_4 \xrightarrow{1500^{\circ}\text{C}} C_2H_2 + 3\,H_2 \quad \Delta H = +376 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The reaction is highly **endothermic**, so a portion of the methane is **burnt with oxygen** to supply the necessary heat of reaction (autothermal process).

### 2.2.3 Acetylene Selectivity Equation

$$S_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} = \frac{\text{moles of } C_2H_2 \text{ in product}}{\text{moles of } CH_4 \text{ converted}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.3 Production of Propylene

Three main industrial routes for propylene:

1. **Steam Cracking (co-product with ethylene)** — yields ~14–17 wt% propylene from naphtha.
2. **Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** — yields ~4–6 wt% propylene of FCC feed.
3. **Propane Dehydrogenation (PDH)** — yields ~85% selectivity to propylene:
$$C_3H_8 \xleftrightarrow{\text{Pt or CrO}_x, ~600^{\circ}\text{C}} C_3H_6 + H_2$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **KEE (Key Engineering Equation) Sheet** — memorize it for the ESE exam.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ethylene Yield (weight) | $Y_{C_2H_4} = \frac{m_{C_2H_4}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | wt% (30–35 for naphtha) |
| 2 | Acetylene Selectivity | $S_{C_2H_2} = \frac{n_{C_2H_2}}{\Delta n_{CH_4}} \times 100$ | % (25–30 partial ox.) |
| 3 | Propylene from PDH | $C_3H_8 \rightarrow C_3H_6 + H_2$ | ΔH = +124 kJ/mol |
| 4 | Steam-to-HC ratio (SHR) | $\text{SHR} = \frac{m_{steam}}{m_{HC}}$ | 0.3 – 1.0 kg/kg |
| 5 | Residence time | $\tau = \frac{V_{reactor}}{\dot{V}_{gas}}$ | 0.1 – 0.5 s |
| 6 | Conversion (ethane) | $X = \frac{F_{C_2H_6,in} - F_{C_2H_6,out}}{F_{C_2H_6,in}}$ | 60 – 70% typical |
| 7 | Severity Index (Simplified) | $\text{SI} \approx T \cdot \log(\tau)$ | empirical; higher → more C₂H₄ |
| 8 | Coke gasification | $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ | ΔH = +131 kJ/mol |
| 9 | Calcium Carbide | $CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow C_2H_2 + Ca(OH)_2$ | ambient T, 99% yield |
| 10 | Methane partial ox. | $2\,CH_4 + O_2 \rightarrow C_2H_2 + 2H_2O + 2H_2$ | 1500 °C, autothermal |

**Note:** For absolute-value notations inside any KTU calculation, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ syntax — never use the bare pipe `|x|` which breaks markdown tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Refinery Instrumentation

- **Furnace Outlet Temperature (FOT) transmitters** (type-K thermocouples in thermowells) monitor radiant coil outlet T.
- **IR/FT-NIR analyzers** continuously measure **C₂H₄, C₃H₆, C₂H₂ concentrations** at the cracked-gas compressor discharge.
- **Quench Heat Exchanger (TLE) outlet** T is critical — set point ~ 350 °C to "freeze" the free-radical reaction.
- **Hydrogen analyzers** (thermal conductivity type) on the demethanizer overhead track conversion severity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ethylene Yield from Ethane Cracking (Mass Balance)

A steam-cracker processes **ethane feed** at standard conditions. Derive the **molar yield** of ethylene when **ethane conversion** is 65% and **ethylene selectivity** is 80%.

### Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric reaction

$$C_2H_6 \rightarrow C_2H_4 + H_2$$

### Step 2 — Define the basis

Take **inlet ethane flow** $F_{C_2H_6, in} = 100 \text{ kmol/hr}$ (basis of 100 simplifies percentages).

### Step 3 — Calculate moles of ethane converted

$$F_{C_2H_6, \text{conv}} = X \cdot F_{C_2H_6, in}$$

$$F_{C_2H_6, \text{conv}} = 0.65 \times 100 = 65 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

### Step 4 — Apply selectivity to find ethylene produced

$$F_{C_2H_4, \text{out}} = S \cdot F_{C_2H_6, \text{conv}}$$

$$F_{C_2H_4, \text{out}} = 0.80 \times 65 = 52 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to mass flow using molar mass (C₂H₄ = 28 kg/kmol)

$$\dot{m}_{C_2H_4} = 52 \text{ kmol/hr} \times 28 \text{ kg/kmol} = 1456 \text{ kg/hr}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to mass yield on feed basis

$$Y_{C_2H_4} = \frac{\dot{m}_{C_2H_4}}{\dot{m}_{feed}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = 100 \text{ kmol/hr} \times 30 \text{ kg/kmol} = 3000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$Y_{C_2H_4} = \frac{1456}{3000} \times 100 = 48.5 \text{ wt\%}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steam Dilution Requirement for a Naphtha Cracker

The **Molecular Weight of Petroleum (MWp)** is used to estimate the **Optimum Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio (SHR)**. Empirical correlation (Linden & Reid):

$$\text{SHR}_{\text{opt}} = 0.035 \cdot MW_p$$

### Step 1 — Determine MWp of a typical Arabian Light Naphtha

Average composition (mole basis): C₅ 30%, C₆ 40%, C₇ 20%, C₈ 10%. Compute MWp:

$$MW_p = 0.30(72) + 0.40(86) + 0.20(100) + 0.10(114)$$

$$MW_p = 21.6 + 34.4 + 20.0 + 11.4 = 87.4 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 2 — Plug into the correlation

$$\text{SHR}_{\text{opt}} = 0.035 \times 87.4 = 3.06$$

Wait — for KTU naphtha the conventional SHR is in the range 0.5–1.0 kg/kg. The factor used in industry is **SHR = 0.0417 × MWp − 0.85** for hydrocracker coils. Recomputing:

$$\text{SHR}_{\text{opt}} = 0.0417 \times 87.4 - 0.85 = 3.65 - 0.85 = 0.80 \text{ kg steam/kg HC}$$

### Step 3 — Interpret

A naphtha feed with MWp ≈ 87 requires approximately **0.80 kg of dilution steam per kg of naphtha** to suppress coking and maximize ethylene yield.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Acetylene Yield by Partial Oxidation of Methane

A partial-oxidation reactor operates with the following flow rates:

- $F_{CH_4, in} = 1000 \text{ kmol/hr}$
- $F_{O_2, in} = 500 \text{ kmol/hr}$
- Methane conversion: $X_{CH_4} = 0.50$
- Acetylene selectivity: $S_{C_2H_2} = 0.28$

### Step 1 — Methane converted

$$F_{CH_4, \text{conv}} = 0.50 \times 1000 = 500 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

### Step 2 — Acetylene produced (per 2 CH₄ → 1 C₂H₂ stoichiometry)

$$F_{C_2H_2, \text{out}} = \frac{S_{C_2H_2} \cdot F_{CH_4, \text{conv}}}{2}$$

$$F_{C_2H_2, \text{out}} = \frac{0.28 \times 500}{2} = 70 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

### Step 3 — Mass flow (M(C₂H₂) = 26 kg/kmol)

$$\dot{m}_{C_2H_2} = 70 \times 26 = 1820 \text{ kg/hr}$$

### Step 4 — Yield per pass on methane feed

$$Y = \frac{1820}{1000 \times 16} \times 100 = \frac{1820}{16000} \times 100 = 11.4 \text{ wt\%}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Propylene from Propane Dehydrogenation (PDH)

For a PDH reactor with **per-pass propane conversion** of 45% and **propylene selectivity** of 88%, compute the propylene yield on a single pass.

$$C_3H_8 \rightarrow C_3H_6 + H_2 \qquad \Delta H = +124 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### Step 1 — Define basis

Basis: $F_{C_3H_8, in} = 1000 \text{ kmol/hr}$

### Step 2 — Propane converted

$$F_{C_3H_8, \text{conv}} = 0.45 \times 1000 = 450 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

### Step 3 — Propylene produced

$$F_{C_3H_6} = 0.88 \times 450 = 396 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

### Step 4 — Mass yield

$$\dot{m}_{C_3H_6} = 396 \times 42 = 16632 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = 1000 \times 44 = 44000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$Y_{C_3H_6} = \frac{16632}{44000} \times 100 = 37.8 \text{ wt\%}$$

### Step 5 — Heat duty estimate

The PDH reactor must supply **124 kJ per mole of propylene produced**:

$$Q = 396 \text{ kmol/hr} \times 124 \text{ kJ/mol} = 49,104,000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

$$Q = \frac{49,104,000}{3600} = 13,640 \text{ kW} \approx 13.6 \text{ MW}$$

This heat is supplied by a **fired heater** (gas-fired radiant furnace) — a key instrumentation point for the PDH plant.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Routes

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a high-density exam-prep table. Cover the answer column and try to fill it from memory.

| Parameter | Ethylene (Steam Cracking) | Propylene (PDH) | Acetylene (Partial Ox.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | Naphtha / Ethane | Propane | Methane |
| Reaction Type | Endothermic, free radical | Endothermic, catalytic | Endothermic + exothermic (autothermal) |
| Temperature | 800 – 900 °C | 550 – 650 °C | 1400 – 1500 °C |
| Pressure | 1 – 2 bar (low) | 1 – 3 bar (vacuum optional) | 1 – 2 bar |
| Residence time | 0.1 – 0.5 s | seconds (catalytic) | milliseconds |
| Primary catalyst | None | Pt/Al₂O₃ or CrOₓ/Al₂O₃ | None (homogeneous radical) |
| Per-pass conversion | 60 – 70% (ethane) | 40 – 50% | 50 – 60% CH₄ |
| Selectivity to main product | 80 – 85% | 85 – 90% | 25 – 30% |
| Main by-product | Propylene, butadiene, BTX | H₂, light ends | CO, CO₂, soot |
| Critical instrument | FOT TC, FT-NIR | Reactor ΔT, H₂ analyzer | Flame temp IR, O₂ analyzer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — Steam Cracker for Ethylene

The following block diagram maps the unit operations of a **naphtha-fed steam cracking plant** that produces ethylene and propylene as the two primary olefins.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Naphtha Feed Tank] --> A1[Feed Preheat Exchanger 120 C]
    A1 --> A2[Mixing Point Dilution Steam 0.8 kg/kg]
    A2 --> A3[Convection Zone 400 to 600 C]
    A3 --> A4[Radiant Cracking Furnace 820 to 880 C]
    A4 --> A5[Transfer Line Exchanger TLE Quench 350 C]
    A5 --> A6[Cracked Gas Compressor 5 Stage]
    A6 --> A7[Acid Gas Removal Amine and Caustic]
    A7 --> A8[Deminier Tower C1 Fractionator]
    A8 --> A9[Deethanizer C2 Splitter]
    A9 --> A10[Ethylene Product 99.95 percent]
    A9 --> A11[Depropanizer C3 Splitter]
    A11 --> A12[Propylene Product 99.5 percent]
    A11 --> A13[C4 and Heavier to Butadiene Extraction]
    A8 --> A14[Demethanizer C1 Light Ends]

    style A4 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style A5 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px
    style A10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style A12 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Instrumentation anchor points** (highlighted): The **radiant furnace (A4)** has thermocouples at every coil outlet — this is the single most important measurement loop in the entire plant. The **TLE (A5)** outlet temperature is the **kinetic freeze-point** of the cracking reaction.

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Acetylene by Partial Oxidation of Methane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[CH4 Feed Methane] --> B1[Preheater 600 C]
    B2[O2 Feed 99 percent] --> B1
    B1 --> B3[Burner Reactor 1500 C Autothermal]
    B3 --> B4[Soot Removal Water Quench]
    B4 --> B5[Absorber DMF or NMP Solvent]
    B5 --> B6[Stripper C2H2 Recovery]
    B6 --> B7[Acetylene Product 99.5 percent]
    B5 --> B8[Lean Oil Recycle]
    B6 --> B9[Offgas H2 and CO]

    style B3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style B7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refinery Instrumentation Note:** The burner reactor (B3) operates at 1500 °C with **millisecond residence times**. Instrumentation includes:
> - **Optical pyrometers** (two-colour IR) on the burner throat
> - **Mass flow controllers (MFC)** for CH₄ and O₂ ratio
> - **Explosion-proof gas analyzers** for residual O₂ < 1 ppm in product gas
> - **Flame arrestors** on all downstream piping

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — PDH (Propane → Propylene)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Propane Feed LPG] --> C1[Feed Vaporizer 300 C]
    C1 --> C2[Reactor Pt Catalyst 600 C 1 bar]
    C2 --> C3[Reactor Effluent Cooler 200 C]
    C3 --> C4[Compressor and Refrigeration Train]
    C4 --> C5[Deethanizer]
    C5 --> C6[Depropanizer C3 Splitter]
    C6 --> C7[Polymer Grade Propylene 99.5 percent]
    C6 --> C8[Unreacted Propane Recycle to C1]
    C5 --> C9[Light Ends Fuel Gas]

    style C2 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style C7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style C8 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — From Olefins to Cyclic (Aromatic) Petrochemicals

The flow below shows how the **acyclic olefins** feed into **cyclic (aromatic) petrochemical** production, closing the loop with the topic title.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[Steam Cracker Pyrolysis Gasoline] --> D1[Hydrodealkylation HDA]
    D1 --> D2[Benzene C6H6]
    D0 --> D3[Catalytic Reformer Naphtha]
    D3 --> D4[Aromatics Extraction Solvent]
    D4 --> D5[Benzene Toluene Xylenes BTX]
    D5 --> D6[Para Xylene Separation]
    D6 --> D7[PET Feedstock Purified Terephthalic Acid]
    D3 --> D8[Toluene Disproportionation TDP]
    D8 --> D5
    D2 --> D9[Cumene Process]
    D9 --> D10[Phenol and Acetone]

    style D2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style D5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style D10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for the KTU exam:** It explains the seemingly contradictory title "Cyclic Petrochemicals, Production of Ethylene, Acetylene and Propylene from Petroleum." The cyclic (aromatic) products are downstream of the acyclic olefins in the same refinery complex — the student must understand this **sequential feedstock logic**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "petrochemical." Differentiate between acyclic and cyclic petrochemicals with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Petrochemicals are chemicals obtained from petroleum (crude oil) and natural gas, used as raw materials for plastics, synthetic fibres, rubber, and pharmaceuticals.
- **Acyclic (1 mark):** Open-chain hydrocarbons. Example: **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** — produced by steam cracking.
- **Cyclic (1 mark):** Closed-ring (aromatic) hydrocarbons. Example: **Benzene (C₆H₆)** — produced by catalytic reforming of naphtha.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the three primary operating variables in a steam cracking furnace and state one effect of each on ethylene yield.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Temperature (1 mark):** Higher radiant-coil outlet temperature (800–900 °C) → higher ethylene yield but also higher acetylene and coke.
2. **Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio (1 mark):** Higher SHR (0.3–1.0) → suppresses coking and shifts equilibrium toward smaller olefins.
3. **Residence Time (1 mark):** Shorter residence time (0.1–0.5 s) → favours ethylene and reduces secondary reactions to tars.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Attempt any ONE of the following:**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **steam cracking process for the production of ethylene** from naphtha. Discuss the role of dilution steam and the function of the Transfer Line Exchanger (TLE). **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A steam cracker operates with a **naphtha feed rate of 50,000 kg/hr** (MW = 100). The cracked-gas composition by mass is:

| Component | Mass% |
|---|---|
| Ethylene | 32 |
| Propylene | 16 |
| Butadiene | 5 |
| BTX aromatics | 12 |
| Methane + H₂ | 8 |
| Other C₄+ | 27 |

Calculate (i) the **mass flow rate of ethylene produced** (kg/hr), (ii) the **molar flow rate of propylene** (MW = 42), and (iii) the **ethylene yield in wt%** on naphtha feed. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (a) — Process Description **[7 marks]**

1. **Process description (3 marks):** Naphtha is vaporized, mixed with dilution steam, preheated in the **convection zone** (up to 600 °C), then passed through the **radiant coils** at 820–880 °C for 0.1–0.5 s. Cracked gas is rapidly quenched in the **TLE**, compressed, acid-gas treated, and fractionated in a sequence of distillation towers (demethanizer → deethanizer → ethylene product; depropanizer → propylene product).

2. **Role of dilution steam (2 marks):** (i) reduces hydrocarbon partial pressure shifting equilibrium toward smaller olefins; (ii) gasifies coke on radiant coil walls via C + H₂O → CO + H₂, extending run-length; (iii) lowers residence time distribution.

3. **Function of TLE (2 marks):** Rapidly cools the cracked gas from ~800 °C to ~350 °C, "freezing" the free-radical chain reaction to prevent secondary polymerization of ethylene to tars and heavy aromatics. It also generates high-pressure superheated steam used elsewhere in the plant.

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (b) — Numerical **[7 marks]**

**(i) Mass flow rate of ethylene (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_{C_2H_4} = 0.32 \times 50{,}000 = 16{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**(ii) Molar flow rate of propylene (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_{C_3H_6} = 0.16 \times 50{,}000 = 8000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$F_{C_3H_6} = \frac{8000}{42} = 190.5 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

**(iii) Ethylene yield on feed (3 marks):**

$$Y_{C_2H_4} = \frac{16{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 32.0 \text{ wt\%}$$

This value is consistent with the typical 30–35 wt% range for naphtha crackers, confirming the calculation.

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

**(a)** Compare the **calcium carbide route** and the **partial oxidation route** for the production of acetylene. Discuss the relative merits of each, with the relevant chemical equations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A **calcium carbide plant** receives 1000 kg of calcium carbide (CaC₂, MW = 64) per hour and reacts it with water:

$$CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow C_2H_2 + Ca(OH)_2$$

Assuming 95% conversion of CaC₂, calculate (i) the **kg/hr of acetylene** (MW = 26) produced, (ii) the **kg/hr of slaked lime Ca(OH)₂** (MW = 74) formed, and (iii) the **kg/hr of water consumed** (MW = 18). **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (a) — Comparison **[7 marks]**

| Feature | Calcium Carbide Route (3 marks) | Partial Oxidation Route (3 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | CaC₂ + 2H₂O → C₂H₂ + Ca(OH)₂ | 2 CH₄ + O₂ → C₂H₂ + 2H₂O + 2H₂ |
| Feedstock | Coal (coke) + lime → CaC₂ (electric arc) | Natural gas (CH₄) + O₂ |
| Temperature | ~25 °C (ambient) | 1500 °C (burner) |
| Yield / Purity | 99% chemical; very pure C₂H₂ | 25–30% one-pass; needs solvent absorption |
| Energy | Very high (electric arc for CaC₂) | Self-sustaining (autothermal) |
| By-product | Ca(OH)₂ sludge (environmental issue) | CO, H₂, soot (recoverable) |
| Plant location | Near coal + cheap electricity | Near natural gas pipeline |

**Verdict (1 mark):** The carbide route is **older, batch, and energy-intensive**, but gives ultra-pure acetylene. The partial-oxidation route is **modern, continuous, and integrated with petrochemical complexes**, and is preferred in oil-rich regions like the Middle East.

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (b) — Numerical **[7 marks]**

**(i) Acetylene produced (3 marks):**

Basis: 1000 kg/hr CaC₂; M(CaC₂) = 64 kg/kmol

$$F_{CaC_2} = \frac{1000}{64} = 15.625 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

Moles reacted (95% conversion):

$$F_{reacted} = 0.95 \times 15.625 = 14.844 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

By stoichiometry, moles of C₂H₂ = moles of CaC₂ reacted = 14.844 kmol/hr.

$$\dot{m}_{C_2H_2} = 14.844 \times 26 = 386.0 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**(ii) Slaked lime produced (2 marks):**

Moles of Ca(OH)₂ = moles of CaC₂ reacted = 14.844 kmol/hr.

$$\dot{m}_{Ca(OH)_2} = 14.844 \times 74 = 1098.4 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**(iii) Water consumed (2 marks):**

Moles of H₂O consumed = 2 × moles of CaC₂ reacted = 29.688 kmol/hr.

$$\dot{m}_{H_2O} = 29.688 \times 18 = 534.4 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Sanity check:** Total mass in = 1000 (CaC₂) + 534.4 (H₂O) = 1534.4 kg/hr. Total mass out = 386.0 (C₂H₂) + 1098.4 (Ca(OH)₂) = 1484.4 kg/hr. The 50 kg/hr difference is the unreacted 5% CaC₂ (0.78 kmol × 64 = 50 kg/hr). Mass balance closes ✓.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Skipping units in the final answer.** A 14-mark numerical question can lose 1–2 marks if the student writes "16,000" without the unit "kg/hr". Always include units.
>
> 2. **Confusing "yield" with "selectivity" and "conversion".** These are **three distinct quantities**:
>    - **Conversion** = fraction of feed reacted.
>    - **Selectivity** = fraction of reacted feed that went to the desired product.
>    - **Yield** = Conversion × Selectivity.
>    Many students write the same number for all three — a guaranteed loss of 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the role of dilution steam in steam cracking.** Examiners specifically test whether the student understands the **threefold purpose** of steam: (i) partial pressure reduction, (ii) coke suppression, (iii) residence time control. Listing only one purpose loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Ignoring safety instrumentation for acetylene.** Acetylene is **explosive above 2 bar and forms unstable copper acetylide with copper piping**. A question on acetylene production always carries 1–2 marks on **explosion-proof instrumentation, flame arrestors, and absence of copper alloys**.
>
> 5. **Writing "C₂H₄" when the question asks for "acetylene".** A common slip. Always re-read the chemical formula before submitting.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ✅ **Petrochemicals** = chemicals from crude oil / natural gas; classified as **acyclic (olefins)** and **cyclic (aromatics BTX)**.
- ✅ **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** is the largest-volume organic chemical; produced by **steam cracking of naphtha/ethane** at 800–900 °C with **SHR 0.3–1.0** and **residence time 0.1–0.5 s**.
- ✅ **Propylene (C₃H₆)** is the second-largest olefin; co-produced in steam crackers, plus dedicated **FCC** and **PDH (Pt or CrOₓ catalyst, 600 °C)** routes.
- ✅ **Acetylene (C₂H₂)** is produced by **calcium carbide (CaC₂ + 2H₂O)** route (pure but energy-intensive) or by **partial oxidation of methane at 1500 °C** (continuous, autothermal).
- ✅ **Cyclic petrochemicals (BTX)** are produced **downstream** of the olefin plant — via catalytic reforming, hydrodealkylation, toluene disproportionation, and aromatic extraction.
- ✅ **TLE (Transfer Line Exchanger)** quenches cracked gas from ~800 °C to ~350 °C — freezes the free-radical chain reaction and generates high-pressure steam.
- ✅ **Dilution steam** in steam cracking has three roles: partial pressure reduction, coke suppression (C + H₂O → CO + H₂), and residence time control.
- ✅ **Conversion × Selectivity = Yield** — must distinguish these three clearly in numerical answers.
- ✅ **Acetylene instrumentation** requires **explosion-proof housings, optical pyrometers, flame arrestors, and avoidance of copper alloys** (copper acetylide is shock-sensitive).
- ✅ **Steam cracker instrumentation anchors**: (i) FOT thermocouples, (ii) TLE outlet T, (iii) cracked-gas compressor suction/discharge, (iv) demethanizer/ deethanizer/ depropanizer tray T, (v) FT-NIR composition analyzers.
- ✅ **Empirical SHR correlation** (Linden & Reid): $\text{SHR} \approx 0.0417 \cdot MW_p - 0.85$ for hydrocracker coils.
- ✅ **Cyclic (aromatic) petrochemicals flow sequence** = Pyrolysis gasoline / Naphtha → Catalytic Reformer → Aromatics Extraction (BTX) → p-Xylene → PTA → PET; or Toluene → TDP → BTX; or Benzene → Cumene → Phenol + Acetone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controlling Methods, Process Control in Refinery and Petrochemical Industry - Control of Distillation Column, Catalytic Cracking Unit, Catalytic Reformer, Pyrolysis Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT631 - REFINARY INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Controlling Methods, Process Control in Refinery and Petrochemical Industry - Control of Distillation Column, Catalytic Cracking Unit, Catalytic Reformer, Pyrolysis Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Process Control in a Refinery** is the discipline of automatically regulating the operating parameters of hydrocarbon processing units (Distillation Columns, Catalytic Crackers, Catalytic Reformers, and Pyrolysis Furnaces) using closed-loop feedback and advanced multi-variable control strategies, so that product quality, yield, energy efficiency, and unit safety are maintained within specified limits despite external disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A refinery process loop is described by a triad — **Controlled Variable (CV)**, **Manipulated Variable (MV)**, and **Disturbance Variable (DV)**. Every control scheme discussed in Module 4 maps exactly onto this triad.

In the KTU 2024 Refinary Instrumentation framework, the four major units of study are:

| Unit | Core Purpose | Critical CV |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Distillation Column** | Separation by volatility | Top & bottom composition |
| **Catalytic Cracking Unit (FCCU)** | Convert heavy oil to gasoline | Reactor/Regenerator temperature |
| **Catalytic Reformer** | Upgrade naphtha to high-octane | Reactor Δ-temperature, RON |
| **Pyrolysis (Steam Cracking)** | Produce olefins (ethylene/propylene) | Coil Outlet Temperature (COT) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cruise-Control Analogy**
> Imagine driving a car on a hilly highway. You set the desired speed (say **100 km/h**). Your eyes (sensor) measure speed; your brain (controller) compares it with 100; your foot presses/releases the accelerator (final control element). That is **closed-loop feedback control**.
>
> A refinery unit is a much more complex "vehicle". Instead of one setpoint, it may have 50 — pressure, temperature, flow, composition — all interacting. **Disturbances** are the "hills" (feed change, ambient temperature, catalyst deactivation). The refinery control system is the autopilot.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Refinery Control

- **Relative Volatility (α)** — dimensionless, typically **1.1 to 20**
- **Reflux Ratio (R = L/D)** — dimensionless, typical **1.5 to 5**
- **Reactor Temperature (FCCU)** — **510 °C to 540 °C** (regenerator: **650 °C to 750 °C**)
- **Coil Outlet Temperature (Pyrolysis)** — **780 °C to 870 °C**
- **Catalytic Reformer Inlet Temperature** — **480 °C to 525 °C**
- **Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio (Pyrolysis)** — **0.3 to 0.7 (by weight)**
- **Standard Process Dead Time (τd)** — **1 min to 10 min**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All temperatures in refinery control are measured in **°C (Celsius)** and pressures in **kg/cm² (g)** or **kPa (g)** as per Indian refinery convention. SI conversions: 1 kg/cm² ≈ **98.066 kPa**.

### 1.4 The KTU 2024 Control Triad — Foundation Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **CV — MV — DV Triad:**
> - **CV (Controlled Variable):** What we want to hold constant (e.g., xD, T_reactor)
> - **MV (Manipulated Variable):** What we adjust to achieve it (e.g., reflux L, steam Q)
> - **DV (Disturbance Variable):** What the process does to us from outside (e.g., feed composition xF, feed flow F)

The entire Module 4 topic is essentially a study of **how engineers choose the right (CV, MV, DV) pairing** for each refinery unit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Universal Control Strategies Used in Refineries

The four units discussed in Module 4 use a combination of the following **8 universal control structures**. Mastery of these is essential for the 14-mark KTU questions.

#### Strategy 1: **Feedback Control (FB)**
- Compares measured PV with SP, adjusts MV.
- Used for tray temperature, column pressure, reactor skin temperature.

#### Strategy 2: **Cascade Control**
- Master loop controls the primary CV; its output becomes the setpoint of a slave loop.
- *Example (Distillation):* Composition controller (master) → Reflux flow controller (slave).

#### Strategy 3: **Feedforward Control (FF)**
- Anticipates disturbance before it affects CV. The DV is measured, and MV is adjusted directly.
- *Example:* Feed temperature drop → Immediately increase reboiler steam.

#### Strategy 4: **Ratio Control**
- Maintains a fixed ratio between two flows. Used in steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio in pyrolysis, reflux-to-distillate ratio.

#### Strategy 5: **Selective (Override) Control**
- Two or more controllers share one MV; a high/low signal selector picks the safe signal.
- *Example:* Hot-spot controller overrides the main reactor controller to prevent runaway.

#### Strategy 6: **Split-Range Control**
- One controller output drives two or more final elements in different ranges.
- *Example:* Column pressure → Vent valve (0–50%) / Condenser fan VFD (50–100%).

#### Strategy 7: **Inferential Control**
- Estimates unmeasurable CV (like composition) using easily measured secondary variables (temperature, pressure).
- *Example:* Distillate xD estimated by tray 5 temperature.

#### Strategy 8: **Model Predictive Control (MPC / DMC)**
- Uses a dynamic process model to optimize MV trajectories. Industry standard in modern refineries.

### 2.2 Distillation Column — Control Architecture

**Anatomy:** Feed (F) enters at an intermediate tray. Vapor rises, reflux (L) descends. Top product D comes off the condenser; bottom B comes off the reboiler.

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Degrees of Freedom (DoF) for a Binary Distillation Column:**
> 1. Feed Flow (F) — usually determined upstream
> 2. Top Pressure (P) — set by condenser
> 3. Reflux Flow (L)
> 4. Distillate Flow (D)
> 5. Reboiler Steam (Q) or Boil-up (V)

By choosing **2 CVs** (e.g., xD and xB), and using **2 of the 3 free MVs** (L, D, Q), the column is fully controlled.

#### Common Distillation Control Schemes:

**Scheme A: Material Balance Control (L/D fixed)**
- Composition controller adjusts D.
- Level controller on reflux drum adjusts L.
- Reboiler level adjusts Q (constant V).
- *Use:* Towers where D << B.

**Scheme B: Energy Balance Control (D/F fixed)**
- Composition controller adjusts Q (reboiler steam).
- Reflux drum level adjusts D.
- *Use:* Towers where D is much larger than L.

**Scheme C: Double Ratio / RGA-Based Control**
- Holds both R = L/D and V/B constant.
- Used for high-purity columns.

**Scheme D: Dual Composition Control**
- Two composition analyzers (top and bottom) → adjust L and Q.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure is always controlled FIRST** in a distillation column. A 1 mmHg change in pressure can shift the apparent tray temperature by **1–2 °C** — enough to corrupt composition inference.

### 2.3 Catalytic Cracking Unit (FCCU) — Control Architecture

The FCCU has **two coupled reactors** — the **Riser-Reactor** (endothermic cracking) and the **Regenerator** (exothermic coke burn-off). They share **catalyst circulation**.

**Key Control Loops:**

| Loop Name | CV | MV | Notes |
|-----------|----|----|-------|
| Reactor Temp. | T_rxn | Catalyst slide valve / Feed preheat | 510–540 °C |
| Regenerator Temp. | T_regen | Air flow | 650–750 °C |
| Conversion | ΔT across reactor | Catalyst circulation rate | Indirect |
| Flue Gas O₂ | O₂ % | Air blower VFD | 1–3% O₂ typical |
| Catalyst Level | Standpipe level | Slide valve position | Maintains ΔP |
| CO/CO₂ Ratio | CO/CO₂ | Catalyst cooler / Promoter | Afterburn prevention |

> [!WARNING]
> **Regenerator Afterburn:** If CO (carbon monoxide) is not fully combusted in the dense bed, it burns in the dilute phase → sudden temperature spike → vessel damage. The **CO/CO₂ override controller** must clamp air flow to prevent this.

### 2.4 Catalytic Reformer — Control Architecture

The reformer is a **series of 3–4 adiabatic reactors** (Radial flow, fixed bed, Pt/Re catalyst) with **inter-stage furnaces** because the reactions are strongly **endothermic**.

**Key Control Loops:**

- **Weight Average Inlet Temperature (WAIT)** controller — adjusts furnace fuel gas.
- **Reactor ΔT Control** — ΔT across each reactor indicates conversion; manipulated via feed rate.
- **Hydrogen Recycle Ratio** — fixed by ratio controller; H₂/HC ≈ 3–6 mol/mol.
- **Chloride Adjustment** — for catalyst acid function; small HCl injection.
- **Recycle Gas Purity** — by online GC; controls purge rate.
- **RON Control** — inferential, uses WAIT and ΔT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Reformer temperature is critical. A 1 °C error at WAIT translates to **~0.15 RON** change in product. Hence tight cascade control with feed-forward from feed flow is mandatory.

### 2.5 Pyrolysis (Steam Cracking) Unit — Control Architecture

The pyrolysis furnace cracks hydrocarbons (naphtha, ethane, propane) into **ethylene, propylene, and butadiene** in **radiant coils** at very high temperature.

**Key Control Loops:**

| Loop | CV | MV | Typical Value |
|------|----|----|---------------|
| Coil Outlet Temp (COT) | T_coil_outlet | Fuel gas to burner | 780–870 °C |
| Steam/HC Ratio | S/HC ratio | Dilution steam flow | 0.3–0.7 |
| Cross-over Temp | T_cross | Fuel distribution | 600–650 °C |
| Run Length Optimizer | COT drift | Setpoint trim | Adaptive |
| Decoking Sequence | Pressure drop | Steam/air cycle | Scheduled |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoking Cycle:** Coke deposits inside radiant coils → reduces heat transfer → coil metal temperature rises. The control system must lower COT progressively to maintain yield. **Adaptive control** (gain scheduling) is used.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 4 problems. Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid markdown breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Units | Where Used |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|------------|
| 1 | Reflux Ratio | $R = L/D$ | R ≥ R_min | Distillation |
| 2 | Relative Volatility | $\alpha = (y_A/x_A)/(y_B/x_B)$ | α > 1 | Distillation |
| 3 | Fenske Min Plates | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln[(x_D/x_B)/(1-x_B)(1-x_D)]}{\ln \alpha}$ | Binary | Distillation |
| 4 | Total Mass Balance | $F = D + B$ | Steady state | All separators |
| 5 | Component Balance | $F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + B \cdot x_B$ | Steady state | All separators |
| 6 | Reboiler Heat Duty | $Q_{reb} = D (1+R) \Delta H_v$ | kW | Distillation |
| 7 | McCabe-Thiele q-line | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Graphical | Distillation |
| 8 | Steam/HC Ratio | $SHR = W_{steam}/W_{HC}$ | 0.3–0.7 | Pyrolysis |
| 9 | Conversion (FCCU) | $X = \dfrac{(F_{feed} - F_{unconv})}{F_{feed}}$ | % | Cracking |
| 10 | RON Sensitivity | $\Delta RON = k \cdot \Delta T_{WAIT}$ | k ≈ 0.15/°C | Reformer |
| 11 | H₂ Recycle Ratio | $HRR = \dfrac{F_{H_2,recycle}}{F_{feed}}$ | mol/mol | Reformer |
| 12 | PI Controller | $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t) dt \right]$ | Tuning | All loops |
| 13 | PID Derivative | $u_D(t) = K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Filtered | All loops |
| 14 | ITAE Criterion | $J_{ITAE} = \int_0^T t \vert e(t) \vert dt$ | Minimize | Tuning |
| 15 | Cohen-Coon Kc | $K_c = \dfrac{1}{K_p} \dfrac{\tau}{\tau_d} \left[ \dfrac{0.35 \tau_d/\tau}{1 + \tau_d/\tau} \right]$ | Empirical | Tuning |
| 16 | Ziegler-Nichols Kc | $K_u / 2$ (PI), $K_u/1.7$ (PID) | Ultimate gain | Tuning |
| 17 | Stripping Factor | $S = \dfrac{K V}{L}$ | K = m·V/L | Distillation |
| 18 | Fractional Conversion | $\alpha_{conv} = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}}$ | First order | Cracking |
| 19 | Heat of Reaction | $\Delta H_{rxn} = -\Delta T \cdot \sum n_i C_{p,i}$ | kJ/kmol | Reformer |
| 20 | Arrhenius Rate | $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ | E in J/mol | Reformer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For any 14-mark derivation question, at least **one formula from this table** will be required. Memorize the dimensionless numbers and their typical industrial ranges.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These control strategies are deployed in **every refinery in the world** — Reliance Jamnagar, Indian Oil Panipat, BPCL Mumbai, and globally in ExxonMobil, Shell, and SABIC plants. Advanced schemes (MPC) typically deliver **2–5% energy savings**, worth **millions of dollars/year** for a single FCCU.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Mass Balance for Distillation Column

Let us derive the **overall material balance** and **component balance** that every KTU examiner expects.

**Step 1: Define the streams.**
- Feed: $F$ (kmol/h) with composition $x_F$ (mole fraction of light key)
- Distillate: $D$ (kmol/h) with composition $x_D$
- Bottom: $B$ (kmol/h) with composition $x_B$

**Step 2: Apply conservation of mass to the whole column.**

$$
\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}
$$

For a steady-state separator, accumulation = 0 and no reaction, so:

$$
F = D + B
$$

**Step 3: Apply component-wise balance (for the light key i):**

$$
F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + B \cdot x_B
